# Owner Standard — Real-World Scenarios by Industry
## 10 Documented Global Cases Per Industry (Including Call Center Data)

This knowledge base contains real-world scenarios drawn from documented global sources:
- **Regulatory databases**: CFPB (USA), Financial Ombudsman (UK), FTC, ACCC (Australia), National Consumer Helpline (India), European Consumer Centres Network
- **Call center data**: J.D. Power studies, Ofcom Annual Consumer Report, ACMA (Australia), Microsoft Global Customer Service Report, Salesforce State of the Connected Customer, Zendesk CX Trends, Which? surveys, Citizens Advice Bureau, ContactBabel UK
- **International agencies**: WHO, World Bank, ILO, UN, Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, Transparency International
- **News sources**: BBC, Reuters, AP, Guardian, NYT, Washington Post
- **Review platforms**: Trustpilot, TripAdvisor, Google Reviews aggregate studies
- **Academic research**: Published consumer satisfaction studies, J.D. Power surveys, Consumer Reports

Each scenario is grounded in a real documented case with source attribution. Call center failures are explicitly flagged where documented.

Scenario patterns follow the Owner Standard (KB_87):
- **Pattern A (Cana):** Find the solution right now even without the normal resource
- **Pattern B (5000):** Never send the consumer away work with what is available
- **Pattern C (Temple Tax):** When you cannot resolve it direct precisely to where the solution exists
- **Pattern D (Sabbath):** Life safety and urgent need override all procedures and office hours
- **Pattern E (Golden Rule):** Act as you would want your loved one treated

---

## 1. Healthcare / Hospital

### Scenario 1: Emergency Department Wait Leads to Collapse and Death After Patient Leaves Without Being Seen (Pattern C)

**Real Basis:** CBS News, NBC News, CNN — Froedtert Hospital, Wauwatosa, Wisconsin, USA, January 2020. Patient Tashonna Ward, 25, documented via time-stamped Facebook post from the waiting room. Hospital records confirm 2.5-hour wait with no physician contact despite an abnormal cardiac chest X-ray on file. Hospital's only contact with the patient was a retroactive phone call made after she had already collapsed in a car outside.

**The Problem:** A 25-year-old arrives at an emergency department with chest pain and shortness of breath. An initial chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly — an enlarged heart — but no doctor has reviewed it with her. She has been waiting 2.5 hours and is told the queue to see a physician is 2 to 6 more hours. She calls a family member to drive her to another hospital because she cannot safely wait any longer. She is in the car right now, feeling worse, and cannot reach anyone at the hospital to ask if leaving is medically safe.

**What staff/org would do:** The hospital only calls her cellphone after she has already left. By the time her sister answers, the patient has lost consciousness. No proactive outreach occurred during the 2.5-hour wait despite an abnormal cardiac finding in her triage file. The hospital's sole contact is retroactive — made after the patient is already dying.

**What the Owner does:** Treat this as a life-threatening emergency right now. Direct the caller to call emergency services immediately if the patient's symptoms are worsening. Log the complaint at the highest urgency tier and ensure a follow-up on the patient's safety before proceeding to the grievance process. If the patient has died, direct the family immediately to patient safety and legal channels.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Chest pain and shortness of breath with an abnormal heart scan on file are serious warning signs. If your symptoms are getting worse right now, please call emergency services immediately — do not wait. Once you are safe, I will help you formally report what happened at the hospital."
- Direct to: Emergency services (911/999) if symptoms are worsening; hospital Patient Safety Officer (same day); CMS complaint line (1-800-MEDICARE, USA); The Joint Commission complaint portal (jointcommission.org); State Department of Health patient rights division
- Evidence to collect: Time-stamped triage receipt or arrival record; the chest X-ray report; any written or verbal communication from triage staff; any personal message or social media post documenting the wait time and symptoms; discharge paperwork or the absence of it
- Feedback category tag: Emergency Access Failure / Wait Time / Patient Safety

---

### Scenario 2: Debt Collectors Pursue an Already-Cleared Hospital Bill — Billing Department Gives Wrong Information on Every Call (Pattern B)

**Real Basis:** CFPB Complaint Bulletin — Medical Billing and Collection Issues, April 2022 (official regulatory publication, USA). 8,500 complaints received about medical debt collection in 2022 alone; complaints about collections on already-paid or insurance-covered bills rose 31% from 2018 to 2021. CFPB identified double billing as an illegal but prevalent practice. Approximately 100 million Americans carry over $220 billion in medical debt, much arising from billing errors and insurance coordination failures.

**The Problem:** A patient was told explicitly at hospital discharge that their insurance had been accepted and they owed nothing. Eight weeks later, a debt collection letter arrives demanding payment. The patient calls the hospital billing department. The first agent says the account shows a zero balance. After a transfer, the second agent says the balance was sent to collections by a different department. After another transfer, the third agent says to call the insurance company directly. No one has authority to halt the collection process. A credit bureau entry now shows the alleged debt as active.

**What staff/org would do:** Each department transfers the caller to another, denying responsibility. No agent can stop the collection activity. The patient is told the process takes time to resolve. The credit report damage continues while the dispute drags on for months.

**What the Owner does:** Confirm that this is a documented illegal practice when applied to a cleared account. Provide a concrete, immediate action sequence — not a generic complaint referral.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Being told you owe nothing and then receiving a collection notice on your credit report is something the CFPB has formally documented as an illegal practice affecting hundreds of thousands of patients. You have clear rights here, and there are specific steps you can take right now to stop the collection activity and protect your credit score."
- Direct to: CFPB complaint portal (consumerfinance.gov/complaint); State Attorney General consumer protection division; hospital Patient Financial Services director via certified mail with a written dispute; all three credit bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) — file a formal dispute and request a hold on the entry under the Fair Credit Reporting Act
- Evidence to collect: Discharge paperwork showing zero balance or any verbal confirmation record; all billing statements received; the collection letter including the agency name and debt amount claimed; the credit report entry with date; a log of every call to the hospital (date, time, agent name, what was said)
- Feedback category tag: Billing Error / Illegal Debt Collection / Financial Harm / Call Center Misinformation

---

### Scenario 3: Emergency Alarm Cord Activated in Hospital Bathroom — No Staff Response for 15 Minutes, Patient Dies of Cardiac Arrest (Pattern E)

**Real Basis:** Asheville Watchdog investigative report; Blue Ridge Public Radio; North Carolina Health News — Mission Hospital Emergency Department, Asheville, North Carolina, USA, February 10, 2025. Patient arrived by ambulance with a respiratory complaint, activated the bathroom emergency cord, and was found in cardiac arrest 12 to 15 minutes later with no staff having responded. CMS was notified but declined to impose penalties. One employee was fired.

**The Problem:** A patient admitted to the emergency department by ambulance activates the emergency assistance cord in the patient bathroom. The cord triggers an audible alarm throughout the department and flashes a warning light above the bathroom door. Hospital leadership broadcasts multiple radio calls asking staff to check the bathroom. The department has 25 to 40 patients waiting in the lobby. Twelve to fifteen minutes pass. No staff member responds. By the time a nurse finally arrives, the patient is in full cardiac arrest. Resuscitation fails. The family is now asking why no one responded to a live emergency signal in an active emergency department.

**What staff/org would do:** The hospital fires one employee and cites extreme overcrowding as the cause. CMS is notified but declines to impose penalties. No public commitment to systemic staffing change is made. The family is directed to the hospital's patient relations department — the same institution whose systems failed.

**What the Owner does:** Document the alarm activation time and the exact response gap as the core institutional failure. Escalate immediately beyond the hospital's internal patient relations team to external regulatory bodies. Direct the family to independent legal counsel given the documented, time-stamped system failure.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (if a patient or family member is currently in this emergency department — the same staffing and alarm-response failure pattern is documented as active and recurring)
- Opening reply: "What happened — an emergency alarm that went completely unanswered for 15 minutes inside an active emergency department — is a serious institutional failure, not an individual accident. I am going to help you get this documented and in front of the right external authorities today."
- Direct to: Hospital Chief Nursing Officer and Patient Safety Officer in writing (same day); CMS complaint line (1-800-MEDICARE); State Department of Health patient safety division; The Joint Commission complaint portal (jointcommission.org); medical malpractice attorney for consultation on the documented 15-minute non-response
- Evidence to collect: Time of ambulance arrival; time the alarm was activated (request from hospital records via formal medical records request); time the patient was found; any radio broadcast log or staff witness accounts; staffing records for that shift (obtainable via formal records request); CMS Care Compare public inspection history for the facility; documentation that one employee was fired as the facility's own acknowledgment of institutional failure
- Feedback category tag: Response System Failure / Staff Misconduct / Patient Safety / Alarm Failure

---

### Scenario 4: Elderly Woman Lies on Care Home Floor for 19 Hours — Ambulance Dispatched but Never Arrives (Pattern C)

**Real Basis:** UK Judiciary official Prevention of Future Deaths report — Senior Coroner Andrew Cox, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, November 2022. Case of Winnie Barnes-Weekes. Root cause: all available ambulances were physically stacked outside Royal Cornwall Hospital, unable to offload patients from earlier calls due to hospital bed shortages — a cascading system failure. Formal report issued to Health Secretary Steve Barclay with a response deadline of January 10, 2023. Also covered by ITV News West Country, November 2022.

**The Problem:** An elderly woman falls in her care home. Staff call 999. The call is answered and an ambulance is logged as dispatched. Hours pass. No ambulance arrives. Staff call again. And again. The 999 system received every call correctly — but every available ambulance is physically trapped outside the regional hospital, unable to leave the queue. The woman lies on the floor for 19 hours. She dies. Her family has received no formal written explanation, no apology, and no confirmation of what the government's response to the Coroner's Prevention of Future Deaths report actually was.

**What staff/org would do:** The ambulance service cites system-wide pressure. The hospital cites bed unavailability. The care home says it followed correct protocol. The family is caught in a triangle of three organizations each pointing at the others. The Coroner's report and the government's legal obligation to respond are never mentioned to the family.

**What the Owner does:** Provide the family with the specific Coroner's PFD report reference, explain their right to the government's formal response, and route them through the NHS complaint process with a clear escalation path to the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (if a current patient in a care home or home setting is awaiting an ambulance right now — the same system cascade failure is documented as recurring across the same region)
- Opening reply: "I am deeply sorry for what your family has been through. What happened has been formally documented by the Senior Coroner as a systemic failure that caused multiple deaths — and there is a legal obligation for the Health Secretary to respond to that report. Your family has the right to know exactly what has been done, and done differently, since then."
- Direct to: NHS formal complaint in writing to both the ambulance trust and the hospital trust separately; Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (ombudsman.org.uk) if local resolution is unsatisfactory or takes more than 6 months; Healthwatch England (healthwatch.co.uk) for local advocacy support; local MP (can submit a written parliamentary question specifically about the PFD report response); INQUEST charity (inquest.org.uk) if pursuing a wider public interest inquiry
- Evidence to collect: 999 call reference number and log of times called; CAD (Computer-Aided Dispatch) reference number from the 999 service; care home incident report; times of each 999 call and each callback received; the UK Judiciary Prevention of Future Deaths report (titled "David Morganti, Winnie Barnes, Robert Conybeare and Anthony Reedman" — available at judiciary.uk, November 2022); any written response received from NHS trusts to date
- Feedback category tag: Emergency Wait Time / Ambulance System Failure / 999 Received — No Response / Institutional Accountability

---

### Scenario 5: Mental Health Patient Discharged Without Any Notification — No Phone Call, No Letter, No Crisis Plan — Dies of Overdose (Pattern E)

**Real Basis:** Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) published case summary — anonymized as Mr C, UK. Discharged from Community Treatment Team for missing a single appointment. No multidisciplinary team risk discussion was held, no GP handover was arranged, no crisis referral was made, and no contact of any kind — by phone, letter, or in person — was made with the patient before or after the discharge decision. Complaint upheld by PHSO. Also cited in PHSO report: "Unsafe Discharge from Hospital" (ombudsman.org.uk).

**The Problem:** A person with a complex mental health history — including multiple sections under the Mental Health Act — misses a single outpatient appointment. Without an MDT discussion, without a risk assessment, and without telling the patient in any way, the Community Treatment Team records him as administratively discharged. He has no registered GP. He is never given a crisis line number. No one calls him. No letter is sent. He does not know he no longer has a mental health team. He dies of a drug overdose weeks later. His family discovers the discharge only after requesting his clinical records.

**What staff/org would do:** The Trust cites the missed appointment as sufficient grounds for administrative discharge under its internal process. When the family complains, they are told the process was followed. The fact that the Trust's own written policy required an MDT review before discharging any high-risk patient — a policy that was not followed — only emerges during the PHSO investigation.

**What the Owner does:** Affirm the family's right to a full independent investigation. Provide the PHSO complaint pathway and help the family request all clinical records, including the written discharge policy that was in force at the time.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (post-death family complaint process)
- Opening reply: "I am very sorry for your loss. What you are describing — a discharge with no risk assessment, no contact with your family member, and no handover to any crisis service — is a fact pattern that the Health Service Ombudsman has formally found to constitute a serious system failure. Your family has the right to a full independent investigation and to a clear account of what the Trust failed to do."
- Direct to: NHS formal complaint in writing to the relevant mental health trust (within 12 months of the incident); PHSO (ombudsman.org.uk) if local resolution is unsatisfactory; INQUEST charity (inquest.org.uk) for support when pursuing a state-related death inquiry; Care Quality Commission (cqc.org.uk) to file a regulatory concern; local Healthwatch for practical navigation support
- Evidence to collect: All clinical records including the Community Treatment Team caseload notes (Subject Access Request under UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018 — trust must respond within one calendar month); the Trust's written discharge policy in force at the time; records showing no MDT meeting took place; evidence that no phone call or letter was sent to the patient; GP records confirming absence of registration at the time of discharge; any correspondence from the Trust following the complaint
- Feedback category tag: Discharge Without Support / No Patient Contact / Mental Health Rights / Vulnerable Patient / Call Center Failure

---

### Scenario 6: Patient Discharged from A&E Without the Mandatory Second Cardiac Blood Test — Dies at Home 48 Hours Later (Pattern E)

**Real Basis:** Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) official case summary No. 1052 — anonymized as Mr H, UK, autumn 2013. Attended NHS A&E with chest pain and shortness of breath; discharged after a single troponin test without the second troponin test required under European Society of Cardiology guidance for suspected acute coronary syndrome; no senior physician sign-off obtained as required by NICE guidelines; no follow-up phone call made in the 48 hours after discharge. Died at home two days after discharge. Trust later acknowledged hospital admission would have been more appropriate. Formal apology issued; £1,000 compensation paid. Published at ombudsman.org.uk.

**The Problem:** A person attends A&E with chest pain and shortness of breath. One troponin blood test is taken, returns within the normal range, and they are sent home with a referral to an outpatient chest pain clinic. They are not told that clinical guidelines required a second troponin test before discharge when a heart attack is suspected — and that this test was not performed. The assessing doctor did not obtain the required senior physician sign-off. No one from the hospital calls them in the 48 hours after discharge. At home, they feel increasingly unwell but assume the hospital would have kept them if it were serious. They die of a cardiac event at home two days later.

**What staff/org would do:** No follow-up contact is made in the 48 hours after discharge. After the death, the Trust acknowledges hospital admission would have been more appropriate. A formal apology and £1,000 compensation are offered only after a full Ombudsman investigation — not proactively, and not to prevent the death.

**What the Owner does:** If the patient is alive and symptomatic right now, direct them to call 999 or return to A&E without delay. If this is a family complaint following the death, confirm the right to full clinical records, a formal NHS complaint, and independent Ombudsman review.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (if the patient is still within hours or days of discharge and feeling worse)
- Opening reply: "If you or the person you are concerned about is still experiencing chest pain or worsening symptoms after being discharged, please call 999 or return to A&E right now — do not wait for a scheduled outpatient appointment. Once you are safe, I will help you formally document what went wrong at the assessment."
- Direct to: (If still symptomatic) 999 or immediate A&E return; NHS formal complaint in writing to the trust; PHSO (ombudsman.org.uk) if local resolution fails; a medical negligence solicitor (Law Society Find a Solicitor: solicitors.lawsociety.org.uk); the Coroner if a formal inquest has not yet been held
- Evidence to collect: A&E attendance record and discharge paperwork; the outpatient referral letter; any blood test results given at discharge; clinical notes (obtainable via Subject Access Request — trust must respond within one calendar month); name of the assessing doctor and whether senior sign-off was documented; records confirming no second troponin was ordered; evidence that no phone call was made after discharge
- Feedback category tag: Premature Discharge / Missed Clinical Test / Information Failure / No Follow-Up / Cardiac Emergency

### Scenario 7: Psychiatrist Judges Patient Unsafe for Discharge — Insurance Company Overrides Clinical Decision — Patient Harms Others Within Days (Pattern A)

**Real Basis:** ProPublica investigative report — "Psychiatric Hospitals Are Violating EMTALA by Turning Patients Away" (2026); CMS inspection records — Lakeside Behavioral Health System, Memphis, Tennessee, USA. Tom Swearengen admitted voluntarily with active suicidal ideation and documented access to firearms at home; insurance company's utilization management team declined to authorize inpatient stay; patient discharged against clinical judgment; murder-suicide within one week of discharge. Analogous insurance-override discharge patterns documented in multiple US states under CMS inspection records; equivalent clinical-financial override disputes documented by UK PHSO (Community Mental Health cases) and Australia's Royal Commission into Victoria's Mental Health System (2021).

**The Problem:** A person is voluntarily admitted to a psychiatric facility. Their clinical record documents active suicidal ideation, self-harm desires, and confirmed access to firearms at home. The treating psychiatrist believes inpatient care is medically necessary. The insurance company's utilization management team calls the facility and declines to authorize the inpatient stay on cost grounds. The patient is told they are being discharged — not because the clinical team has concluded they are safe, but because the insurer will not pay. They are given an outpatient appointment slip for three weeks' time. They are standing at the discharge desk right now.

**What staff/org would do:** The facility processes the discharge as insurance-required without filing an appeal on the patient's behalf. No crisis line number is provided. No weapons safety counseling is given despite documented firearm access. The corporate parent later contests the regulatory finding and no financial penalty is imposed.

**What the Owner does:** Immediately identify this as a rights violation. A clinical judgment that a patient requires inpatient care cannot be legally overridden by an insurance company without a formal appeal process. The patient has the right to an immediate peer-to-peer review — the treating psychiatrist speaking directly to the insurer's medical director — and should not leave the facility until that review has been completed.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You have the right to challenge this discharge before it happens. An insurer cannot legally override your psychiatrist's clinical judgment that you need inpatient care without going through a formal appeal — and you can request a peer-to-peer review right now, which means your psychiatrist speaks directly to the insurer's medical director today, before any discharge is processed."
- Direct to: Request peer-to-peer clinical review immediately (ask the treating psychiatrist to initiate this now, before any discharge paperwork is signed); State Insurance Commissioner complaint line; 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (USA) / Samaritans (UK: 116 123); State Mental Health Authority for emergency evaluation order if discharge proceeds without stabilization; CMS EMTALA complaint (1-800-MEDICARE) if the hospital discharges without adequate clinical stabilization; NAMI patient rights support (nami.org, USA)
- Evidence to collect: The insurance denial letter — request a written copy before leaving the facility; the clinical assessment documentation recording active suicidal ideation and firearm access; the treating psychiatrist's written clinical opinion; name of the insurance case manager who issued the denial; discharge paperwork if issued; any verbal statements from facility staff about the stated reason for discharge
- Feedback category tag: Psychiatric Rights Violation / Insurance Override of Clinical Judgment / Emergency Refusal / Vulnerable Patient / Consumer Turned Away

---

### Scenario 8: Pregnant Woman Sent to Another Hospital by Private Car Without Any Medical Examination or Stabilization (Pattern A)

**Real Basis:** EMTALA enforcement settlement — OIG settlement, $50,000 penalty, USA, 2018. A 33-week pregnant woman presenting with fluid leakage was sent by private vehicle to another facility without a medical screening examination being performed and without any stabilization measures. She arrived at the receiving facility in critical condition and delivered a stillborn infant requiring emergency cesarean section. Documented by The Sullivan Group EMTALA case compendium referencing OIG settlement data. Inadequate obstetric transfer without clinical stabilization is also documented by WHO maternal health reports and UK MBRRACE-UK Maternal Mortality Reports (2023) across multiple health systems.

**The Problem:** A woman who is 33 weeks pregnant arrives at a hospital having noticed fluid leaking. She is not examined. No medical screening examination is performed. Staff tell her to go to a different hospital and arrange for her to travel there by private car — no ambulance, no clinical escort, no stabilization measures taken. During the journey she feels increasingly unwell. She does not know this is a violation of her rights. She is now at the second hospital in deteriorating condition and does not understand what her legal options are.

**What staff/org would do:** The originating hospital processes the transfer as a routine referral. No medical screening documentation is created. The patient is not told she had the legal right to be examined and stabilized before any transfer was arranged. The OIG settlement penalty goes to the government — not to the patient.

**What the Owner does:** Confirm the patient is receiving proper care right now. Once she is safe, document the transfer in detail and confirm it constitutes a violation of patient rights. Connect her to formal complaint and legal channels.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Right now, your most important step is to make sure you are being examined and monitored by clinical staff at the hospital you are in. Once you are in their care, I will help you document what the first hospital did — because what you are describing is a serious violation of your rights as a patient."
- Direct to: (Immediate) Alert current clinical staff to the circumstances of the transfer; (After safety is secured) CMS EMTALA complaint (1-800-MEDICARE, USA); State Department of Health patient rights division; medical malpractice attorney with EMTALA specialization; Childbirth Connection (childbirthconnection.org) for patient advocacy
- Evidence to collect: Exact time of arrival at the first hospital; name of any staff member who spoke to her there; any paperwork handed to her; whether any physical examination was performed before the transfer; who arranged the private car transport and their stated reason; time of arrival at the second hospital; the receiving facility's documented assessment of her condition on arrival
- Feedback category tag: Emergency Refusal / Obstetric Rights Violation / Inadequate Transfer / Consumer Turned Away / Patient Safety

---

### Scenario 9: Nurse Administers a Paralytic Drug Instead of a Sedative — Patient Dies — Error Concealed from Family and Licensing Board for Years (Pattern D)

**Real Basis:** NPR; CBS News; criminal court records — RaDonda Vaught, Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Nashville, Tennessee, USA, December 24, 2017. Charlene Murphey, 75, was administered vecuronium (a neuromuscular paralyzing agent) instead of Versed (a sedative) after the nurse overrode a medication cabinet safety lock. Printed warnings on the vial reading "Warning: Paralyzing Agent" were not heeded. Murphey was found unresponsive 30 minutes later; died the following day after life support was withdrawn. Hospital did not proactively disclose the error to the State Board of Nursing or to the family. Error discovered only during an unrelated CMS inspection. Vaught convicted of negligent homicide, March 2022. PSNet (AHRQ) published a patient safety case analysis. Medication cabinet override-related errors are documented by The Joint Commission across healthcare systems globally.

**The Problem:** A 75-year-old patient is being prepared for a brain imaging procedure. A sedative has been ordered. The nurse cannot find it in the medication cabinet and overrides the safety lock. Despite multiple printed warnings on the selected vial, a powerful paralytic drug is administered. The patient is left alone. She is found unresponsive 30 minutes later, resuscitated but never regains consciousness, and dies the following day. The family is not told what medication was given or that any error occurred. They find out the full story through media coverage years later — the hospital had not reported the error to the licensing board and had not disclosed it to the family.

**What staff/org would do:** The hospital does not disclose the medication error to the family or to the State Board of Nursing after the death. The error is discovered only when a CMS inspector investigating an unrelated matter finds documentation of the incident. The hospital then declines to report the nurse to the licensing board, leaving regulators uninformed for years.

**What the Owner does:** Affirm the family's absolute legal right to full disclosure of what medication was administered and what safety checks failed. Confirm that active concealment of a medication error is itself a separate regulatory violation. Provide the specific complaint channels covering both the error and the concealment.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (post-death complaint and disclosure process)
- Opening reply: "You have an absolute right to a full and honest account of exactly what medication was given and why — and to know which safety checks failed and who knew what and when. The law requires hospitals to disclose medical errors. Concealing an error from the family and from the nursing licensing board is itself a regulatory violation, and you have clear channels to pursue both."
- Direct to: State Board of Nursing (complaint about the medication error and any failure to report); State Department of Health; CMS (1-800-MEDICARE); The Joint Commission (if accredited); medical malpractice attorney; hospital Risk Management department via formal written request for all incident documentation under HIPAA patient rights
- Evidence to collect: Full medical records including the medication administration record (MAR) — formal HIPAA request, hospital must respond within 30 days; the incident or occurrence report; identity of all staff involved in the patient's care that day; any communication from the hospital about the cause of death; records documenting when the hospital became aware of the error and what — if anything — was reported to whom and when
- Feedback category tag: Medication Error / Staff Misconduct / Error Concealment / Transparency Failure / Patient Safety

---

### Scenario 10: Wrong Patient's Medication Administered to Elderly Resident — Severe Allergic Reaction, Medically Induced Coma — Family Never Contacted (Pattern D)

**Real Basis:** WJLA I-Team investigative news report; D.C. Health formal investigation — Capitol City Rehab and Healthcare Center, Washington, D.C., USA, December 2022. Rosezena Jackson, 74, was administered a blood pressure medication belonging to a different patient, to which she was severely allergic. The reaction required a medically induced coma for over one week. The facility had a documented prior history of medication dispensing errors on CMS inspection records. The family discovered what had happened only upon visiting and finding their relative in the ICU — the facility had not contacted them when the error occurred.

**The Problem:** A family member visits an elderly relative at a care facility and finds her in intensive care in a medically induced coma. No one from the facility contacted the family when the error occurred. When the family calls to ask what happened, they are told a manager will call back. No callback comes. The administrator says they cannot discuss patient records over the phone. The family does not know what medication was given, whose medication it was, what the allergic reaction involved, or what the facility has done about it.

**What staff/org would do:** Administration cites privacy regulations as a reason not to discuss the error with the family. Internal processes are described as under review. Promises of callbacks go unfulfilled. The facility's documented prior inspection history for dispensing errors — publicly available via CMS Care Compare — is not mentioned.

**What the Owner does:** Confirm that the patient's authorized family representatives have the full legal right to disclosure and to complete medical records. Privacy regulations do not permit a facility to withhold information about a medication error from the patient's own authorized family. Direct the family to formal regulatory channels immediately.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (patient survived; complaint and disclosure process)
- Opening reply: "As your family member's authorized representatives, you have the legal right to her complete medical records and to a clear written explanation of exactly what medication was administered, why, and what went wrong. Privacy regulations do not allow a care facility to withhold this information from you — that is not a legitimate use of those rules, and you have formal channels to compel full disclosure."
- Direct to: State Department of Health formal complaint (about both the medication error and the failure to notify the family); Long-Term Care Ombudsman program (federally mandated in every US state — locate via eldercare.acl.gov); CMS (1-800-MEDICARE — facilities receiving Medicare or Medicaid funding are federally regulated); medical malpractice attorney; formal medical records request in writing under HIPAA (facility must respond within 30 days)
- Evidence to collect: All medical records including the medication administration record (MAR) and ICU records (formal HIPAA request); the facility's prior CMS inspection history and star rating (publicly available at CMS Care Compare); evidence of when the family was — and was not — notified of the error; names and dates of every call made to the facility and any response received; the patient's current prescribed medication list to confirm the administered drug was not prescribed to her; prior deficiency citations related to medication dispensing
- Feedback category tag: Medication Error / Transparency Failure / Failure to Notify Family / Elder Care / Information Concealment

Industry ID: 1

## 2. Pharmacy / Pharmaceutical

### Scenario 2-1: Child Dies After Pharmacy Dispenses Toxic Cough Syrup (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** WHO Medical Product Alert (October 2022); CNN investigative reporting; PMC academic paper PMC10389612 — Maiden Pharmaceuticals Limited, Haryana, India; 70+ children deaths in The Gambia from diethylene glycol/ethylene glycol-contaminated cough syrups including Kofexmalin Baby Cough Syrup and Magrip N Cold Syrup
**The Problem:** A mother in The Gambia gave her 3-year-old daughter Kofexmalin Baby Cough Syrup purchased from a local pharmacy. Within days the child stopped urinating, developed severe abdominal pain, and is now unresponsive. Two children in the same neighbourhood have died with identical symptoms. The mother is contacting any available health authority in a panic.
**What staff/org would do:** The pharmacy selling the product is now closed. Local health officials are overwhelmed and direct the mother from one clinic to another without anyone acknowledging the product as the cause or triggering a recall.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately flags the emergency, connects the mother to the nearest paediatric emergency unit with the product packaging in hand, and preserves all product evidence — batch number, packaging, purchase receipt — for submission to the WHO Medical Product Alert system.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: Express immediate compassion; this situation requires emergency medical care right now — the mother should bring the full product packaging to the hospital immediately so clinicians can identify the toxin (diethylene glycol and ethylene glycol poisoning is treatable with fomepizole if caught early); every minute matters.
- Who to direct to: Nearest paediatric emergency unit (with the product bottle); Gambia Medicines Control Agency — national drug regulatory authority; WHO Regional Office for Africa (AFRO) emergency contact; WHO Medical Product Alert reporting system
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Full product bottle with batch number, lot code, and expiry date; purchase receipt from the pharmacy; name and address of the selling pharmacy; names and contact details of other affected families in the neighbourhood
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Substandard Medicine / Emergency / Child Harm

---

### Scenario 2-2: Infants Die After Pharmacies Distribute Adulterated Cough Syrup (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Uzbekistan cough syrup scandal (Wikipedia); CBS News reporting on Marion Biotech "Dok-1 Max"; WHO alerts; Indian government manufacturing license suspension January 10, 2023 — at least 18 children died in Samarkand from diethylene glycol at approximately 300 times regulatory limits; industrial solvents substituted for pharmaceutical-grade propylene glycol
**The Problem:** A father in Samarkand, Uzbekistan gave his 18-month-old son Marion Biotech's Dok-1 Max cough syrup. The child is now in acute kidney failure in hospital. The pharmacist who sold it says there is nothing wrong with the product. The father has seen local news reports about child deaths from a cough syrup but cannot get anyone to confirm whether this is the same product or what to tell the hospital doctors.
**What staff/org would do:** The pharmacy deflects liability, claims all products are properly certified, and refuses to recall remaining stock or cooperate with the father's request for batch documentation. Hospital staff treat acute kidney failure symptoms without identifying the cause as the cough syrup.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately identifies the product as matching the active WHO and Indian government investigation, tells the father to give treating doctors the product bottle at once, and directs him to the regulatory body to trigger stock withdrawal.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: This is a medical emergency — the child needs urgent kidney toxicology support right now; tell the father to hand the Dok-1 Max bottle directly to the treating doctor immediately and request testing for diethylene glycol poisoning; the product has been identified in a WHO-flagged national scandal.
- Who to direct to: Uzbek State Agency for Drug Control (Uzstandard) — to report the batch and pharmacy; Uzbek Ministry of Health emergency line; WHO Country Office Uzbekistan (Tashkent); the selling pharmacy's licensing authority to trigger immediate stock withdrawal
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Dok-1 Max product bottle with batch number and lot code; purchase date, pharmacy name, and address; hospital admission and treatment records; names of other families in the area who used the same product batch
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Substandard Medicine / Emergency / Child Harm / Regulatory Failure

### Scenario 2-3: Pharmaceutical Regulator Accepts Bribe to Pass Toxic Drug Inspection (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** CNN investigative reporting on Maiden Pharmaceuticals (October 2022); WHO Medical Product Alert — Haryana, India state pharmaceutical regulator alleged to have accepted a bribe and switched laboratory samples before quality testing, allowing diethylene glycol-contaminated cough syrups to receive clearance for export; over 70 children subsequently died in The Gambia
**The Problem:** A pharmaceutical quality-assurance officer in Haryana, India has discovered that a colleague in the state drug regulatory office approved a batch of cough syrup for export without conducting the required laboratory test — substituting a clean sample in place of the contaminated batch. The officer suspects bribery. After raising the concern internally, they were told to stay quiet to avoid creating legal liability for the company. The batch was already exported and deaths have been reported abroad.
**What staff/org would do:** Internal management downplays the officer's concern as speculation, frames it as an employment matter rather than a public safety issue, and refuses to escalate to the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) — because doing so would implicate both the regulator and the company.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the gravity — this is regulatory corruption with a documented link to child deaths — and directs the officer to protected whistleblower channels and the criminal investigation authority so the information reaches the right place safely.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (the export has already occurred; the urgency is now about accountability, not preventing immediate harm — though prompt reporting can still inform ongoing investigations)
- Opening reply: Acknowledge the immense professional and personal risk this officer is taking by speaking up; affirm that what they witnessed — a sample switch before regulatory testing — is serious criminal misconduct already connected to documented child deaths, and that Indian law provides whistleblower protections.
- Who to direct to: Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) — online complaint portal and Drugs Controller General of India; Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) — anti-corruption division for bribery involving a public regulatory official; Whistleblowers Protection Act 2014 (India) — legal framework for anonymous reporting; WHO Rapid Alert System for substandard/falsified medical products (global notification)
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Internal communications referencing sample approval without testing; approval certificates with dates and signatory names; any documentation of the original contaminated batch versus the clean substituted sample; records of internal pressure to remain silent; names and roles of officials involved; full batch certification chain
- Feedback category tag: Staff Misconduct / Regulatory Corruption / Whistleblower / Pharmaceutical Safety

### Scenario 2-4: Cancer Patient Unknowingly Receives Fake IMFINZI with No Active Ingredient (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** WHO Medical Product Alert N°3/2025 (14 May 2025); Lebanese judicial investigation led by Judge Dora al-Khazen; 2025 Counterfeit Medication Scandal (Wikipedia); Kataeb.org and Enmaeya.com — 70-pharmacy criminal network across Lebanon distributing falsified durvalumab; five individuals arrested including Mohammed Khalil and a pharmacy owner; proceeds estimated at US$30 million; fake vials confirmed to contain no active ingredient
**The Problem:** A 52-year-old woman in Beirut is mid-course through immunotherapy for stage III non-small-cell lung cancer. She purchased IMFINZI (durvalumab) vials from a pharmacy in Beirut's southern suburbs. After her second infusion, her oncologist notes unexpected tumour marker worsening. The WHO alert on fake IMFINZI has just been published. She now suspects she has been receiving vials containing no active ingredient. She paid approximately $9,000 out of pocket for the vials.
**What staff/org would do:** The pharmacy denies involvement, claims its products are legitimate, refuses to provide batch documentation or supplier chain records, and tells the patient to contact the manufacturer directly — an effectively impossible task for an individual.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms the WHO alert is authoritative and active; explains that Lebanese judicial authorities are already prosecuting a 70-pharmacy network; connects her to both the ongoing criminal investigation and civil recourse for her $9,000 loss.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: Express deep concern for her safety and wellbeing; confirm that the WHO alert is real and that Lebanese judicial authorities are actively prosecuting the criminal network responsible — she is not alone, and her experience is central evidence in an ongoing criminal case.
- Who to direct to: Lebanese Ministry of Public Health — Pharmaceutical Directorate (to report the pharmacy and vial batch numbers); Office of Judge Dora al-Khazen — lead judicial investigator per published reporting; WHO Eastern Mediterranean Regional Office; her oncologist to formally document treatment failure timeline against infusion dates; a consumer rights lawyer to pursue civil reclaim of the $9,000
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All vial packaging with batch numbers and serial numbers — do not discard; purchase receipts and invoices from the pharmacy; infusion records and dates; oncologist's tumour marker reports showing unexpected disease progression; any written or WhatsApp communication with the pharmacy
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Product / Financial Harm / Medical Safety / Corruption / Cancer Care

**Real Basis:** WHO Medical Product Alert N°3/2025 (14 May 2025) — falsified IMFINZI (durvalumab) 500mg/10ml confirmed in Turkey; WHO alert warned of fake vials with no active ingredient circulating across Lebanon, Turkey, and Armenia; published by WHO 14 May 2025
**The Problem:** An oncologist in Istanbul is treating a patient with durvalumab for bladder cancer. After reading the WHO alert confirming fake IMFINZI vials in Turkey, she checks the batch numbers of her patient's remaining supply — purchased through a private pharmacy — and cannot find any official batch verification system to cross-check. The distributor assures her verbally that the supply chain is "certified" but cannot provide chain-of-custody documentation. She does not know whether her patient has already received contaminated vials. The patient is currently between treatment cycles.
**What staff/org would do:** The pharmacy distributor gives a verbal assurance of legitimacy but cannot produce chain-of-custody records within the timeframe the oncologist needs. The local AstraZeneca representative is not responding to calls. The oncologist is left without a verification pathway.
**What the Owner does:** Provides the oncologist with immediate and specific authentication pathways — AstraZeneca's product security reporting channel, Turkey's national regulator, and the WHO alert contact — so she can authenticate batch numbers in hours rather than days.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (patient is between cycles; no immediate infusion imminent — but verification is time-sensitive before the next cycle)

- Who to direct to: AstraZeneca Product Security team — the manufacturer maintains a product authentication channel for verified counterfeit reports; Turkish Medicines and Medical Devices Agency (TITCK — Turkiye Ilac ve Tibbi Cihaz Kurumu) — national regulator to report the batch and request an emergency supply chain audit; WHO Medical Product Alert reporting system (alertstatus@who.int); Turkish Medical Association for medico-legal documentation support

### Scenario 2-6: Parents Seek Accountability After Newborn Dies from Wrong Hospital Prescription (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** The Pharmaceutical Journal (2024) — coroner's inquest report; Chelsea and Westminster Hospital NHS Foundation Trust; newborn Aliabase prescribed sodium acid phosphate instead of sodium chloride in May 2024; died May 10, 2024 from hypocalcaemia and bradycardia; coroner confirmed prescribing error directly caused death; hospital responded with staff training on phosphate prescribing
**The Problem:** The parents of newborn Aliabase, who died on May 10, 2024 at Chelsea and Westminster Hospital, have received the coroner's inquest finding that a prescribing error — sodium acid phosphate dispensed instead of sodium chloride — directly caused their baby's death. They want to understand what formal complaint pathway exists against the hospital, whether the staff responsible have faced any accountability beyond a training update, and what compensation or Duty of Candour obligations the hospital carries.
**What staff/org would do:** The hospital's Patient Relations team offers a standard letter of condolence and refers the parents to the internal PALS (Patient Advice and Liaison Service) complaints process — managed by the same institution being complained about. Staff changes are described as "training enhancements" with no disclosure of individual accountability.
**What the Owner does:** Affirms the parents' right to full transparency under the NHS Duty of Candour (Regulation 20), and directs them to independent oversight bodies — not solely the hospital's internal process — including the pathway for civil claims, which carries a time limit.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: Offer profound condolences for the loss of Aliabase; acknowledge the coroner's confirmed finding; affirm that the parents have independent formal rights that go beyond the hospital's internal response, and that some of these rights have time limits they need to know about now.
- Who to direct to: Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) — independent body for NHS complaints when local resolution is unsatisfactory; Care Quality Commission (CQC) — national regulator for reporting systemic prescribing failures; NHS Resolution — for clinical negligence claims under the Clinical Negligence Scheme for Trusts; Action Against Medical Accidents (AvMA) — free specialist legal advice for clinical negligence cases
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Coroner's inquest report and formal findings document; all hospital communications received since Aliabase's death; prescription records obtainable via Subject Access Request under UK GDPR; any Duty of Candour notification letter from the hospital; full clinical notes and medication administration records if obtainable
- Feedback category tag: Wrong Prescription / Patient Safety / Institutional Accountability / Bereavement / NHS Complaint

### Scenario 2-7: Elderly Woman Given Chemotherapy Drug Instead of Diuretic — Pharmacy Provides No Counselling (Pattern A+D)
**Real Basis:** HPSO (Healthcare Providers Service Organization) Pharmacy Malpractice Case Study — published anonymized legal database; Pharmacy Times documented parallel legal case — 78-year-old woman with COPD prescribed metolazone 2.5 mg; pharmacy technician selected methotrexate 2.5 mg from system dropdown; no patient counselling conducted despite chain policy requiring it for high-alert medications; patient took the chemotherapy drug daily for ten days, developed GI bleeding and respiratory failure, and died; settlement exceeded $1,000,000
**The Problem:** A 78-year-old woman with COPD and chronic kidney disease was prescribed metolazone 2.5 mg. She collected her medication from a chain pharmacy with no counselling provided. After ten days of daily doses she has developed severe nausea, vomiting, and GI bleeding. Her daughter brings her to an emergency department. The ED physician reads the bottle label and realises it says "methotrexate" — a chemotherapy drug — not metolazone. The daughter calls the pharmacy immediately.
**What staff/org would do:** The on-duty pharmacist initially insists the dispensing system "wouldn't allow" such an error and refuses to confirm what was dispensed until a supervisor can be reached. The call is transferred three times over 25 minutes with no resolution, while the patient remains in the emergency department.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms the pharmacy's phone response is completely inadequate, that a dispensing record must be produced on emergency request, and gives the daughter the direct escalation paths while her mother is still being treated.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: This is a medical emergency — the ED team needs the dispensing record immediately to guide toxicology treatment; advise the daughter to tell the ED to contact the pharmacy formally for the dispensing record under HIPAA emergency provision, and to call the US Poison Control Center at 1-800-222-1222 right now for methotrexate toxicity guidance.
- Who to direct to: US Poison Control Center — 1-800-222-1222 (call immediately for methotrexate overdose management guidance); State Board of Pharmacy — mandatory incident report; Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) — voluntary national reporting; a pharmacy malpractice attorney for the family once the patient is stabilised
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The medication bottle with the pharmacy label showing "methotrexate"; the GP's original prescription for metolazone; the pharmacy receipt; all calls made to the pharmacy from this point forward with timestamps; hospital admission records and treatment given; written confirmation from the pharmacy of what was actually dispensed
- Feedback category tag: Wrong Drug Dispensed / Emergency / Pharmacy Error / Failure to Counsel / Elderly Vulnerability

### Scenario 2-8: Chain Pharmacy Managers Use Hotline Calls to Override Pharmacists' Safety Refusals (Pattern D+C)
**Real Basis:** CVS Health $5 billion and Walgreens $5.7 billion nationwide opioid settlement (November 2022); NPR; Washington Post; New York Attorney General press release; PMC10713840 — internal industry documents analysis confirming store managers called pharmacists directly after customer complaints to indicate refused opioid prescriptions "should have been filled"; PR Newswire — class action lawsuits filed against CVS, Walgreens, and Costco for refusal to fill valid opioid prescriptions for chronic pain patients
**The Problem:** A licensed chronic pain patient in Ohio with a valid opioid prescription from her pain management specialist has been refused at Walgreens by the pharmacist, who cited a new internal opioid-limiting policy. She calls the Walgreens customer care line to dispute the refusal. Thirty minutes later, the same pharmacist calls back and says he will now fill the prescription — with no explanation for the reversal. She is alarmed: her prescription was refused on clinical grounds, then approved after a management call triggered by her complaint, and she does not know if the original refusal was a legitimate safety decision that has just been overturned because she pushed back.
**What staff/org would do:** The call centre representative closes the ticket as resolved — the prescription was filled. No documentation of the override is provided. The patient's concern that a clinical safety judgment may have been reversed under management pressure is treated as a customer satisfaction issue, not a patient safety incident.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that the reversal pattern the patient experienced is documented in federal opioid litigation — not a routine service recovery — and explains her rights to both report the incident and seek independent protection of her access to legitimately prescribed pain medication.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge the patient's discomfort; affirm that her instinct is correct — the pattern she experienced, where a management call following a customer service complaint reversed a pharmacist's refusal, is a documented systemic practice cited in $10.7 billion in federal opioid settlements; this is not a normal service resolution.
- Who to direct to: Ohio State Board of Pharmacy — to report the management override incident as a potential patient safety event; DEA Diversion Control Division — if she believes the original refusal was clinically unjustified and reversed under non-clinical pressure; Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) — voluntary incident report for national learning; legal counsel referenced in the PR Newswire class action if she has faced a repeated pattern of refusal followed by override
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Exact timestamps of the initial pharmacist refusal, the customer care call, and the callback with reversal; name and ID number of the pharmacist and any manager involved; the original prescription from her pain specialist; written or app-based confirmation from Walgreens of what was dispensed and when; records of all prior fills at this pharmacy; any written explanation given (or not given) for the reversal
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure / Management Override / Pharmacist Safety Decision / Patient Rights / Opioid Access / Misconduct

### Scenario 2-9: Medicare Patient Discovers CVS Billed Government for More Insulin Than Prescribed Over a Decade (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** DOJ press release (SDNY); HHS Office of Inspector General; FierceHealthcare; Healthcare Dive — CVS $37.76 million settlement approved 2023; CVS admitted that pharmacies dispensed more insulin pens to patients than their prescriptions required and falsely under-reported days-of-supply to trigger premature reimbursements from Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, and Federal Employees Health Benefits between 2010 and 2020; CVS accepted responsibility
**The Problem:** A 71-year-old Medicare beneficiary in New Jersey reviews her Medicare Summary Notice and notices she was billed for insulin pen refills every 14 days for years, even though her endocrinologist's prescription specified a 30-day supply. She has been paying a 20% Medicare Part D co-pay on every refill. She calls CVS pharmacy customer service to ask why refills were triggered so frequently. The representative tells her that "your doctor's instructions said to refill when needed" and offers no further explanation or account review.
**What staff/org would do:** CVS customer service closes the call with a vague general explanation, makes no offer to review her billing history, and does not disclose the DOJ settlement — even though CVS formally admitted to this exact conduct and paid $37.76 million to resolve it.
**What the Owner does:** Informs the patient that CVS has formally admitted to this practice and settled with the Department of Justice; she has paid excess co-pays on fraudulently early refills and may be entitled to recover those amounts.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Validate that her concern is entirely well-founded — CVS formally admitted to this exact pattern of premature insulin refills and settled with the Department of Justice in 2023 for $37.76 million; she is not imagining the problem and may be owed money.
- Who to direct to: 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227) — to file a billing complaint and request a full claims review for the past 10 years; HHS Office of Inspector General hotline (1-800-HHS-TIPS / 1-800-447-8477) — to report Medicare billing fraud; DOJ SDNY Civil Division — to inquire about settlement claimant eligibility and recoupment of excess co-pays; a Medicare patient rights advocate or elder law attorney to assist with the co-pay excess recovery process
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Medicare Summary Notices for the past 5–10 years showing insulin pen refill billing dates and claimed days-of-supply; CVS pharmacy receipts and full refill history printout (request this from the CVS pharmacy directly); her endocrinologist's original prescription specifying days-of-supply; Explanation of Benefits (EOB) from her Part D insurer showing billed dates versus prescription dates
- Feedback category tag: Overcharging / Medicare Fraud / Financial Harm / Systematic Billing Error / Elderly Vulnerability

### Scenario 2-10: Cancer Patient in Armenia Refused Refund After Purchasing Confirmed Counterfeit Drug (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** WHO Medical Product Alert N°3/2025 (14 May 2025) — falsified IMFINZI (durvalumab) injection 500mg/10ml confirmed in Armenia; fake vials contain no active ingredient; part of a regional criminal network with confirmed distribution in Lebanon, Turkey, and Armenia
**The Problem:** A 58-year-old man in Yerevan, Armenia purchased two vials of IMFINZI (durvalumab) from a private pharmacy for his lung cancer treatment. After the WHO Medical Product Alert was published confirming fake IMFINZI in Armenia, he returned to the pharmacy with the WHO alert printed out and demanded a refund and the name of the supplier. The pharmacist refused the refund, claimed the products were "genuine from their supplier," declined to show any chain-of-custody documentation, and told the patient to contact the manufacturer if he had concerns. He was turned away at the counter with no written refusal and no receipt for the returned vials.
**What staff/org would do:** The pharmacy stonewalls — no refund, no supplier disclosure, no acknowledgment of the WHO alert — because admitting the product is counterfeit would expose the pharmacy to criminal liability. The patient has no visible escalation path and has already spent the equivalent of US$8,000 on the vials.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms the patient's rights and the pharmacy's legal obligations under Armenian pharmaceutical and consumer protection law — they cannot refuse to acknowledge a WHO-confirmed counterfeit alert or deny refund documentation to a patient — and connects him to the criminal investigation and regulatory enforcement channel.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no immediate physical emergency; the patient's medical harm and financial loss have already occurred — the urgency is regulatory and legal)
- Opening reply: Affirm clearly that the pharmacy's refusal is both legally wrong and indefensible — a WHO Medical Product Alert is a binding notification to Armenian national regulators; the patient has the right to a refund, the right to know his supplier's identity, and the right to report this pharmacy to authorities without retaliation; he should not leave without documenting everything.
- Who to direct to: Scientific Centre of Drug and Medical Technology Expertise (SCDMTE) — Armenia's national medicines regulatory authority, to report the pharmacy and batch numbers; Armenian State Inspection Body for Healthcare — to file a formal complaint against the pharmacy for refusing to acknowledge a WHO alert and denying a consumer refund; Consumer Rights Protection Agency of Armenia — for the financial harm and unlawful refusal; WHO Armenia Country Office (Yerevan) — to report the confirmed counterfeit vials and pharmacy non-cooperation; a consumer rights lawyer in Yerevan for civil reclaim of the US$8,000
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Both vial packages with batch numbers and serial numbers — do not hand them back to the pharmacy under any circumstances; the WHO alert printout he brought to the pharmacy with the date of his visit noted; a written account of the conversation with the pharmacist including date, time, staff names, and exact words used; purchase receipts for the vials; any written refusal the pharmacy provides (request this in writing); a record of any other patients at the same pharmacy who received the same product
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Product / Refusal of Service / Consumer Rights Violation / Financial Harm / Regulatory Complaint / Cancer Care

## 3. Finance / Banking

### Scenario 3-1: Zelle Payment Fraud Denied — Transfer Loop Between Bank and Payment Network (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** CFPB federal lawsuit against Early Warning Services, JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo (filed December 20, 2024; consumerfinance.gov/enforcement); CFPB consumer complaint database narratives; CNBC and NPR reporting on $870M combined consumer losses across three banks over seven years; banks collectively reimbursed only 38% of fraud claims.
**The Problem:** A Chase bank customer transferred $1,400 via Zelle to someone they believed was their landlord after receiving a spoofed email with familiar details. The money reached a fraudster. The customer called Chase's fraud line immediately. The representative told them the transfer was "authorized by you" and Regulation E protections do not apply to authorized transfers. Over the next two hours, they were transferred four times — between Chase's fraud department and Zelle's customer service line — with neither party accepting responsibility. Chase has now issued a written denial. The customer's rent is still due.
**What staff/org would do:** Script the denial using "authorized transfer" language; cite Regulation E; transfer the consumer to Zelle, who transfers them back to Chase; close the case; offer no fraud investigation reference number; decline to initiate an inter-bank recall.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately acknowledge the loss; assign a senior fraud analyst rather than a front-line representative; open a formal fraud investigation; initiate a payment recall request to the receiving bank; provide a written case reference number; inform the consumer of their rights under the evolving regulatory landscape for socially-engineered authorized fraud.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (active financial loss; funds potentially recoverable if recall initiated quickly)
- Opening reply: "I'm so sorry this happened — losing money to fraud and then being passed between departments is completely unacceptable. Time matters here, so let me give you the fastest path forward right now."
- Direct to: File a complaint immediately with the CFPB at consumerfinance.gov/complaint (the CFPB filed federal suit against Chase specifically for this denial pattern in December 2024); contact your state Attorney General's consumer protection division; demand Chase initiate a payment recall in writing — they have the inter-bank mechanism to do this and are required to investigate under Regulation E; if Chase issues a second denial, contact your state banking regulator
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot or printout of the fraudulent email/message that triggered the transfer; timestamps and representative names/IDs from every call to Chase and Zelle; any reference numbers given; Chase's written denial letter; bank statement showing the transfer; your real landlord's correct payment details as proof of confusion
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud / Authorized Push Payment Denial / Call Center Transfer Loop

### Scenario 3-2: Unemployment Benefits Card Frozen — No Recovery Path (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** CFPB Consent Order against U.S. Bank, December 19, 2023 (ReliaCard prepaid debit card program; $21M CFPB fine + $15M OCC fine; CFPB found U.S. Bank "failed to provide an easy method to verify identities"; cardholders locked out for "a few weeks to a month or more"); CNBC and Banking Dive reporting.
**The Problem:** A recently unemployed consumer in Ohio receives state unemployment insurance benefits via a U.S. Bank ReliaCard prepaid debit card — their only current income. Their card was frozen after the bank's fraud detection system flagged it. They cannot buy groceries or pay rent. They have called U.S. Bank three times over two days. Each time a representative tells them their account is "under review for security purposes," provides no timeline, cannot explain what triggered the freeze, and cannot tell them what documentation would resolve it. No escalation path has been offered. There is no emergency access mechanism.
**What staff/org would do:** Read from the security review script; provide no timeline; list no specific verification pathway; offer no emergency fund release; transfer the consumer back to the same automated queue; take no exception action for financial hardship.
**What the Owner does:** Flag the case as a vulnerable-customer hardship situation immediately; open an expedited review track; provide the consumer with a specific list of identity documents that will satisfy verification within 24 hours; issue a partial emergency release of funds if same-day verification cannot be completed; escalate to a senior compliance officer given the active risk of harm.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (no income, no food or rent access — immediate risk of harm)
- Opening reply: "This is a genuine emergency — being locked out of your only income source when you have no other options is something we need to treat with full urgency. I am going to give you every avenue available to get this resolved today."
- Direct to: Contact your state's unemployment agency directly and ask them to intervene with U.S. Bank on your behalf — they have a direct relationship with the bank as the issuing authority; file an emergency complaint with the CFPB at consumerfinance.gov/complaint (the CFPB specifically fined U.S. Bank $21M in December 2023 for this exact practice with ReliaCard); contact your state banking regulator; call 211 (United Way helpline) for emergency food and rent assistance while the account is frozen
- Evidence to collect: All call logs — date, time, duration, representative name or ID for each call; any written or SMS notification of the freeze; screenshot or photo of the ReliaCard showing frozen status; your unemployment eligibility determination letter; any identity documents you offered during calls
- Feedback category tag: Account Access Denial / Government Benefits Disruption / System Failure

### Scenario 3-3: Unauthorized Overdraft Enrollment — Unexplained Fees on Account (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** CFPB enforcement action against Atlantic Union Bank, 2023 ($5M consumer refunds + $6.2M civil penalty for improperly enrolling customers in overdraft protection without consent, often during calls placed for unrelated reasons); CFPB Supervisory Highlights Issue 37 (Winter 2024) documenting "authorize positive" overdraft fee timing manipulation; Regions Bank ordered to refund $141M in similar overdraft violations.
**The Problem:** A consumer notices three $35 overdraft fees on their bank statement over six weeks — $105 total — for transactions they believed their account had sufficient funds to cover. They call their bank and are told they "enrolled in overdraft protection" during a call made six weeks ago for a different purpose. The consumer has no recollection of agreeing to this and suspects the representative enrolled them without clear explanation. The bank refuses to refund the fees and says their enrollment record is confirmed. When the consumer asks to hear the call recording to verify consent was given, they are told the recording is available for the bank's internal use but cannot be shared with the customer.
**What staff/org would do:** Pull up the enrollment record; state the customer agreed to overdraft protection; read from the fee disclosure script; decline to refund; decline to share the call recording; offer no unenrollment without the customer explicitly requesting it.
**What the Owner does:** Pull the actual call recording; review whether proper affirmative consent language was used and whether the enrollment was offered as an opt-in while the customer was calling for another reason; if consent is ambiguous or improper, reverse all three fees immediately; unenroll the customer from overdraft protection going forward; document the resolution.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Charges on your account that you do not remember agreeing to are something we take seriously. You have a right to understand exactly how and when that enrollment happened, and I want to help you get answers and your money back if this was done without your proper consent."
- Direct to: File a complaint with the CFPB at consumerfinance.gov/complaint — the CFPB's Supervisory Highlights 2024 and its 2023 enforcement action against Atlantic Union Bank specifically identified unauthorized call-center overdraft enrollment as an illegal practice; send a written request to the bank (email or certified letter) asking for the date and transcript or recording of the call on which they claim you consented, and the exact consent language used — banks are required to respond to written disputes; your state banking regulator can also investigate
- Evidence to collect: Bank statements showing the three $35 fees and the dates; a log of every call you made to the bank in the six weeks before the alleged enrollment and what you were calling about; any welcome email or letter about overdraft coverage (note the date); the bank's written refusal to refund; your phone call log showing the date and duration of the call they claim was your enrollment call
- Feedback category tag: Unauthorized Service Enrollment / Hidden Overdraft Fee / Call Center Misconduct

### Scenario 3-4: Bank IT Outage — Account Locked, Card Declined, Call Center Non-Functional (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** TSB Bank IT system migration failure, UK, April 2018; 1.9 million customers locked out; FCA and PRA fined TSB £48.6M combined (£29.75M FCA + £18.9M PRA); 114,399 formal complaints filed; TSB paid over £370M in compensation; Financial Ombudsman decisions (e.g., DRN1571723) documented customers receiving inaccurate information for approximately nine months after the incident; estimated call wait times exceeded two hours during the outage period.
**The Problem:** A TSB bank customer in Manchester cannot access their online banking account and their debit card is being declined at the supermarket checkout. They called TSB's customer service line — after 85 minutes on hold, the call disconnected. On a second call, a representative gave them a different account balance figure than their last paper statement showed, could not explain the discrepancy, and told them the account was "being looked into" with no timeline. Three direct debits are scheduled for today. The customer is getting contradictory information each time they call.
**What staff/org would do:** Provide inconsistent balance information across calls; give no timeline for resolution; not acknowledge the wider system outage publicly; not inform the customer of their rights to fee refunds caused by the outage; keep the customer cycling through the same script with no escalation.
**What the Owner does:** Proactively communicate the system outage scope; provide a dedicated priority queue for customers with time-sensitive financial obligations; guarantee in writing to cover any charges incurred due to the outage; offer a branch-based emergency cash option; assign a case reference number so the customer has continuity across contacts.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (direct debits due today; card declined; no access to funds; actively receiving wrong information from customer service)
- Opening reply: "I can hear how stressful this is — direct debits due today and a card that won't work is a real emergency. I am going to give you immediate steps and your rights, because the bank has specific obligations when an outage causes you financial harm."
- Direct to: Go directly to your nearest TSB branch with photo ID and ask for emergency access assistance; if branch cannot resolve same day, escalate immediately to the Financial Ombudsman Service (financial-ombudsman.org.uk or 0800 023 4567) — you do not need to wait the full 8 weeks if you face imminent financial harm from a service failure; ask TSB in writing to confirm they will cover any missed direct debit fees as a result of the outage; contact the FCA consumer helpline (0800 111 6768) to report you are receiving inaccurate information across multiple calls
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of error messages on online banking; a photo of the card-declined receipt at the supermarket; your last paper or app statement showing the balance before the outage; a written record of both call attempts (date, time, duration, what you were told); any direct debit bounce notification letters or emails you receive as a result
- Feedback category tag: Service Outage / Account Access Emergency / Call Center Misinformation
