### Scenario 3-5: Car Finance Hidden Commission — Complaint Rejected Without Evidence (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** UK motor finance Discretionary Commission Arrangement mis-selling scandal; FCA CP25/27 consultation (March 2026) confirming mass redress scheme estimated at £7.5 billion; Court of Appeal ruling October 25, 2024 held undisclosed commissions were unlawful; Which? and MoneySavingExpert documented 99%+ initial rejection rate by lenders; over 4 million consumers have already filed complaints; average compensation estimated at £830 per agreement.
**The Problem:** A consumer in Birmingham purchased a car on finance in 2019. They recently learned that their lender paid a hidden commission to the dealership that arranged the loan, which gave the dealer financial incentive to charge a higher interest rate — without disclosing this conflict of interest to the consumer. They filed a formal complaint with their lender. The lender's response letter states that "commission arrangements are standard industry practice and were disclosed in the terms and conditions." The consumer does not recall seeing any commission disclosure and believes they were charged a higher interest rate than they would have been without the conflict. The letter says the lender considers the complaint closed.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue a boilerplate rejection citing standard industry practice; refuse to provide the specific commission amount paid to the dealer; offer no explanation of the Court of Appeal ruling; close the case; include a brief mention of the FOS without explaining that the FOS is actively upholding these complaints in consumers' favour.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the complaint; not issue a blanket rejection; inform the consumer of the Court of Appeal's October 2024 ruling that Discretionary Commission Arrangements were unlawful without full informed consent; suspend final resolution pending the FCA's announced mass redress scheme; proactively provide the consumer with the details of their finance agreement including the commission paid.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "This is a recognised legal issue that has now been confirmed by the Court of Appeal, and a mass compensation scheme is being set up covering millions of UK consumers. Do not accept this rejection — you have strong grounds to challenge it."
- Direct to: Do not accept the bank's closure letter — escalate immediately to the Financial Ombudsman Service (financial-ombudsman.org.uk or 0800 023 4567); use the free eligibility checker at MoneySavingExpert (moneysavingexpert.com/reclaim/car-finance-discount-commission) to file your claim and be registered for the FCA's mass redress scheme; the FCA mass redress scheme announced March 2026 may process compensation automatically, but you must have a complaint on record; contact Citizens Advice (citizensadvice.org.uk) for free support with the FOS process
- Evidence to collect: Your original car finance agreement paperwork including all pages of small print; the lender's rejection letter with the date (critical — FOS time limits apply from this date); any communications from the dealership at point of sale; records of all interest payments made under the agreement; the dates the finance was taken out and fully repaid or still running (claims cover agreements from 2007 to 2024)
- Feedback category tag: Financial Mis-Selling / Hidden Commission / Consumer Rights Violation / Transparency Failure

### Scenario 3-6: APP Scam Impersonating HMRC — Bank Refuses Recall, Four-Representative Transfer Loop (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** UK Financial Ombudsman Service Annual Report 2024/25 (305,726 new complaints; APP scam complaints up 83% to 4,956 in Q2 2024; FOS upheld 34% of all complaints in consumers' favour; Barclays received 5,207 FOS complaints in H2 2023 — highest of any single UK bank); PSR PS23/3 mandatory APP reimbursement rules effective October 7, 2024.
**The Problem:** A consumer in London received a call from someone claiming to be from HMRC stating their bank account had been flagged for suspicious activity and they needed to move their savings to a "government-protected account" immediately. The caller knew their name, partial account number, and address. Believing this was genuine, the consumer transferred £3,200. Within thirty minutes they suspected fraud and called Barclays. The first representative told them the transfer was "authorised by you" and there was "nothing we can do." They were transferred to a fraud specialist, who told them to "contact the receiving bank directly." The consumer does not know which bank the fraudster used. After 75 minutes across four representatives, no recall has been initiated and no fraud report reference number has been provided.
**What staff/org would do:** Apply the "authorized transfer" denial across every representative; provide no inter-bank payment recall initiation; give no internal fraud report reference; tell the consumer to contact the receiving bank themselves, knowing the consumer has no mechanism to do this; take no immediate protective action on the account.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately initiate a Faster Payments recall request to the receiving bank through the inter-bank scheme — this is the bank's responsibility, not the consumer's; log a formal internal fraud report with a reference number; under the UK's mandatory APP reimbursement rules (PSR PS23/3, effective October 2024), assess reimbursement eligibility without requiring the consumer to pursue the receiving bank themselves; place a note on the account.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (funds may still be recoverable if the receiving bank freezes the fraudster's account immediately; minutes matter)
- Opening reply: "Time is genuinely critical right now — the faster Barclays contacts the receiving bank, the higher the chance your money is still there to be frozen and returned. I am going to tell you exactly what to say to Barclays and what they are legally required to do under rules that came into effect in October 2024."
- Direct to: Call Barclays back immediately and use the specific phrase "I am requesting an inter-bank Faster Payments recall under the PSR mandatory APP reimbursement scheme — please initiate this now and give me a fraud reference number"; if Barclays refuses, escalate on the same call to a supervisor; file with the Financial Ombudsman Service immediately at financial-ombudsman.org.uk (you do not need to wait 8 weeks in active fraud cases); also report to Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk or 0300 123 2040) and the HMRC phishing line (phishing@hmrc.gov.uk)
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the transfer confirmation in your banking app the moment it was made; a record of the fraudster's call — note the time, caller ID shown, and every detail they knew about you; a written record of every Barclays call including representative names and what was said; any texts or emails from the fraudster; the timestamp when you first reported to Barclays (critical for establishing liability under the October 2024 reimbursement rules)
- Feedback category tag: APP Scam / HMRC Impersonation / Authorized Push Payment / Call Center Failure / Recall Refused

### Scenario 3-7: M-Pesa SIM Swap Fraud With Insider Facilitation — Deflected to Police (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Rest of World and Nation Africa investigative reporting (2022-2023); documented case of Nairobi police officer Peter Mwanzo — KSh 597,100 ($4,575) drained including mobile loans taken in his name (January 2022); Safaricom's FY2022 annual report confirmed 24 employees sacked for fraud, 10 specifically for SIM swap involvement; Kenya DCI arrested an 8-member SIM swap syndicate that defrauded users of KSh 500M/$3.8M (February 2023); CBK 2022 report found 25.9% of mobile money users had lost money through cybercrime; precedent-setting Kenyan court ruling in 2023 established Safaricom operator liability for SIM swap losses.
**The Problem:** A consumer in Nairobi wakes to find their phone SIM has stopped working — initially dismissed as a network issue. When they visit a Safaricom service center, they are told their SIM was replaced at a different branch the previous day. Their M-Pesa account shows KSh 85,000 in savings transferred out and a KSh 30,000 mobile loan disbursed and withdrawn in their name — neither transaction initiated by them. The fraudster used the new SIM to pass all M-Pesa verification. At the service center, staff tell the consumer to "go to the DCI" — no fraud reference number is opened, no transaction reversal is attempted, and no investigation into which branch employee processed the unauthorized SIM swap is initiated.
**What staff/org would do:** Refer consumer to the Directorate of Criminal Investigations; decline to open an internal fraud investigation; decline to attempt transaction reversal; decline to suspend the destination accounts; provide no fraud reference number; take no action against the branch that processed the unauthorized SIM replacement.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately flag the consumer's account for fraud investigation; escalate internally to identify which branch processed the SIM swap and who authorized it; suspend accounts to which funds were transferred pending investigation; provide a formal fraud reference number; advise the consumer that a 2023 Kenyan court ruling has established operator liability for SIM swap losses facilitated through internal processes; initiate an internal investigation into the branch employee involved.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (a mobile loan is accruing interest in the consumer's name right now; the longer the delay, the larger the debt grows)
- Opening reply: "This is extremely serious — your savings are gone and a loan has been taken in your name without your knowledge. Safaricom has a legal responsibility here that goes beyond telling you to call the police. Let me walk you through every step you need to take today."
- Direct to: Return to the Safaricom service center and demand — in writing — that they open a formal fraud investigation and provide a fraud reference number; tell them explicitly that a Kenyan court ruled in 2023 that Safaricom bears operator liability for SIM swap losses processed through their network, including where employees facilitated the swap; simultaneously report to the DCI Cybercrime Unit (+254 722 582 220, DCI Headquarters, Kiambu Road, Nairobi) AND the Communications Authority of Kenya (+254 709 040 000, complaints@ca.go.ke) as the telecoms regulator; contact the CBK Consumer Protection Department (consumerprotection@centralbank.go.ke) specifically about the mobile loan taken in your name — you can dispute this debt while the fraud investigation is open; if Safaricom refuses to investigate, consult a lawyer about filing a claim citing the 2023 operator liability precedent
- Evidence to collect: Written record from the service center visit showing they acknowledged the SIM was replaced at another branch; M-Pesa transaction history screenshots showing the unauthorized transfers and loan disbursement with exact timestamps; your own phone's record showing the SIM stopped working (time and date); any SMS received in the hours before the SIM stopped working; the name and branch location of the Safaricom center you visited; Safaricom's refusal to open a fraud report in writing
- Feedback category tag: Mobile Money Fraud / SIM Swap / Insider Staff Misconduct / Complaint Deflection

### Scenario 3-8: Unauthorized Digital Transaction — Bank Delays Response Past Regulatory Limit (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** RBI Integrated Ombudsman Scheme Annual Report 2023-24 (934,355 total complaints — a 32.81% increase year-on-year; mobile and electronic banking the single largest category at over 20% of all cases; Business Standard reported a "68% spike in bank complaints" with mobile banking as the top concern); RBI Circular RBI/2017-18/15 requiring banks to provide provisional credit within 10 working days for disputed unauthorized transactions; the mandatory 30-day bank-level resolution period that must expire before RBI Ombudsman access is triggered means every one of the 934,000 complaints first failed internal bank resolution.
**The Problem:** A consumer in Mumbai finds an unauthorized UPI debit of ₹47,000 from their savings account — a transaction they did not initiate, sent to an account number they do not recognize. They filed a written unauthorized transaction complaint with their bank the same day, receiving a complaint reference number. It has now been 28 days. Every time they call customer care they are told the investigation is "ongoing" with no update and no timeline. No provisional credit has been issued to their account. The funds were earmarked for an upcoming medical procedure. They are now two days away from the 30-day mark when they can escalate to the RBI Ombudsman.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue the "under investigation" script on each call; provide no provisional credit; give no specific timeline; at day 30 or later, tell the consumer to escalate to the RBI Ombudsman as if that were a satisfactory outcome rather than a failure of internal process.
**What the Owner does:** Under RBI Circular RBI/2017-18/15, issue provisional credit within 10 working days for a credible unauthorized transaction complaint; assign a named investigation officer who contacts the consumer; accelerate given the documented medical need; communicate a firm resolution date.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (approaching 30-day threshold — prepare escalation documents now so you can file immediately at day 30)
- Opening reply: "I understand how stressful this is, particularly when those funds were needed for medical reasons. The bank has already breached its RBI-mandated provisional credit obligation. Let me explain exactly what you are owed and how to escalate effectively on day 30."
- Direct to: On day 30 if unresolved, file immediately with the RBI Integrated Ombudsman via cms.rbi.org.in — it is free and fully online and there is no fee; also ask your bank in writing for the name and contact of their internal Nodal Officer for banking ombudsman complaints — all banks are required to have one and must escalate unresolved cases to them; RBI consumer education and protection helpline: 14448 (toll-free); report the UPI fraud to the National Cybercrime Reporting Portal at cybercrime.gov.in (file a cybercrime complaint); reference RBI Circular RBI/2017-18/15 when demanding the provisional credit in writing
- Evidence to collect: Your original complaint reference number and the date filed; bank statement screenshot showing the unauthorized UPI transaction with the destination account number; all call records — dates, times, what you were told, any reference numbers given; any SMS alerts from your bank around the time of the unauthorized transaction; medical documentation showing the urgency of the ₹47,000 (useful for making the case for expedited treatment at the Ombudsman)
- Feedback category tag: Digital Payment Fraud / Unauthorized UPI Transaction / Resolution Delay / Regulatory Escalation Required

### Scenario 3-9: Mobile Money Fraud Attributed to Victim — Insider Threat Dismissed (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Bank of Ghana fraud reports (13,451 fraud cases in 2023; mobile money fraud exceeded GH¢10M; fraud losses rose from GH¢33M in 2021 to GH¢56M in 2022; Bank of Ghana data shows over 50% of all financial fraud cases in Ghana involve insider threats from employees within financial or telecom institutions); documented April 2025 case where a customer lost GH¢11,000 from her MTN MoMo wallet, MTN MobileMoney Ltd conducted an investigation, concluded "no evidence of a technical breach," attributed the loss to "social engineering," and declined to refund (Modern Ghana, TechNext24, Graphic Online).
**The Problem:** A market trader in Accra discovers GH¢11,000 has been withdrawn from her MTN MoMo wallet without her authorization. She did not share her PIN with anyone and has biometric verification enabled. She reported the loss to MTN immediately. Three weeks later, MTN sent a formal letter concluding their investigation found "no evidence of a technical breach" and attributing the unauthorized withdrawal to "social engineering by a third party." The letter offers no refund, no breakdown of the investigation findings, no explanation of what social engineering evidence was identified, and no information about any internal appeals process. This was her entire savings.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue the social engineering conclusion letter; close the case with no refund; provide no investigation evidence; offer no appeals pathway; take no steps to examine whether an internal MTN employee may have facilitated the unauthorized access — despite Bank of Ghana data showing 50%+ of Ghana financial fraud involves insiders.
**What the Owner does:** Require the investigation report to be provided to the consumer in writing, including what specific evidence supports the social engineering conclusion; escalate internally given the statistical likelihood of insider involvement; open an appeals process; refer proactively to the Bank of Ghana Consumer Protection Department; not close the case until the insider-threat possibility is examined.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Losing your entire savings and then being told it was your own fault — without any evidence being shown to you — is not an acceptable resolution. MTN has obligations under Bank of Ghana regulations, and the data on Ghana mobile money fraud shows this conclusion deserves serious scrutiny. Let me help you push back effectively."
- Direct to: File a formal complaint with the Bank of Ghana Consumer Protection and Market Conduct Department (consumerprotection@bog.gov.gh or +233 (0)30 266 6174); send a written request to MTN MobileMoney Ltd demanding the full investigation report and the specific evidence used to conclude social engineering — they are required to provide this under Bank of Ghana consumer protection rules; file with the National Communications Authority of Ghana (complaints@nca.org.gh) as the telecoms regulator overseeing MTN; reference publicly that Bank of Ghana's own data shows over 50% of Ghana financial fraud involves insider threats and that the investigation should have examined this possibility; consider consulting a lawyer about a civil claim — MTN has been successfully challenged in documented cases
- Evidence to collect: MTN's written "social engineering" conclusion letter with the date; your own security practices documentation (PIN never shared, biometric enabled, no suspicious calls received); MTN MoMo transaction history showing the unauthorized withdrawal with timestamp; any suspicious calls, SMS, or contact you received in the days before the loss; full record of every interaction with MTN customer service from initial report through the conclusion letter; your registration documents for the MoMo wallet
- Feedback category tag: Mobile Money Fraud / Victim-Blaming / Insider Misconduct / Complaint Dismissal

### Scenario 3-10: Bank Refuses Scam Reimbursement After 45-Day Internal Review — AFCA Escalation Required (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** AFCA Annual Review 2023-24 (record 104,861 complaints; scam-related complaints surged 81% to approximately 11,000 cases; Consumer Action Law Centre reported banks systematically fail to reimburse scam victims at first contact; 40% of scam cases that progressed to full AFCA investigation resulted in no compensation; 60% resulted in full or partial compensation — meaning AFCA escalation meaningfully changes outcomes); Consumer Action Law Centre documentation of "authorised transfer" denial scripts mirroring the US Zelle pattern.
**The Problem:** A consumer in Sydney received a call from someone claiming to be from ANZ's fraud prevention team, who stated that their account had been compromised and instructed them to transfer their savings to a "secure account" set up in their name for protection. The caller had the consumer's full name, partial card number, and recent transaction details. The consumer transferred AU$8,500. They reported the fraud to ANZ within two hours of the transfer. After a 45-day internal investigation, ANZ sent a final decision letter stating the transfer was "authorised by you" and they are not entitled to reimbursement. The letter contains a single sentence mentioning AFCA as a complaints option, with no further explanation.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue the "authorised transfer" denial; refuse to discuss whether ANZ's own fraud prevention team warning systems flagged the unusual outgoing transfer in real time; issue the AFCA mention as a procedural requirement rather than a genuine referral; provide no explanation of the consumer's prospects at AFCA or the 2-year time limit for filing.
**What the Owner does:** Review whether ANZ's own systems generated any real-time warning about the transfer that was not acted on; acknowledge that impersonation scams where the fraudster poses as the bank's own fraud team represent a recognized failure of internal fraud prevention, not a consumer error; provide a substantive explanation of AFCA and the consumer's actual rights; not hide behind the "authorised" framing when the consumer was manipulated into authorising by someone impersonating the bank.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (internal process exhausted — now in the AFCA filing window, which has a strict 2-year time limit from ANZ's decision date)
- Opening reply: "I know receiving that denial after 45 days of waiting is devastating. But this is genuinely not the end of the road — AFCA exists exactly for situations like this, and in 2023-24, 60% of scam cases that reached AFCA resulted in full or partial compensation. Let me walk you through how to file effectively."
- Direct to: File with AFCA immediately at afca.org.au or call 1800 931 678 — the 2-year limitation period runs from the date of ANZ's final decision letter, so file now; in your AFCA application specifically note that (1) the fraudster impersonated ANZ's own fraud prevention team using your private account details, (2) ANZ did not warn you of an unusual outgoing transfer in real time despite this being a recognized impersonation scam pattern, and (3) ANZ's denial script uses "authorised transfer" language identical to the pattern AFCA overturned in 60% of comparable cases in 2023-24; report the fraud to Scamwatch (scamwatch.gov.au) and the Australian Cyber Security Centre (cyber.gov.au/report); contact Consumer Action Law Centre (consumeraction.org.au or 1800 466 477) for free support with the AFCA process
- Evidence to collect: ANZ's final decision letter with the date (start your 2-year AFCA clock from this date); a full transcript or summary of every interaction with ANZ from the initial fraud report through the final decision; bank statement showing the AU$8,500 transfer; any communications from the fraudster — call log showing caller ID, any texts or emails; evidence of the timing between the fraudster's call and ANZ's real fraud prevention calls to you previously (demonstrating why you believed the caller was genuine); your own phone's incoming call records showing when the fraudster called and the number displayed
- Feedback category tag: Scam Reimbursement Refusal / Bank Impersonation Fraud / AFCA Escalation Required / Authorised Push Payment

Industry ID: 3

## 4. Insurance

### Scenario 4-1: Elderly Medicare Patient Denied Rehab Coverage by AI Algorithm While Still in Recovery (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Class action lawsuit against UnitedHealth Group / NaviHealth, U.S. District Court Minnesota, November 2023; CBS News investigation 2023; Becker's Payer Issues 2023–2025; court filings documenting denial rate jump from 10.9% (2020) to 22.7% (2022) after nH Predict algorithm deployment; approximately 90% of denials reversed by federal administrative law judges on appeal.
**The Problem:** An 83-year-old Medicare Advantage patient has been in a skilled nursing rehabilitation facility for 16 days following hip fracture surgery. Her treating physician has certified she requires a minimum of 30 days of rehabilitation to regain the ability to walk independently. Today, her family received a notice that UnitedHealthcare is terminating coverage for her stay effective in 3 days — the insurer's nH Predict algorithm has calculated her "expected" recovery window has elapsed. The physician's appeal has been ignored. Without continued coverage, the facility is preparing to discharge her to a family home where no one can provide clinical care.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue a standard termination of benefits letter citing plan terms. Route family calls to general member services with no direct access to the case managers who applied the algorithm. Tell the family they may submit a formal appeal — a process that takes weeks — without disclosing the expedited appeal right or the 24-hour external review option available for ongoing urgent care under Medicare rules.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms this is a medically urgent situation. Tells the family there is a legal right to an expedited appeal under Medicare Advantage rules — insurers must respond within 24 hours for urgent ongoing care denials. Advises them to demand in writing that the insurer convert this to an expedited review immediately. Tells them to have the physician document in writing why discharge in 3 days poses imminent clinical risk. Notes that federal administrative law judges reverse approximately 90% of these AI-driven rehab denials when appealed, but that under 1% of patients ever actually appeal.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a serious situation and you have legal rights the insurer is obligated to honour. Your family member's physician has certified she needs continued care — that carries real legal weight under Medicare. Here is what you can do right now to stop the discharge clock."
- Direct consumer to: Demand an expedited Medicare appeal immediately in writing — insurers must respond within 24 hours for ongoing urgent care; call 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227) to request an expedited appeal; contact a local State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) counsellor for free guidance (shiphelp.org); file a complaint with the U.S. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) at medicare.gov; ask the facility's social worker to assist — they handle these situations routinely
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Written copy of the termination notice with the stated effective date; the treating physician's written certification of continued need; all prior correspondence with the insurer; the facility's discharge plan; a written log of every call made to the insurer including date, time, hold duration, representative name, and what was said
- Feedback category tag: Claim denial — AI-driven coverage termination overriding physician judgment; Medicare Advantage; expedited appeal rights; elderly patient; rights violation

---

### Scenario 4-2: Insurer's Physicians Clicked Through 60,000 Claim Denials in One Month Without Reading Individual Files (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** ProPublica and The Capitol Forum investigative report, March 2023 — Cigna PXDX automated batch denial system; U.S. Congressional inquiry launched 2023; class action lawsuit filed California federal court, 2023; 80% of Cigna Medicare Advantage appeals subsequently overturned; one physician documented denying approximately 60,000 claims in a single month, averaging 1.2 seconds per denial.
**The Problem:** A 47-year-old man received a denial letter from Cigna for a cardiac stress test his cardiologist ordered following an abnormal EKG. The letter states a Cigna physician reviewed the claim and determined the test is "not medically necessary." The claim was submitted yesterday and the denial letter arrived today. He has since read the ProPublica investigation revealing that Cigna physicians used an automated batch system to deny tens of thousands of claims per month without opening individual files — spending an average of 1.2 seconds per denial. He wants to know whether his claim was legally reviewed at all, and what rights he has to demand transparency and challenge the decision.
**What staff/org would do:** Defend the denial as medically reviewed per internal protocols. Route the consumer to a standard written appeal process taking 30–60 days. Provide no information about the PXDX system, how the denial was generated, or how much time was spent on his specific case. State only that the decision was made by a licensed physician and is therefore compliant.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the consumer's concern as legitimate and factual — the PXDX investigation is real, on the public record, and has been the subject of Congressional scrutiny and a class action lawsuit. Advises that the consumer has the right to request the specific physician reviewer's name, credentials, and the stated basis for their determination — information the insurer must provide under the ACA and ERISA. Advises filing both a formal internal appeal and a simultaneous complaint with the state insurance commissioner.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: Ask the consumer whether the cardiologist has flagged the withheld test as clinically time-sensitive — if so, flag is_urgent=true; otherwise proceed as priority complaint
- Opening reply: "Your concern is well-founded and based on documented fact. The investigation you are referring to is real, it led to a Congressional inquiry, and it directly affects your right to know how your specific claim was handled. Here is what you can do to challenge this."
- Direct consumer to: File a formal internal appeal in writing citing the cardiologist's referral and requesting disclosure of the reviewer's name, credentials, and the time and basis of their review; submit a complaint to the state Department of Insurance (searchable at naic.org/state_web_map.htm); if employer-sponsored plan, file with the U.S. Department of Labor Employee Benefits Security Administration (EBSA) at 1-866-444-3272; for Medicare Advantage plans, file with CMS at medicare.gov
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Denial letter including date, any reviewer name referenced, and stated reason; cardiologist's referral or order for the test; date the claim was submitted versus date of denial (document the timeline); the plan's Summary of Benefits and Coverage; the ProPublica article URL for reference in formal complaints (propublica.org)
- Feedback category tag: Claim denial — algorithmic batch denial without individual physician review; staff misconduct; rights violation; transparency failure; cardiac diagnostic test

### Scenario 4-3: Consumer Spends Four Hours on Hold Over Four Days Just to Confirm Health Insurance Enrollment Before Surgery (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** United Policyholders consumer documentation report "Calling Your Health Insurer? Prepare to Hold" (2023–2024); documented case of Silver Spring, Maryland consumer spending four hours over four days to confirm enrollment; separate documented account of San Diego insurance broker spending up to two hours on hold with Covered California when helping clients resolve coverage questions; industry benchmark of 13-minute average hold time.
**The Problem:** A recently self-employed woman signed up for health insurance through her state marketplace after leaving her employer. Her surgery is scheduled in nine days. She needs to confirm her enrollment is active and her surgeon is in-network before the hospital will proceed. She has called her insurer four times over four days and spent just over four hours on hold. On the first call she was told to call back. On the second she was transferred twice and then disconnected. On the third she was told there was a "processing delay" with no timeline given. On the fourth call today she was finally told there may be a problem with her enrollment. She does not know what the problem is, whether her surgery will be covered, or who can actually resolve this.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue routing to the general member services queue. Offer to "submit a ticket" with no resolution timeline. Decline to transfer to an enrollment specialist directly. Advise checking the online portal, which shows enrollment as pending.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as both a practical time-sensitive problem — surgery in 9 days — and a documented structural failure of insurer call center systems. Advises on specific escalation tactics that bypass the standard queue: contacting the state marketplace (not the insurer) directly to verify and resolve enrollment status; requesting a Navigator or Certified Application Counsellor who can intervene on her behalf at no cost; and obtaining a written coverage determination letter from the insurer before the surgery date.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: Flag as time-sensitive with surgery 9 days away — escalate urgency framing accordingly
- Opening reply: "Four calls and four hours on hold just to confirm you are enrolled — that should not be happening, and you should not be navigating this alone with surgery approaching. Here is how to reach people who can actually fix this faster than the standard member services line."
- Direct consumer to: Call the state insurance marketplace help line directly (separate number from the insurer — for ACA plans, 1-800-318-2596 for Healthcare.gov); find a free Navigator or Certified Application Counsellor at findacenter.healthcare.gov who can intervene with the marketplace on your behalf; request in writing (email) that the insurer provide a written coverage determination letter before the surgery date; contact the state Department of Insurance consumer assistance if the insurer continues to be unresponsive
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshots of the online portal showing enrollment status on each date checked; a written log of all four calls with dates, durations, hold times, names of any representatives, and what each person said; the hospital's written pre-surgery coverage requirement; the surgeon's in-network status documentation if available
- Feedback category tag: Call center runaround — four calls, four hours on hold, repeated transfers, one disconnection, no resolution; health insurance enrollment failure; pre-surgery coverage uncertainty; information failure

---

### Scenario 4-4: Loss Adjuster Causes Further Structural Damage, Homeowner Left Without Running Water and Electrical Hazard for Eleven Months (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** UK Financial Ombudsman Service case records 2024/25; documented complaint against Aviva and appointed loss adjuster Sedgwick (FOS described loss adjuster scope as "deplorably insufficient" and cash settlement as "woefully inadequate"); FOS buildings insurance complaints at 10-year high in Q2 2024/25; FOS upheld 41% of buildings insurance complaints in consumers' favour; Aviva later acknowledged further dialogue before settlement "would have been preferable."
**The Problem:** A homeowner submitted a buildings insurance claim to Aviva nine months ago after discovering rising damp and water ingress in two ground-floor rooms. Aviva appointed Sedgwick as their loss adjuster. During Sedgwick's initial inspection, their contractor broke the external water feed pipe and damaged the damp proof course, creating a new and larger leak. The subsequent cash settlement offer does not include the additional damage caused by Aviva's own loss adjuster and fails to address a serious health and safety hazard identified by the homeowner — water is now in direct contact with electrical wiring in the floor cavity. The homeowner has been without running water since the incident. It has now been eleven months. Aviva's only communication is a letter saying the case is "under review." Calls to Aviva are transferred to Sedgwick; calls to Sedgwick are told to wait for instructions from Aviva.
**What staff/org would do:** Repeat that the case is under review. Route calls between Aviva claims handling and Sedgwick with no single contact owning the problem. Keep the original inadequate settlement offer on the table. Take no action on the electrical hazard.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately identifies the water-near-wiring hazard as a potential risk to life that must be treated as an emergency today, entirely separately from the insurance dispute. Advises the homeowner to get an independent registered electrician to assess and document the hazard urgently and to place Aviva on written notice of a life-safety risk requiring emergency contractor attendance. Advises escalating to the Financial Ombudsman Service without further delay — the internal complaint period has clearly elapsed.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Eleven months without running water is not a delay — it is a failure. And water near electrical wiring is a risk to life that needs to be treated as an emergency today, separate from the insurance dispute. Here is what to do right now for your safety, and in parallel for the insurance complaint."
- Direct consumer to: Emergency (today): contact a registered NICEIC or NAPIT electrician to assess and document the electrical hazard — do not delay this; send Aviva a written notice by email today stating a health and safety risk exists and requesting emergency contractor attendance within 48 hours, keeping a copy. Insurance complaint: file with the Financial Ombudsman Service immediately (financial-ombudsman.org.uk or 0800 023 4567) — the eight-week internal complaint period is clearly expired; also put Sedgwick's conduct causing additional damage on the record in your FOS submission as the insurer is responsible for their appointed loss adjuster
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Photographs and video of all visible damage including the area where water is near wiring; a complete written log of all communications with Aviva and Sedgwick including dates; original claim reference number and date of submission; the original settlement offer in writing; any contractor reports or scope of work documents from Sedgwick; the registered electrician's written hazard assessment; receipts for any costs incurred including alternative accommodation, bottled water, and laundry
- Feedback category tag: Delayed payout — multi-party claim causing 11-month resolution failure; additional damage caused by insurer's own loss adjuster; unresolved electrical and water safety hazard; staff misconduct; vulnerability

### Scenario 4-5: Insurer Refuses Travel Claim Under Blanket COVID Exclusion for Random Government-Mandated Testing — FOS Overturns Decision (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** UK Financial Ombudsman Service published decision DRN-4409621 (provisional decision August 23, 2023) — FOS investigator upheld complaint against Vitality Insurance, finding the COVID-19 "known event" exclusion was "not fair and reasonable" as applied to random government-mandated testing; 4,466 travel insurance complaints received by FOS in 2023/24 — highest level in ten years outside the COVID pandemic; approximately 20% of travel insurance claims rejected 2022–2024 per FCA data.
**The Problem:** A couple booked a family holiday to Southeast Asia in late 2022 and purchased travel insurance. At their destination airport on arrival, their teenage son was randomly selected by local authorities for a mandatory COVID test under government entry protocols still in place at the time. He tested positive. The family incurred significant additional costs — extended hotel stay, rebooking flights, daily meals, and medical consultation — totalling over £3,200. The insurer rejected the entire claim citing a blanket COVID-19 exclusion stating that COVID is a "known event" since March 2020 and therefore excluded from all coverage. When they called the insurer to query the decision, the call center agent told them "no further internal review is available" and directed them to use the formal eight-week written complaint process if they disagreed.
**What staff/org would do:** Maintain that the blanket exclusion applies. Provide no explanation of what specific scenarios the exclusion was designed to cover. Give no information about the Financial Ombudsman Service or the consumer's right to external review. Require the consumer to navigate the full eight-week complaint process before any escalation.
**What the Owner does:** Informs the consumer that the UK FOS has already adjudicated on a virtually identical case — decision DRN-4409621 — and found that a broad COVID "known event" exclusion applied to costs arising from random government-mandated testing was not fair and reasonable, because the exclusion wording did not specifically cover that fact pattern and the consumer had no means to avoid the testing. This gives the consumer a strong and documented basis for an FOS complaint.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: No
- Opening reply: "The insurer's refusal is not the end of this. The UK Financial Ombudsman has already ruled on a case that is almost identical to yours and found in the consumer's favour. You have a strong basis to escalate, and here is exactly what to do and what to say."
- Direct consumer to: File a formal written complaint with the insurer first — this is required before the FOS will accept a case; if not resolved within eight weeks or if the insurer issues a final response you reject, escalate to the Financial Ombudsman Service (financial-ombudsman.org.uk or 0800 023 4567, free of charge); reference FOS decision DRN-4409621 explicitly in your FOS submission; contact Citizens Advice (citizensadvice.org.uk) or Which? for free guidance on drafting a strong complaint letter
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Copy of the policy wording including the exact COVID exclusion clause; official government travel advisory confirming the mandatory random testing protocol was in place at the destination at the date of travel; all receipts for extended hotel, flights, meals, and medical costs; the insurer's written rejection letter; a written record of the call center conversation where further review was refused; booking confirmation showing the trip was booked before the testing requirement existed
- Feedback category tag: Small print exclusion — blanket pandemic clause applied to random government-mandated testing; FOS precedent available (DRN-4409621); consumer refused internal escalation by call center; transparency failure; information failure

### Scenario 4-6: Ghost Broker Sells Void Motor Insurance — Consumer Discovered Uninsured When Stopped by Police (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** City of London Police / Action Fraud UK annual data 2024 — 817 ghost broker reports, 24% year-on-year increase over 2023; social media car insurance scam reports nearly doubled (90 to 179 cases), losses £185,369; Aviva reported 22% surge in detected ghost broking cases since 2023; 84% of young drivers affected experienced declined claims, falsified policy details, or police stop while unknowingly uninsured; Sheikh Tanvir Uddin, 22, sentenced to 32 weeks' imprisonment (suspended 12 months) as serial ghost broker, 2024.
**The Problem:** A 20-year-old driver purchased what appeared to be a legitimate car insurance policy through a social media contact presenting as an insurance broker, at roughly half the price quoted on comparison sites. She received a policy document bearing a recognisable insurer's name and a real-looking policy number. Three months later she was stopped by police during a routine check. The Police National Computer showed her vehicle as uninsured. When she called the number on her certificate, the insurer confirmed the policy had been cancelled two weeks after inception — the ghost broker had taken out the policy using falsified personal data to lower the premium, then cancelled it and kept her payment. She received no cancellation notice and had been driving without insurance for over two months. She now faces potential police proceedings for driving without insurance, despite being the victim of a criminal fraud.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm the policy no longer exists. Direct her to the police and Action Fraud. Decline any liability since the policy was obtained by the broker using falsified data. Provide no practical guidance on her legal exposure or how to regularise her insurance position.
**What the Owner does:** Validates that she is the victim of a known and documented criminal fraud type — Action Fraud recorded 817 cases of this exact pattern in 2024. Tells her that reporting to Action Fraud promptly and preserving all evidence of the social media transaction is important for any police proceedings against her and for any civil claim against the broker if identifiable. Advises her to obtain genuine insurance immediately before driving again.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: Flag as time-sensitive — she must not drive again until a genuine policy is in place; immediate financial and legal risk
- Opening reply: "You are the victim of a criminal fraud. Action Fraud UK recorded 817 cases of exactly this pattern in 2024. What happened to you has a name — ghost broking — there is a specific reporting process for it, and your status as a fraud victim matters for any police proceedings. Here is what to do now."
- Direct consumer to: Report to Action Fraud immediately (actionfraud.police.uk or 0300 123 2040) specifying ghost broking — get a crime reference number; obtain genuine motor insurance today before driving again under any circumstances; contact the Motor Insurers' Bureau (mib.org.uk) if you were involved in any accident while uninsured — they provide limited compensation for uninsured accidents; preserve all social media evidence of the broker; consult a solicitor about any police proceedings — victim-of-fraud status is a recognised mitigating factor and prosecutors have issued guidance on this
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshots of all social media messages, advertisements, and communications with the ghost broker (do not delete anything); the original policy certificate, certificate of motor insurance, and any schedule documents; any receipts or bank transfer records showing your payment; the broker's phone number, social media accounts, and any account names used; the police report reference from the roadside stop; the insurer's confirmation that the policy was cancelled and when
- Feedback category tag: Policy voided without consumer notice — ghost broker criminal fraud; consumer turned away at point of claim; financial fraud; corruption; young driver vulnerability; Action Fraud referral

### Scenario 4-7: Cancer Patient's Chemotherapy Claim Rejected — Drug Not on Insurer Formulary Despite No Alternative Existing (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India) Annual Report 2024-25; BusinessToday India, December 2024 — disallowed and repudiated health insurance claim value rose 19.1% year-on-year to Rs 26,000 crore (approximately USD 3.1 billion) in FY2023-24; 11% of health insurance claims denied outright, 6% pending as of March 2024; 2,15,569 grievances received on IRDAI Bima Bharosa portal in 2023-24.
**The Problem:** A 55-year-old school teacher in Pune is undergoing chemotherapy for stage II breast cancer. Her employer-provided health insurance policy has a stated oncology cover limit of Rs 10 lakh. After her third chemotherapy cycle, the insurer rejected an Rs 85,000 hospitalisation and treatment claim. The stated reason is that the specific chemotherapy drug her oncologist prescribed is not on the insurer's approved formulary. Her oncologist has confirmed in writing that no formulary-approved alternative drug exists for her specific cancer subtype. The insurer's rejection letter names no medical reviewer, provides no clinical justification, and states only that the claim is "not admissible under policy terms." She cannot self-fund further treatment and her oncologist has said delaying the next cycle will affect her prognosis.
**What staff/org would do:** Maintain the formulary exclusion applies. Offer no information about the IRDAI grievance process or Insurance Ombudsman. Decline to connect her with any medical reviewer. Provide no timeline for any reconsideration.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as both a clinical necessity case and a regulatory rights case. Informs the consumer that she can file a grievance directly with IRDAI through the Bima Bharosa portal, which carries formal regulatory weight and triggers a mandated response from the insurer. Advises approaching the Insurance Ombudsman for Maharashtra within 30 days of the insurer's final rejection. Notes that IRDAI data shows the regulator is actively tracking the 19.1% rise in rejected claims and has issued guidance on formulary disputes where no alternative drug exists.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Rejecting a chemotherapy claim when your oncologist has confirmed there is no alternative drug is a decision that can and should be challenged through formal regulatory channels — and India's insurance regulator takes cases like this seriously. Here is what to do immediately to protect your treatment."
- Direct consumer to: File a grievance on the IRDAI Bima Bharosa portal immediately (bimabharosa.irdai.gov.in) — this creates a formal regulatory record the insurer must respond to; contact the Insurance Ombudsman for Maharashtra (Mumbai office, 022-22044960) within 30 days of receiving the rejection letter; contact a Consumer Forum under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 if the Ombudsman process is too slow given your treatment timeline; ask your oncologist to prepare a formal letter of medical necessity specifically addressing why no formulary alternative exists for your cancer subtype
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The written rejection letter with date and reference number; the oncologist's letter confirming no formulary-approved alternative drug exists for this cancer subtype; all hospitalisation bills, prescription records, and treatment summaries; the original policy document with the oncology cover terms and any formulary reference; records of any prior approved claims for earlier cycles of the same treatment; a log of any call center interactions with dates and what was told
- Feedback category tag: Claim denial — formulary exclusion applied despite no clinical alternative; oncology; active treatment disruption; financial harm; IRDAI escalation available; consumer refused service

### Scenario 4-8: Health Insurance Pre-Authorization Stuck for Eight Weeks — Six Calls, No Supervisor, No Callback, Surgery Cannot Proceed (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** IRDAI Annual Report 2024-25 — 2,15,569 grievances received on Bima Bharosa portal in 2023-24 (94,843 general insurance); IRDAI's explicit acknowledgment that the Bima Bharosa portal was created because consumers have no effective escalation channel through standard insurer call centers; 6% of health claims pending unresolved as of March 2024 across the sector.
**The Problem:** A 38-year-old IT professional in Bengaluru filed a health insurance pre-authorization request eight weeks ago for a planned orthopaedic surgery to correct a degenerative knee condition affecting his ability to work. The claim shows as "under review" on the insurer's app and has not moved. He has called the insurer's toll-free number six times over the eight weeks. Each call provides a different answer: documents are incomplete despite successful uploads, the case is with the internal medical team, or the case has been transferred to another department. On the sixth call he asked to be transferred to a supervisor and was told no supervisor was available and that he would receive a callback within 48 hours. No callback came. The hospital will not schedule surgery without the pre-authorization in writing.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue routing to the general call center queue. Issue no escalation callback. Accept no ownership of the case across the six interactions. Continue marking the claim "under review" with no resolution date.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms this is a documented systemic failure — IRDAI created the Bima Bharosa portal specifically because insurer call centers routinely fail to resolve legitimate pre-authorization requests and complaints. Guides the consumer to the formal regulatory escalation pathway, which creates a legal obligation for the insurer to respond within IRDAI's mandated timeframe, and to the insurer's Grievance Redressal Officer, which the insurer is required to designate under IRDAI rules.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: Flag as time-sensitive — ongoing delay to medically indicated surgery; escalate accordingly
- Opening reply: "Six calls and eight weeks with no resolution is exactly why India's insurance regulator built a formal complaints portal. Filing there creates a legal obligation for the insurer to respond within a regulated timeframe. Here is how to use it and what else to do in parallel."
- Direct consumer to: File on the IRDAI Bima Bharosa portal immediately (bimabharosa.irdai.gov.in) — this is the primary escalation route for exactly this situation; contact the insurer's Grievance Redressal Officer (GRO) in writing by email — IRDAI requires every insurer to have one and to respond within 15 days; contact the Insurance Ombudsman for Karnataka (Bengaluru office, 080-22210771) within 30 days if the insurer has issued no response; put your complaint to the insurer in writing (email to their complaints address) so you have a timestamped record
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshots of the pre-authorization status on the insurer app for each date checked, showing eight weeks of inaction; a written log of all six calls with dates, duration, representative names if given, and what each said; copies of all documents uploaded to the portal with upload confirmation timestamps; the hospital's written requirement for pre-authorization before scheduling; the original policy document; the orthopaedic surgeon's referral and recommendation for surgery
- Feedback category tag: Call center runaround — six contacts over eight weeks, no supervisor access, no callback, no resolution; pre-authorization delay blocking surgery; health insurance; information failure; IRDAI formal escalation available

### Scenario 4-9: Motor Insurance Claim Unresolved Four Months — No Repairer Assigned, Hire Car Withheld, Delivery Driver Losing Income (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) Annual Review 2024-25 — 34,231 general insurance complaints received, 17% increase over 2023-24; motor vehicle claim delays are the single largest complaint category at 25% of all general insurance complaints; AFCA described motor delays as "a longstanding pain point for customers"; 45% of general insurance complaints resolved at first AFCA referral stage — indicating systemic internal failure before external escalation; AFCA stated industry needs "to do more to prevent avoidable complaints."
**The Problem:** A self-employed delivery driver in Melbourne lodged a motor vehicle insurance claim four months ago after a not-at-fault collision. The insurer accepted liability in principle within the first two weeks. Since then, no repairer has been assigned. Every two to three weeks he calls the claims line and is told a repairer is "being sourced due to parts and workshop availability." He has not been offered a hire vehicle despite his policy explicitly including a hire vehicle provision. His monthly income has fallen by approximately AUD 2,400 because the vehicle is off the road. Every call is handled by a different claims consultant who starts the conversation from scratch with no apparent record of prior contact.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue advising a repairer is being sourced. Decline to activate the hire vehicle provision until a repairer has formally accepted the job. Route each incoming call to whichever consultant is available, requiring the consumer to re-explain the situation from the beginning each time.
**What the Owner does:** Identifies that four months without a repairer assigned is well outside any reasonable claims handling standard and now constitutes a complaint-worthy failure. Advises that the consumer can file directly with AFCA, which resolved 45% of general insurance complaints at the very first referral stage — often faster than internal processes. Also advises putting the hire vehicle demand in writing immediately to create a documented record and trigger the insurer's obligation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: No life risk, but flag as ongoing financial harm — AUD 2,400 per month income loss
- Opening reply: "Four months without a repairer, and no hire car despite your policy covering it — AFCA deals with this exact situation regularly. Motor claim delays are their single largest complaint category. You have a clear basis to escalate now, and it typically moves faster than waiting for the insurer to act."
- Direct consumer to: Send one final written complaint to the insurer by email giving 10 business days to respond with a repairer assigned and hire vehicle activated — AFCA requires evidence of an insurer complaint attempt before accepting a case; then file immediately with AFCA (afca.org.au or 1800 931 678) — free, decisions are binding on insurers up to AUD 1,085,000; specifically demand in your insurer complaint that the hire vehicle provision be activated from the original claim date; contact the Insurance Council of Australia (insurancecouncil.com.au) to note a systemic complaint about claim handling standards
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Original claim reference number and the written liability acceptance from week two; a complete log of every call made with dates, hold times, names, and what each consultant said; the policy document evidencing the hire vehicle provision; income records documenting the monthly loss — invoices, bank statements, platform earnings records; photographs of the damaged vehicle; any repair estimates obtained independently; evidence of any written or verbal promises made about the repairer timeline
- Feedback category tag: Delayed payout — four-month motor claim with no repairer assigned and hire car withheld; call center failure (no continuity between contacts); financial harm to self-employed consumer; AFCA escalation available

### Scenario 4-10: Life Insurance Claim Denied After Partner's Death — Insurer Voids Policy Citing Eight-Year-Old GP Cholesterol Consultation as Non-Disclosure (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** AFCA Annual Review 2024-25 — life insurance denial of claim complaints rose 40% in 2024-25 over the prior year; general insurance complaints up 17%; AFCA explicitly stated insurers need "to do more to prevent avoidable complaints"; non-disclosure disputes are the primary driver of life insurance denial complaints received by AFCA; AFCA noted high rates of insurer-side errors in post-death underwriting reviews.
**The Problem:** A 44-year-old nurse in Sydney lost her partner to a sudden cardiac event six months ago. He held a life insurance policy of AUD 750,000. They have one dependent child. The insurer conducted a post-death underwriting review and issued a denial letter three months after the death, stating the policy is void because the deceased did not disclose a GP consultation about elevated cholesterol that occurred eight years before the policy was taken out. That consultation resulted only in lifestyle advice — no medication was prescribed and no heart disease diagnosis was made. The insurer's position is that this constitutes material non-disclosure voiding the entire policy. The denial letter offers no settlement and no partial payment. The consumer was not told about her right to escalate to AFCA and was given only the insurer's internal 45-day dispute process.
**What staff/org would do:** Maintain the void determination on the basis of non-disclosure of a material pre-existing condition. Route the consumer to the internal dispute process. Provide no information about AFCA rights, applicable time limits, or whether a proportionate partial settlement is available. Keep the complete void as the standing position.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the extreme difficulty of this situation — grief, a dependent child, and a complete loss of expected financial protection. Advises that non-disclosure disputes are the most commonly challenged category of life insurance denial at AFCA, and that AFCA applies a proportionality test: a GP consultation for cholesterol eight years prior with no prescription and no diagnosis is unlikely to meet the legal threshold of "material non-disclosure" under Australian contract law and the Insurance Contracts Act 1984. Strongly advises filing with AFCA without delay.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Losing your partner and then receiving a letter voiding the policy he arranged for your family is devastating, and I want you to know that this denial is not necessarily final. Non-disclosure cases like this are among the most commonly overturned at AFCA, and the specific facts here — a GP visit eight years ago with no prescription and no diagnosis — give you real grounds to challenge it. Here is what to do."
- Direct consumer to: File a complaint with AFCA immediately (afca.org.au or 1800 931 678) — free, no cost, time limits apply so do not delay; contact the Insurance Law Service for free specialist legal advice (1300 663 464, insurancelawservice.org.au); request the full GP records from that consultation in writing from the GP practice — confirming no diagnosis was made and no medication prescribed, this is the central evidence; if the claim exceeds AFCA's jurisdiction limit, consult a specialist insurance lawyer — the Insurance Law Service can advise on this; ask the GP for a letter confirming the consultation was a routine lifestyle discussion only
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The denial letter in full including the specific non-disclosure ground cited; the original policy documents and the application form as completed; complete GP records from the specific consultation in question (request formally in writing from the practice); the death certificate and cause of death; any independent medical evidence establishing the cardiac event was unrelated to the cholesterol consultation; proof of all premium payments made; the couple's financial and insurance records establishing their reliance on the policy; a written record of what the insurer said about the internal dispute process and what was not said about AFCA
- Feedback category tag: Claim denial — post-death non-disclosure void; proportionality dispute; life insurance; financial harm; dependent child; grief and vulnerability; AFCA escalation available; transparency failure

Industry ID: 4

## 5. Telecommunications

### Scenario 5-1: Unable to Call Emergency Services During National Network Outage (Pattern A+D)
**Real Basis:** ACMA investigation into Optus national network outage, 8 November 2023; Optus pays $8M in fines for Triple Zero failure — LightReading; Wikipedia — 2023 Optus outage
**The Problem:** It is 6am. A customer's elderly parent has collapsed at home. The customer dials Triple Zero (000) from their Optus mobile — the call fails. They try again. It fails again. They have no landline. Their neighbour is on the same carrier. They are unable to reach emergency services because the entire Optus network is down — a nationwide outage that will last 14 hours, affecting over 10 million people. There is no push notification from Optus, no outage notice on the website, and no public guidance on how to reach emergency services from an affected mobile.
**What staff/org would do:** No call center is reachable during the outage. The Optus website shows no outage notice at 6am. Customers who later discover they can use Wi-Fi calling through a different network receive no proactive guidance from Optus during the outage itself.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately treat this as a life-safety emergency. Provide practical alternatives to reach emergency services right now, advise the customer to seek help from anyone nearby on a different carrier, and document this as a critical service failure requiring regulatory notification.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: Acknowledge this is a life-safety emergency first. Provide immediate alternatives — use a landline, go to a neighbour with a different carrier, use any available Wi-Fi calling app on a different network, or ask someone nearby with a working phone to call for help. Every second matters before addressing the complaint.
- Who to direct the consumer to: ACMA (Australian Communications and Media Authority) at acma.gov.au for a formal outage complaint once the emergency is resolved; Telecommunications Industry Ombudsman (TIO) at 1800 062 058 for compensation inquiry; the ACMA-Optus $8M enforcement finding is on the record and supports formal redress
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of failed emergency call attempts with exact timestamps, any Optus app or SMS notifications received during the outage period (or documented absence thereof), neighbour or witness confirmation that the same carrier had no service
- Feedback category: Emergency Access Failure / Network Outage

---

### Scenario 5-2: Small Business Refused Cash Compensation After Outage Destroyed a Trading Day (Pattern A+D)
**Real Basis:** Optus compensated users $1.2m for lost income from 2023 outage — ITNews; Wikipedia — 2023 Optus outage; ACMA investigation findings
**The Problem:** A small café owner lost an entire trading day because their EFTPOS payment terminals depend on the Optus network to process card payments. On 8 November 2023, the 14-hour outage meant the café could only accept cash — costing an estimated $3,200 in lost card sales. The owner contacts Optus to claim compensation. The response: 200 GB of bonus mobile data. The owner's business does not run on mobile data. They ask for cash compensation to cover documented income loss and are told this is not available — only service credits.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent confirms the outage is acknowledged. They offer the 200 GB data package. When the owner requests cash, the agent reads a script stating Optus's liability is limited to service credits. No escalation to a compensation panel is available from the front line. The customer is told to monitor the website.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the real financial loss and confirm clearly that a data credit is not adequate compensation for documented business income loss. Validate the customer's right to pursue cash redress through formal channels and record the refusal.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge the genuine business harm and make clear that bonus data is not a substitute for documented revenue loss. Confirm the customer's right to pursue cash compensation through formal channels and that their refusal experience should itself be on record.
- Who to direct the consumer to: Telecommunications Industry Ombudsman (TIO) at 1800 062 058 — can award up to $1,500 per complaint; for larger documented losses, advise the small business claims tribunal in the relevant state; ACCC (Australian Competition and Consumer Commission) if Optus's compensation terms are found to be misleading under Australian Consumer Law
- Evidence to collect: EFTPOS terminal records showing transaction volume drop on 8 November 2023 compared to comparable trading days, business financial records for that period, written refusal from Optus (email confirmation or transcript of the call), documentation of the data credit offer
- Feedback category: Financial Harm / Compensation Refusal

---

### Scenario 5-3: Overcharged for Years, Call Center Told Customer the Bill Was Correct (Pattern B+D)
**Real Basis:** Ofcom fines O2 £10.5m for overcharging customers; Ofcom enforcement decision 2021; billing failure ran 2011–2019 affecting 250,000 customers
**The Problem:** A customer reviews their monthly O2 mobile bill and notices a recurring charge they do not recognise — a duplicate line item that appears to have been present for some time. When they call O2's customer service line to dispute it, the agent reviews the account and states: "The bill is correct, all charges are valid." The customer pushes back. The agent repeats the same line and offers to send a bill summary. No mention is made of any systemic billing fault. The customer ends the call unsatisfied but uncertain — perhaps they are wrong.
**What staff/org would do:** The first-line agent has no visibility of the systemic billing error affecting over 250,000 customers. They verify that the charge exists in the billing system and confirm it is legitimate. The complaint is logged as resolved at first contact.
**What the Owner does:** Take the customer's dispute seriously regardless of what the agent says. Flag that a systemic billing error is the documented cause of this type of charge at O2, and that verbal confirmation from an agent is not the end of the matter.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Validate the customer's concern directly. An agent telling a customer their bill is correct does not make it correct — O2's own billing system contained an error that front-line agents had no access to for years. The customer has the right to a formal written explanation and refund review.
- Who to direct the consumer to: Ofcom complaint portal at ofcom.org.uk/complaints if O2 does not resolve within 8 weeks; Ombudsman Services: Communications (free ADR, binding on O2); cite the 2021 Ofcom enforcement decision against O2 for systemic overcharging; request a written itemised explanation of all charges under Consumer Rights Act 2015
- Evidence to collect: Minimum three months of bills showing the disputed charge, date and reference number of the call where the agent confirmed the charge as correct, formal written complaint submitted to O2 with response received
- Feedback category: Financial Overcharge / Billing Error / Agent Misinformation

---

### Scenario 5-4: Exit Fee Collected on a Contract That Was Legally Unenforceable (Pattern B+D)
**Real Basis:** Ofcom fines BT £2.8m for failing its EE and Plusnet customers; July 2023; 1.1 million customers found to be in void contracts
**The Problem:** A customer who joined EE 18 months ago decides to switch to a cheaper provider and calls to cancel. The agent states that an Early Termination Fee of £187 applies because the customer is still inside their minimum contract term. The customer pays the £187, trusting the agent is correct. What the agent does not know — and what EE never disclosed to the customer — is that EE failed to supply legally required pre-contract information and contract summaries to over one million customers, rendering those contracts unenforceable under Ofcom's General Conditions. The customer has paid an exit fee in a contract that did not legally bind them.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent processes the exit fee payment as routine. No flag is raised about the underlying contract compliance failure. The customer is not told they had the legal right to leave without charge. The charge is collected and the interaction is closed.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately flag this as a rights violation. Inform the customer about the Ofcom enforcement finding and their clear entitlement to a full refund of the exit fee under a contract that was never legally binding.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge that paying an exit fee is distressing, particularly when there is a specific regulatory finding that may mean it should never have been charged. Explain that the Ofcom July 2023 enforcement action against EE found over one million contracts were non-compliant and customers had the right to leave without penalty.
- Who to direct the consumer to: Ofcom complaint portal at ofcom.org.uk/complaints — cite the July 2023 BT/EE/Plusnet enforcement decision and request refund of exit fees under Ofcom General Conditions; if EE does not resolve within 8 weeks, escalate to Ombudsman Services: Communications for a binding determination
- Evidence to collect: Contract documents — particularly the absence of a pre-contract information summary and contract summary, which is itself the evidence; payment receipt or bank statement showing the exit fee deduction; any written communication from EE confirming the fee was mandatory
- Feedback category: Rights Violation / Unlawful Exit Fee / Contract Compliance

---

### Scenario 5-5: Three Agents, Three Different Answers About the Same Bill (Pattern B+C)
**Real Basis:** Ofcom Q4 2024 complaints data — Virgin Media and Three cited with billing and pricing as top complaint category; Communications Ombudsman Q3 2024 rise; Citizens Advice "Hanging on the Telephone" research 2024
**The Problem:** A Virgin Media customer is being charged £12 per month more than the price quoted when they signed up 14 months ago. They call customer service. Agent A says the increase was in the terms they agreed to and is non-negotiable. They call back and reach Agent B, who says this was a mid-contract price rise and offers a £6 monthly goodwill discount. They call a third time to confirm the discount is applied and Agent C says no discount is on the account and the current price is the correct contractual price. Not one agent can see the notes from the previous calls. Not one agent has authority to authorise a billing correction unilaterally.
**What staff/org would do:** Each agent interprets the account independently. None can access what the previous agent discussed or promised. The customer ends each call with a different understanding of their contract and charges. The issue remains unresolved after three separate contacts.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge that receiving three contradictory answers from the same company is a service failure, not a customer misunderstanding. Help the customer build a documented record of each contact and provide a clear route to formal escalation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge the exhaustion of calling three times and getting three different answers. Confirm that this is a recognised call center failure — not something the customer misunderstood — and that there is a formal path to get a single definitive written answer.
- Who to direct the consumer to: Ofcom complaint portal after 8 weeks unresolved; Ombudsman Services: Communications (free, binding on Virgin Media) — request a deadlock letter from Virgin Media to trigger ADR eligibility immediately; cite the right to a written explanation of any mid-contract price change under the Electronic Communications (Consumer Contracts) Regulations
- Evidence to collect: Dates, times, and agent reference numbers for all three calls, any SMS or email following any call that referenced a discount or confirmed a price, a copy of the original signed contract or welcome email showing the agreed monthly price
- Feedback category: Call Center Failure / Misinformation / Billing Dispute

---

### Scenario 5-6: Cancellation Request Refused, Hidden Number, Extended Hold, Billed Again the Following Month (Pattern C+B)
**Real Basis:** FTC action against Vonage — $100 million in refunds to 389,106 consumers; FTC complaint filed November 2022; refunds disbursed October 2023
**The Problem:** A small business owner wants to cancel their Vonage internet phone service. They email Vonage — no response. They use live chat — an agent tells them cancellation must be done by phone and provides no number. They search the website for over 20 minutes before finding a cancellation number buried several pages deep. They call and are told the wait time is 45 minutes. They hang up. The following month, Vonage charges an early termination fee. The month after that, Vonage charges the full monthly subscription again — the account was never cancelled. The customer has now been billed for two months after their initial cancellation attempt.
**What staff/org would do:** Email and chat agents deflect to phone. The phone line is deliberately understaffed with restricted hours. When a customer does reach a retention agent, they face further pressure to stay. No cancellation confirmation is issued. Charges continue.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge that this is a documented pattern of deliberate consumer entrapment — not an isolated mistake. Confirm the customer's rights under FTC enforcement findings and guide them to dispute all charges made after the first cancellation request.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge that this matches a pattern the FTC found to be illegal and that the customer has clear grounds to dispute all charges made after their first documented cancellation attempt. This is not a service failure — it is a documented practice the FTC has already acted against.
- Who to direct the consumer to: FTC complaint portal at ftc.gov/complaint; dispute all post-cancellation charges directly with the credit card or bank as unauthorised billing; contact Vonage in writing by certified mail stating the original cancellation date; note that the FTC refund programme distributed funds to 389,106 consumers in October 2023 and any eligible customer who did not receive a refund can contact the FTC administrator
- Evidence to collect: Date-stamped emails and chat transcripts of all cancellation requests, screenshots of the Vonage website showing how the cancellation phone number was buried, bank or card statements showing all charges after the first cancellation request
- Feedback category: Consumer Entrapment / Cancellation Refusal / Call Center Barrier
