### Scenario 5-7: SIM Transferred by Agent Despite Active PIN, Cryptocurrency Accounts Drained Overnight (Pattern E+A)
**Real Basis:** T-Mobile hit with $33M arbitration award over SIM swap attack; fraud February 2020; arbitration finalised 2023–2024; SecurityWeek; MobileIDWorld
**The Problem:** A customer woke at 3am to find their phone had lost mobile service entirely. Within 90 minutes, attackers who had persuaded a T-Mobile agent to transfer the customer's phone number to a SIM card they controlled — bypassing an eight-digit PIN the customer had specifically set on the account — used the hijacked number to intercept two-factor authentication codes and drain cryptocurrency accounts worth over $2 million. The customer calls T-Mobile the following morning. The agent confirms a SIM change was processed and that it was "verified successfully." When the customer asks how an eight-digit PIN was bypassed, the agent says they cannot see that information and transfers the call to the fraud team — which has a three-hour hold time.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent confirms the SIM transfer but cannot explain the PIN bypass. No immediate account freeze or number restoration is offered at first contact. The fraud team escalation wait exceeds three hours. T-Mobile's broader response is to deny responsibility and attempt to keep the arbitration award sealed from public view.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as an active financial crime with additional accounts still potentially at risk. Confirm every immediate step to stop further access and preserve all evidence, before addressing any complaint process.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: Acknowledge this is a serious financial crime. Before anything else, the customer must contact every financial institution linked to the compromised phone number to freeze accounts and disable SMS-based two-factor authentication — additional accounts may still be at risk right now.
- Who to direct the consumer to: All financial institutions immediately to freeze accounts and disable SMS MFA; FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov; FCC Consumer Complaint Center at consumercomplaints.fcc.gov; FTC at ftc.gov/complaint; T-Mobile arbitration is available under their subscriber agreement — at the scale of financial loss involved, legal counsel is strongly recommended
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the phone losing service with exact timestamp, all cryptocurrency exchange transaction records from the night of the attack, T-Mobile account records showing the SIM change timestamp, any prior written confirmation from T-Mobile that the eight-digit PIN was active and required for account changes
- Feedback category: Security Failure / SIM Swap Fraud / Agent Misconduct

### Scenario 5-8: Discovered Carrier Sold Location Data for Years — Call Center Agents Have No Script (Pattern A+E)
**Real Basis:** FCC fines AT&T $57M, T-Mobile $80M, Verizon $47M, Sprint $12M for selling customer location data without consent; FCC enforcement April 2024; Krebs on Security reporting
**The Problem:** A customer reads a news report confirming the FCC has fined all four major US wireless carriers — including their own — for selling precise real-time GPS location data to third-party aggregators for years without obtaining valid customer consent. The customer calls their carrier's customer service line to ask: was their location data sold, to whom, for what purpose, and how can they opt out going forward? The agent who answers has no information about this matter. They do not know what data was shared, who received it, or what the customer can do. They offer to transfer the call. The customer is transferred twice and then disconnected.
**What staff/org would do:** No briefing or script was provided to call center staff about the FCC enforcement action. Customers are transferred between departments and disconnected. No written disclosure of what data was shared is made available to customers. No opt-out mechanism is proactively communicated.
**What the Owner does:** Validate the customer's concern as legitimate and grounded in a real regulatory enforcement action. Provide the formal channels through which the customer can demand disclosure and file a complaint that creates a paper trail.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge that the customer's alarm is entirely justified and based on a documented FCC enforcement action. Confirm that they have the right to a formal account of what data was collected and shared, and that the agent's inability to answer is itself part of the failure pattern the FCC identified.
- Who to direct the consumer to: FCC Consumer Complaint Center at consumercomplaints.fcc.gov; submit a formal written data disclosure request to the carrier citing the April 2024 FCC enforcement findings; if based in California, submit a CCPA data access request demanding a list of all third parties the carrier shared location data with; Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF) at eff.org for further privacy guidance
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the news article with publication date, dates of calls where agents were unable to provide information (document the transfers and disconnection), any prior privacy opt-out settings the customer had configured with the carrier, carrier billing records establishing their account was active during the period of the FCC investigation
- Feedback category: Privacy Violation / Transparency Failure / Data Sold Without Consent

---

### Scenario 5-9: Unauthorised Charge Persists Three Months, Complaint Never Formally Registered (Pattern C+D)
**Real Basis:** TRAI received 44,734 telecom complaints in 2023–24; Rs 2.69 crore in penalties for Quality of Service violations; TRAI complaint framework documentation; Storyboard18 reporting
**The Problem:** A customer in India notices a recurring charge of Rs 49 per month on their postpaid mobile bill for a "value added service" they never subscribed to. They call the carrier's customer care line. After 22 minutes on hold, the call is answered and then disconnected before they can speak. On the second call, after 18 minutes on hold, an agent gives a verbal assurance the charge will be removed — but does not issue a docket number. The following month, the charge appears again. When the customer calls to follow up, the agent has no record of any previous complaint. This cycle has now repeated three times across three months.
**What staff/org would do:** Each call produces a verbal assurance and no docket number. Without a docket number in the TRAI Telecom Consumer Complaint Monitoring System, the customer cannot formally escalate. The 7-day mandatory resolution window restarts every time. The operator faces no accountability because the complaint is never formally entered into the system.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the frustration of a complaint loop that resets every month. Explain that being denied a docket number is itself a regulatory failure — not a normal outcome — and provide the bypass path to force formal registration.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge this is a documented failure pattern in India's telecom complaint process. A carrier is legally required to issue a docket number and resolve within 7 days — denying one is a regulatory breach, not a normal outcome. The customer has the right to escalate directly.
- Who to direct the consumer to: TRAI TCCMS portal at tccms.trai.gov.in — customers can register complaints directly when carriers fail to issue a docket number within 3 days; TRAI consumer helpline at 1800-110-420 (toll-free); after registering on TCCMS, if unresolved within 10 days the customer can approach the carrier's Appellate Authority, details of which must appear on the carrier's website and billing statements
- Evidence to collect: Bank or UPI statements showing the Rs 49 charge across all affected months, dates and approximate times of all calls made to customer care, screenshot of the bill showing the "value added service" label, any SMS received from the carrier acknowledging a complaint (or documented absence of such SMS)
- Feedback category: Unauthorised Charge / Complaint Access Barrier / Vulnerability

### Scenario 5-10: Paid for Broadband Activation, Technician Never Came, Refund Denied (Pattern C+D)
**Real Basis:** TRAI 2023–24 annual data — non-activation of services documented as a major complaint category; TRAI Quality of Service regulation violations; financial penalties imposed on operators in 2023–24
**The Problem:** A customer in India paid Rs 1,499 for a new broadband connection. The technician visit was scheduled, then cancelled by the operator without notice. A second appointment was made and also did not occur. When the customer calls to request either service activation or a refund, the agent tells them "the order is under processing" and to wait 5 to 7 more business days. Three weeks have now passed since payment. The customer has no broadband, no confirmed activation date, and no refund — and cannot dispute the charge with their bank because the operator has issued no written cancellation or delay notice.
**What staff/org would do:** Each call receives a "processing" response. No supervisor is available. No written confirmation of the delay is issued. A refund request, when submitted, is given a 10 to 15 business day timeline with no written acknowledgment. The agent offers to re-raise a technician visit but cannot commit to a date.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge that payment without service delivery after three weeks is a consumer protection violation. Confirm the customer's right to either a firm activation date or a full refund within a defined and enforceable period, and provide the escalation path.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: Acknowledge that paying for a service and receiving neither activation nor a refund after three weeks is not acceptable and is actionable. Confirm clearly that the customer has the right to a firm commitment — a date for service or a full refund — and that verbal "processing" assurances are not sufficient.
- Who to direct the consumer to: TRAI TCCMS portal at tccms.trai.gov.in to formally register the non-activation complaint; carrier Appellate Authority (details must appear on the carrier's website under regulatory compliance — mandated by TRAI); National Consumer Helpline at 1800-11-4000 (toll-free) or consumerhelpline.gov.in for disputes involving paid amounts; if no resolution within 30 days, the customer can file at the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission
- Evidence to collect: Payment receipt or bank/UPI statement confirming Rs 1,499 debit with date, any SMS or email confirming the broadband order and scheduled installation dates, a written record of each call to customer care including dates and any agent names provided, documentation of both failed technician visits
- Feedback category: Service Non-Activation / Refund Denial / Consumer Protection

Industry ID: 5

## 6. Energy / Utilities / Water

### Scenario 6-1: Vulnerable Mother with Newborn Has Prepayment Meter Forcibly Installed (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** The Times undercover investigation, January 2023; Ofgem enforcement and British Gas settlement (up to £70 million debt write-off); Bloomberg regulatory report, 2023 — Arvato Financial Solutions agents documented breaking into homes of customers with active vulnerability flags including a mother with a four-week-old baby
**The Problem:** A mother who gave birth four weeks ago is at home with her newborn when a debt collection agent arrives under warrant and forces entry to install a prepayment meter. Her account shows an outstanding debt of £480 but also carries a flag indicating a newborn in the household. She was never offered a payment plan or referred to a hardship fund. She calls her energy supplier in distress — the baby's room is now cold and she cannot afford to top up the meter immediately.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent reads the outstanding debt on screen, confirms the meter installation was carried out under a valid warrant, and offers to send a top-up instructions leaflet. The account vulnerability flag is visible but not acted upon. No hardship referral is made.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately flags this as a Priority Services Register (PSR) case requiring emergency intervention. Suspends any further debt action. Arranges an emergency credit top-up under the supplier's hardship provision. Escalates to a vulnerability team specialist within minutes. Opens a formal complaint and documents the PSR flag breach in writing.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a serious situation involving your newborn's safety and welfare. What has happened to you should not have happened — suppliers have a legal duty to protect households with babies under 12 months from forced meter installations. You have rights here and we are going to help you use them right now."
- Direct to: Ofgem consumer helpline (0808 223 1133); Citizens Advice Extra Help Unit for vulnerable consumer escalation; the supplier's dedicated vulnerability team (request by name on every call); Priority Services Register enrolment if not already enrolled
- Evidence to preserve: Photograph the installed meter showing the installation date; request written confirmation of the warrant from the supplier; keep all debt correspondence and call logs noting times and agent names; preserve any maternity or medical records confirming the newborn's age
- Feedback category tag: Vulnerability rights violation / Forced meter installation / Welfare emergency — is_urgent

---

### Scenario 6-2: Energy Ombudsman Binding Decision Ignored — Customer Still Waiting 14 Months Later (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Ofgem formal compliance engagement with OVO Energy, July 2023; Ofgem press release September 2024; £2.37 million settlement (£378,512 direct compensation plus £2 million to the Energy Industry Voluntary Redress Scheme); 1,395 customers affected with delays up to 18 months; 706 classified as vulnerable
**The Problem:** A customer lodged a complaint with their energy supplier about a billing dispute. After eight weeks without resolution they escalated to the Energy Ombudsman, which ruled in their favour and issued a binding decision requiring a credit of £340 and a formal written apology. That decision was issued 14 months ago. The credit has never appeared. No apology has been received. Every call to the supplier results in being told the case is "being processed" and being transferred to a different team, where the process starts again from the beginning.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent acknowledges the Ombudsman reference number, places the customer on hold, and returns to say the case is with the "resolutions team" and will be actioned "within 28 days." This is the third time the customer has been given this answer in six months.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately retrieves the Energy Ombudsman decision document and the compliance deadline. Confirms the decision has not been actioned. Opens a compliance breach case internally. Contacts the Energy Ombudsman's compliance monitoring team directly to report non-implementation. Confirms in writing to the customer what specific actions will occur and by what date.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "You are owed a remedy by a binding legal decision and your supplier is not delivering it. This is a compliance failure, not a process delay, and you have the right to escalate it immediately — both to the Energy Ombudsman's compliance team and to Ofgem directly."
- Direct to: Energy Ombudsman compliance team (0330 440 1624) to report that a binding decision is not being implemented; Ofgem consumer complaints line referencing the OVO enforcement action; Citizens Advice consumer helpline (0808 223 1133) for support preparing the escalation
- Evidence to preserve: Copy of the Energy Ombudsman decision with reference number and compliance deadline; full call log with dates, agent names, and what was said at each call; any written communications from the supplier; bank statements confirming the credit has not arrived
- Feedback category tag: Non-compliance with Ombudsman decision / Call center transfer loop / Complaint delay — vulnerable customer

---

### Scenario 6-3: Supplier's Call Center Fails to Signpost Ombudsman After Nine Weeks — Back-Bill of £1,200 Unresolved (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** UK Energy Ombudsman Annual Report July 2023–June 2024; 92,938 cases accepted; 58% were billing disputes; only 43% of consumers were correctly signposted to the Energy Ombudsman by their supplier; 77% of cases upheld in the consumer's favour; over £16 million paid to wronged customers in 2023 alone; back-billing disputes totalled 3,218 separate cases
**The Problem:** A customer has been disputing a back-bill for £1,200 — their supplier claims it covers 18 months of estimated rather than actual meter readings and demands immediate payment. The complaint was opened nine weeks ago. The customer has called five times. Each call center agent says the complaint is "still under review." On the fifth call an agent suggests contacting Citizens Advice or Trading Standards. No one has mentioned the Energy Ombudsman or the legal eight-week threshold after which the customer is entitled to independent arbitration.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent reads from a script stating complaints are handled "in the order received" and suggests the customer "be patient." When pressed, the agent offers to submit a callback request. The Energy Ombudsman is not mentioned. No deadlock letter is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms the complaint has been open for nine weeks, exceeding the eight-week legal threshold. Informs the customer of their right to a deadlock letter and their right to escalate to the Energy Ombudsman at no cost. Provides the correct contact details. Does not close the internal complaint.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "After eight weeks your supplier is legally required either to resolve your complaint or to issue you a deadlock letter — and you have the automatic right to take your case to the Energy Ombudsman, a free and independent body. You are already past that threshold. The fact that no agent told you this is itself a failure."
- Direct to: Energy Ombudsman (0330 440 1624; ombudsman-services.org/energy); Ofgem for reporting failure to signpost; Citizens Advice if support is needed preparing the Ombudsman submission
- Evidence to preserve: All call records with dates and agent names; the original back-billing notice with amounts and the period claimed; any meter reading history provided or withheld by the supplier; written confirmation of when the complaint was formally opened
- Feedback category tag: Information failure / Wrong escalation signposting / Back-billing dispute — call center failure

### Scenario 6-4: Thames Water Refuses Meter Re-Read Dispute Escalation While Sending Debt Collection Letters (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Consumer Council for Water (CCW) Household Complaint Handling Report 2023–2024; meter-related complaints rose 30% in a single year; Thames Water rated "poor" on both CCW performance measures; CCW escalated complaints up 29%, highest in nearly a decade; Ofwat issued largest-ever fine of £122.7 million against Thames Water, 2024
**The Problem:** A Thames Water customer has received a bill for £2,400 based on a meter reading that is triple their previous year's usage with no evidence of a leak. They have called three times over six weeks; each time they are told a meter re-read "has been requested." No re-read has occurred. A debt collection letter has now arrived. The agent tells the customer to pay the bill and "apply for a refund if the re-read shows a discrepancy."
**What staff/org would do:** The agent confirms the bill reflects an accurate reading as of the reading date and that the re-read request is "in the queue" with no date available. The agent cannot suspend debt action. The call ends without any committed timeline or written confirmation.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately suspends debt action pending resolution of the dispute. Commits in writing to a meter re-read within five working days. Informs the customer of their right to escalate to CCW if not resolved within ten working days. Documents all prior contacts on the account.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "You are not obligated to pay a disputed bill of this size while a meter accuracy investigation is outstanding — and your supplier should have suspended debt action the moment you raised the dispute. Let us help you move this to a resolution track with a deadline."
- Direct to: Consumer Council for Water (CCW) — free independent escalation body for water complaints; Ofwat if Thames Water does not respond within the regulatory timeframe; Thames Water's formal written complaints address (different from the billing line)
- Evidence to preserve: Photograph your meter today showing the current reading; request in writing the full meter reading history for the past two years; save all bills, letters, and call logs; note whether any construction or plumbing work occurred near your property
- Feedback category tag: Billing dispute — meter accuracy / Debt threat while dispute active / Escalation refused

### Scenario 6-5: Resident Reports Gas Smell to Utility Emergency Line — Fire Services Are Never Notified (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** NTSB Accident Report PAR1902 — Merrimack Valley gas explosions, September 13, 2018; CBS Boston and WBZ-TV reporting on Columbia Gas call center protocol; Lawrence Fire Chief Brian Moriarty public testimony that fire services were not notified of gas odor calls received by Columbia Gas; NiSource criminal guilty plea and $143 million restitution; Town of Andover and MEMA After Action Report
**The Problem:** A resident in a dense residential neighbourhood smells gas strongly inside her home. She calls the gas company's emergency line. The operator takes her address, advises her to open windows and wait outside, and tells her a technician will be dispatched. She does not call 911 because she believes the utility is handling it. Forty minutes later no technician has arrived. The smell is intensifying and a neighbour's house has begun to smoke.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center operator logs the call as a standard gas odor service request and dispatches a field technician based on normal queue priority. No simultaneous notification to fire or emergency services is initiated. There is no mandatory step in the operator's script to trigger emergency dispatch.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately instructs the caller to call 911 now. Simultaneously triggers an emergency services notification from the call center regardless of whether 911 has been called. Escalates to a supervisor for emergency coordination. Treats this as a life-safety event, not a standard service queue call.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Please call 911 immediately if you have not already done so — this is a live gas emergency and fire services must be notified now, independently of anything the gas company does. Do not re-enter your home under any circumstances."
- Direct to: 911 immediately (parallel to all other steps); National Gas Emergency number; relevant state Public Utility Commission to report the call center protocol failure once the emergency is resolved
- Evidence to preserve: Note the exact time you called the utility and what you were told; after the emergency is resolved, request the utility's dispatch log for your address from that time under applicable state public records law; retain all phone records showing the call time
- Feedback category tag: Gas safety emergency / Call center failed to notify emergency services / is_urgent

### Scenario 6-6: $16,752 Auto-Debited in Four Days During Declared Grid Emergency — Account Overdrawn (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Texas Tribune, NPR, CNN, ABC News, and KTVU FOX 2 coverage of Griddy Energy and Winter Storm Uri, February 2021; Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton lawsuit against Griddy; Griddy bankruptcy filing; ERCOT wholesale price cap at $9,000 per megawatt-hour for approximately two days; individual customer debits documented in class-action filings; 400+ formal complaints to the State of Texas within two weeks
**The Problem:** A Griddy Energy customer in Texas had $16,752 automatically debited from his bank account across four days during Winter Storm Uri, when ERCOT held the wholesale electricity price at the regulatory ceiling. His account is now overdrawn. He called Griddy during the storm and was told there was no mechanism to pause the automatic debit. He called his bank to stop the charges and was told this would trigger overdraft fees on top of amounts already taken. He cannot switch providers because ERCOT suspended all switching during the grid emergency.
**What staff/org would do:** The Griddy call center — overwhelmed with thousands of simultaneous calls — provides recorded messaging or a long hold queue. The agent who eventually answers confirms the charges reflect actual wholesale market rates as disclosed in the customer's contract and cannot authorise a billing suspension or reversal.
**What the Owner does:** Documents every auto-debit amount and date. Confirms the ERCOT emergency declaration dates and the duration of the price cap period. Advises the customer to dispute the charges with their bank under Regulation E and to file an emergency complaint with the Texas Attorney General's Consumer Protection Division, which has an active investigation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What has happened to your account is the subject of an active Texas Attorney General investigation. You are not alone — hundreds of customers experienced the same automatic debits during this storm. There are specific steps you can take right now to begin recovering these funds."
- Direct to: Texas Attorney General Consumer Protection Division (800-621-0508); Texas Public Utility Commission consumer complaint line; your bank's dispute resolution team citing Regulation E for electronic fund transfer disputes; any active class-action participation — document your case now for future inclusion
- Evidence to preserve: Download and save all bank statements showing each debit with exact dates and amounts; save all Griddy account records or screenshots; document the ERCOT emergency declaration dates and the period prices were held at the cap; keep records of all calls to Griddy including hold times and what agents said
- Feedback category tag: Financial overcharge / Auto-debit during declared emergency / No customer protection mechanism — is_urgent

### Scenario 6-7: Resident Reports Discolored Water for Three Weeks — Told It Is Safe — Children Are Now Ill (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Wikipedia documented Flint water crisis timeline; NRDC Flint report; CDC CASPER case study; NIH PMC peer-reviewed publications; Consumer Notice timeline; 18-month period of official public dismissal April 2014–October 2015; approximately 140,000 individuals affected; at least 12 Legionnaires' disease deaths; more than 1,700 residents filed EPA complaints totalling approximately $722 million
**The Problem:** A mother in a low-income neighbourhood has called the city water department three times over three weeks about water that is visibly brown and smells metallic. A call center operator told her on each occasion that the water "meets all regulatory standards" and is "safe to drink." Her two young children have developed skin rashes and hair loss since the water quality changed. She cannot afford bottled water. She is calling again and being told the same thing.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center operator reads the official regulatory compliance status on screen — no active violations — and closes the call with a reference number. No home inspection is scheduled. No independent testing is offered. The complaint is logged as resolved.
**What the Owner does:** Treats discolored water combined with child health symptoms as a potential water contamination emergency regardless of official compliance status. Advises the family to stop drinking or cooking with tap water immediately. Connects the complainant to a state environmental or public health authority for independent testing. Does not rely on the utility's self-reported compliance data.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your children's health symptoms combined with a visible and ongoing change in water quality are serious warning signs that must be treated as a health emergency, regardless of what official sources say. Please stop using the tap water for drinking, cooking, or bathing your children immediately while this is investigated by an independent authority."
- Direct to: US EPA Safe Drinking Water Hotline (800-426-4791); State Department of Environmental Quality or Department of Health to file a formal complaint separate from the municipal utility; local pediatrician for immediate blood-lead level testing; legal aid organisations if contamination is confirmed
- Evidence to preserve: Collect a sample of the discolored water in a clean sealed bottle labelled with date and time; photograph the water colour; keep all medical records for children's symptoms with dates of onset; document every call to the city with dates, agent names, and what was communicated; retain any written correspondence received
- Feedback category tag: Water safety — health emergency / Official complaint dismissed / Children at risk — is_urgent

### Scenario 6-8: Gas Utility Customer Transferred Four Times — Billing Dispute Unresolved After Seven Months (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** J.D. Power 2024 U.S. Gas Utility Residential Customer Satisfaction Study; 63,363 online interviews across 86 large gas utility brands; customer care satisfaction fell 26 points in a single year — the largest single-dimension decline in the study's history; billing and payment satisfaction fell 22 points; October 2024
**The Problem:** A residential gas customer has been trying to resolve a billing dispute for seven months. His bill doubled following a rate adjustment that was applied retroactively across six months of prior consumption, generating an unexpected $1,140 back-bill with no prior notification. He has called four times. Each call results in a transfer — first to billing, then to rate adjustments, then to customer accounts, then back to billing. No one has ever sent him a written summary of the dispute and no case has been formally opened in his name.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent apologises, confirms the back-bill is "under review," and transfers the customer to the rate adjustments department. The rate adjustments line goes to voicemail. No callback is received.
**What the Owner does:** Opens a formal written complaint record, assigns a case reference number, and communicates it to the customer in writing before the call ends. Commits to a single point of contact in the resolutions team. Does not transfer the customer again without first fully briefing the receiving agent on the complete case history.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Seven months without a written summary of your dispute or a single point of contact is a systemic failure, not an oversight. You have the right to a formal written response and to escalate to your state Public Utility Commission if the company does not resolve this in writing within a defined deadline."
- Direct to: State Public Utility Commission — each US state has one and accepts formal consumer complaints online; state Attorney General Consumer Protection Division if retroactive rate application was not properly disclosed; local consumer legal aid if the amount warrants formal dispute
- Evidence to preserve: Request in writing — email or certified letter — a full billing history for the past 12 to 24 months; save all call records with dates, agent names, and department names; request written confirmation of the rate change effective date and how and when it was communicated to customers; keep all paper bills and statements
- Feedback category tag: Back-billing dispute / Transfer loop — call center failure / No written case management

### Scenario 6-9: Indore Resident Reports Foul Tap Water Twice — Complaint Closed Without Inspection — Cholera Outbreak Follows (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Wikipedia documented Indore water contamination crisis December 2024–January 2025; Down to Earth investigative report; Outlook India coverage; 15 deaths, approximately 270 hospitalised; sewage from a police outpost toilet draining directly into a municipal drinking water pipeline; Comptroller and Auditor General audit had previously identified systemic infrastructure flaws in Indore's water system; authorities described by Down to Earth as having remained "unconcerned" about audit findings
**The Problem:** A resident of Bhagirathpura, Indore, has called the municipal water authority twice in the past two weeks about tap water that is visibly discolored and foul-smelling and is causing stomach cramps in her household. Both calls resulted in the complaint being logged and closed with no inspection. This morning two family members have severe diarrhoea and vomiting. Neighbours are also reporting illness. She is calling the complaint line again but it is engaged.
**What staff/org would do:** The municipal call center operator logs the complaint against the previous reference number, notes "complaint repeated," and schedules a routine inspection for the following week. No escalation to public health authorities is initiated. No boil-water advisory is issued to the neighbourhood.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately escalates to the district health officer. Advises the resident to stop all consumption of tap water. Connects the resident to the district hospital emergency line. Documents this as a potential cluster event requiring immediate multi-household investigation rather than a routine single-address complaint.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "The symptoms you are describing — severe diarrhoea, vomiting, stomach cramps appearing in multiple people — combined with visibly polluted water are signs of a possible waterborne disease outbreak. This is a public health emergency. Please take your family members to hospital immediately and do not use the tap water for any purpose."
- Direct to: District hospital emergency line; District Collector's office for immediate escalation beyond the municipal body; Madhya Pradesh Public Health Engineering Department; National Consumer Helpline India (1800-11-4000); Madhya Pradesh Chief Minister's helpline (181)
- Evidence to preserve: Collect a water sample in a sealed bottle labelled with date and time; photograph the water colour; preserve all medical records and hospital receipts; document every prior complaint call with reference numbers and dates; contact neighbours to document their own symptoms and complaints separately
- Feedback category tag: Water contamination emergency / Prior complaints dismissed / Health crisis — is_urgent

### Scenario 6-10: Ranchi Resident Complains About Sewage-Contaminated Water for Six Weeks — Only Temporary Repair, Problem Returns (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Down to Earth investigative report on contaminated municipal water across Indian cities 2023–2024; 5,500+ people sick and at least 34 dead nationwide over a 12-month period; Ranchi identified with over 300 locations of damaged pipelines exposing drinking water to sewage; Patna's Kankarbagh Housing Colony documented for repeated complaints met only with temporary repairs allowing recurrence; governance described as "reactive, with interventions triggered only after people fall ill"
**The Problem:** A family in Ranchi has reported sewage-contaminated tap water to their ward office six weeks in a row. After the first complaint, a crew arrived and repaired a section of pipe. Within ten days the problem returned. The second and third complaints have produced no response. The family has been buying bottled water at significant personal expense for six weeks and cannot sustain this. Written complaints submitted to the municipal corporation have never been acknowledged.
**What staff/org would do:** The ward office receives the call, logs it, and says a team will be dispatched. The team does not arrive. On a follow-up visit to the ward office, a staff member confirms the complaint is registered and tells the resident to wait for the scheduled inspection cycle with no timeline given.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that a temporary repair followed by rapid recurrence is a systemic infrastructure problem requiring escalation beyond ward level. Documents the full complaint history with dates. Escalates in writing to the Municipal Commissioner and the Public Health Engineering Department, not only the ward office, and requests a written response within a defined period.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "A problem that keeps returning after temporary repairs is not a routine service issue — it is a governance failure. Your complaint history across six weeks is now your strongest tool, and you have escalation routes that go well above the ward office."
- Direct to: Municipal Commissioner's office — formal written complaint by registered post with all prior complaint reference numbers; Jharkhand Public Health Engineering Department; National Consumer Helpline India (1800-11-4000); State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission if the municipal body fails to respond within 30 days; local civil society organisations or media if systemic neglect is confirmed
- Evidence to preserve: Keep all written complaint copies with submission dates and ward office stamps or receipts; photograph the tap water each time the problem recurs; retain all bottled water receipts as evidence of costs incurred; document dates the repair crew visited and what work they said was done; obtain medical records if any household member fell ill during this period
- Feedback category tag: Water quality — chronic complaint / Temporary repair and recurring failure / Municipal governance failure

## 7. Government / Public Services

### Scenario 7-1: Automated Welfare Debt Notice Demands Repayment of Money Never Owed (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Royal Commission into the Robodebt Scheme, Australia (2023); Federal Court — Amato v Commonwealth (2019); AI Incident Database Incident 57; $1.2 billion class action settlement; Victoria Legal Aid published analysis
**The Problem:** A former Centrelink recipient in Australia receives a certified letter demanding repayment of $4,800 in welfare "overpayments" from three years ago. The letter states the debt was calculated using ATO annual income data and that her next tax refund will be intercepted within 28 days if the debt is not resolved. She has payslips showing her actual fortnightly earnings were consistently below the payment threshold throughout the entire period, but the Centrelink online portal displays no upload function. The automated phone line tells her she must "prove the debt is incorrect."
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm the debt is "in the system" and therefore valid; advise her to lodge a written dispute that will take 12 or more weeks to review; proceed with tax refund interception in the meantime with no pause or hold applied.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the serious financial and emotional distress of receiving an unexpected large debt demand from a government agency; flag this as a well-documented algorithmic averaging error; instruct her to preserve all payslips and income documents immediately; direct her to free legal help.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (impending tax refund interception within 28 days)
- Opening reply: acknowledge the shock of receiving this demand — this is a known, documented failure of an automated system that wrongly accused hundreds of thousands of Australians; she is not alone and she has strong grounds to fight it
- Direct to: Centrelink formal Review of Decision (lodged online or in person at Services Australia); National Debt Helpline 1800 007 007 (free, specialist community legal centre referral); welfare rights units at community legal centres; Royal Commission compensation claims pathway if still open
- Evidence to collect: All payslips covering the disputed period; bank deposit records; ATO income summaries; every piece of Centrelink correspondence with dates; screenshot of the online portal showing the demand and absence of an upload function
- Feedback category tag: Financial Harm / Automated System Failure / Urgent

### Scenario 7-2: Unemployment Fraud Accusation Issued Entirely by Algorithm — Wages Being Garnished (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Wisconsin Law Review — "Automated Stategraft: Faulty Programming and Improper Collections in Michigan's Unemployment Insurance Program" (2021); Undark investigative report on Michigan MiDAS (2020); AI Incident Database Incident 373; Ford School of Public Policy MiDAS explainer (2024); $20 million class action settlement
**The Problem:** A laid-off factory worker in Michigan receives a certified letter from the Michigan Unemployment Insurance Agency charging her with intentional misrepresentation of her unemployment claim. The letter demands $14,200 in repayment plus quadruple fraud penalties totaling $56,800. She has already discovered her wages from a new part-time job are being garnished — she found out when her paycheck arrived short. She was never notified before garnishment began and received no opportunity to respond. When she calls the UIA, an automated system confirms her case is "in collection status." A live agent, reached after 35 minutes on hold, tells her: "The determination has been made — you can appeal in writing." She has her original layoff notice and a letter from her former employer confirming the separation.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm the automated determination is valid; state that appeals take several months and that wage garnishment continues during the appeal period; provide no interim relief.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as an emergency — active wage garnishment from a determination the system made with no human review is causing immediate financial harm.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (wages actively being garnished; household income reduced now)
- Opening reply: acknowledge this is alarming — having wages seized over a fraud accusation she did not commit is an emergency, and this automated system was later found to have a 93% error rate in independent analysis; her documentation puts her in a strong position
- Direct to: Michigan UIA appeals process — written appeal to the Michigan Office of Administrative Hearings and Rules (MOAHR); Michigan Legal Aid (michiganlegalhelp.org); ACLU of Michigan; if the class action settlement is still open, class action claims line; Michigan Attorney General Consumer Protection division
- Evidence to collect: Original separation notice from employer; employer letter confirming layoff (not resignation); all UIA correspondence with reference numbers; bank records showing garnishment amounts and dates; pay stubs showing the deductions
- Feedback category tag: Financial Harm / Automated System Failure / Wrongful Garnishment / Urgent

### Scenario 7-3: Tax Penalty for On-Time Return — 4 Hours on Hold, Then Disconnected (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** National Audit Office — "Taxpayers let down by poor HMRC customer service" press release (2024); UHY Hacker Young analysis of HMRC wait time doubling (2024); TaxWatch UK investigation; NAO finding that 72% of HMRC calls in 2023/24 were "failure demand" — calls caused by agency errors
**The Problem:** A self-employed graphic designer in Manchester receives a penalty notice from HMRC for a late Self Assessment return — but her online HMRC account shows the return was marked as submitted with a confirmation timestamp. The online portal provides no mechanism to challenge the penalty; a blue banner reads "Call HMRC to query this." She calls at 9:14am. After 1 hour 52 minutes on hold, the line goes silent and then plays a disconnect tone. She calls again at 2pm. After 2 hours 11 minutes she reaches an agent who tells her: "I can see the note on your account but I can't overturn penalties — you'll need to write to us by post." The agent confirms the penalty is accruing interest daily and the written process takes up to 8 weeks.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm she must appeal in writing; allow interest to accrue on the penalty during that 8-week period; provide no interim hold on the debt.
**What the Owner does:** Validate the absurdity of a government-caused loop — her return was submitted on time, she has the proof, and the resolution route is clear once she knows it.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (penalty is accruing interest but no immediate garnishment)
- Opening reply: acknowledge the extreme frustration of spending nearly 4 hours on hold to be told to write a letter — this is one of the most common HMRC failure patterns documented by the National Audit Office; she has clear grounds for a successful appeal
- Direct to: HMRC statutory appeal — Self Assessment Penalty Appeal form SA370 (available on gov.uk); include the submission confirmation timestamp as primary evidence; if HMRC does not respond within 8 weeks, escalate to the Adjudicator's Office (gov.uk/adjudicator-office); LITRG (Low Incomes Tax Reform Group) free helpline for self-employed taxpayers
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot or PDF of the HMRC online account submission confirmation with timestamp; the penalty notice with reference number and charge date; a call log recording both call dates, start times, hold duration, and agent name (if obtained on the second call); any automated confirmation email from HMRC at the time of original submission
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure / Bureaucratic Loop / Information Failure

### Scenario 7-4: Disability Benefit Denied — Agent Will Not Explain the Assessment Score (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Disability Rights UK citing DWP official statistics (2022); ME Association citing The Independent (2022); Citizens Advice PIP guidance; DWP Statistics — PIP and ADP Official Statistics to February 2022; 80,000 decisions overturned at mandatory reconsideration; 43% reversal rate documented
**The Problem:** A 41-year-old woman in Glasgow with fibromyalgia and chronic fatigue syndrome has her Personal Independence Payment claim rejected. The decision letter states only that she "did not score enough points in the daily living component" — it provides no activity-by-activity breakdown and no explanation of how the assessor scored each descriptor. She calls the DWP and asks the agent to explain which activities were assessed and at what level. The agent says: "I'm not able to discuss the assessment outcome over the phone — that has to go through mandatory reconsideration in writing." She explains she is housebound on most days and asks whether she can submit the reconsideration verbally. The agent says no. She asks what the current mandatory reconsideration wait time is. The agent says "a few months."
**What staff/org would do:** Refer her to the mandatory reconsideration written process and end the call, with no further assistance, no explanation of her rights, and no information about the assessment report she is legally entitled to request.
**What the Owner does:** Recognise this is a known systemic failure — nearly half of challenged PIP decisions are reversed before they even reach tribunal — and give her the concrete tools to challenge it.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no immediate income loss, but welfare rights require time-sensitive action)
- Opening reply: acknowledge how exhausting and disempowering it is to be denied with no explanation after going through an assessment; confirm that 43% of decisions challenged at mandatory reconsideration are reversed by DWP itself — her instinct to fight this is correct and well-founded
- Direct to: Citizens Advice (free specialist PIP welfare help — citizensadvice.org.uk); Disability Rights UK factsheet on mandatory reconsideration; Scope helpline 0808 800 3333; Welfare Rights Officer at her local council; deadline is 1 month from the decision letter date (extendable for good cause — she should call DWP to request an extension if needed given her health)
- Evidence to collect: Request the Disability Assessor's report in writing from DWP immediately (she is legally entitled to it under the Data Protection Act); keep the original decision letter; document all daily activities and how her conditions affect them using the official PIP descriptor framework; gather letters from her GP, specialist, and any carers
- Feedback category tag: Service Refusal / Rights Violation / Disability / Information Failure

### Scenario 7-5: Police Officer Demands Cash Bribe to Clear Passport Verification (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** LawRato.com documented legal complaint (primary source — complaint text on record); Ministry of External Affairs Rajya Sabha Parliamentary Question No. 1049 on corruption in passport verification in West Bengal; Moneylife — passport applicant files Anti-Corruption Bureau complaint (Bengaluru); Transparency International CPI 2024 (India: 38/100, ranked 96th of 180 countries)
**The Problem:** A 29-year-old software engineer in Bengaluru has submitted his first passport application. His new employer abroad has given him a six-week deadline before the job offer lapses. Three weeks after submitting, the constable assigned to his mandatory police verification visits his apartment. The officer reviews nothing in writing, looks around the flat, and then says: "There are some complications in your file. These things take a long time to clear. If you want it done quickly it's ₹1,200." When the applicant asks what complications, the officer shows him nothing. The applicant is afraid that if he refuses, the officer will submit an adverse verification report, blocking his passport entirely. The Passport Seva Kendra helpline, when called, confirms only that his application is "under police verification" with no estimated date.
**What staff/org would do:** The application remains stuck at "under verification" with no movement; the helpline offers no escalation pathway for misconduct reported over the phone and redirects all complaints to the written Ministry of External Affairs grievance process.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as urgent given the job offer deadline; acknowledge the vulnerability and fear the applicant is experiencing; provide concrete reporting routes that do not require paying the bribe.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (job offer expires in approximately three weeks; bribery demand is active)
- Opening reply: acknowledge the fear and pressure he is under — this is an abuse of authority by a public official and he should not pay; this pattern has been documented across multiple Indian cities and there are reporting routes specifically designed for it
- Direct to: Karnataka Anti-Corruption Bureau online complaint portal (acb.karnataka.gov.in) or ACB helpline 1064 — request a trap operation; Passport Seva customer care 1800-258-1800 (document the call date and reference number); MEA Grievance Portal at passportindia.gov.in under the Grievance section; Lokayukta Karnataka for systemic corruption complaints; Bengaluru Police Commissioner's office complaint cell
- Evidence to collect: Note the constable's full name, badge number, date and time of visit, and exact words used; do not pay under any circumstances; if he feels safe doing so, he may record the conversation on his phone (Karnataka follows one-party consent); keep his job offer letter with the deadline date as evidence of urgency; retain all passport application receipts and reference numbers
- Feedback category tag: Corruption / Staff Misconduct / Urgent

### Scenario 7-6: Driving License Revoked for Unpaid Fine — Driver Was Never Told and Loses His Job (Pattern F)
**Real Basis:** ACLU case page — Johnson v. Jessup; ACLU of North Carolina settlement press release (March 2022); Southern Coalition for Social Justice press release; Duke University Wilson Center study documenting racial disparity in suspensions 2013–2017; CBS17 North Carolina reporting; federal court settlement requiring NCDMV to notify 185,000 eligible drivers
**The Problem:** A 36-year-old Black father of three in Durham, North Carolina is pulled over for a broken tail light. The officer tells him his license was suspended more than two years ago for an unpaid $180 traffic fine. He had no idea — he never received any suspension notice at his current address. He is arrested at the scene. The following morning he loses his job: his employer is a delivery company that requires a valid license. He cannot drive to pick up his children from school. To reinstate his license, he must pay $700+ in accumulated fees plus the original fine — money he does not have, particularly now that he is unemployed. When he calls the NCDMV, an automated system confirms his license is suspended and lists the reinstatement fee, but provides no information about hardship hearings or the 2022 federal court settlement.
**What staff/org would do:** Inform him of the reinstatement fees; provide no information about the judicial settlement, the hardship hearing process, or the disproportionate impact of the scheme on Black drivers; make no proactive outreach.
**What the Owner does:** Recognise this as a documented pattern of discriminatory enforcement; ensure he knows his rights under the 2022 federal court settlement and where to get free legal help.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (immediate crisis of arrest has passed; financial and employment harm is ongoing)
- Opening reply: acknowledge the cascading harm this has caused — losing a job because of a suspension he was never notified of, on a debt that disproportionately targets people in financial hardship, is precisely what a 2022 federal court settlement found to be unjust
- Direct to: Southern Coalition for Social Justice (919-323-3380) — represented plaintiffs in the settlement and can advise on eligibility; ACLU of North Carolina; Legal Aid NC (legalaidnc.org) for free representation in the hardship hearing; North Carolina DMV Failure to Pay Hardship Hearing request; the settlement requires NCDMV to proactively notify 185,000 eligible drivers — he should ask whether he is on that list
- Evidence to collect: The original traffic citation; any DMV correspondence he did receive at any address; arrest record from this incident; employment termination notice; bank statements showing financial hardship; documentation of any prior attempts to check his license status
- Feedback category tag: Discrimination / Rights Violation / Financial Harm / Racial Disparity

### Scenario 7-7: Unemployment Benefits Cut Off by Algorithm — Single Mother Has No Income and Rent Is Due (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** StateScoop — "FTC complaint targets automated public benefit fraud detection" (2021); Time Magazine — "How Government Fraud Detection Algorithms Are Leaving Innocent People Without Benefits" (2021); Center for American Progress analysis of Pondera Fraud Detect deployment; EDD reported 4–8 hour hold times and mass call disconnections at COVID peak (2020–2021)
**The Problem:** A single mother in Fresno, California wakes to find her EDD unemployment deposit has not arrived. Rent is due in four days. She has two children. Her EDD online account shows her claim as "pending review — no payment issued." No letter, no email, no text message has been sent to explain why. She has been receiving payments without issue for four months. She calls the EDD at 8am and the automated system tells her the estimated wait is "over 4 hours." She stays on hold while her children nap. At the 3 hour 17 minute mark, the call drops without connecting her to anyone. She calls again — same automated message. She has never been told what triggered the "pending review" flag.
**What staff/org would do:** The portal continues to show "pending review" indefinitely; no notice is issued; phone lines remain inaccessible with dropped calls; no callback option is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as a financial emergency — no income, children to feed, rent due in four days — and provide immediate parallel actions.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (no income, rent due in 4 days, children in the household)
- Opening reply: acknowledge this is a crisis — having payments stopped without any warning or explanation while responsible for children and facing imminent rent arrears is a genuine emergency; this is a known pattern of automated benefit suspension that affected over a million California claimants, the majority of whom were found to be completely legitimate
- Direct to: EDD appeals portal (appeals.edd.ca.gov — she can file online without reaching the call center); California Legal Aid Foundation (lawhelpca.org) for free urgent EDD help; 211 Fresno for emergency rental assistance and food access; CalFresh (food stamps) emergency application; local food banks for immediate food access while the appeal processes
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the EDD account showing "pending review" and the date payments stopped; bank records showing the last payment received and the gap; all EDD correspondence; a log of every call attempt with date, time, hold duration, and outcome (dropped call notation)
- Feedback category tag: Automated System Failure / Financial Emergency / Urgent / Vulnerability

### Scenario 7-8: Monthly Benefit Payment Reduced With No Explanation — Agency Refuses to Say Why (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Citizens Advice press release — "Universal Credit recipients unfairly paying £111m a year due to government mistakes" (2022); Citizens Advice research report "Designing Out Deductions" (2022); DWP Universal Credit guidance on deductions; 2 million households affected; 47% of overpayment debts caused by DWP errors
**The Problem:** A part-time home care worker in Coventry receives her Universal Credit payment and finds it is £87 less than the previous month. No notification was sent to her online journal. She logs in and sees only the reduced payment figure with the label "deductions applied." There is no breakdown, no reference number, no letter. She calls the UC helpline and waits 24 minutes. The agent looks at her account and says: "There's a deduction running for a previous overpayment — it looks like it may go back to before Universal Credit started." When she asks for the calculation showing what the original overpayment was and how it was worked out, the agent says: "I don't have that information on my screen. You'd need to do a mandatory reconsideration in writing." She asks whether the deduction can be paused while she investigates. The agent says no.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm the deduction will continue every month; refer her to the written mandatory reconsideration process; provide no document showing the origin or calculation of the alleged overpayment.
**What the Owner does:** Recognise this is exactly the pattern Citizens Advice documented — silent deductions caused by government error, with no transparency and no right to pause — and give her the tools to challenge it.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (payment reduced but not stopped; no immediate crisis)
- Opening reply: acknowledge that receiving a lower payment with no explanation is deeply unsettling, especially when you depend on it — she has a legal right to know the basis for this deduction and to challenge it; Citizens Advice found that almost half of all UC overpayment debts are caused by DWP's own errors
- Direct to: Citizens Advice (citizensadvice.org.uk or local bureau — they have specialist welfare rights advisors); request in writing from DWP a copy of the original overpayment decision and the calculation (she is entitled to this under DWP guidance — include this in her mandatory reconsideration letter); Welfare Rights Officer at Coventry City Council (free and specialist); if the deduction is causing hardship, request a Budgeting Advance or hardship payment through her UC online account
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of her online journal showing the reduced payment amount, the date, and the absence of any notification or breakdown; her previous payment records showing the change; any historic correspondence from HMRC or legacy DWP benefits; a record of the call including date, time, agent's name (if given), and what was said
- Feedback category tag: Transparency Failure / Information Failure / Financial Harm / Refusal of Explanation

### Scenario 7-9: Disability Benefit Application Denied After 7 Months — Told to Wait Another 8 Months (Pattern A/B)
**Real Basis:** Urban Institute analysis of SSA disability data (2024); SSA Office of Inspector General audit data; USAFacts disability benefit wait-time project; Disability Rights Advocates / DREDF qualitative investigation on access barriers (2025, covering 2024 experiences); SSA FY2024 data showing 231-day average initial decision wait and 1.26 million case backlog peak
**The Problem:** A 54-year-old man in rural Ohio with Type 2 diabetes and stage 3 chronic kidney disease applied for Social Security Disability Insurance seven months ago. He has been unable to work since his diagnosis and has now exhausted all savings. He receives a denial letter stating he "does not meet the duration and severity requirements." He calls SSA to understand which specific medical evidence was lacking and whether his nephrologist's letter was reviewed. After 28 minutes on hold, the agent tells him: "I'm not able to discuss the medical review by phone — you'll need to file a reconsideration in writing within 60 days. Current wait on reconsideration is approximately 7 to 8 months." He asks what he should live on during those 8 months. The agent says: "I don't have resources to provide on that."
**What staff/org would do:** Provide the reconsideration form number and end the call; offer no information about emergency income alternatives, legal representation, or how to build a stronger medical submission.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge his serious health and financial vulnerability; provide concrete guidance on both the appeal and emergency income support so he is not left with nothing during the wait.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no garnishment or immediate crisis, but serious health and financial vulnerability)
- Opening reply: acknowledge how frightening and exhausting this is — being seriously ill, out of savings, and told to wait another 8 months after already waiting 7 is a genuine hardship; the reconsideration process does have a reasonable chance of success, especially with stronger medical documentation, and there are income and health options available now
- Direct to: SSA reconsideration form SSA-561 (ssa.gov/forms/ssa-561.html) — must be filed within 60 days of the denial letter date; National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives (NOSSCR) attorney referral — SSDI attorneys charge no upfront fee and are capped by law at 25% or $7,200, whichever is less; Ohio Legal Help (ohiolegalhelp.org); Ohio Medicaid for continuing healthcare coverage while SSDI is pending; community health center (HRSA-funded clinics — findahealthcenter.hrsa.gov) for low-cost nephrology care
- Evidence to collect: The denial letter including the specific codes cited; all medical records covering kidney function tests (eGFR readings), diabetes management history, and treatment logs; a letter from his nephrologist explicitly addressing SSA Adult Listing 6.06 (chronic kidney disease criteria) by name; a function report documenting how his conditions affect daily activities
- Feedback category tag: Service Refusal / Vulnerability / Chronic Illness / Wait and No Resolution / Bureaucratic Loop

### Scenario 7-10: Passport Application 14 Weeks In — Flight in 9 Days, Agent Says "On Track" (Pattern D/B)
**Real Basis:** UK Parliament Public Accounts Committee — "Hundreds of thousands of passport applicants let down by unacceptable delays despite planning efforts" (2022); National Audit Office investigation into HM Passport Office performance (2022); 360,000 applicants waited more than 10 weeks; 134,000 applications transferred to paper process with no notification; documented agent misinformation about processing status
**The Problem:** A 47-year-old woman in Leeds submitted her passport renewal 14 weeks ago, paying the standard fee. Her flight to Portugal is in nine days — her elderly father is undergoing surgery and she is his next of kin. She tracks her application on the HMPO online portal, which shows "in progress." She calls the HMPO helpline at 8:12am and is given an estimated wait of 51 minutes. At the 1 hour 29 minute mark she reaches an agent. The agent checks the system and says: "Your application is on track — there are no issues." She asks what the current standard processing time is. The agent says "up to 10 weeks." Her application has been in for 14 weeks. She asks why she is past that window. The agent says: "I can see it's in progress — that's all the system shows me."
**What staff/org would do:** Repeat that the application is "in progress" and end the call; offer no escalation pathway, no explanation of why it is past the published window, and no fast-track option.
**What the Owner does:** Recognise this is a genuine emergency — nine days until a flight, already four weeks past the published maximum, and the agent has given information the system literally cannot support.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (flight in 9 days; father's surgery; already past the published maximum processing window)
- Opening reply: acknowledge the urgency and the distress of getting contradictory information after a 90-minute wait — nine days is not enough time for the standard queue, and the agent saying "on track" when the application is 40% past the published maximum is a known failure mode; there are specific urgent service pathways she needs to use today
- Direct to: HMPO Premium Service or Fast Track appointment — book online at gov.uk/get-a-passport-urgently (one-week service costs £177 on top of the standard fee; a passport office appointment in person is required); contact her MP's constituency office today (MPs routinely intervene with HMPO for compassionate urgent cases and this qualifies — father's surgery is compelling grounds); Resolver.co.uk to log a formal complaint immediately; if the premium appointment is unavailable in her region, the nearest passport office may have cancellations (check Newport, Durham, Glasgow, Liverpool, Peterborough, London)
- Evidence to collect: Application reference number and original submission date; the online tracking screenshot showing "in progress" alongside today's date; flight booking confirmation with departure date; medical documentation of her father's surgical appointment; a record of the call including date, start time, hold duration, and the agent's exact words about being "on track"
- Feedback category tag: Delay / Emergency / Information Failure / Call Center Failure / Urgent

## 8. Embassy / Immigration

### Scenario 8-1: Passport Confiscated, Wages Withheld — Debt Bondage on Stadium Construction Site (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Amnesty International primary investigation, Qatar Khalifa International Stadium migrant workers, March 2016; ILO formal complaint (Convention No. 29 Forced Labour), 2014–2017; Business and Human Rights Centre documentation
**The Problem:** A Nepalese metalworker on a World Cup stadium construction site in Qatar paid USD 2,400 in recruitment fees before arrival. His employer confiscated his passport on his first day and has now withheld two months of wages. He cannot change jobs, cannot leave the country, and was verbally threatened by a supervisor when he asked about the overdue payment. He is filing a grievance through Riviwa's voice channel because he has no functioning internet access.
**What staff/org would do:** The site supervisor dismisses the complaint as a misunderstanding about Qatari custom, tells the worker the passport is being held for safekeeping, and promises wages will be paid soon. No written record of the complaint is created. The worker is told to return to work.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-equipped HR and compliance officer at the contractor receives the alert immediately, logs it as a formal grievance under forced labour indicators, freezes further deductions on the worker's account, and initiates a passport return protocol. The officer contacts the worker in Nepali via Riviwa's translation layer and begins documenting the full recruitment fee chain.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — your passport being held by your employer and your wages being withheld — is a serious violation of your rights under international law and Qatar's own Labour Law. You are not alone, and this is not acceptable. We are recording this as a formal complaint right now."
- Who to direct to: Qatar Ministry of Labour complaint portal (adlsa.gov.qa); ILO Project Office in Doha; Migrant Rights legal helpline (migrant-rights.org); Embassy of Nepal in Doha (+974 4467 1548) for emergency consular support
- Evidence to collect and preserve: photograph of any pre-departure contract showing promised wages; screenshots of any supervisor messages containing threats; note supervisor's name, date, and time of each incident; formally request written documentation of the employer's passport retention policy
- Feedback category tag: Labour Rights Violation / Forced Labour Indicator / Financial Exploitation / Passport Confiscation

### Scenario 8-2: British Tourist Held 19 Days — Signed Border Statement Under Exhaustion and Duress (Pattern C/D)
**Real Basis:** Newsweek, 2025 ("British Tourist Detained by ICE for 19 Days Warns Against All US Travel"); confirmed ICE detention record, Tacoma Northwest ICE Detention Facility, Washington State, USA
**The Problem:** A 27-year-old British graphic designer arrived at a US land border after being denied entry to Canada. Exhausted and disoriented, she was asked by a CBP officer to sign a summary statement that characterised her house-sitting arrangement as work in exchange for accommodation. She signed without reading it fully. She is now held at an ICE detention facility in Washington State. Her family in Wales does not know where she is. She has reached Riviwa via a facility phone.
**What staff/org would do:** The ICE intake officer processes her as a tourist visa violator, places her in general detention, and advises that a hearing will be scheduled. No contact is made with the British Consulate. She is handed a printed rights sheet in English only.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled detention facility complaint officer immediately creates a case record, flags it for mandatory consular notification under Vienna Convention Article 36, and contacts the British Consulate-General in Seattle within 30 minutes. The officer provides the detainee's location, booking reference, and the stated basis for the charge, and arranges a phone call to her family in the UK.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Being detained in a foreign country is frightening, and you have rights right now — including the right to consular assistance from the UK government. We are documenting this case and helping you activate those rights immediately."
- Who to direct to: UK Consular Emergency Line (+44 20 7008 5000, available 24/7); ACLU National Immigration Project; Immigration Equality legal aid (US immigration detention); submit a written Vienna Convention Article 36 consular access request to the facility duty officer now
- Evidence to collect and preserve: retain a copy of the signed border statement; write down the exact words the CBP officer used before asking her to sign; note the time, location, and officer badge number; preserve any electronic messages showing the house-sitting arrangement was unpaid
- Feedback category tag: Wrongful Detention / Consular Access Denied / Coerced Statement / Due Process Failure

### Scenario 8-3: UK eVisa Displays Trafficking Survivor's Abuser Name — Student Loses Job Offers, Helpline Charges Per Minute (Pattern E/D)
**Real Basis:** Computer Weekly, 2024–2025 ("UK's error-prone eVisa system is anxiety-inducing"); ILPA Parliamentary submission ("The Digital Status Crisis"); Right to Remain; Seraphus immigration lawyers; WhatDoTheyKnow FOI request on UKVI contact centre performance metrics, declined by Home Office on cost grounds
**The Problem:** A woman who survived human trafficking and was granted refugee status in the UK is trying to generate a right-to-work share code via the UKVI eVisa portal. The system is returning her trafficking name — the name her abuser registered her under — and her trafficker's date of birth, not her own. She has called the UKVI helpline three times over three days. Each call costs 12 pence per minute from a landline. She has been on hold for more than 40 minutes per attempt and disconnected twice without reaching a live agent. She is now at risk of losing her housing benefit because the name on the eVisa does not match any of her identification documents.
**What staff/org would do:** When an agent is eventually reached, the UKVI call centre staff opens a routine Change of Conditions case, gives a standard 20 working day processing estimate, and does not flag the trafficking disclosure or escalate to the vulnerable persons unit.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled UKVI case handler who receives the complaint immediately applies the Safeguarding Protocol for vulnerable persons, flags the case to the Modern Slavery and Vulnerable Persons Hub, suspends the incorrect eVisa generation, issues a temporary manual right-to-work letter bearing the correct identity, and contacts the employer directly with a confirmation reference.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The error you have described — your eVisa displaying a name linked to your trafficking situation — is a serious safeguarding failure that UKVI is required to treat as a priority. You should not be paying per-minute call charges to resolve a government system error. We are escalating this under safeguarding protocols now."
- Who to direct to: Migrant Help UK (0808 8010 503, free 24/7 for trafficking survivors); Unseen UK Modern Slavery Helpline (08000 121 700, free); Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) if UKVI does not resolve within 20 days; local MP's constituency office for parliamentary intervention on the UKVI case file
- Evidence to collect and preserve: screenshot the eVisa share code output showing the incorrect name and date of birth; save a log of all UKVI call dates, times, and durations (for a cost recovery claim); obtain written confirmation from the benefit provider of any action taken on the mismatched identity; document all job offers received and lost due to the error
- Feedback category tag: IT System Failure / Safeguarding — Trafficking Survivor / Identity Error / Call Centre Failure / Cost Barrier to Complaint

### Scenario 8-4: Embassy Contractor of Six Years Told to Leave Kabul — "Nobody Called, Nobody Emailed" During Taliban Takeover (Pattern D/E)
**Real Basis:** Washington Post, September 5, 2021; NPR; US State Department OIG Report AUD-MERO-23-33 (classified, existence confirmed by OIG public record); Wikipedia, 2021 Kabul airlift
**The Problem:** A man who worked as a contractor at the US Embassy in Kabul for six years was told to leave the compound on August 15, 2021, as Taliban forces entered the city. Embassy staff had collected his and his family's personal details weeks earlier as part of evacuation contingency planning. He was told someone would contact him. No contact came. He is now sheltering with his family in a Kabul building as the security situation deteriorates rapidly. He has intermittent internet access and is submitting this through Riviwa's emergency channel.
**What staff/org would do:** The State Department consular duty officer has no record of the contractor in the active evacuation roster. The officer directs him to call the Bureau of Consular Affairs public emergency line (202-501-4444), which routes to an automated message advising callers not to rely on the US government for evacuation — a message that was simultaneously live and incorrect.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled State Department emergency officer immediately cross-references the contractor's name against the pre-collected contingency data set, confirms the record exists, classifies the case as Priority 1 (documented US government connection, confirmed imminent threat), creates a live case ID with a direct callback number, and routes the family's location to the nearest active evacuation coordination point.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your safety and your family's safety is the priority right now. You provided your information to the Embassy weeks ago, and that record exists. We are treating this as a Priority 1 emergency case immediately — stay in your current location until you receive a direct call from us."
- Who to direct to: State Department 24-hour Operations Center (+1 202-647-4000, not the automated public line); contact your home country embassy if you hold non-US citizenship; International Rescue Committee Afghanistan emergency contacts; if you hold any US documentation, contact a US Congressional representative's constituent services office immediately
- Evidence to collect and preserve: retain any written or verbal communications from Embassy staff who collected the family's information; note any staff names, dates, and what was said; preserve employment records or contractor identity documents; note current safe location and share it with a trusted contact outside Afghanistan
- Feedback category tag: Emergency Evacuation Failure / Consular Duty of Care Breach / Communication Breakdown — Is Urgent
