### Scenario 8-5: US Government Emergency Consular Helpline Played Automated Message Telling Citizens in Crisis "Don't Expect Help" (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Newsweek, March 2026 ("State Dept Scraps Message Telling Americans in Middle East It Won't Help"); US Bureau of Consular Affairs emergency line 202-501-4444; State Department acknowledgement that the message was operated by a third-party vendor and was outdated
**The Problem:** An American citizen in a Middle East country is experiencing an active security emergency. They call the US State Department's official emergency consular line — the number listed on their travel advisory and their Smart Traveler Enrollment Program registration confirmation. An automated recording tells them: "Do not rely on the US government for assisted departure or evacuation." They are now unsure whether any help is coming. They are calling back to report the message and ask what they should do.
**What staff/org would do:** The call routes to the same automated system. A subsequent daytime call reaches a consular phone bank where the agent is unaware the automated message exists and cannot confirm current evacuation policy. The agent advises the caller to monitor travel.state.gov.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled State Department duty officer who receives this complaint identifies the automated message immediately as an active misinformation incident, escalates to the Bureau of Consular Affairs Operations Center for emergency correction, issues a direct outbound callback to the citizen with accurate current guidance, and opens a priority welfare case for the citizen's physical situation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "The automated message you heard was incorrect and did not reflect current US government policy. It has since been corrected. You are entitled to consular assistance. We are connecting you to the right channel right now — please stay on this line."
- Who to direct to: State Department 24-hour Operations Center (+1 202-647-4000, staffed by live agents); nearest US Embassy or Consulate direct line; update your STEP registration at step.state.gov with your current location; if the US Embassy is unreachable, contact the embassy of a Five Eyes partner country — the UK, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand routinely provide consular assistance to allied nationals
- Evidence to collect and preserve: note the exact wording of the automated message, including the date and time you heard it; save any travel advisory emails or STEP registration confirmations showing 202-501-4444 as the officially listed emergency number; record timestamps of each call attempt; document any conflicting guidance received from different agents
- Feedback category tag: Call Centre Failure / Emergency Information Error / Third-Party Vendor Failure / Transparency Failure — Is Urgent

### Scenario 8-6: Man Held Four Years in Australian Immigration Detention Despite Holding a Valid Bridging Visa — Internal Complaint Went Unanswered (Pattern C/E)
**Real Basis:** Commonwealth Ombudsman, "Righting Wrongful Detention," August 2025 (PDF); Commonwealth Ombudsman December 2018 investigation report; UNHCR Australia overview; UN Working Group OHCHR press release December 2025; case of "Mr G" as documented in Ombudsman records
**The Problem:** A man has been held in Australian immigration detention for 14 months. He holds a valid Bridging visa and has provided the reference number to facility staff on multiple occasions. The Department of Home Affairs has not corrected the system record. The internal complaint form he submitted six weeks ago has received no written response. He is now submitting a formal grievance through a Riviwa terminal installed at the facility.
**What staff/org would do:** The duty officer checks the internal system, which displays a refused Partner visa application and no active visa status in the current view — the Bridging visa was never updated in the system's display record. The officer tells the detainee his status is under review and that the legal aid queue is currently 10 weeks long.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled facility compliance officer immediately creates a Priority Review case, contacts the Department of Home Affairs Visa Verification line for an urgent status cross-check, and requests the detainee's complete visa history as a matter of emergency. The officer confirms the Bridging visa status in writing within 24 hours and escalates to the Commonwealth Ombudsman immigration team if the Department has not confirmed within 48 hours.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "If your Bridging visa is valid, your continued detention may not be lawful, and you have the right to an immediate independent review. We are raising this as an urgent case and connecting you with independent legal support today — not in 10 weeks."
- Who to direct to: Commonwealth Ombudsman immigration complaint line (1300 362 072); Refugee Advice and Casework Service (RACS) — 02 9114 1600; Asylum Seeker Resource Centre (ASRC) legal clinic; Federal Court of Australia habeas corpus application process via Legal Aid NSW
- Evidence to collect and preserve: secure and preserve your Bridging visa grant letter and visa reference number; obtain written confirmation showing the date your internal complaint was filed; note every staff member who reviewed your case, the date, and their response; formally request a written copy of your current official visa status from the Department of Home Affairs
- Feedback category tag: Wrongful Detention / Data Error / Processing Failure / Internal Complaint Ignored / System Record Discrepancy

### Scenario 8-7: Legal US Visa Holder Denied Insulin for Six Days Inside ICE Detention — Collapsed and Hospitalised (Pattern C/D)
**Real Basis:** Human Rights Watch, July 21, 2025 ("You Feel Like Your Life Is Over: Abusive Practices at Three Florida Immigration Detention Centers"); The Hill; Globe and Mail; Harpinder Singh Chauhan case, Krome North Service Processing Center, Broward Transitional Center, and Federal Detention Center Miami
**The Problem:** A 55-year-old British entrepreneur with a valid E-2 investor visa, a documented diabetes diagnosis, and a known heart condition has been in ICE detention at a Florida facility for nine days. He has been denied insulin for five consecutive days despite disclosing his conditions at intake and at two subsequent medical request submissions. He is experiencing symptoms consistent with diabetic emergency — extreme thirst, confusion, and inability to stand without dizziness. A fellow detainee is submitting this complaint on his behalf via Riviwa because he is too weak to type.
**What staff/org would do:** The facility medical officer notes that insulin is on order and informs the fellow detainee that the sick man needs to submit a paper medical request form, which will be reviewed at the next scheduled medical rotation — four days away. The duty guard logs medical concern noted with no escalation flag.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled facility compliance officer receives the alert as a medical emergency, immediately contacts the on-site medical contractor for an urgent glucose check and insulin administration, notifies the ICE Field Office Detention Compliance Officer, and documents the welfare check with a time-stamped record completed within 30 minutes.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a medical emergency. Withholding insulin from a person with documented diabetes is a serious and potentially life-threatening harm. We are escalating this right now — the four-day medical rotation schedule is not acceptable in this situation."
- Who to direct to: ICE Enforcement and Removal Operations medical complaint escalation line; DHS Office of Inspector General Hotline (1-800-323-8603, available 24/7); ACLU Florida immigration detention unit (+1 305 576 2337); UK Consulate-General Miami (+1 305 400 6400) for an urgent consular welfare visit
- Evidence to collect and preserve: record the exact date insulin was last administered before the denial began; document the exact words used by the medical officer and duty guard on each occasion; collect names and badge numbers of all staff involved; ask fellow detainees who witnessed the request denials to record what they observed in writing; preserve all paper medical request forms submitted with dates
- Feedback category tag: Medical Emergency / Staff Misconduct / Wrongful Detention / Consular Welfare — Is Urgent

### Scenario 8-8: 1,300 Migrants Expelled to Libyan Desert Without Any Process — 28 Deaths, 16-Year-Old Among Those Expelled (Pattern C/D)
**Real Basis:** Human Rights Watch, July 6, 2023 ("Tunisia: Crisis as Black Africans Expelled to Libya Border"); HRW October 10, 2023 follow-up; NPR, August 23, 2023 ("Rights groups say Tunisia expels migrants into the desert, where some have died"); Al Jazeera
**The Problem:** A 16-year-old Cameroonian girl and an adult travelling companion were seized by Tunisian National Guard officers and transported by truck to the Libya border zone on July 2, 2023. They were given no documents, no water, and no information about their rights. The companion has reached a mobile signal and is filing a Riviwa emergency report. They are in the desert border zone with no shelter, no food, and no identity documents. The temperature is above 40 degrees Celsius and conditions are deteriorating.
**What staff/org would do:** There is no functioning state complaint channel in the border zone. The Tunisian National Guard has no publicly accessible grievance process. The Libyan border zone has no functioning authority capable of processing asylum claims. No one responds.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled humanitarian organisation receiving the alert immediately initiates an emergency geolocation trace, dispatches the nearest mobile protection team with water and medical supplies, contacts UNHCR's Libya emergency desk and the IOM Libya response team, and activates child protection protocols because a minor is involved.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your safety and the safety of the child with you is the only priority right now. Help is being directed to your location. Stay in place if it is safe to do so. Your report has been received and is being acted on immediately."
- Who to direct to: UNHCR Libya Emergency (+218-21-333-8000); IOM Libya Emergency Operations (iom.int/countries/libya); Alarm Phone desert distress support line (+33 4 86 51 71 61); Cameroonian Embassy in Tunis (+216 71 894 027) for the minor's consular protection and repatriation coordination
- Evidence to collect and preserve: share GPS coordinates or the nearest visible landmark immediately; note the time and location where National Guard officers seized them; record any officer identification seen on uniforms or vehicles; describe the truck, the route taken, and the number of other people expelled at the same time; preserve photographs if it is safe to take them
- Feedback category tag: Emergency Expulsion / Child Protection — Unaccompanied Minor / Racial Discrimination / Refoulement Risk / No Due Process — Is Urgent

### Scenario 8-9: Labor Rights Blogger Held in Solitary Confinement and Disappeared — Family Cannot Locate Him for 16 Days (Pattern C/D)
**Real Basis:** Human Rights Watch, May 28, 2021 ("Qatar: Kenyan Labor Rights Activist in Solitary Confinement"); Amnesty International joint statement; Committee to Protect Journalists (CPJ); Malcolm Bidali / pen name "Noah," case documentation; charged with spreading disinformation, released after fine, subsequently expelled from Qatar
**The Problem:** A 28-year-old Kenyan security guard and labor rights blogger who documented migrant worker abuses was removed from his dormitory by state security officers four days ago. His colleagues do not know where he is being held. His family in Kenya learned of his detention only through a third-party contact. No arrest warrant or formal charges have been provided to his family or employer. He has had no access to a lawyer. His employer's HR department has filed a missing person report with the Kenyan Embassy in Doha, which is now submitting a Riviwa grievance on behalf of his family.
**What staff/org would do:** The Qatari Ministry of Interior inquiry line states it cannot confirm or deny the whereabouts of any individual in state custody. The security services do not respond to the embassy's written request for 72 hours. The employer's legal team is told the matter is under investigation, with no further detail provided.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled Kenyan Embassy duty officer in Doha activates the Consular Emergency Protocol immediately, sends a formal diplomatic note to the Qatar Ministry of Foreign Affairs demanding the person's location and the charges against him within 24 hours, and simultaneously contacts the Committee to Protect Journalists and Amnesty International's Urgent Action Network to alert them to the case.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The removal of a person into state custody without access to a lawyer, without charges, and without family notification is a serious human rights concern under international law. The embassy is right to escalate this formally. We are recording every detail now and activating every available channel."
- Who to direct to: Amnesty International Urgent Action Network (amnesty.org/en/get-involved/urgent-action/); Committee to Protect Journalists Emergency Line (+1 212 465 1004); UN Special Rapporteur on Torture complaint submission (ohchr.org/en/special-procedures); Kenyan Ministry of Foreign Affairs Consular Division (+254 20 3318888) for diplomatic escalation and parliamentary pressure
- Evidence to collect and preserve: document the last known contact with the blogger — date, time, and what was said; preserve all published articles and social media posts that may have prompted the arrest; collect names of colleagues who witnessed his removal; record every attempt to contact Qatari authorities with date, method, and response received; preserve any employer communications referencing his work or complaints filed
- Feedback category tag: Enforced Disappearance / Retaliation Against Whistleblower / Press Freedom Violation / Government Misconduct / Consular Access Refused

### Scenario 8-10: Asylum Seekers Held in Transit Zone for Eleven Weeks With No Detention Order and No Complaint Channel Inside the Zone (Pattern C/A)
**Real Basis:** European Court of Human Rights, R.R. and Others v. Hungary (2021), Article 5 violation; ECHR Guide on Immigration Case-Law, February 2026 update; Statewatch ECHR immigration detention judgment; J.R. and Others v. Greece (2018), Article 5 violation (Vial hotspot, Chios island, Afghan nationals held one month without individual detention order)
**The Problem:** A family that fled persecution has been held in a Hungarian border transit zone for 11 weeks. They applied for asylum on arrival. They have received no decision on their application and no hearing date. They cannot leave the transit zone. They have never been served with a formal detention order. There is no complaint form available inside the zone. A legal aid volunteer visiting the zone is submitting this Riviwa complaint on their behalf.
**What staff/org would do:** The border authority officer states the family is not detained but is in an administrative processing zone pending case review. The officer explains that no formal detention order is required under the current administrative framework and that processing timelines depend on circumstances. No legal remedy information is provided. The family is directed to a printed information sheet written in Hungarian.
**What the Owner does:** The Riviwa-enabled border authority compliance officer who receives the complaint immediately opens a formal case record, provides the family with their rights under the EU Asylum Procedures Directive in their language, notifies the legal aid coordinator and UNHCR Hungary office, and schedules a formal asylum hearing within five working days with written confirmation of the date provided to the family.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — being held in a transit zone for nearly three months without a formal detention order and without a hearing — is a violation of your rights under the European Convention on Human Rights, as confirmed by the European Court. You have the right to a legal remedy, and help is available to you now."
- Who to direct to: UNHCR Hungary (+36 1 374 3200); Hungarian Helsinki Committee legal aid (+36 1 321 4323, specialist in transit zone cases); European Court of Human Rights application portal (echr.coe.int) — the family may qualify for interim measures under Rule 39 if removal or further harm is threatened; ECRE (European Council on Refugees and Exiles) for referral to a member legal organisation
- Evidence to collect and preserve: document every day of holding from the date of arrival, including each interaction with border authority staff; request copies of all written communications received — if none have been issued, the absence of a detention order is itself evidence for the ECHR application; note the language in which information was or was not provided; record names and badge numbers of all officers encountered
- Feedback category tag: Unlawful Detention / Asylum Process Denial / No Legal Remedy / Refusal of Service / Rights Violation — ECHR Article 5

Industry ID: 8

## 9. NGO / Development

### Scenario 9-1: Inflated Aid Costs Reach Displaced Beneficiaries (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** DOJ Press Release (16 March 2021); USAID OIG case node 5210 and node 42 — International Rescue Committee $6.9M False Claims Act settlement over Syrian procurement kickbacks, 2012-2015; 12 entities debarred, $239M in procurements suspended
**The Problem:** A Syrian internally displaced person living in a temporary settlement near the Turkish border has received food rations marked as USAID-funded through IRC's cross-border program. A community leader who tracks delivery manifests has noticed that quantities received match neither the manifest totals nor the costs appearing in IRC's published project summaries. He wants to flag this but IRC field workers tell him "that is procurement — not our area."
**What staff/org would do:** Refer the community leader back to the field coordinator, who logs a "community query" with no follow-up timeline or escalation path. The complaint never reaches the procurement oversight function and is closed informally within days.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the community leader's observation specifically — confirms which delivery date and location he is describing, thanks him for maintaining records, and routes the concern to a compliance officer rather than the field coordinator. Records the specific manifest discrepancy with date, item type, and quantity before the conversation ends.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (fraud is historical; no immediate physical safety risk present)
- Opening reply: "Thank you for keeping these delivery records — your observations matter and are exactly what accountability depends on. A discrepancy between manifest quantities and actual deliveries needs to reach a compliance team, not just a field coordinator. I am recording the specifics you have shared right now so this cannot be closed informally."
- Direct to: USAID OIG hotline (1-800-230-6539 / oig.usaid.gov/hotline); IRC Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP) published contact if available; UNHCR protection desk if present in the settlement
- Evidence to preserve: Delivery manifests (photographs of all copies), ration receipt records, any written communications from IRC field staff, dates and quantities of each delivery with location noted
- Feedback category tag: financial-fraud / procurement-irregularity / beneficiary-rights

---

### Scenario 9-2: Health Facility Billed for Deliveries That Never Matched Contract Terms (Pattern A/D)
**Real Basis:** USAID OIG press release (node 7359); Chemonics International / Zenith Carex Nigeria $3,119,582 False Claims Act settlement (2024) — fraudulent tonnage-based billing on PEPFAR/USAID health supply chain deliveries 2017-2020; Chemonics self-disclosed after parallel investigation
**The Problem:** A nurse in charge of a rural Nigerian health facility under a PEPFAR/USAID supply chain program has been receiving deliveries of HIV medications and malaria commodities, but quantities received consistently fall short of delivery notes. When she raises this with the logistics driver, he says paperwork is "handled at the depot." She has raised the discrepancy informally with a logistics supervisor for several months. She has received no written response to any of her verbal queries.
**What staff/org would do:** The logistics supervisor tells the nurse that the discrepancies are "a documentation formatting issue" that will be "corrected in the next cycle." No written acknowledgment is created, the verbal complaint is treated as resolved, and the shortfall continues into the following quarter.
**What the Owner does:** Creates a written record of the nurse's complaint immediately — facility name, dates, commodity type, quantities expected versus received — and escalates it as a supply chain integrity issue, not a formatting matter. Confirms to the nurse that this will be reviewed by someone outside the logistics chain she has already tried.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (ongoing delivery shortfalls are serious but not immediately life-threatening in this moment)
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — delivery records that do not match actual quantities received, month after month — is not a documentation formatting issue. It is a supply chain discrepancy that requires a written investigation. I am recording your complaint in full right now so it cannot be dismissed with another informal conversation."
- Direct to: USAID OIG Nigeria (oig.usaid.gov/hotline); Chemonics program oversight contact for the specific contract; Nigeria's National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) monitoring desk; PEPFAR country team
- Evidence to preserve: All delivery notes (keep every copy), inventory count records, photographs of received commodities against delivery note quantities, dates and signatures on paperwork, written record of all prior verbal complaints with names of staff spoken to
- Feedback category tag: financial-fraud / supply-chain-integrity / delivery-failure / overcharging

---

### Scenario 9-3: Aid Worker Sexual Exploitation — Victim Attempts to Report and Is Told to Wait (Pattern C/E)
**Real Basis:** Oxfam GB internal investigation report; UK Charity Commission statutory inquiry (2018-2020) — Roland van Hauwermeiren and six Oxfam staff in Haiti post-earthquake 2011; alert received July 2011; matter suppressed internally until media exposure February 2018; Haiti suspended Oxfam's operating licence
**The Problem:** A Haitian woman who received Oxfam post-earthquake shelter assistance has been subjected to sexual coercion by an Oxfam staff member who controlled access to building materials. She has reported this to a second Oxfam staff member at a distribution point and was told to "come back when the country director is available." She has waited three days with no appointment, no written acknowledgment of her report, and no referral to any protection service.
**What staff/org would do:** Manage the complaint at field level without escalating to safeguarding. Arrange a private verbal apology from the accused staff member and close the matter internally, citing reputational sensitivity. The woman is never informed whether her complaint was investigated or what action was taken.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a safeguarding emergency requiring immediate escalation outside the local management chain. Does not ask the woman to wait further. Provides written acknowledgment of her complaint on the spot, refers her immediately to an independent protection body, and ensures she is not left in further contact with the accused or with staff who report to him.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You should not be asked to wait three days, and your complaint must not be handled by the same office the accused staff member works within. I am recording your account right now and escalating it outside the local team immediately. You have done nothing wrong by coming forward, and this will not affect your access to assistance."
- Direct to: UNHCR Protection Desk (Haiti); PSEAH (Protection from Sexual Exploitation, Abuse and Harassment) Coordinator if present in the operation; Oxfam's independent safeguarding hotline (international number); Haiti's Institut du Bien-Etre Social et de Recherches (IBESR) for formal protection referral; UK Charity Commission international safeguarding referral route
- Evidence to preserve: Dates and locations of incidents, names or descriptions of the staff member involved, any witnesses, written record of the three-day wait and who she spoke to at the distribution point, any items received under coercion
- Feedback category tag: sexual-exploitation-abuse / safeguarding-emergency / complaint-suppression / is-urgent

### Scenario 9-4: Malaria Grant Funds Diverted — Community Health Worker Receives No Nets to Distribute (Pattern A/B)
**Real Basis:** Global Fund OIG press release (December 2010 / January 2011); AP Enterprise investigation (January 2011); Science/AAAS — approximately $4 million stolen from $22.6 million Mali grants via false invoices, fake competitive bids, and forged per diem records; two malaria grants suspended, TB grant terminated; 16 people arrested
**The Problem:** A community health worker in Mali is delivering malaria prevention outreach under a Global Fund program. He has not received the insecticide-treated nets he was supposed to distribute for three consecutive months. His supervisor tells him the nets were "procured and delivered" according to program records. The worker has never received them, and neither have any of the households he serves. He is told this is a "logistics delay" and to keep attending training events for his per diem.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue paying the community health worker per diems to attend training events — many of which are themselves falsely documented — while the net distribution gap is logged as a logistics variance. No one outside the grant implementer's finance team reviews the discrepancy between reported delivery and actual receipt.
**What the Owner does:** Records the specific gap with precision — program period, net quantities expected per household, number of months of non-delivery, number of households affected, and the supervisor's specific response — and flags this as a grant compliance issue requiring reporting outside the implementer chain.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (ongoing malaria risk is serious but this complaint mechanism is not an emergency channel)
- Opening reply: "Three months between program records showing delivery and your receiving nothing to distribute is not a logistics delay — it is a program compliance gap. I am recording the dates, quantities, and households affected exactly as you have described. This needs to reach an oversight body above your supervisor's level."
- Direct to: Global Fund OIG (theglobalfund.org/en/secretariat/inspector-general/report-fraud/); Mali's Contrôleur Général des Services Publics; any in-country USAID or bilateral donor monitoring team with access to the grant
- Evidence to preserve: Training attendance records with dates, any written distribution schedules received, household visit records showing no nets were distributed, all communications with the supervisor about the delay with dates noted
- Feedback category tag: financial-fraud / program-delivery-failure / grant-compliance / beneficiary-harm

### Scenario 9-5: Aid Organization Claims 130,000 Housed — Community Has Seen Nothing Built (Pattern B/D/E)
**Real Basis:** ProPublica/NPR joint investigation (June 2015); US Senate investigation report (2016) — American Red Cross raised $488 million for Haiti earthquake relief; publicly claimed to have "provided homes to more than 130,000 people"; investigation found only 6 permanent homes built; accounting described as "complex, yet inaccurate"; Red Cross declined to disclose specific expenditures
**The Problem:** A Haitian community leader in the Campeche area was told by a Red Cross field representative in 2012 that a new residential community would be constructed on a site near his neighborhood. It is now three years later, nothing has been built, and when he contacts the Red Cross field office to ask for an update, he is told to "check the website" and informed that the organization "cannot discuss specific project locations." The Red Cross website carries a press release stating 130,000 people have been helped with the funds raised.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the community leader to a public affairs contact who repeats published statistics and declines to provide any site-specific accounting of where the $488 million was spent. The leader is not told why the Campeche project was never started, and no follow-up is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the specific promise made by the field representative — records the project site name, the date of the original representation, and the failure to deliver — and does not redirect the community leader to a website. Informs him of the formal accountability channels that exist for precisely this kind of gap.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Being told to check a website when you are asking about a specific commitment made to your community in person is not an answer. I am recording the specific project site, the date you were told construction would begin, and the fact that nothing has been delivered three years later. You are entitled to an accounting of what happened to that commitment."
- Direct to: US Senate Finance Committee (conducted Red Cross investigation 2016); American Red Cross Ombudsman office; InterAction (US-based NGO accountability body); Haiti's Commission Nationale de Reconstruction d'Haiti (CNRH) for oversight of reconstruction commitments; journalists who have previously investigated this case as a transparency lever
- Evidence to preserve: Any written or verbal commitments from Red Cross representatives (names, dates, specific promises made), photographs of the undeveloped site over time, any media coverage of the Campeche project announcement, correspondence with the Red Cross field office showing non-response
- Feedback category tag: transparency-failure / undelivered-commitment / beneficiary-accountability / information-failure

---

### Scenario 9-6: Internal Ethics Line Transfers to the Same HR Team — Second Call Goes Unanswered (Pattern C/E)
**Real Basis:** UK Charity Commission statutory inquiry report (GOV.UK, March 2020) — Save the Children UK mismanagement of complaints against CEO Justin Forsyth (2012, 2015) and policy director Brendan Cox (2015); complaints resolved through personal apologies without formal investigation; Board of Trustees not informed; Charity Commission found the organization "let down complainants and the public"
**The Problem:** A Save the Children UK employee submitted a formal written complaint in 2015 about repeated inappropriate behavior by a senior director. Six weeks later she received a letter stating the matter had been "resolved through a direct conversation" between the director and HR. She was not told whether a formal investigation had taken place, who had reviewed her complaint, or what action — if any — had been taken. The director remains in post. She has since called the organization's internal ethics line twice: the first call was transferred to the same HR team she had already complained to; the second call went unanswered.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue another letter confirming the complaint is "on file" and offer the complainant a pastoral support call with a different HR adviser, framing the meeting as wellbeing support rather than any form of investigative follow-up.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that the complainant has received no information about whether her complaint was formally investigated, and that two calls to the ethics line failed to connect her to any independent reviewer — the first being routed back to the same HR team, the second simply not answered. Records the full timeline of events as a documented complaint channel failure, and provides the complainant with external reporting routes that bypass the organization's internal system.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (immediate physical safety is not at stake, but ongoing workplace harm and retaliation risk are present)
- Opening reply: "A resolution that consists of 'a direct conversation' between the accused and HR — with no information to you about whether an investigation occurred, who saw your complaint, or what was decided — is not a resolution. Your first ethics line call being transferred to the same HR team, and your second call going unanswered, are both documented failures of that complaint channel. I am recording the full timeline right now."
- Direct to: UK Charity Commission (charitycommission.gov.uk — statutory inquiry and safeguarding referral route for registered charities); ACAS (independent UK workplace dispute service — acas.org.uk); Protect (UK whistleblowing charity, formerly Public Concern at Work — 020 3117 2520); Employment tribunal route if internal process has been exhausted
- Evidence to preserve: Original written complaint with submission date, the "resolution" letter in full, record of both ethics line call dates and outcomes (transfer destination for call 1; date and time of unanswered call 2), any communications from HR, contemporaneous notes of the director's behavior with dates and any witnesses
- Feedback category tag: staff-misconduct / complaint-channel-failure / call-center-failure / safeguarding / ethics-line-failure

### Scenario 9-7: UN Peacekeeper Exploitation — Woman Turned Away at Base Gate (Pattern C/E)
**Real Basis:** HRW report (January 2020); UN OIOS Audit Report 2022/030 (MONUSCO conduct and discipline); The Conversation peer-reviewed community study (2022) — ongoing sexual exploitation by MONUSCO peacekeepers in DRC; 302 of 426 sexual misconduct allegations still pending as of August 2022 (71% unresolved rate); 44 of 426 substantiated (10% resolution rate)
**The Problem:** A woman in eastern DRC has been sexually exploited by a UN peacekeeper who approached her while she was collecting food aid at a distribution point. She has attempted to report this at the MONUSCO base gate, where she was turned away by a uniformed guard and told that "these matters go through the troop command, not us." She does not know what "troop command" means or how to access it. She is afraid that if she keeps attempting to report, she will lose access to the food distribution program the base runs.
**What staff/org would do:** Log her appearance at the gate as a "community inquiry" with no follow-up. The guard provides no reporting form, no contact number, and no name of a civilian conduct officer. The incident never enters the MONUSCO misconduct database, adding to the 302 untracked pending cases.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a safeguarding emergency. Does not send her back to the military gate. Connects her immediately with a civilian protection officer — completely separate from the military chain — and provides a medical and psychosocial support referral. Records the specific base, date, unit markings of the peacekeeper if she can recall them, and the gate guard's response as a separate accountability incident.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You were turned away and you should not have been. Reporting a UN peacekeeper must go through a civilian channel — not through the military structure of the peacekeepers involved. I am recording your account right now and connecting you with a civilian protection officer. This will not affect your access to the food distribution program."
- Direct to: MONUSCO Conduct and Discipline Team (civilian office, not military chain of command); UN Women DRC protection desk; UNHCR Protection Officer in the area; DRC's Ministère du Genre, de la Famille et de l'Enfant; MSF or IRC protection referral pathway if present locally
- Evidence to preserve: Date and location of exploitation, description of the peacekeeper including uniform markings, rank insignia, or national contingent if observable, any witnesses, medical records, specific record of the gate visit — date, time, what the guard said, and whether any paperwork was offered
- Feedback category tag: sexual-exploitation-abuse / safeguarding-emergency / complaint-access-failure / peacekeeping-misconduct / is-urgent

### Scenario 9-8: Resettlement Complainant Threatened by Local Officials After Filing World Bank Panel Complaint (Pattern D/E)
**Real Basis:** World Bank Inspection Panel Investigation Report (documents.worldbank.org); HRW "At Your Own Risk" (June 2015) — Bujagali Hydropower Dam, Uganda; 10 Inspection Panel complaints filed over 16 years; complainants reported being threatened by local authorities; Panel found violations across five Bank policies including involuntary resettlement, inadequate compensation, and information disclosure
**The Problem:** A Ugandan farmer displaced from his land for the Bujagali hydropower dam filed a formal complaint with the World Bank Inspection Panel in 2007, arguing that the compensation paid did not cover the agricultural income he lost when his land was acquired. Two weeks after his complaint was registered, local government officials visited his temporary shelter and warned him that filing complaints with "international bodies" would jeopardize his remaining compensation payments. He has since stopped attending community meetings about the project because other community members have been told the same thing.
**What staff/org would do:** The World Bank project team responds to the Panel that "community consultation processes are ongoing" and that compensation has been provided "in accordance with national law." The local authority intimidation of complainants is not reported to the Panel. The farmer's specific complaint is addressed within a general omnibus response rather than individually.
**What the Owner does:** Records the specific threat as a separate and serious incident — who delivered it, what was said, when, and where — entirely distinct from the original resettlement complaint. Informs the farmer that threatening people who file complaints is itself a violation of the World Bank's accountability framework and that the Panel needs to know this happened.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (the threat has already occurred; immediate physical harm is not indicated in this moment, though the chilling effect on his rights is ongoing)
- Opening reply: "Being visited by local officials and warned to stop your complaint two weeks after you filed it is not a coincidence — it is retaliation, and it is something the World Bank Inspection Panel needs to hear about separately from your original complaint about compensation. I am recording the specific threat, who delivered it, and when."
- Direct to: World Bank Inspection Panel directly (inspectionpanel.org — accepts individual and community submissions and has a mandate to investigate retaliation); World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS); International Accountability Project (IAP) — an NGO that provides direct support to communities filing World Bank complaints; Advocates Coalition for Development and Environment (ACODE) in Uganda
- Evidence to preserve: Detailed record of the threatening visit including date, the officials' names or titles, and exact words used, names of any witnesses present, any prior written communications with the World Bank project team or implementing authority, original displacement notice and compensation documentation showing the gap
- Feedback category tag: retaliation-against-complainant / resettlement-violation / complaint-access-failure / consumer-turned-away / rights-violation

### Scenario 9-9: State Department Grant Operator Submits False Certifications — University Media Center Never Built (Pattern A/D)
**Real Basis:** DOJ press release (February 2021); SIGAR; CourtListener case record 1:20-cr-00258 — Oyetayo Fagbenro pleaded guilty to diverting $1.38 million from $6.9 million State Department grants for Afghan university media centers; false certifications submitted December 2012 claiming all funds properly spent; sentenced 366 days confinement, $775,000 restitution ordered
**The Problem:** A faculty coordinator at an Afghan university was informed in 2011 that a State Department-funded media center would be constructed on campus within 18 months, enabling journalism students to produce broadcast content. It is now 2013 and no construction has begun. When the coordinator contacts the US embassy program officer, he is told that "implementation is ongoing and disbursements are tracking to schedule." The grant operator has submitted written certifications claiming all funds have been properly spent and requesting further disbursements.
**What staff/org would do:** The program officer logs the coordinator's query as "acknowledged" and forwards it to the grant operator's office, which provides a written status update claiming construction is in "procurement phase." No site visit is conducted, the false certification is accepted at face value, and the disbursement request is processed.
**What the Owner does:** Records the faculty coordinator's account precisely — grant name, campus location, the promised completion date, and the current physical state of the site — and treats the gap between the operator's claimed status and the ground reality as a compliance discrepancy requiring physical verification, not administrative reassurance.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You were promised a media center that has not started, and you are being told that disbursements are tracking normally. Those two facts cannot both be true. I am recording the specific gap — the promised timeline, the physical state of the site today, and the response you received from the program office — so this can reach someone with authority to conduct a site verification rather than accept a written status update."
- Direct to: US State Department OIG (oig.state.gov/hotline); SIGAR Fraud, Waste and Abuse hotline (sigar.mil/fraud/index.aspx); US Embassy Public Affairs Section for the specific grant oversight contact; the Afghan university's administration to formally document non-delivery in writing
- Evidence to preserve: Original grant agreement or program announcement with promised deliverables and timeline, any written communications about construction schedules, photographs of the undeveloped site with dates, the written status update from the grant operator, date the false certification was submitted if accessible through any public record
- Feedback category tag: financial-fraud / grant-delivery-failure / corruption / construction-non-delivery / false-certification

### Scenario 9-10: Refugee Woman Coerced for Food Ration — Aid Worker Controls the Distribution List (Pattern C/E)
**Real Basis:** UNHCR/Save the Children UK joint investigation 2002; UN OIOS follow-up investigation; UK Parliament written evidence SEA0042 — 42 agencies and 67 named individuals implicated in food-for-sex exploitation across refugee camps in Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone (2001-2002); investigation directly drove creation of the Humanitarian Accountability Partnership (HAP) and ultimately the Core Humanitarian Standard
**The Problem:** A refugee woman in a camp in Sierra Leone has been told by the food distribution agent — an employee of a humanitarian NGO — that she will receive her household's full ration only if she complies with his demands. She has a child under two years old and the household's nutrition depends on that ration. She tried to tell a female camp worker what was happening, and the female worker said "he is the one who controls the distribution lists — I cannot get involved." The woman does not know who else to tell, and she fears that reporting formally will result in her ration being cut or her household being removed from the list entirely.
**What staff/org would do:** The female camp worker passes the complaint to the distribution supervisor — a colleague of the accused — who notes it as "under observation." No formal report is made, no safeguarding referral is generated, and the woman's ration for the following week is "administratively delayed."
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a safeguarding emergency requiring immediate escalation outside the entire distribution chain. Separates the woman's ration access from the complaint process immediately — confirms in writing that she and her child will receive food regardless of the complaint outcome and regardless of what the accused distribution agent does next. Records the accused agent's name, position, location, and specific conduct, and escalates directly to a protection officer who has no operational connection to the distribution team.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You and your child will not lose your food ration because you have reported this — I want you to hear that first. What is being done to you has a name: it is called food-for-sex exploitation, and it violates every humanitarian standard that exists. I am escalating this right now to a protection officer with no connection to the distribution team. The person controlling the list cannot affect this escalation."
- Direct to: UNHCR Protection Desk (Sierra Leone operation); SGBV (Sexual and Gender-Based Violence) coordinator if present in the camp; IRC Women's Protection and Empowerment program in country; Sierra Leone Police Family Support Unit (FSU); OCHA inter-agency protection cluster lead; camp manager from a different agency if a multi-agency camp structure exists
- Evidence to preserve: Date and location of each incident of coercion, the distribution agent's name, position, and physical description, the female camp worker's name and her exact words, record of the "delayed ration" with the date it occurred, any witnesses, the child's health or nutrition records showing dependency on the ration, any prior written interactions with the agency
- Feedback category tag: sexual-exploitation-abuse / safeguarding-emergency / food-for-sex / complaint-access-failure / vulnerability / is-urgent

Industry ID: 9

## 10. Retail / Consumer Products

### Scenario 10-1: Exploding Juicer Causes Abdominal Injury Before Product Recall (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** CPSC recall announcement, Empower Brands PowerXL Pro Performance Juicer, June 2023; US PIRG Education Fund "Safe at Home in 2024" consumer product safety report — 261 consumer complaints, 47 injuries, 469,000 units recalled across the USA and Canada
**The Problem:** A 53-year-old woman is using her PowerXL juicer at home when the unit explodes, shooting a metal component into her abdomen. She is in pain and bleeding. She cannot find a phone number that connects to a live person at the retailer — only an automated returns portal. Her husband calls the store where it was purchased; staff say they have "no information about any recall" and direct him to Empower Brands' 1-800 number, which plays hold music for 38 minutes before disconnecting.
**What staff/org would do:** Tell the family the appliance "may have been misused" and that any claim must go through the manufacturer. Empower Brands, when eventually reached, opens a warranty claim and requires the product to be returned at the consumer's expense for inspection before any remedy is considered.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately acknowledges this as a physical injury emergency. Does not ask the consumer to return the product before seeking medical care. Documents the full incident and flags it for urgent escalation. Instructs the consumer not to use the product and to preserve all physical evidence.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is serious — your safety comes first. Please seek medical attention now if you have not already. We are documenting everything and will support you at every step."
- Direct the consumer to: CPSC SaferProducts.gov to file an incident report online; CPSC consumer hotline 1-800-638-2772; nearest emergency medical care if not already obtained; Empower Brands product liability claim once the consumer is safe
- Evidence to preserve: photographs of the injury and the damaged appliance, the broken metal component (sealed in a bag, do not discard), purchase receipt with date and retailer name, any medical records from treatment
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Physical Injury / Defective Good / Urgent

---

### Scenario 10-2: Counterfeit Cosmetics from Temu — Caused Skin Rash, Refund Refused With Only Template Replies (Pattern B + C + E)
**Real Basis:** FTC enforcement action against Whaleco Inc. (Temu), $2M civil penalty under INFORM Consumers Act, 2024; European Commission investigation into Temu for counterfeit and unsafe goods, October 2024; documented Trustpilot complaint patterns cited in FTC proceedings — template-only responses, refusal to process refunds when consumers explicitly invoked their rights
**The Problem:** A consumer paid $67 for what appeared to be CeraVe skincare products listed on Temu. The items arrived with packaging that differs from genuine CeraVe — the font is slightly wrong, the batch code is absent, and the product has an unusual chemical smell. After applying the cream she developed a skin rash. She has contacted Temu customer support four times over 12 days. Each response is a different template — first a "quality guarantee" assurance, then a request to upload photos, then notice that the seller has been "notified," then a message that the "investigation is ongoing" with no timeline. No refund has been issued.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue sending template responses asking for further photographs. Eventually close the case with a partial store-credit offer redeemable only on future Temu purchases. No acknowledgment that the product may be counterfeit or the cause of the rash.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the health risk posed by the rash. Does not ask the consumer to keep waiting through an endless chain of template messages. Advises on the chargeback right and documents the transaction details for regulatory referral.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (rash is uncomfortable but not life-threatening; escalate to urgent if the reaction worsens or spreads)
- Opening reply: "I'm sorry — receiving a potentially fake product that has caused a skin reaction is both frustrating and a genuine health concern. Let me help you get a refund and report this properly."
- Direct the consumer to: FTC ReportFraud.ftc.gov to report the counterfeit goods; contact their bank or credit card issuer immediately to initiate a chargeback — Temu's repeated non-response and failure to resolve constitutes grounds for reversal; report the counterfeit listing to CeraVe/L'Oreal USA brand protection; if located in the EU, file through the BEUC complaint portal and the EU ODR platform
- Evidence to preserve: screenshots of all four Temu chat responses with dates, photographs of the packaging discrepancies against genuine CeraVe, photos of the skin rash, order confirmation and payment record, the product itself (do not use further, retain for potential testing)
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Goods / Refund Refused / Online Platform Fraud / Financial Harm

---

### Scenario 10-3: Bought Based on Thousands of Fake Reviews — Company Was Suppressing All Negative Feedback for Four Years (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** FTC enforcement action against Fashion Nova, $4.2M settlement finalized January 2022; FTC refund distribution to 148,351 consumers, January 2025 — company used a third-party tool to automatically post 4- and 5-star reviews while holding all reviews below 4 stars, never approving or posting the held reviews between late 2015 and November 2019
**The Problem:** A consumer purchased three dresses from an online fashion retailer totaling $184, choosing the brand specifically because it showed thousands of reviews with a 4.8-star average and she could not find a single negative review on the site. The items arrived poorly made — stitching came apart on first wear. When she tried to post a 2-star review, the submission appeared to go through but the review never appeared publicly. A friend sent her a news article confirming the company was fined $4.2 million by the FTC for systematically hiding all customer reviews below 4 stars for years. She contacts support to request a refund and is offered a 20% discount code on her next order.
**What staff/org would do:** Apologize for the "quality issue," offer the discount code, and state that refunds are only available within 30 days of purchase. The review suppression is not acknowledged. No information about the FTC settlement or consumer refund eligibility is volunteered.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that the consumer was deceived before purchase — the reviews she relied on were a manufactured impression, not an accurate record of customer opinion. Does not deflect with discount codes. Explains the FTC settlement and the consumer's potential eligibility for a refund from the settlement fund.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You were deceived at the point of decision — the reviews you relied on were deliberately hidden from the public. You deserve both a refund and the full picture of what happened."
- Direct the consumer to: FTC refund page for Fashion Nova claimants at ftc.gov — check eligibility and filing window; if the refund window has closed, file a complaint at FTC ReportFraud.ftc.gov; dispute the charge with their credit card issuer for goods that did not match their advertised quality if within the dispute period
- Evidence to preserve: screenshots of the product page showing the star rating and review count at time of purchase, order confirmation, photographs of the defective stitching, records of the discount-code-only response from customer service
- Feedback category tag: Deceptive Advertising / Review Manipulation / Information Failure / Staff Misconduct

---

### Scenario 10-4: Elderly Woman Told Her Refund Rights "Expired" — Call Centre Staff Misrepresented Australian Consumer Law (Pattern C + A)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v Mosaic Brands (Noni B, Rivers, Katies), Federal Court of Australia, $25.05M penalty ordered August 2025 — 739,114 items not delivered within advertised timeframes; company falsely represented to consumers that refund rights did not apply after six months of purchase, directly misrepresenting Australian Consumer Law
**The Problem:** A 74-year-old woman ordered winter clothing worth AUD $312 from Katies eight months ago. The parcel was marked "in transit" for weeks and tracking then stopped permanently. She has called Katies customer service three times. On the first call she was placed on hold for 22 minutes and disconnected. On the second call she was told to wait because of "supply chain delays." On the third call today a customer service agent told her that her refund rights have expired because "our policy is that refund requests must be submitted within six months of purchase." She accepted this and hung up. A neighbour told her this did not sound right, and she is now contacting Riviwa.
**What staff/org would do:** Reiterate the 6-month policy as though it is law. Offer a store credit valid only for future Katies purchases. Decline to explain the legal basis for the policy. Not mention the ACCC investigation.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately corrects the misinformation. Australian Consumer Law does not impose a 6-month refund limit — the right to a refund for non-delivery applies for a "reasonable time" with no fixed cap. The 6-month rule told to this consumer is the exact misrepresentation for which Mosaic Brands was penalised $25.05 million.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (elderly and financially vulnerable consumer was given false legal information by a call centre agent and believed she had no remaining rights; her statutory entitlements are fully active)
- Opening reply: "Your neighbour was right — what you were told about the 6-month limit is legally incorrect, and it is the exact misrepresentation an Australian court ordered $25 million in penalties for. Your rights have not expired. Let's get this resolved."
- Direct the consumer to: ACCC online complaint form at accc.gov.au/consumers/complaints; the relevant state fair trading office (Consumer Affairs Victoria, NSW Fair Trading, or equivalent); call Katies again and state explicitly "I am exercising my rights under Australian Consumer Law — non-delivery of goods entitles me to a full refund, with no six-month limit"; if not resolved within 7 days, proceed to the state consumer tribunal (VCAT in Victoria, NCAT in New South Wales)
- Evidence to preserve: order confirmation email, all tracking screenshots including the stopped tracking record, written notes on each of the three calls with date, time, and the agent's exact words about the 6-month policy, any written confirmation from Katies of their refusal
- Feedback category tag: Refund Refused / Consumer Rights Violation / Call Centre Misinformation / Vulnerable Consumer

---

### Scenario 10-5: Smartphone Used in Pool Exactly as Advertised — Samsung Denied Warranty Citing Water Damage (Pattern D + A)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v Samsung Electronics Australia, Federal Court, $14M penalty 2022 — Samsung advertised smartphones being used in swimming pools and at the beach; Samsung admitted in court that water resistance did not perform in pool or salt water conditions; consumers who made warranty claims for exactly that type of damage were simultaneously refused under the "water damage exclusion"
**The Problem:** A consumer bought a Samsung Galaxy S21 specifically because Samsung's advertising campaign showed the phone being used in a swimming pool. She used the phone briefly at the edge of a lap pool — not submerged, only exposed to water splashing during a swimming lesson she was supervising — and it stopped working the same evening. She took it to a Samsung Service Centre, where a technician confirmed water ingress as the cause and refused the warranty claim, citing a "water damage exclusion clause." When she pointed out that Samsung's own advertisement depicts the phone in a pool, the staff member said "the advertising shows ideal conditions" and offered her a $200 discount on a new Samsung device.
**What staff/org would do:** Read from the warranty script: water damage exclusion applies regardless of advertising claims. Offer the replacement discount as final resolution. Not acknowledge the contradiction between the advertising and the warranty exclusion. Not disclose the ACCC court action.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the documented legal contradiction: Samsung admitted in Federal Court that its water resistance advertising was misleading, and warranty claims for exactly this type of damage were unlawfully denied. Under Australian Consumer Law, goods must conform to their advertised description — the warranty exclusion cannot override this statutory guarantee.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The warranty refusal you received contradicts both Australian Consumer Law and a Federal Court finding against Samsung Australia on exactly this issue. You have a valid legal claim here."
- Direct the consumer to: ACCC complaint portal at accc.gov.au; cite the 2022 Federal Court order against Samsung Electronics Australia specifically on water resistance misrepresentation; write a formal letter to Samsung citing Australian Consumer Law s 56 (goods must match their description and advertising) and demanding repair, replacement, or refund; if Samsung refuses in writing, proceed to the relevant state consumer tribunal
- Evidence to preserve: the Samsung advertisement showing pool use (screenshot or video link, or note the campaign name), proof of purchase with model number, the Samsung Service Centre's written refusal citing the water damage exclusion, a record of the $200 counter-offer, photographs of the phone
- Feedback category tag: False Advertising / Warranty Denied / Staff Misconduct / Consumer Rights Violation

---

### Scenario 10-6: Funeral Flowers That Were Never Discounted — Bloomex Fake Strikethrough Prices and Hidden Checkout Fees (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** ACCC enforcement action against Bloomex Australia, $1M penalty — company systematically displayed false "was/now" strikethrough prices on products that had never been sold at the higher "was" price; also engaged in drip pricing by adding mandatory fees at checkout not disclosed in the advertised price; false star ratings also found; cited in Holding Redlich 2024 Australian Consumer Law enforcement review
**The Problem:** A consumer ordered a sympathy flower arrangement from Bloomex for a colleague's funeral. The website advertised the arrangement at AUD $54, displayed as reduced from $89 — a claimed saving of 39%. At checkout, three additional fees appeared without prior disclosure: a handling fee of $12.95, a fuel surcharge of $7.50, and a same-week premium of $7.00, bringing the total to $81.45. She paid because the flowers were time-sensitive. After the funeral she discovered through a news article that the $89 "was" price has never existed — Bloomex had never sold this product at that price. She contacts Bloomex to request a refund of the undisclosed fees; the agent tells her "all fees are disclosed at checkout" and offers a $10 voucher.
**What staff/org would do:** Insist the fees were technically visible during checkout and that the customer accepted them by completing the purchase. Offer the $10 voucher as final resolution. Not acknowledge that the strikethrough "was" price was fictitious.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that both the fake discount and the mandatory fees added without prior disclosure are practices the ACCC found to be unlawful under Australian Consumer Law. The consumer was deceived at the point of choice, not after it.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What you have described — a discount from a price that never existed, and fees that appeared only at checkout — are the exact practices the ACCC took enforcement action against Bloomex for. You are right to challenge this."
- Direct the consumer to: ACCC complaint portal at accc.gov.au; the relevant state fair trading office; cite Australian Consumer Law ss 18 and 29 (misleading representations about price) and the ACCC's own enforcement action against Bloomex as direct precedent; contact the issuing bank or credit card provider to dispute the undisclosed fee component as a misrepresented charge
- Evidence to preserve: screenshot of the advertised price showing the strikethrough "was/now" display, screenshot of the checkout page showing when and how fees appeared, the order confirmation email with the total charged, all customer service correspondence including the $10 voucher offer
- Feedback category tag: Drip Pricing / False Discount / Deceptive Advertising / Transparency Failure / Corporate Misconduct

---

### Scenario 10-7: Flipkart Marked Order "Delivered" — Item Never Arrived, Six Calls Over Three Weeks With No Resolution (Pattern C + A + E)
**Real Basis:** India National Consumer Helpline (NCH) data 2023-24 — 1.236 million consumer calls received, 36.1% from e-commerce complaints; Flipkart the most-complained-about platform with over 1.6 lakh (160,000) documented grievances; non-delivery and refund not received the top documented complaint categories; Ministry of Consumer Affairs data via FACTLY analysis
**The Problem:** A consumer in Mumbai paid INR 8,500 for an air purifier ordered from Flipkart. Tracking changed to "Delivered" on a Tuesday morning — but no delivery was made. He was home all day. No delivery agent visited, no OTP was sent to his registered number (Flipkart requires OTP confirmation for high-value deliveries), and no parcel was left with a neighbour. He has called Flipkart customer support six times over three weeks. Every call produces the same response: "Our system shows the item was delivered. We are unable to issue a refund." He has spoken to four different agents; none have escalated the case. He has already filed a complaint with the National Consumer Helpline at 1800-11-4000 and asks what else he can do.
**What staff/org would do:** Repeat "delivered per system records" and close the ticket. Suggest he check with neighbours. Issue a new reference number with no resolution commitment or timeline.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the consumer has already taken every correct step. Confirms that six contacts without resolution and a GPS "delivered" record with no OTP confirmation is a strong, escalation-ready case under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, which explicitly covers e-commerce platforms.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no safety risk; significant financial harm of INR 8,500; time-sensitive for formal escalation)
- Opening reply: "You have done everything right, and Flipkart's refusal to act after six contacts on a provably undelivered order is exactly the kind of platform failure the Consumer Protection Act 2019 was designed to address. Let's escalate this formally."
- Direct the consumer to: Follow up on the existing NCH complaint using the reference number; escalate to the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 — e-commerce platforms are explicitly covered and a written complaint can be filed without a lawyer; use the Umang app or NCH app to submit a written record if not already done; if paid by credit or debit card, file a chargeback with the issuing bank citing non-delivery — the absence of an OTP delivery confirmation is strong evidence
- Evidence to preserve: order confirmation and tax invoice, all six customer service chat or call logs with dates and agent names if given, screenshots of the tracking page showing "Delivered" status, documentary evidence that no OTP was sent to the registered mobile on the delivery date (mobile carrier records or simply a screenshot of SMS history for that day), a written statement confirming the consumer was home
- Feedback category tag: Non-Delivery / Refund Refused / Platform Failure / Wait Without Resolution

---

### Scenario 10-8: Empty iPhone Box Delivered — Apple Refuses Refund or Replacement, Consumer on Fourth Transfer (Pattern C + E)
**Real Basis:** UK Government Consumer Detriment Survey 2024 (NatCen) — 38.5 million UK consumers affected by unresolved product or service problems; Which? "Most Frustrating Consumer Problems of 2024" — documented case of a consumer receiving an empty Apple iPhone box via DPD and being refused both a replacement and a refund by Apple until Which? intervened
**The Problem:** A consumer in Bristol ordered an iPhone 15 Pro at £1,199 directly from Apple online. DPD delivered a sealed Apple box. When she opened it at home the box was completely empty — no device, no accessories, no documentation. She called Apple immediately. After 90 minutes on the phone and three transfers, an Apple representative told her that the package was weighed at Apple's fulfilment centre before dispatch and the weight confirmed the phone was inside, so Apple will not issue a replacement or refund. She is told to report the theft to the police and contact her home insurer. She has no home insurance. She has now been put on hold a fourth time and is in tears.
**What staff/org would do:** Maintain the weight-verification position as the final word. Provide a crime reference template for the police. Suggest contacting an insurer. Decline any further internal escalation. Close the case as post-delivery theft and outside Apple's liability.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as an urgent, high-value loss situation. Apple's weight-verification argument places the entire burden of proof on the consumer for a delivery chain Apple contracted and controlled. Under the UK Consumer Rights Act 2015, Apple's obligation is to deliver the goods — if the item was not received, the contract has not been fulfilled and a refund is legally due regardless of what DPD's records show.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (£1,199 financial loss on an essential device; consumer is distressed; all Apple contact has been exhausted; no insurer available as backstop)
- Opening reply: "I understand how devastating this is — £1,199 and no phone, refused at every turn. Apple's position has a direct legal flaw in your favour under UK law. Let's address this right now."
- Direct the consumer to: UK Consumer Rights Act 2015 — Apple bears the obligation to deliver; if the item was not received, Apple must issue a full refund; send Apple a formal written complaint immediately (written complaints trigger a different escalation obligation than phone calls); if paid by credit card, invoke Section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act 1974 — purchases over £100 make the card issuer jointly liable with Apple, meaning the bank must refund even if Apple refuses; if paid by debit card, raise a Chargeback claim with the bank for non-delivery; Citizens Advice Consumer Helpline 0808 223 1133; Which? free legal rights guidance on undelivered online orders; if Apple does not resolve within 14 days of the written complaint, proceed to the Retail Ombudsman or Money Claim Online (Small Claims Court) for claims up to £10,000
- Evidence to preserve: DPD delivery confirmation with timestamp and any GPS or photo proof-of-delivery record, video of opening the empty box if recorded (film immediately if not already done), all Apple call reference numbers and the written record of each call, a written request to Apple to confirm their final position by email, bank or card payment records
- Feedback category tag: Non-Delivery / Refund Refused / High-Value Financial Loss / Call Centre Escalation Failure

### Scenario 10-9: EU Return Rights Rejected — Polish Retailer Refuses Refund to German Consumer for Unwanted Clothing (Pattern C + E)
**Real Basis:** European Consumer Centres Network (ECC-Net) Annual Report 2023 — 125,000+ consumer inquiries handled across 29 centres; 22,500 cases required direct trader intermediation; EUR 9M recovered; cross-border e-commerce returns and refusal to honour the EU 14-day cooling-off right were the dominant complaint category; 33% surge in cross-border cases in 2023
**The Problem:** A consumer in Berlin ordered three items of clothing totalling €340 from an online fashion retailer based in Poland. The clothes arrived and do not fit. She emailed the Polish retailer within 9 days of delivery to exercise her right of withdrawal under the EU Consumer Rights Directive — the 14-day cooling-off right that applies to all distance contracts within the EU, with no obligation to give a reason. The retailer replied that "returns from Germany are not accepted" due to logistics costs, and offered a 15% store-credit voucher instead. A second email sent five days later was ignored.
**What staff/org would do:** Reiterate the no-returns-from-Germany policy. Re-send the store-credit offer. Ignore further correspondence. The retailer may be deliberately exploiting the assumption that cross-border consumers will not pursue a €340 dispute across a border.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that EU Consumer Rights Directive Article 9 (Directive 2011/83/EU) gives consumers 14 days to withdraw from distance contracts with no requirement to give a reason, and no geographic exemption within the EU. A Polish retailer selling to a German consumer has identical obligations as if the consumer were Polish. The "no returns from Germany" policy is unlawful.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (€340 financial harm; the 14-day window is still open based on the 9-day contact — act promptly)
- Opening reply: "The retailer's no-returns-from-Germany rule contradicts EU law directly. Your 14-day right of withdrawal applies in full, and there are clear, free escalation paths designed for exactly this situation."
- Direct the consumer to: ECC Germany (ecc.de) — free mediation service specifically for cross-border EU consumer disputes, staffed with legal experts; the EU Online Dispute Resolution platform (ec.europa.eu/consumers/odr) — file a formal dispute against the Polish retailer; cite EU Consumer Rights Directive 2011/83/EU Article 9 explicitly in any further written communication with the retailer; if the retailer continues to refuse after ODR filing, contact the consumer protection authority in Poland (UOKiK) via ECC Germany as intermediary; contact PayPal or the card issuer for a payment dispute if the retailer does not engage within 30 days of the ODR submission
- Evidence to preserve: order confirmation showing the German delivery address, delivery tracking confirmation with date of receipt, the email sent within 14 days exercising the right of withdrawal with a clear timestamp, the retailer's response refusing the return, the second unanswered email with its send timestamp
- Feedback category tag: Cross-Border Return Rights / Refund Refused / EU Consumer Law Violation / Rights Violation

### Scenario 10-10: Seven Calls, Three Disconnections, No Resolution — Elderly Consumer Trapped in Retail Call Centre Loop Over Hazardous Appliance (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Retail and global call centre benchmarks 2023-24: 67% of customers abandon calls before reaching a live agent (Zendesk industry data); retail sector records the highest escalation rate of any measured sector at 18-24% of all contacts (Stealth Agents / industry contact centre research); CSAT drops an average of 22 points when a ticket is escalated (SQM Group contact centre research); Salesforce "State of the AI Connected Customer" 2024 — 76% of customers more likely to switch brands after a single bad service experience; UK Consumer Detriment Survey 2024 — 48% of consumer problems left unresolved
**The Problem:** A 71-year-old retired teacher in Nairobi ordered an electric kettle from a major retail chain's online store for KES 3,200. After three uses, the kettle overheats severely and the plastic handle has begun to melt — a burn and fire hazard. Over ten days she has called the retailer's customer service line seven times. Four calls were abandoned after waiting 15 to 40 minutes without reaching anyone. On two calls she reached an agent, was placed on hold for a supervisor, and the call disconnected. On the seventh call she was transferred to a product faults queue that played automated music for 27 minutes before disconnecting automatically. She is not comfortable using online complaint forms and does not know where else to turn.
**What staff/org would do:** If eventually reached: open a new ticket, provide a reference number, tell her the escalation team will contact her within 5 to 7 business days. No one calls back. The case sits in the escalation queue because the purchase value falls below the automatic senior-agent review threshold.
**What the Owner does:** Takes full ownership immediately without transferring. Recognises the consumer is elderly, has no digital escalation path available to her, has made seven documented attempts without resolution, and is still in possession of a product that poses an active fire and burn hazard. Addresses the safety concern first, then the resolution pathway.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (a melting handle on a heating appliance is a fire and burn hazard; the product must not be used; seven contacts and ten days without resolution for a safety-critical defect is unacceptable)
- Opening reply: "Please stop using the kettle and unplug it right away — a melting handle on a heating appliance is a real safety hazard. Seven calls and ten days without any resolution is completely unacceptable, and I am going to help you get this resolved without another transfer."
- Direct the consumer to: Stop using and unplug the appliance immediately; report the safety defect to the Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS) product quality complaint line and the Competition Authority of Kenya (CAK) consumer complaint portal; send a written formal complaint to the retailer by email or physical letter headed "FORMAL COMPLAINT — SAFETY DEFECT" — written complaints trigger a legal obligation to respond in most jurisdictions and cannot be closed by an automated hold; if a family member or neighbour can assist, submit the complaint through the retailer's website simultaneously to create a timestamped written record; as a last-resort escalation, contact the retailer publicly via their social media account — documented industry data shows public posts on brand accounts resolve call-centre-resistant cases significantly faster than phone queues
- Evidence to preserve: proof of purchase, photographs of the melted handle and any discolouration or burn marks on the appliance, a written log of all seven call attempts including the date, time, and duration of each attempt and any reference numbers received
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Defective Good / Call Centre Loop / Vulnerable Consumer / Elderly

Industry ID: 10
