## 11. Food & Consumables

### Scenario 11-1: Contaminated Deli Meat, Active Outbreak, Regulatory Complaint Intake Shows Zero Reports During Deaths (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** USDA-FSIS internal review report, October 22, 2024 ("No consumer complaints have been documented for this establishment for the past year"); CDC Listeria outbreak data — Boar's Head Provisions, 61 ill, 10 deaths, 19 states; FSIS Boar's Head January 2025 public review (18 pages); U.S. Senator Blumenthal federal investigation announcement; PissedConsumer reviews for Boar's Head customer service, 2024

**The Problem:** A 67-year-old immunocompromised consumer hears the Boar's Head deli meat recall on the news and realises she ate liverwurst from this brand three days ago. She now has a fever, muscle aches, and a stiff neck — early Listeria symptoms. She calls the company's published recall hotline (1-800-352-6277). After 22 minutes on hold a representative answers, says the company cannot provide medical advice, and reads back the same scripted recall notice language she already heard on the news. She asks whether her specific lot number is in the recall. The representative says he cannot confirm individual lot numbers. She asks whether the company had prior knowledge of contamination. He says he has no information on that. She asks about medical cost coverage. He says to "keep your receipt" and call back with a case number — no case number is issued. She hangs up. Unknown to her, the FSIS internal report produced that same month states verbatim: "No consumer complaints have been documented for this establishment for the past year" — despite 65+ critical violations logged since January 2022 and 10 consumer deaths.

**What staff/org would do:** Place the caller on hold, read from the recall script, disclaim medical advice, offer no referral to emergency services, collect no complaint data, issue no case number, and close the interaction. The company's complaint intake records the interaction as zero consumer escalations — consistent with the documented FSIS finding.

**What the Owner does:** Recognises this is a medical emergency, not a customer service call. Confirms the product is recalled and explains Listeria symptoms are time-critical in older and immunocompromised individuals. Immediately provides the nearest emergency department information and instructs the consumer to tell the ER she consumed Listeria-recalled deli meat. Notes that the FSIS regulatory complaint intake system showed zero complaints during an active deadly outbreak — meaning the formal system failed — and provides the correct FSIS consumer complaint line. Helps the consumer document symptoms and purchase for a potential legal claim.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a medical emergency. Fever, muscle pain, and stiff neck after consuming recalled Listeria deli meat are warning signs of serious infection. Please go to an emergency room now and tell them you ate Listeria-recalled Boar's Head liverwurst. Do not wait. Once you are safe, I will help you with the company and regulatory complaint."
- Direct the consumer to: Nearest emergency room (Listeria in elderly/immunocompromised persons has a 20-30% fatality rate without treatment); FSIS Consumer Complaint Monitoring System (1-800-674-6854); CDC FoodSafety.gov illness reporting; food safety attorneys handling Boar's Head class actions (filed U.S. District Courts, 2024)
- Evidence to collect: product packaging with lot number and use-by date (photograph all sides); purchase receipt; written log of symptoms with exact dates and times; full record of the customer service call (date, time, hold duration, what the representative said); all medical records from the emergency visit
- Feedback category tag: food_safety_emergency / listeria / product_recall / institutional_complaint_failure / regulatory_capture

---

### Scenario 11-2: Fake Paneer Sold by Vendor, FSSAI Complaint Portal Unresolved for Seven Months, Vendor Using Local Connections to Block Inspection (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** FSSAI surveillance data 2024 — 83% of paneer samples in Noida/Greater Noida failed quality norms; 40% declared unsafe for consumption; Jharkhand seizure of 4,000 kg fake paneer (2024); FSSAI Food Safety Connect portal statistics 2024-25: 7,705 adulteration complaints received, 1,753 (22%) unresolved; Newsgram, YouthInc Magazine, Medical Dialogues reporting (2024-2026)

**The Problem:** A family in Noida, Uttar Pradesh purchased paneer from a local shop for a dinner function. Three family members became ill within six hours — nausea, vomiting, and cramps. An informal laboratory test on the remaining paneer found it adulterated with starch, vegetable fat, and non-dairy whiteners, consistent with documented fake paneer operations. When the family returned to the vendor, he denied any problem and threatened to call his "contacts" at the municipal council. The family filed a complaint through FSSAI's Food Safety Connect portal in January 2024. Seven months later the portal still shows "Under Review." No inspector has visited the shop. The vendor continues to operate. When the family calls the NCH helpline about the FSSAI status, the representative confirms the complaint is "forwarded to the district food safety officer" but gives no timeline and says they cannot follow up on individual FSSAI cases.

**What staff/org would do:** The FSSAI portal auto-acknowledges and routes the complaint to a district food safety officer whose caseload far exceeds capacity. The DFSO may issue a notice; the vendor's local contacts facilitate delay. After months the case may be marked "resolved" without any enforcement visit. The NCH helpline correctly states it cannot compel FSSAI action.

**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the vendor's threatening behaviour as intimidation and flags it as a separate reportable offense. Explains the documented 22% non-resolution rate in FSSAI's own portal and provides a parallel escalation path through the State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, which operates independently of the FSSAI administrative chain and has enforcement powers. Advises that media exposure of documented adulteration cases has historically accelerated FSSAI action where the administrative route is stalled. Recommends the family retain the adulterated sample in a sealed, refrigerated container for independent testing.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (acute illness has passed; ongoing community risk from the vendor continuing to operate)
- Opening reply: "I am sorry your family was made ill and that the vendor responded with threats rather than accountability. What you are describing — adulterated paneer and a complaint stuck for months — is a documented pattern in India right now. The FSSAI portal alone may not be enough. Let me show you the routes that actually have enforcement power."
- Direct the consumer to: State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (file at edaakhil.nic.in; parallel to FSSAI and independently adjudicated); FSSAI escalation to the State Commissioner of Food Safety (separate from district-level DFSO); local media with documented lab report (shown to accelerate enforcement in similar cases); Anti-Adulteration Cell where available in the state; NCH helpline 1915 (for the consumer complaint against the vendor, separate from the FSSAI regulatory complaint)
- Evidence to collect: informal laboratory adulteration test report; sealed sample of the remaining paneer (refrigerated, labelled with purchase date); purchase receipt; screenshots of the FSSAI Food Safety Connect complaint showing submission date and current "Under Review" status; written account of the vendor's threatening statements; medical bills from illness treatment; names and contact details of all family members who fell ill
- Feedback category tag: food_adulteration / vendor_intimidation / complaint_delay / fssai_portal_failure / staff_misconduct / corruption

### Scenario 11-3: Cantaloupe Salmonella — Serial Recall Expansions, Consumer Given Clearance on Day One, Ate the Product, Now Ill (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** CDC Salmonella outbreak investigation update, November 2023 (Malichita/Rudy brand cantaloupe, 407 ill in 44 US states, 6 deaths); Canada PHAC public health notice; CFIA recall warnings issued in four waves — November 1, 14, 17, and 24, 2023; CBC News; Health Canada food recall process documentation; 153 confirmed Canadian Salmonella cases, 6 Canadian deaths

**The Problem:** A retired teacher in Winnipeg, Manitoba purchased a Malichita brand cantaloupe from a major grocery chain on October 28, 2023. On November 3 she heard about the first recall on the radio and called the store's customer service line. A representative checked the recall list and told her the recalled lots were specific California batches and her melon's lot number was not included — she was safe to consume it. She ate the cantaloupe. On November 18 a second expanded recall now included her lot number and a broader date range. She called the store again; transferred three times, eventually confirmed her lot was now recalled — but she had already eaten the product. She began experiencing diarrhoea and stomach cramps two days later. She called the store a third time to report her illness. She was placed on hold for 31 minutes and disconnected.

**What staff/org would do:** Each recall expansion is treated as a separate event with no notification to consumers who received prior clearance. Store representatives have no unified tracking of which customers bought product across the multiple recall waves. The consumer is told to "contact Health Canada" or "call your doctor." No proactive notification, no compensation, and no follow-up on the disconnected call.

**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the documented serial recall failure — four expansions over 24 days meant consumers who acted on the first notice were still at risk, a pattern the CFIA recall record confirms. Validates that this was not the consumer's error. Confirms her symptoms are consistent with Salmonella (onset 12-72 hours post-consumption, diarrhoea is the primary symptom). Advises her to seek medical care and to report her illness to the local public health unit, which contributes to outbreak monitoring data. Helps her document the sequence of store calls and the conflicting information she received as evidence of retail notification failure.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (mild Salmonella illness is typically self-limiting; escalate to urgent if the consumer is elderly, immunocompromised, or experiences bloody diarrhoea, high fever, or severe dehydration)
- Opening reply: "This was not your fault. The recall was expanded four times over 24 days, which means consumers who did exactly the right thing on day one were still left at risk. Your illness needs to be reported — both for your own medical care and to make sure your case is counted in the outbreak data."
- Direct the consumer to: Health Canada food safety complaints (canada.ca/en/health-canada/services/food-nutrition/food-safety/information-consumers/report-complaint.html); CFIA consumer complaints 1-800-442-2342; local public health unit (to report Salmonella case — this adds to outbreak investigation); CFIA food recall database for all four November 2023 recall notices (archived at inspection.canada.ca); personal injury lawyer if medical costs are significant
- Evidence to collect: grocery receipt or loyalty card record showing purchase date; photograph of product label with lot number and best-before date; written log of all calls to the store — dates, times, hold durations, representatives' names, and exactly what each said; CFIA recall notices for all four November waves (print or screenshot each); medical records from the illness
- Feedback category tag: food_recall / serial_recall_failure / consumer_notification_failure / call_center_misinformation / salmonella / canada

### Scenario 11-4: Food Adulteration Complaint on National Helpline — Six Months of "Pending with Company", No Inspection, No Resolution (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** India Ministry of Consumer Affairs disclosure, March 2025 — National Consumer Helpline food and beverage complaints nearly doubled from 27,754 (2023) to 53,228 (2024); Karnataka highest volume at 7,811 complaints; FSSAI Food Safety Connect portal 2024-25: 7,705 adulteration complaints, 1,753 (22%) unresolved as of reporting; Storyboard18, Medical Dialogues, Newsgram reporting (2024-2026)

**The Problem:** A grocery shop owner in Bengaluru — Karnataka, the state with the highest food and beverage complaint volume in India in 2024 — discovered that a batch of branded spice powder he purchased for resale contained adulterants: brick dust and artificial colour. He filed a complaint on India's National Consumer Helpline (NCH) in February 2024. He was given a docket number and told the matter would be forwarded to the company within 30 days. In August 2024, six months later, the complaint portal shows "Pending with Company." The company replied once denying any issue. NCH treated that denial as a closure of the reply loop, effectively marking the complaint as "responded to" without verifying whether the consumer's grievance was addressed. He cannot get anyone at NCH to reopen the case. When he calls the helpline (1915), he is queued, eventually answered, told the case is "forwarded for arbitration," given no date, and the call ends. He has called eight times over three months.

**What staff/org would do:** NCH routes the complaint to the company; the company denies; NCH treats the company's response as sufficient closure. No inspector is dispatched. No penalty is assessed. The helpline does not distinguish between "responded to" and "resolved."

**What the Owner does:** Explains the documented NCH limitation — company denial alone does not constitute resolution under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. Escalates to the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (CDRC), which has independent adjudication powers and can compel refunds, penalties, and corrective action. Advises filing a parallel complaint with the state food safety commissioner's office, which has enforcement authority separate from the consumer protection chain.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Six months of 'Pending with Company' with a denial response that closed your case is not a resolution — it is a documented failure of the NCH process. You have the right to escalate to a body that can actually compel an outcome, and that route is available to you now."
- Direct the consumer to: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (file online at edaakhil.nic.in; claims under Rs 50 lakh; filing fee nominal; no lawyer required for straightforward adulteration cases); FSSAI state-level food safety commissioner (separate enforcement authority from NCH); Spices Board of India (separate regulatory jurisdiction for adulterated spice products); NCH grievance redressal officer (if helpline staff are not reopening a legitimate case, escalate within the NCH hierarchy)
- Evidence to collect: original purchase invoice for the adulterated spice batch; sealed sample of the adulterated product (labelled with purchase date); photographs of the adulterated product alongside a normal sample; NCH complaint docket number and all portal status screenshots with dates; record of all NCH calls — dates, times, and what was said; company's denial response from the NCH portal; any informal or formal laboratory test report on the adulterated product
- Feedback category tag: food_adulteration / ncH_process_failure / complaint_no_resolution / consumer_helpline_wait / india

### Scenario 11-5: Food Poisoning Reported to Authorities — Formally Told the System Does Not Investigate Individual Reports (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** UK Food Standards Agency official consumer guidance, "Report a food safety or hygiene issue" (gov.uk/food-safety); Citizens Advice official guidance, "Reporting to Trading Standards" (2024); multiple UK local council published food complaint policies citing FSA guidance — Chelmsford, Derby, Surrey, BCP Council; FSA Annual Report of Incidents, Resilience and Prevention 2024/25

**The Problem:** A single mother in Manchester spent four days bedridden after eating a takeaway meal she believes caused food poisoning. She missed four days of work, losing income she cannot recover, and incurred £180 in GP and prescription costs. She called Citizens Advice for guidance. Citizens Advice directed her to Trading Standards at her local council. She filed the Trading Standards report online with her medical records, receipt, and the restaurant's name. Three weeks later she received an email stating: "Individual reports of suspected food poisoning are not routinely investigated because it is very difficult to determine which meal, from all the food eaten in the last few days, may have caused illness." Case closed. She called Citizens Advice again. The adviser said she could consult a solicitor but could not advise further. She cannot afford a solicitor. There is no other free pathway she has been told about.

**What staff/org would do:** Apply the published FSA policy — close the individual complaint without investigation. Offer no pathway for refund, medical cost recovery, or confirmation the restaurant will be inspected. Citizens Advice correctly states its scope ends at the referral stage. The system by design produces a closure outcome before a case officer reviews the specifics — the threshold for investigation is formally set above any single illness report.

**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges this policy failure and names it plainly: the formal system discriminates against lower-income consumers who have no practical access to private legal redress, while higher-income consumers can obtain accountability through solicitors. Identifies two routes the consumer was not told about: the Environmental Health Officer (EHO) at her local council, who operates independently of Trading Standards and can act on intelligence reports without the single-poisoning investigation threshold; and Small Claims Court, which allows claims under £1,000 without a solicitor at minimal filing cost.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Being told your complaint 'cannot be investigated' is not the end of the road — but it is a real policy failure that hits people without legal resources the hardest. There are two routes you were not told about, and one of them does not cost you anything."
- Direct the consumer to: Environmental Health Officer at Manchester City Council (can act on intelligence reports without individual investigation threshold — file as an intelligence report, not a standard complaint); Small Claims Court (gov.uk/make-court-claim-for-money; claims under £1,000 require no solicitor; court fee approximately £35 for claims under £300); FSA complaint about local authority enforcement failure (fsasupport@food.gov.uk — if the EHO also refuses to act on a documented illness); Citizens Advice Extra services for low-income consumers facing discrimination in public service access
- Evidence to collect: GP medical records showing illness dates and diagnosis; takeaway receipt or delivery app order record (Deliveroo/Just Eat/Uber Eats records show exact order details); all Trading Standards correspondence including the formal closure letter — this document is the key evidence of rights denial; bank statements showing income loss during illness days; any other social media reviews or complaints from the same restaurant showing a pattern
- Feedback category tag: food_poisoning / complaint_refused / institutional_discrimination / trading_standards_failure / access_to_justice / rights_violation / uk

---

### Scenario 11-6: Lead-Contaminated Children's Applesauce Pouches — Company Files Bankruptcy Before Any Refund or Medical Compensation Can Be Reached (Pattern A+E)
**Real Basis:** CDC MMWR reports, April and July 2024 — 566 confirmed, probable, and suspected cases of elevated blood lead levels in children across 44 US states, the District of Columbia, and Puerto Rico; FDA advisory on WanaBana, Schnucks, and Weis applesauce pouches; WanaBana Chapter 7 (liquidation) bankruptcy filing; Motley Rice law firm and lawsuit-information-center.com class action documentation; NBC News; North Carolina Health News investigative report; Dollar Tree distribution documentation (2023) — product sold at $1.25 per pouch at discount retailer serving lower-income communities

**The Problem:** A mother from Charlotte, North Carolina purchased WanaBana apple cinnamon applesauce pouches from Dollar Tree throughout 2023 — affordable at $1.25, marketed as a healthy snack, sold in brightly coloured packaging. In October 2023, news reports revealed the pouches contained extremely high lead levels from cinnamon sourced in Ecuador that was deliberately adulterated with lead chromate — a chemical added to increase the cinnamon's weight and sale value. Her two-year-old daughter's blood test came back at 8.5 micrograms per deciliter (CDC action level: 3.5 mcg/dL). Lead exposure at this age causes permanent neurological damage. She tried to call WanaBana to demand medical cost coverage. WanaBana did not respond to media or consumer inquiries and subsequently filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy — liquidating the company before affected families could obtain any refund, medical coverage, or accountability. There is no customer service line. Dollar Tree's representative refers her back to WanaBana. There is no entity left to call.

**What staff/org would do:** WanaBana has dissolved. Dollar Tree deflects to the manufacturer. No refund process, no medical fund, and no customer service pathway exists. The consumer receives nothing.

**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges this is among the most severe documented food safety outcomes — deliberate supply chain fraud that poisoned 566 children, sold through a discount retailer whose own investor disclosures document a customer demographic of lower-income families, compounded by corporate dissolution that removed all direct accountability. Explains that bankruptcy does not extinguish class action claims — creditors can file claims against the liquidation estate, and class action lawsuits allow families to pool claims when individual losses are insufficient to sustain solo litigation. Connects the consumer to the active class action filings and explains how to submit a creditor's claim in the WanaBana Chapter 7 proceeding.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (acute poisoning is past; ongoing concern is permanent neurological harm and long-term developmental monitoring)
- Opening reply: "I am so sorry about your daughter. What happened was caused by deliberate fraud — lead was added to cinnamon to increase its sale weight and price. The company closing does not mean you have no options. There are legal routes available through the bankruptcy court and class action process."
- Direct the consumer to: Motley Rice and other plaintiffs' firms handling WanaBana class action lawsuits (consumer can opt into existing class action or obtain independent representation); WanaBana Chapter 7 bankruptcy court creditor's claims filing (U.S. Bankruptcy Court — file before the claims bar date); local health department for ongoing free developmental lead monitoring (many US local health departments provide free lead monitoring for children under 6 with elevated blood lead); state attorney general's consumer protection office (several state AGs pursued separate enforcement action against WanaBana and Dollar Tree)
- Evidence to collect: Dollar Tree purchase receipts or credit/debit card statements showing purchase history throughout 2023; product packaging with lot numbers (FDA recall list specifies covered lots); child's blood lead test results and all medical records from 2023 onward; all developmental monitoring records; medical billing records for every lead-related appointment; documentation of WanaBana's Chapter 7 filing (publicly available via U.S. Bankruptcy Court PACER); any correspondence attempted with WanaBana or Dollar Tree
- Feedback category tag: food_safety_lead_contamination / supply_chain_fraud / company_dissolution / financial_harm / child_vulnerability / economic_discrimination / dollar_tree

### Scenario 11-7: Nine Years of Pursuing a National Consumer Forum Case — Dismissed in April 2024, No Affordable Appeal Route (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** NCDRC (National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission) ruling, April 2024, dismissing Government of India's Rs 640 crore (approximately USD 77 million) complaint against Nestle India; original FSSAI recall and nationwide ban, June 2015; FSSAI test results: lead at 17.2 ppm (permissible limit 2.5 ppm — 6.88 times above limit); undeclared MSG despite "no added MSG" labelling; 400 million packets recalled; Just-Food.com, Grokipedia, Vintage Legal, India court records

**The Problem:** A father of three from Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh served Maggi noodles to his family regularly between 2012 and 2015. After the FSSAI recall in 2015 confirmed lead levels nearly seven times the permissible limit and undeclared MSG in the "no MSG" labelled product, he gave a sworn statement supporting the government's NCDRC complaint, believing India's highest consumer protection body would obtain accountability for the millions of consumers exposed. He tracked the case for nine years. In April 2024 the NCDRC dismissed the government's complaint, ruling that post-reformulation tests showed lead within limits and that there was insufficient evidence of "actual harm" to individual consumers. When he called the NCDRC consumer helpline (1800-11-4000) to understand what happened to his case, he was told the matter was closed and he would need to consult a lawyer to challenge the ruling in the Supreme Court. He cannot afford Supreme Court litigation.

**What staff/org would do:** The NCDRC helpline staff confirm the case is dismissed and direct the consumer to seek legal counsel. They provide no information on alternative remedies, no guidance on individual filing options separate from the government case, and no referral to free legal aid. The call ends with no actionable pathway.

**What the Owner does:** Explains that the NCDRC dismissed the government's specific filing on evidentiary grounds particular to that complaint — which does not extinguish the consumer's individual claim based on documented exposure during the 2015 window, before reformulation. Individual claims in lower consumer forums carry a lower evidence threshold than the NCDRC's standards for a Rs 640 crore government action. Notes that district consumer forums allow pro se filing for claims under Rs 50 lakh and that the Supreme Court Legal Services Committee provides free legal aid for eligible consumers wishing to challenge the NCDRC ruling.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Nine years is a long time to wait, and the NCDRC dismissal is genuinely painful. But the dismissal of the government's Rs 640 crore case on specific evidentiary grounds is not the same as your individual case being over. Here is what is still available to you."
- Direct the consumer to: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission for individual filing (pro se, under Rs 50 lakh, file at edaakhil.nic.in); Supreme Court Legal Services Committee (free legal aid for eligible consumers — nalsa.gov.in); Jago Grahak Jago consumer helpline (1800-11-4000 — press for escalation beyond the standard case status update); Consumer Coordination Council and other consumer advocacy organisations who tracked the Maggi case and may provide legal solidarity support
- Evidence to collect: any proof of Maggi purchase in 2015 — receipts, photographs, or family witness accounts; medical records from 2015 if any illness was documented; the NCDRC case reference number; personal copy of the sworn statement given to the consumer advocacy group; FSSAI 2015 recall notice (publicly archived) establishing that the product was recalled for lead at 17.2 ppm
- Feedback category tag: food_safety_complaint_dismissed / institutional_refusal / nine_year_wait / consumer_protection_failure / india / nestle_maggi

### Scenario 11-8: Dairy-Free Label, Allergic Reaction, Manufacturer Insists Label Is Accurate, No Product Withdrawn (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** FDA targeted survey results, September 17, 2024 — 210 dark chocolate products labelled "dairy-free" tested; 13 (6.2%) found to contain milk allergens; FDA warning letter to Knickerbocker 365 Inc., September 30, 2024 — inadequate allergen management on shared equipment for wheat, milk, and walnut; UK Food Standards Agency Annual Report of Incidents, Resilience and Prevention 2024/25 (documents systemic allergen incident patterns); FSA official enforcement guidance on allergen labelling under UK retained food law

**The Problem:** A woman in Birmingham with a clinically confirmed milk allergy purchases dark chocolate bars labelled prominently "Dairy-Free" from a health food chain. She has bought the same product before without incident. This time, within an hour of eating two squares, she develops hives, lip swelling, and difficulty swallowing — anaphylaxis warning signs. She uses her EpiPen and attends A&E. When she recovers she emails the manufacturer with her medical records attached. Two weeks later the company replies: "Our product is manufactured on dedicated dairy-free equipment and the dairy-free declaration on our packaging is accurate. We are confident in our quality assurance procedures." She also contacts the FSA consumer team. The FSA acknowledges her complaint, notes it has been logged as a food incident report, and advises the product will be reviewed "as part of ongoing surveillance" — with no specific timeline, no product test commitment, and no product withdrawal.

**What staff/org would do:** The manufacturer deflects with a quality assurance statement and does not offer product recall or re-testing. The FSA logs the complaint as a systemic incident rather than initiating a targeted product investigation. No proactive consumer warning is issued. The product remains on sale.

**What the Owner does:** Explains that an international FDA survey published in September 2024 found 6.2% of "dairy-free" dark chocolate products in the market contained undeclared milk — meaning the label cannot be trusted at face value in this product category, and the company's quality assurance confidence is directly contradicted by documented market evidence. Notes the FSA has powers under retained UK food law to compel product sampling and withdrawal when a documented medical event is linked to a specific product. Escalates the complaint to the FSA Incident Management System with a formal request for targeted product sampling — individual documented medical events strengthen enforcement cases. Advises on the personal civil claim route under UK consumer law for mislabelling.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (acute medical emergency has passed; EpiPen used successfully, A&E attended and consumer recovered)
- Opening reply: "You are right to push back. 'Our quality assurance says it is fine' is not an acceptable response when you have medical evidence of an allergic reaction. An FDA survey published in 2024 found 6.2% of dairy-free dark chocolates in the market contain milk — this is a documented industry-wide problem, not a one-off. Here is how to force an actual investigation."
- Direct the consumer to: FSA Incident Management formal reporting (reportingform@food.gov.uk — cite the product name, batch code, and medical records for a targeted product investigation request); Trading Standards at Birmingham City Council (can compel product testing under the Food Safety Act 1990 and UK allergen labelling regulations); Anaphylaxis UK (anaphylaxis.org.uk; helpline 01252 542029 — provides consumer rights guidance specific to allergen mislabelling); personal injury solicitor experienced in allergen mislabelling claims (well-established liability area in UK courts)
- Evidence to collect: product packaging with batch code, best-before date, and "Dairy-Free" front-of-pack claim (photograph all six sides); A&E discharge summary and clinical records documenting the anaphylaxis event; photographs of the allergic reaction where possible; all correspondence with the manufacturer — especially the quality assurance denial letter; FSA complaint reference number and all FSA responses; EpiPen prescription records and usage record; purchase receipt or loyalty card record; prior purchase records showing the same product was consumed safely before (demonstrates reliance on the label)
- Feedback category tag: allergen_mislabeling / false_label / anaphylaxis / information_failure / dairy_free / food_safety / uk

### Scenario 11-9: Recalled Product Still on Shelf — Grocery Store Hotline Has No Information, Consumer Left Without Answer (Pattern B+D)
**Real Basis:** U.S. PIRG Education Fund "Food Recall Failure" report (2022) — 84% of the 26 largest US grocery chains received a failing grade on recall notification; 80% had no publicly documented consumer notification process; no chain provided in-store information on where recall notices are posted; Aldi, Publix, and Walmart named as failing chains; PIRG "Food for Thought 2024" follow-up report confirming: "No one has to contact grocery stores or restaurants. No one has to notify consumers."

**The Problem:** A consumer at a major US supermarket chain (rated failing by PIRG) receives a news alert on her phone: a ready-to-eat deli product she buys regularly has been recalled for Listeria contamination. She is standing in the store at that moment. She walks to the deli counter and asks the staff member if the recalled product is on the shelves. The staff member says she has heard nothing about a recall and sends her to customer service. Customer service contacts a supervisor, who also has no recall notification. The supervisor suggests she call the corporate customer service number. She calls from the parking lot — 14 minutes on hold. A representative answers and tells her "recalls are handled by the manufacturers" and that the store has no system to tell her whether her specific store stocked the specific lot numbers in the recall. She is told to check the FDA recall database herself. She does. The FDA database lists the manufacturer's recall notice. It does not tell her which stores received the recalled product.

**What staff/org would do:** No store in the chain has a documented internal process for notifying customers who bought recalled products — consistent with the PIRG finding that 80% of major chains lack any documented process. The corporate call centre representative deflects to the manufacturer. No supervisor with specific product knowledge is escalated to. The consumer leaves the store without knowing whether the product she was about to purchase is safe.

**What the Owner does:** Confirms the recall is real, provides the specific product name and lot numbers from the current FDA recall notice, and confirms this is an active Listeria risk. Notes that the structural failure the consumer experienced is documented — the PIRG 2024 report explicitly states "No one has to contact grocery stores or restaurants. No one has to notify consumers." Advises how to report both the product concern and the store's lack of recall response to the FDA, and how to request a full purchase history from the chain's loyalty card system to identify any previously purchased recalled items.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no illness yet; consumer is acting proactively)
- Opening reply: "You did exactly the right thing, and the system failed you at every step. Eight out of ten major US grocery chains have no documented process for notifying customers about recalled products — this is a structural gap, not an isolated mistake. Here is how to get the information you need and report the failure."
- Direct the consumer to: FDA recall database (fda.gov/safety/recalls-market-withdrawals-safety-alerts) for specific lot numbers; FDA Consumer Complaint Coordinator for the region (to report recalled product remaining on shelves — this triggers a compliance follow-up); USDA-FSIS hotline 1-888-MPHotline (1-888-674-6854) for meat and poultry recalled items; PIRG "Food for Thought 2024" report (pirg.org) — if the consumer wishes to pursue public advocacy pressure on the chain's recall notification failure
- Evidence to collect: screenshot of the FDA or FSIS recall notice with lot numbers and recall date; dated written record of the in-store and phone interactions (time, hold duration, what each staff member said); photograph of the product on shelf during the recall period if accessible; loyalty card or credit/debit card purchase records showing any prior purchases of the recalled product; name of the store location, address, and corporate customer service reference number if given
- Feedback category tag: product_recall / call_center_failure / consumer_notification_void / retail_accountability / listeria / food_safety / usa

### Scenario 11-10: "Safe" Cookie Contains Undeclared Peanuts — Company Had Six Months' Notice, Consumer in Anaphylaxis (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** NBC News, ABC News, Washington Post, Food Safety News reporting on the death of Orla Baxendale, January 11, 2024; FDA warning letter to Stew Leonard's Holdings LLC, November 18, 2024; Cookies United (Islip, NY) recipe change from soy nuts to peanuts, July 2023; Stew Leonard's continued selling old-labelled product until December 31, 2023; recall issued January 23, 2024 — 12 days after Baxendale's death; wrongful death lawsuit filed 2024; approximately 500 packages sold under the false label

**The Problem:** A woman with a documented severe peanut allergy has purchased Vanilla Florentine Cookies from Stew Leonard's for two years without incident. She checks every label carefully as a matter of survival — the label clearly lists "soy nuts," which she can safely eat. In January 2024 she eats several cookies at home. Within minutes she is in anaphylaxis. She uses her EpiPen. An ambulance is called. In hospital she is stabilised. When she contacts Stew Leonard's customer service after discharge to ask what happened, she is told the cookies are "clearly labelled" and that the company "takes allergen safety very seriously." She is not told that the manufacturer, Cookies United, changed the recipe from soy nuts to peanuts in July 2023. She is not told that Stew Leonard's was notified by the supplier in July 2023 that new peanut labels were being printed. She is not told that Stew Leonard's continued selling the old "soy nuts" labelled product for six months. She is not told that another woman with a peanut allergy died from this same cookie on January 11, 2024. She is given a case number and told someone will follow up within five business days.

**What staff/org would do:** Read from the label that was on the product at time of purchase — which states "soy nuts." Provide scripted allergen safety assurance language. Not disclose what the company knew about the ingredient change since July 2023. Not disclose the January 11 death of Orla Baxendale. Issue a case number with a five-day follow-up window and potentially not follow up.

**What the Owner does:** Immediately establishes medical safety before anything else. Once confirmed stable, explains the full documented timeline: the recipe changed in July 2023, the company received supplier notification that month, the old-labelled product continued to be sold for six months, and the FDA warning letter issued November 18, 2024 formally documents the company's prior knowledge of the discrepancy. Explains that a recall issued on January 23, 2024 — 12 days after a consumer death — confirms peanuts were present in the product throughout the sale window. Urges the consumer to seek a food safety attorney because the FDA warning letter and regulatory record constitute a strong evidentiary foundation for a personal injury claim.

**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "First: are you breathing normally? If you have any remaining throat tightness, difficulty breathing, or dizziness, call emergency services immediately — anaphylaxis can return. If you are stable: what happened to you was not a labelling accident. The company had six months of notice that this cookie contained peanuts and did not update the label or pull the product. You have a right to know this and to pursue legal accountability."
- Direct the consumer to: Emergency services (911 or local equivalent) if any symptoms persist or return — anaphylaxis can be biphasic; FDA MedWatch to report the adverse allergen event (fda.gov/safety/medwatch); Stew Leonard's FDA warning letter (publicly accessible, November 18, 2024 — documents the company's prior knowledge as the primary evidentiary record); personal injury or food safety attorney (class action and wrongful death suits were filed in 2024; the regulatory record substantially supports individual claims); FARE — Food Allergy Research and Education (foodallergy.org) for consumer rights guidance and legal referrals specific to allergen mislabelling
- Evidence to collect: the product packaging with the old "soy nuts" label — this is the primary evidence of mislabelling and must be preserved; hospital and A&E admission records showing the anaphylaxis event, exact date, and treatment; all correspondence with Stew Leonard's including the case number and any follow-up; purchase receipts showing this product was purchased repeatedly over two years (establishes documented reliance on the label); doctor's records confirming the pre-existing severe peanut allergy diagnosis; EpiPen prescription history; any prior purchase records of this specific cookie without incident
- Feedback category tag: allergen_mislabeling / undeclared_allergen / anaphylaxis_emergency / company_prior_knowledge / false_label / food_safety_urgent / vulnerability / usa

## 12. Electronics & Technology

### Scenario 12-1: Phone Caught Fire During Documented Recall — Carrier Refused to Process Claim (Pattern B + E)
**Real Basis:** CPSC official recall notices for Samsung Galaxy Note 7 (September and October 2016); NPR reporting "The Troubled Galaxy Note 7 Leaves Some Samsung Customers Frustrated" (September 2016); 92 US reports of overheating batteries, 26 burn injuries, 55 property damage incidents including fires in cars and a garage; consumers documented being transferred between AT&T, Samsung, and Best Buy across five or more visits with no resolution
**The Problem:** A consumer's Samsung Galaxy Note 7 has caught fire inside their car. The CPSC recall was already in effect. They called AT&T's support line and were transferred three times — to Samsung, back to AT&T, then to Best Buy — before the call dropped. The car interior is damaged. They do not know how to file for property damage compensation and cannot get a straight answer from any party.
**What staff/org would do:** Transfer the caller between the carrier, Samsung, and the retailer indefinitely, each directing them to "the other party." Eventually offer a basic phone exchange form while ignoring property damage to the vehicle entirely, or suggest the consumer file a personal insurance claim.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately confirm the consumer's physical safety, acknowledge this as a documented recall incident with legal remedies specifically covering property damage, and give them a direct path to the CPSC claim process — not the carrier's standard exchange queue.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "First — are you and anyone with you physically safe right now? What happened to your phone is connected to a federal product recall, which means you have a legal right to full compensation — including damage to your car — not just a replacement handset. Let me get you to the right place directly."
- Direct to: CPSC official recall page (cpsc.gov) and CPSC property damage claim process under the recall; Samsung's dedicated recall line (separate from standard consumer support); FTC complaint portal (reportfraud.ftc.gov) if the carrier is actively obstructing a valid recall claim; document the car fire separately if the vehicle was damaged while parked
- Evidence: Photograph the burned phone and car interior damage before any cleanup; preserve the original purchase receipt, box, and IMEI; save all call logs including dates, hold times, and names of every representative spoken to; file a formal CPSC incident report at cpsc.gov/report documenting both injury and property damage separately
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Battery Fire / Recall Obstruction

### Scenario 12-2: Authorized Repair Technician Stole Explicit Personal Photos and Posted Them Online (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Apple/Pegatron Technology Services Inc. case — a 21-year-old consumer sent her iPhone to Apple's official subcontractor PTSI at its Elk Grove, California facility for a screen repair (January 2016); technicians employed through a third-party staffing vendor stole explicit personal photos and video and posted them to the victim's Facebook page; Apple reportedly settled for millions of dollars; case only became public in 2019 through unrelated insurance litigation; reported by iFixit and Tom's Guide
**The Problem:** A consumer sent their iPhone to Apple's official repair service for a cracked screen. Three days after getting the phone back, friends are calling to say private photos have appeared on their social media account. The photos were stored only on the device. The consumer has just understood what has happened and is in shock.
**What staff/org would do:** Apple standard support would suggest the social media account was hacked by an external actor, deny any possibility of data access during a screen repair, and offer no escalation beyond a standard tech support ticket — deflecting responsibility entirely to a third-party staffing vendor with no direct consumer contact.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the full severity without minimization, treat this immediately as a potential criminal offence requiring law enforcement involvement, and help the consumer secure evidence before any further harm or deletion occurs.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "I am very sorry this has happened to you. What you are describing is a serious violation — potentially a criminal offence — and you deserve immediate, concrete support. Before anything else: can you take a screenshot of the unauthorized post with the timestamp visible, before removing it? That screenshot is your evidence."
- Direct to: Local law enforcement for a criminal complaint regarding theft and unauthorized distribution of private material; FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (ic3.gov) for digital privacy crimes; Apple's formal legal escalation team in writing — not standard support — citing the PTSI precedent specifically; a consumer rights attorney if seeking civil remedy (the original victim reportedly recovered millions)
- Evidence: Screenshot every unauthorized post before deletion, capturing timestamps; preserve all repair receipts, shipping tracking records, and any Apple device tracking documentation; request in writing from Apple the complete chain of custody for the device and the names of every technician who handled it
- Feedback category tag: Data Privacy / Staff Misconduct / Authorized Repair Breach

### Scenario 12-3: RAM Sold at Advertised Speed Does Not Work at That Speed — Support Deflects to BIOS (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Corsair Gaming $5.5 million class action settlement (preliminary approval July 2, 2025; claim deadline October 28, 2025) — DDR4 and DDR5 memory kits advertised as running at stated speeds without requiring manual XMP or EXPO BIOS profile activation, a configuration step not disclosed at point of sale; reported by Tom's Hardware and ClassAction.org; consumer forum records document Corsair support directing consumers to BIOS tutorials rather than acknowledging the misrepresentation
**The Problem:** A consumer purchased a Corsair DDR5 memory kit labelled "6000MHz" for a new gaming build. After installation, the system runs at 4800MHz — the JEDEC default — because XMP/EXPO profiles must be manually enabled in the BIOS, a fact not mentioned in advertising or packaging. Corsair customer support told them to "enable XMP in your BIOS" and closed the ticket. When the consumer enabled XMP, the system became unstable and crashed. The support case is now closed and the consumer is being asked to pay for return shipping.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the consumer to BIOS configuration guides, suggest their motherboard may be incompatible, decline any refund on the grounds that "the product physically functions," and close the ticket.
**What the Owner does:** Tell the consumer this is a documented and settled marketing misrepresentation affecting their exact product, explain their right to participate in the class action, and identify their parallel path to a retailer refund under "not as described."
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "This is not a user error — this is a settled legal dispute. Corsair has agreed to a $5.5 million class action settlement covering exactly this issue: DDR4 and DDR5 kits advertised at speeds that only activate after BIOS changes that were never disclosed in the marketing. You may be entitled to compensation, and you have a right to a refund from the retailer regardless."
- Direct to: Corsair class action settlement claim page (via ClassAction.org — claim deadline October 28, 2025); the retailer for a refund under "not as described" consumer rights; FTC complaint portal (reportfraud.ftc.gov) if Corsair continues to deflect
- Evidence: Keep the original packaging showing the advertised speed prominently; take a screenshot of the BIOS screen showing the actual clock speed at boot before any XMP changes are applied; save all Corsair support chat logs and the closed ticket reference number; retain the purchase receipt
- Feedback category tag: Product Misrepresentation / Advertised Performance / Class Action Eligibility

### Scenario 12-4: Repair Shop Accessed Personal Files During a Battery Replacement — History Was Cleared to Cover It (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** University of Guelph study "No Privacy in the Electronics Repair Industry" (ArXiv preprint, November 2022) — 6 of 16 repair technicians (37.5%) accessed customer data without permission during repairs requiring no system access; 2 technicians copied data to external devices; 3 deleted Windows Quick Access and Recently Accessed Files logs in apparent attempts to cover their activity; none of the 18 shops posted any privacy notice; reported by The Register and The Globe and Mail
**The Problem:** A Canadian consumer dropped off their laptop at a local repair shop for a battery replacement — a job that requires no access to the operating system. After getting the laptop back, they noticed Windows Quick Access history had been cleared and found an unfamiliar USB device listed in Windows' device history. Personal documents and financial files were on the machine. No privacy terms or conditions were ever provided by the shop.
**What staff/org would do:** The shop would deny any data access, claim the history was "cleared as a standard part of the service process," provide no explanation for the unknown USB device entry, and offer no further avenue — no privacy policy exists to hold them to.
**What the Owner does:** Validate the consumer's concern as well-founded based on documented research patterns, explain exactly what the signs indicate, and guide them to preserve evidence and report to the appropriate authority before logs are overwritten.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "Your concern is completely founded. A peer-reviewed Canadian study found that 37.5% of repair technicians accessed customer data without permission during jobs that required no system access — and deliberately deleting Windows access history was one of the documented methods used to cover those tracks. What you have described matches this pattern exactly. Here is what to do before any remaining logs are lost."
- Direct to: Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre (antifraudcentre.ca) to report the incident; Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (priv.gc.ca) for a formal privacy complaint against the repair business; local police if financial data was on the machine
- Evidence: Before touching the laptop further, open Windows Event Viewer and check the System log for USB device connection events with timestamps; take a screenshot of the Quick Access history (or its absence) and the device manager's USB device history; keep the repair receipt showing the work order was a battery replacement only — it establishes no data access should have occurred
- Feedback category tag: Data Privacy / Repair Shop Unauthorized Access / Evidence Preservation

### Scenario 12-5: New Phone Developed a Display Fault After Four Days — Authorized Service Centre Refused Replacement (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Delhi District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission-I, Case CC/282/2024 — Samsung Galaxy A35 purchased March 22, 2024; horizontal line appeared across display March 25 (three days later); Samsung authorized service centre refused DOA replacement citing a missing IMEI sticker despite the IMEI being clearly visible on the phone's own screen (dial *#06#) and printed on the original box; Commission ruled this constituted unfair trade practice and deficiency in service; ordered full refund of ₹30,999 plus ₹25,000 in compensation and legal expenses (ruling October 2025, cited by IndiaLaw.in)
**The Problem:** A consumer in India bought a new Samsung smartphone four days ago. A horizontal line now runs across the display — a clear manufacturing defect. They visited the Samsung authorized service centre and asked for a replacement under the Dead on Arrival (DOA) policy. The representative refused, citing a missing IMEI sticker on the back of the handset — even though the IMEI is visible on the phone's own screen and printed on the original box. The consumer is being sent away with a defective phone and no remedy.
**What staff/org would do:** Insist that the missing sticker voids the DOA claim, offer a paid repair as the only alternative, and give no written documentation of the refusal — only a verbal denial with no further escalation path offered.
**What the Owner does:** Tell the consumer that this exact refusal has already been ruled an unfair trade practice by the Delhi Consumer Commission, give them the precise documentation to collect before leaving the service centre, and direct them to file.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "Do not accept this refusal. The Delhi Consumer Commission issued a ruling on a case identical to yours — Samsung A35, display defect within days of purchase, refusal citing a missing IMEI sticker — and found Samsung's position to be an unfair trade practice. The IMEI is verifiable on your own screen and your box. The service centre's refusal is not legally defensible. Here is what to collect right now before you leave."
- Direct to: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (file at your nearest district forum — you can claim refund, compensation, and legal costs); National Consumer Helpline at 1800-11-4000 (free, open 8am to 8pm) for immediate guidance; consumerhelpline.gov.in for online complaint filing in regional languages
- Evidence: Dial *#06# on the phone to display the IMEI on screen and photograph it; photograph the IMEI on the original box; photograph the display defect clearly under good light; ask the service centre representative to give their refusal in writing — if they decline, note the exact date, time, representative's name, and the words used verbatim before leaving
- Feedback category tag: DOA Refusal / Warranty Rights / Consumer Commission

### Scenario 12-6: Six Weeks of Unfulfilled Callbacks for a Defective Laptop; Different Agent Every Call (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** India National Consumer Helpline (NCH) — monthly complaint registrations rose from 37,062 (2017) to 1,70,585 (2025); inbound calls reached 1,55,138 per month (December 2024); electronics purchased online identified as a major complaint cluster driving this volume; NCH introduced AI-enabled sector-wise grievance analysis in 2024; Government of India Press Information Bureau (PIB) official releases and NCH portal (consumerhelpline.gov.in); the NCH functions as the last-resort escalation channel after corporate call centers fail to resolve
**The Problem:** An Indian consumer purchased a laptop online and received a unit with a completely non-functional keyboard. They filed a complaint with the e-commerce platform six weeks ago. Every time they call the platform's helpline — four calls in six weeks, each with 30 to 45 minutes on hold — they reach a different agent who has no record of previous interactions. Each agent promises a callback within 48 hours that never arrives. The laptop remains unusable and the consumer feels completely stuck.
**What staff/org would do:** Acknowledge the complaint number, promise a callback, log nothing with continuity across agents, and repeat the cycle — while the warranty period quietly counts down to expiry.
**What the Owner does:** Break the loop by providing a formal escalation pathway with legal enforcement consequences, so the company faces consequences for non-response rather than suffering none.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "Six weeks of unfulfilled callbacks from different agents with no case continuity is not a coincidence — it is a pattern. You have a right to resolution under India's Consumer Protection Act 2019, and escalating to the National Consumer Helpline gives your complaint legal weight the company cannot simply ignore. Here is the route with actual enforcement power."
- Direct to: National Consumer Helpline 1800-11-4000 (free, 8am to 8pm, available in regional languages); consumerhelpline.gov.in for online complaint filing; e-Daakhil portal (edaakhil.nic.in) to file directly with the Consumer Commission if the company does not respond meaningfully within 30 days of the NCH complaint
- Evidence: Write down the date, call duration, agent name or ID if given, and the exact promise made during every call to date; save all order confirmation emails, delivery records, complaint reference numbers, and any written communication from the platform; photograph the keyboard defect clearly
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure / No Resolution / E-Commerce Electronics

### Scenario 12-7: Fitbit Call Center Said Warranty Rights Expired on a Replacement Device — That Statement Is Illegal (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v. Fitbit LLC — AU$11 million penalty ordered by the Federal Court of Australia (December 2023); Fitbit admitted that call center staff told 40 consumers their replacement devices carried no warranty rights because the "original purchase date" had passed; 18 further consumers were falsely told refunds required return within 45 days and only from Fitbit's own online store; both sets of statements were false under Australian Consumer Law; consumers incurred expense purchasing new devices because they believed their rights had expired; ACCC official media release and Gadget Guy AU reporting
**The Problem:** An Australian consumer's Fitbit replacement device — originally issued because their first device was defective — has now also failed. They called Fitbit customer service and were told: "Your device does not meet the requirements for a replacement based on the original purchase date." The representative stated this confidently and with finality. This statement is false under Australian Consumer Law. The consumer now believes they have no rights remaining and is about to purchase a new device at full price.
**What staff/org would do:** Repeat the original-purchase-date policy, offer a discounted upgrade as "a goodwill gesture," and close the case — the script is designed to end with a denial, and the majority of consumers accept it as authoritative.
**What the Owner does:** Tell the consumer directly and clearly that the statement they were given is both false and has been penalised by law, explain their actual statutory rights, and provide the direct ACCC enforcement path.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "What the Fitbit representative told you is false — and it is not a misunderstanding. Fitbit was ordered to pay AU$11 million by the Australian Federal Court for telling consumers exactly what you were just told. Under Australian Consumer Law, your consumer guarantee rights apply to each replacement device from the date it was supplied to you — not from the date you bought the original. You do not need to buy a new device."
- Direct to: ACCC consumer complaints portal (accc.gov.au/consumers/consumer-rights-and-guarantees); your state Fair Trading office (e.g. NSW Fair Trading, Consumer Affairs Victoria) for a formal complaint with state-level enforcement power; if Fitbit continues to refuse, a consumer rights attorney can pursue the matter citing the Federal Court precedent
- Evidence: Write down the exact words the representative used and the time of the call; if a follow-up email was sent, save it; keep all documentation for both the original device and the replacement — purchase receipts, any warranty correspondence, and both devices' serial numbers
- Feedback category tag: Consumer Rights Violation / Call Center False Information / Warranty Denial

---

### Scenario 12-8: Counterfeit Laptop Charger Overheated and Started to Melt — Marketplace Seller Has Disappeared (Pattern C + B)
**Real Basis:** UK Trading Standards Operation Furlong (2021–2022) — 30,000 counterfeit laptop chargers seized by Redbridge and London Trading Standards, 48 high-risk failure reports, counterfeit fuses found to contain no ballast whatsoever; Electrical Safety First testing — 397 of 400 (99.25%) counterfeit chargers failed UK electrical safety tests; genuine laptop charger contains 60+ safety components vs. approximately 25 in a counterfeit; UK Government publication "Trading Standards Successes 2021–2022" (GOV.UK) and Electrical Safety First charity testing data
**The Problem:** A UK consumer bought a replacement laptop charger from a third-party seller on an online marketplace at a significant discount. Last night the charger became extremely hot, the plastic casing began to melt, and a burning smell filled the room. They unplugged it. This morning they tried to contact the seller for a refund but the marketplace listing has vanished and the seller account is inactive. They are asking whether they can just buy another cheap charger.
**What staff/org would do:** The marketplace platform instructs the consumer to "contact the seller directly" — but the seller account no longer exists. Platform policy offers no expedited resolution for disappeared third-party sellers, and the standard resolution window may not have opened yet.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately treat this as a continuing fire safety risk, advise the consumer not to use any unverified replacement, and direct them to report the product and pursue their refund through the two channels still available to them: the platform's buyer protection scheme and their bank.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "Do not plug in any replacement until you have a genuine charger from an authorised source. A melting charger is a fire and electric shock hazard — UK Trading Standards investigations found counterfeit laptop chargers sold online in London that contained fuses with no safety ballast at all. You need to act on three things right now: your immediate safety, getting your money back, and reporting the product."
- Direct to: Report the product immediately to the Office for Product Safety and Standards (OPSS) at gov.uk/product-safety-concerns; contact the marketplace platform's buyer protection team for a refund (most major platforms have policies covering non-delivery or unsafe goods even when sellers disappear); raise a chargeback with your bank or card provider — Section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act applies if paid by credit card over £100; report to Citizens Advice (0808 223 1133) which forwards to Trading Standards; purchase a genuine replacement only from the laptop manufacturer's website or a UK-authorised retailer
- Evidence: Do not dispose of the charger — it is physical evidence; photograph the melted casing and any markings or logos; check for a CE or UKCA mark (counterfeits typically lack these or display poorly reproduced versions); save the order confirmation email and any marketplace seller details before the listing disappears entirely
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Electronics / Fire Hazard / Disappeared Marketplace Seller

---

### Scenario 12-9: Paid for a Genuine Apple Charger at a Shop — Received a Counterfeit; Shop Refuses Any Refund (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Southwark Council Trading Standards prosecution of Safi Electronics and Communications Ltd., Rye Lane, Peckham, London — 631 counterfeit Apple chargers discovered (131 on display, 500 in concealed storage); criminal conviction under the Electrical Equipment (Safety) Regulations 1994 and the Trade Marks Act 1994; Trading Standards UK newsroom (CTSI), 2017; Electrical Safety First — 18 million UK adults (34% of the adult population) reported purchasing counterfeit or substandard electrical goods online or in shops
**The Problem:** A UK consumer purchased what appeared to be a genuine Apple charger from a mobile phone accessories shop in South London. At home, a friend with an authentic charger noticed the Apple logo looked slightly wrong, the cable felt different, and the plug pins were not the right weight. They returned to the shop and asked for a refund. The shopkeeper told them "all sales are final" and insisted the charger is "genuine, just from a different batch." The consumer paid £25 and is being turned away.
**What staff/org would do:** Deny the product is counterfeit, cite a "no returns" policy with confidence, and attempt to end the conversation quickly — relying on the consumer's uncertainty about their legal rights.
**What the Owner does:** Inform the consumer that no "no returns" policy can override the Consumer Rights Act 2015 when goods are not as described, explain that selling a counterfeit as genuine is a criminal matter, and give them the two parallel routes: refund and Trading Standards report.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening: "A shop cannot enforce a 'no returns' policy when the product is not what it was described to be. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 you have an absolute right to a full refund within 30 days for goods not as described — and selling a counterfeit product as a genuine branded item is a criminal offence under UK trade marks law. You have two routes running simultaneously: your refund and a Trading Standards report."
- Direct to: Citizens Advice consumer helpline (0808 223 1133) — they pass the complaint directly to Trading Standards for enforcement, which can prosecute the retailer; raise a chargeback with your bank or card provider if paid by debit card, or invoke Section 75 of the Consumer Credit Act if paid by credit card; report the product to OPSS (gov.uk/product-safety-concerns) as an unsafe counterfeit electrical item
- Evidence: Keep the charger and any packaging — do not return it to the shop; photograph the product alongside an official Apple charger image from apple.com showing the differences; save the receipt; note the exact shop name, address, and the date of purchase and the date and words of the refusal; photograph the shop exterior if you can
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Product / Refusal of Refund / Trading Standards

### Scenario 12-10: Phone Battery Purchased at a Market Stall Is Now Swelling — Consumer Does Not Know It Is Counterfeit (Pattern C + B)
**Real Basis:** UK Trading Standards Operation Altham (2021–2022) — Greenwich Trading Standards and the Redbridge Regional Investigation Team seized large quantities of counterfeit phone batteries and unsafe laptop chargers from multiple premises in Woolwich and Ilford (London); products confirmed counterfeit and non-compliant with UK electrical safety standards; prosecution pursued; UK Government publication "Trading Standards Successes 2021–2022" (GOV.UK); Electrical Safety First — 397 of 400 counterfeit batteries and chargers failed UK electrical safety tests; 18 million UK adults purchased counterfeit electrical goods
**The Problem:** A UK consumer bought a replacement phone battery from a market stall in East London for considerably less than the manufacturer's own part. It seemed to work for two weeks. The back of the phone is now being physically pushed apart by a visibly swollen battery. The consumer does not know the battery is counterfeit, does not recognise swelling as a fire hazard, and is asking whether they should buy another cheap battery to swap it out.
**What staff/org would do:** Not applicable — there is no support channel for a market stall vendor. The seller is untraceable, there is no receipt, and no route back to whoever supplied the product exists.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately treat this as a fire and explosion safety emergency, explain clearly what lithium-ion swelling means and why the phone must not be used or charged, and give step-by-step instructions for safe handling and disposal before any harm occurs.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "Stop using the phone immediately and do not charge it. A swelling lithium-ion battery is a fire and explosion risk — this is called thermal runaway, and it can escalate without warning. Do not put it in a pocket, place it near anything flammable, leave it on a soft surface, or put it in general household waste. Here is exactly what to do right now."
- Direct to: If the battery is hot, emitting a smell, or any smoke is visible — call 999 immediately and move away from the device; for safe disposal when it is not actively dangerous, contact your local council's household hazardous waste service or take the device to a Currys, Argos, or council recycling point that accepts lithium batteries; report the market stall seller to Citizens Advice (0808 223 1133) which forwards to Trading Standards; report the unsafe product to OPSS at gov.uk/product-safety-concerns; purchase a genuine replacement battery only from the phone manufacturer's official service centre or an authorised retailer
- Evidence: Photograph the swelling visible on the device without applying any pressure to the battery; save any transaction record from the market stall — a bank payment, text message to the seller, or physical receipt; note the market location, stall description, and approximate date of purchase as specifically as possible for a Trading Standards report
- Feedback category tag: Counterfeit Battery / Thermal Runaway / Fire Safety Emergency

Industry ID: 12

## 13. Transport / Public Transit

### Scenario 13-1: Flight Cancellation Crisis — Call Center Inaccessible for Days (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** US Department of Transportation enforcement order, December 2023 — Southwest Airlines $140M civil penalty for 2022 holiday meltdown; DOT named call center failure as a standalone consumer protection violation affecting hundreds of thousands of passengers; NPR; CNN Business.
**The Problem:** The passenger's flight was cancelled during a mass operational meltdown. They have been on hold for over 4 hours trying to rebook through the airline's call center. When a call finally connects, the agent cannot access the rebooking system and delivers a scripted apology with no actionable options. The passenger is stranded at the airport with no confirmed route home, no hotel voucher, and no information about when any onward flight exists.
**What staff/org would do:** Transfer the call to another queue, offer a future travel voucher, and tell the passenger to check the airline app — which is also non-functional due to the volume of simultaneous requests across hundreds of thousands of affected passengers.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the stranding situation as urgent. Confirms the passenger's rights under DOT rules: full refund or rebooking at no charge on the next available flight on any carrier; reimbursement of reasonable meals and accommodation if the cancellation was within airline control. Advises documenting all out-of-pocket expenses and directs to the DOT complaint portal if the airline fails to provide options.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a serious situation and I want to help you get through it. Being stranded with no rebooking path is not acceptable — here is exactly what you are owed right now and how to get it, even if the call center remains unreachable."
- Who to direct to: US DOT Aviation Consumer Protection (airconsumer.dot.gov); airline's airport duty manager in person rather than the call center queue; DOT complaint form at transportation.gov/airconsumer; DOT consumer hotline 202-366-2220
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the cancellation notice with timestamp; receipts for all expenses incurred (hotel, meals, ground transport); record of all call attempts with start and end times; any agent names or reference numbers received; note if the airline's app or website was inaccessible and when
- Feedback category tag: Emergency / Call Center Failure / Flight Cancellation / Consumer Rights

### Scenario 13-2: Wheelchair Destroyed by Airline — 72 Hours, No Loaner, No Contact (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** US DOT $50M landmark penalty against American Airlines, October 2024, for mishandling 10,760+ wheelchairs and mobility scooters between 2019 and 2023; DOT enforcement order; NPR; Paralyzed Veterans of America formal complaints; The Century Foundation "Trips Not Taken" report documenting 227-day replacement waits.
**The Problem:** A passenger with a physical disability arrived at baggage claim to find their power wheelchair returned with a buckled frame and broken joystick — completely unusable. The baggage agent photographed it, issued a damage report reference number, and said someone would "be in touch." It has now been 72 hours. No contact has been made. The passenger has no mobility device and cannot perform daily activities. Calls to the general baggage line result in transfers between baggage, disability services, and customer relations, with each department having no record of the other's involvement.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the passenger to wait for the claims department to assess the wheelchair's monetary value before making any offer; provide no loaner device; transfer calls between three departments, none of which can authorise a replacement or rental reimbursement.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a disability rights emergency, not a standard baggage claim. Confirms rights under the Air Carrier Access Act: the airline must repair or replace the wheelchair promptly and must provide a loaner equivalent device during any repair or replacement period. Escalates directly to DOT's aviation disability hotline and advises the passenger not to accept a monetary settlement without knowing the full replacement cost.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Losing access to your wheelchair is not a baggage inconvenience — it is a mobility emergency and you have specific legal rights that require immediate action from the airline. I am going to help you use those rights right now."
- Who to direct to: DOT Aviation Disability Hotline: 1-800-778-4838; file disability complaint at transportation.gov/airconsumer; Paralyzed Veterans of America (pva.org) for advocacy; ACAA rights at transportation.gov/airconsumer/passengers-disabilities
- Evidence to collect: Damage report reference number and issuing agent's name; photographs of all damage before anything is moved or repaired; written confirmation of when and where the damage was discovered and reported; receipts for any mobility equipment rental, accessible transport, or care costs incurred; documentation of all contact attempts with dates and outcomes
- Feedback category tag: Disability Rights / Accessibility Emergency / Baggage Mishandling / Vulnerability

### Scenario 13-3: Passenger Forcibly Removed from Overbooked Flight — Police Called Instead of Supervisor (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** United Airlines / Dr David Dao incident, 9 April 2017; Chicago O'Hare airport; Wikipedia (2017 United Express passenger removal); NBC News (confidential settlement); Fortune (DOT decision not to issue civil penalty); CNBC; The Points Guy (policy changes enacted post-incident).
**The Problem:** A passenger holding a confirmed paid ticket has been selected for involuntary bumping on an overbooked flight. They cannot give up their seat — they have a medical commitment the following morning that cannot be postponed. Gate agents offered $800 in travel vouchers to volunteers; no one accepted. Rather than increasing the offer or consulting a supervisor with authority to raise the compensation, staff have called airport police and told the passenger they must leave the aircraft or face arrest. No written statement of their DOT rights has been provided.
**What staff/org would do:** Frame the situation as a law enforcement matter and have the passenger physically removed; the initial statement later blames the passenger for being "disruptive," deflecting from the airline's failure to follow its own overbooking compensation procedures or seek a higher volunteer offer.
**What the Owner does:** Identifies this as an involuntary bumping situation governed by DOT rules. Confirms that airlines must seek volunteers before any involuntary removal; that the mandatory compensation for involuntary bumping is up to 400% of the one-way fare (capped at $1,550 under DOT rules); that the passenger must receive a written statement of their rights at the gate. Advises the passenger not to physically resist but to document everything and pursue rights after the fact, where legal standing is strong.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is happening right now and your safety matters most — do not physically resist. But document everything around you, because your legal rights here are clear and enforceable after you are safely off the aircraft."
- Who to direct to: DOT Aviation Consumer Protection complaint portal: airconsumer.dot.gov; airline's customer relations division in writing post-incident; small claims court or civil attorney if mandatory DOT compensation is not paid; DOT consumer hotline 202-366-2220
- Evidence to collect: Boarding pass and booking confirmation; note the voluntary compensation amount offered and to whom; names and badge numbers of gate agents and any law enforcement personnel; video or audio recording of events if safely possible; written note of whether a DOT rights statement was provided; medical documentation confirming why the travel date was non-negotiable
- Feedback category tag: Involuntary Denial of Boarding / Staff Misconduct / Emergency / Consumer Rights
