### Scenario 13-4: UK261 Flight Delay Compensation Rejected — Identical Claims Later Paid Under Regulator Pressure (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** UK Civil Aviation Authority enforcement action against Wizz Air, July 2023; CAA compelled re-examination of 25,486 previously rejected claims; approximately 6,000 found to be valid; £1.24M in additional refunds paid; coverage for flights to and from UK airports from 18 March 2022; Simple Flying; Travel Weekly.
**The Problem:** A passenger's flight was delayed 4 hours and 10 minutes. They submitted a claim for £350 compensation under UK261 (the UK's post-Brexit equivalent of EU261). The airline rejected the claim citing "extraordinary circumstances" — a technical fault. The passenger has called customer service twice; both times a scripted response confirmed the rejection and said further review at that level was not possible. The passenger believes the fault was within the airline's operational control, not an external event.
**What staff/org would do:** Send a template rejection letter with the phrase "extraordinary circumstances" without specifying which external event caused the delay; offer a £50 travel voucher as a goodwill gesture; confirm on any follow-up call that the decision is final.
**What the Owner does:** Explains that "extraordinary circumstances" under UK261 is a precise legal definition that excludes the vast majority of technical faults — only manufacturer defects or hidden faults that could not have been detected through scheduled maintenance typically qualify. Regulatory enforcement history, including the CAA finding that this airline wrongly rejected thousands of comparable claims, supports challenging the rejection directly.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Your frustration is completely valid. 'Extraordinary circumstances' is a specific legal term, and technical faults within the airline's maintenance control rarely qualify. Here is how to challenge this rejection — and why the odds are in your favour."
- Who to direct to: AviationADR (aviationadr.org.uk) — the UK's free alternative dispute resolution body for aviation; UK Civil Aviation Authority passenger complaint portal (caa.co.uk/passengers); Citizens Advice for UK261 rights guidance (citizensadvice.org.uk)
- Evidence to collect: Original flight booking confirmation; airline's rejection letter with the exact reason stated; flight tracking data showing actual vs scheduled departure and arrival times (flightradar24.com or similar); any airline communication identifying the specific cause of delay; dates and reference numbers for all contacts with the airline; keep the travel voucher offer in writing as evidence the airline acknowledged the delay
- Feedback category tag: Compensation Refusal / Flight Delay / UK261 Rights / Financial Harm

### Scenario 13-5: Train Delay Repay Claim — 6 Weeks, No Response, CRM System Has No Record (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Office of Rail and Road (ORR) enforcement action against West Midlands Trains, 2023; ORR finding WMT was processing only 18% of complaints within the 20-working-day legal obligation; broken CRM system forcing customers to restate cases on every contact; ORR Annual Rail Consumer Report 2023–2024; UK overall complaint handling satisfaction rate of 31% (2024–25).
**The Problem:** A rail commuter submitted a Delay Repay compensation claim for a 35-minute delay — which qualifies for 50% of the single fare under the operator's Delay Repay scheme. Six weeks have passed with no response. When they call the customer relations line, the agent has no record of the original claim and asks them to submit again. After resubmitting, a further 10 days pass with no acknowledgment. A third call produces the same request to resubmit from a different agent who also cannot find any case history.
**What staff/org would do:** Ask the customer to resubmit via the online form each time, provide a generic acknowledgment with no case reference number, and advise waiting a further 20 working days — resetting the clock with each resubmission.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that the 20-working-day legal response obligation under the National Rail Conditions of Travel has been breached multiple times. Explains the escalation path to the Rail Ombudsman, which the passenger can use after the operator fails to resolve the complaint or after 40 working days from the original submission. Advises the passenger to preserve all submission evidence as proof of the timeline.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The operator has missed its legal response deadline more than once. You do not need to keep resubmitting — you now have the right to take this to the independent Rail Ombudsman, at no cost to you."
- Who to direct to: Rail Ombudsman (railombudsman.org) — free, independent, and binding on operators; Transport Focus (transportfocus.org.uk) for passenger advocacy; ORR passenger information at orr.gov.uk/consumers
- Evidence to collect: Original claim submission — screenshot or confirmation email with the submission date; every subsequent contact with dates, agent names if given, and any reference numbers; train ticket or contactless payment record for the journey; timetabled vs actual times (available via National Rail journey history or the operator's app); any automated acknowledgment emails received at any stage
- Feedback category tag: Compensation Delay / Call Center Failure / Response Obligation Breach / Rail

### Scenario 13-6: Guide Dog User Refused by Licensed Taxi Driver — Third Refusal This Month (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** UK House of Commons Transport Committee "Access Denied: Rights versus Reality in Disabled People's Access to Transport," 2023; evidence from Stephen Anderson that guide dog user taxi refusals had doubled since the end of Covid lockdowns; Guide Dogs UK data; 70+ pieces of written evidence submitted; Committee finding that disabled access progress had gone into reverse since the pandemic.
**The Problem:** A blind passenger with a registered guide dog has just been refused by a licensed taxi driver who states he has a pet allergy. This is the third refusal the passenger has experienced in four weeks. They are now stranded and running late for a hospital appointment. The taxi company's dispatch line, when called, says they will "note it on the driver's record" and advises trying to book another cab — but makes no attempt to send an alternative vehicle and offers no acknowledgment of the legal position.
**What staff/org would do:** Log the complaint as a driver preference issue; suggest the passenger use a different service; take no immediate enforcement action and provide no written confirmation that the refusal has been recorded as a potential Equality Act breach.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an active discrimination incident and a medical appointment emergency simultaneously. Explains the legal position: refusing a guide dog user is unlawful disability discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 and a specific criminal offence under Section 168 of the Act; a driver may only be exempt if they hold a current medical exemption certificate issued by the local licensing authority — they cannot self-declare an allergy at the point of contact. Provides both immediate practical help and the reporting pathway.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is unlawful discrimination and I want to help you get to your appointment and hold this driver accountable. Here is what you are entitled to, what the driver's legal position actually is, and exactly who to call right now."
- Who to direct to: Local council taxi licensing department — report the driver and request sight of any medical exemption certificate on file; Equality Advisory and Support Service (EASS): 0808 800 0082; Guide Dogs UK advocacy team (guidedogs.org.uk/about-us/policy-campaigns); police non-emergency line 101 if the driver was abusive or threatening
- Evidence to collect: Taxi licence plate number; driver badge number if visible on the vehicle or driver; exact time, date, and location of each refusal; name or booking reference of the taxi company or app used; written record of dispatch operator's response and what action they said they would take; note whether the driver claimed to hold a medical exemption certificate
- Feedback category tag: Disability Discrimination / Service Refusal / Equality Act / Vulnerability

### Scenario 13-7: Fatal Bus Accident — Family Cannot Reach Compensation Process Across Four Departments (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Mumbai BEST (Brihanmumbai Electric Supply and Transport) bus accident, Kurla, December 2024; 7 killed, 49 injured; driver arrested for culpable homicide; BEST initial offer of ₹2 lakh per deceased family widely criticised as inadequate; Maharashtra Chief Minister separately announced ₹5 lakh from relief fund; Free Press Journal; Lok Mat Times; The News Minute.
**The Problem:** A family member of one of the victims killed in the bus accident is attempting to initiate a compensation claim. BEST's main administrative number is not being answered. When a call eventually connects after repeated attempts, the family member is transferred to the operations department, then to the legal department, then told to attend a BEST office in person. At the office, staff say the compensation scheme has not been finalised and to call back the following week. No written information about eligibility, amounts, or the process is provided.
**What staff/org would do:** Provide inconsistent information across departments, direct the family between phone lines and physical offices without resolution, and offer no written confirmation of the compensation process or timeline — leaving the family without clarity on whether to accept the ₹2 lakh administrative offer or pursue legal action.
**What the Owner does:** Provides immediate clarity on the legal landscape. Confirms that the family has two parallel paths: the BEST administrative payment (now enhanced by CM Relief Fund to ₹5 lakh) and an independent Motor Accident Claims Tribunal claim for full civil damages, which is not extinguished by accepting administrative relief. Directs to free legal aid to navigate both paths simultaneously within limitation periods.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "I am deeply sorry for what your family is going through. While no process can address your loss, you have legal rights that must be acted on now to protect your family's full entitlement. Here is how to move forward without being passed between offices."
- Who to direct to: Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) — engage a lawyer to file a claim within three years under the Motor Vehicles Act; National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) for free legal aid: nalsa.gov.in or call 15100; Maharashtra State Legal Services Authority (MSLA) for state-level representation; National Consumer Helpline: 1915 for guidance on administrative claims
- Evidence to collect: Death certificate; copy of the FIR (First Information Report) filed against the driver; all BEST correspondence with dates and reference numbers; written record of the ₹2 lakh and ₹5 lakh announcements; hospital, mortuary, and funeral expense records; documentation establishing dependents and their relationship to the deceased
- Feedback category tag: Fatal Accident / Emergency / Compensation / Vulnerability / Staff Misconduct

### Scenario 13-8: State Bus Operator Contests Child Death Award Through Successive Courts (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Supreme Court of India 2024 ruling restoring ₹8.55 lakh Motor Accident Claims Tribunal award after the Madras High Court reduced it; Tamil Nadu State Transport Corporation held liable for death of a 10-year-old child; parallel 2024 Patna High Court ruling enhancing compensation to ₹33.21 lakh with 6% interest; India Legal; Samvida Law case analysis.
**The Problem:** The parents of a child killed by a state-run bus have been awarded compensation by the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal. The state transport corporation has now appealed to the High Court, which reduced the award amount. The parents cannot afford extended litigation. When they contact the transport corporation's administrative office to ask whether any interim payment is possible while the appeal runs, they are told the matter is "before the court" and no communication is possible outside legal proceedings — effectively leaving the family without any compensation for an indeterminate period.
**What staff/org would do:** Refer all communication to the state's legal department; make no interim payment; advise the family to await the judicial process without providing any estimate of duration or any information about interim relief mechanisms available under the Motor Vehicles Act.
**What the Owner does:** Clarifies that the family is not powerless during the appellate process. The Supreme Court has consistently restored MACT awards reduced by High Courts in comparable 2024 cases. The Motor Vehicles Act provides a mechanism for interim payment pending appeal. Free legal representation is available through state legal services authorities. Accepting the administrative "wait" framing would delay legitimate relief by years unnecessarily.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The state transport corporation's position — that you must simply wait — is not your only option. The Supreme Court ruled in favour of families in exactly this situation in 2024, and you can apply for interim compensation now. Here is how."
- Who to direct to: State Legal Services Authority (SLSA) for free legal representation in High Court proceedings; District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) to apply for interim compensation under Section 168A Motor Vehicles Act; National Consumer Helpline: 1915; Supreme Court Legal Aid Committee if the matter reaches that level
- Evidence to collect: Full MACT judgment with the original award amount; High Court appeal notice and any interim orders already passed; the transport corporation's written refusal to make interim payment; all original accident documentation; school enrolment and age documentation for the child; evidence of the family's financial dependence on the deceased
- Feedback category tag: Compensation Dispute / Public Transport Liability / Escalation Refused / Transparency Failure

### Scenario 13-9: Lost Bag — Airline Offers ₹2,450, Consumer Forum Awards ₹45,000 in Equivalent Case (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Air India lost baggage consumer forum case, Mumbai District Consumer Forum, 2025; HappyFares analysis of 47 public consumer forum filings 2023–2025 finding 60% resulted in awards higher than airline's initial offer with a median escalation duration of 187 days; INGRAM / National Consumer Helpline data; pattern of airlines offering systematically below-minimum settlements at first contact.
**The Problem:** An Air India passenger's checked bag was lost on a domestic route. The airline's claims team has offered ₹2,450 as full and final settlement — approximately the cost of a single item of clothing. The bag contained electronics, professional documents, and clothing valued considerably higher. Three follow-up calls over four weeks have each produced the same scripted answer: the settlement is fixed under airline policy and cannot be changed at this level. No written explanation of how the ₹2,450 figure was calculated has been provided.
**What staff/org would do:** Maintain the ₹2,450 offer as non-negotiable, reference an internal liability cap without citing its legal basis, and advise the passenger to accept in writing or close the case — relying on the friction and unfamiliarity of escalation to prevent most passengers from pursuing the claim further.
**What the Owner does:** Explains that airline internal policy caps do not override the passenger's rights under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 or, for international flights, the Montreal Convention. The consumer forum award data shows that in 60% of equivalent baggage cases, courts awarded substantially more than the initial airline offer — the median uplift is 18 times the airline's first figure. Gives a clear, simple escalation path with realistic time expectations.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "₹2,450 is almost certainly well below your legal entitlement. Airlines rely on passengers not escalating — but consumer forums consistently award far more in identical cases, and the process is less complex than it sounds. Here is how to pursue this."
- Who to direct to: National Consumer Helpline: 1915 and INGRAM portal (consumerhelpline.gov.in) for complaint registration; District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission under the Consumer Protection Act 2019 — no lawyer required for claims under ₹50 lakh; DGCA passenger complaint portal at dgca.gov.in; for international flights, the Directorate of Air Transport
- Evidence to collect: Booking confirmation and baggage receipt/tag numbers; Property Irregularity Report (PIR) filed at the airport with date and reference number; itemised list of lost bag contents with purchase receipts, credit card statements, or estimated replacement values; all airline correspondence including the ₹2,450 offer letter; receipts for essential replacement purchases made while the bag was missing; record of all call contacts with dates and any agent reference numbers
- Feedback category tag: Baggage Loss / Financial Harm / Under-Compensation / Consumer Forum

### Scenario 13-10: Transit Card Overcharges on Public Holiday — No Proactive Refund, Form Loop Only (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Myki (Melbourne) contactless transit card system failure to apply the Public Holiday Daily Fare Cap, January 2022; passengers systematically overcharged rather than charged at the lower public holiday rate; Public Transport Victoria made no proactive remediation or outreach to affected cardholders; passengers required to identify the error individually and submit online refund forms manually; documented in Waking Up in Geelong; Wikipedia Myki criticism section; Myki history of GPS zone errors causing incorrect fare calculations.
**The Problem:** A passenger reviews their Myki account transaction history and notices they were charged the standard daily fare cap on a public holiday, when the lower public holiday cap should have applied — an overcharge of $4.60. They call the Myki contact line and are told to submit an online refund form. After submitting, they receive an automated confirmation but no further communication for three weeks. A second call produces the same instruction: submit the form and wait up to 30 days. The agent gives no information about whether this is a known system error affecting other passengers, and makes no attempt to process the refund during the call.
**What staff/org would do:** Script: redirect to the online form, quote the 30-day processing window, and provide no information about whether the overcharge was systemic or isolated — placing the entire burden of identification, documentation, and follow-up on the individual passenger.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the systemic nature of the issue. Myki has a documented history of fare cap and zone calculation errors; the public holiday cap failure in January 2022 affected multiple passengers, none of whom received proactive notification. Confirms the passenger's right to a full refund and explains that if the form process does not resolve within the stated timeframe, a formal complaint to the Public Transport Ombudsman is available at no cost.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "This is a genuine system error and you are entitled to a full refund. The form is the right first step, but here is what to do if it stalls — and why this matters beyond the $4.60 alone."
- Who to direct to: Public Transport Victoria formal complaint pathway at ptv.vic.gov.au — distinct from the refund form submission; Public Transport Ombudsman Victoria (ptovic.com.au) if PTV does not resolve within 30 days; Consumer Affairs Victoria (consumer.vic.gov.au) for systemic overcharging complaints affecting multiple passengers
- Evidence to collect: Myki account transaction history showing the public holiday date, fare charged, and the correct public holiday cap amount; screenshot of the refund form submission confirmation with submission date and time; record of all follow-up calls with dates, agent names if provided, and any reference numbers given; note any automated acknowledgment emails received at each stage and their content
- Feedback category tag: Fare Overcharge / System Error / Transparency Failure / Financial Harm

Industry ID: 13

## 14. Logistics / Supply Chain

### Scenario 14-1: Missing Freight Denied — Delivery Receipt Technicality (Pattern A/D)
**Real Basis:** BBB complaint database (bbb.org/us/ct/greenwich/profile/logistics/xpo-logistics-inc-0111-87118220); WHNT News investigative report on XPO Logistics complaints, 2021–2024; XPO Logistics accumulated 285 BBB complaints, 133 unaddressed
**The Problem:** A business owner's shipment of 2 pallets (10 items, total value $40,500) was delivered short — only 1 pallet with 2 items arrived. The driver did not disclose the shortage, so the delivery receipt was signed without exception notation. The business owner has video footage clearly showing only one pallet being offloaded. XPO Logistics has denied the claim in full on the grounds that the consignee signed the delivery receipt without noting "short," and the customer service representative says the case is closed with no further escalation available.
**What staff/org would do:** Reiterate the liability exclusion clause — "the consignee signed the delivery receipt without notation, therefore no shortage claim is valid" — and close the case without requesting or reviewing the video evidence. Offer no escalation pathway beyond the original claims department.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the significant financial loss, validates the business owner's frustration, asks for the video evidence immediately, and confirms this is not a closed matter — a carrier's liability cannot be extinguished by a procedural signature when documented countervailing evidence of shortage exists.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (serious financial harm but no immediate safety risk)
- Opening reply: "This is a serious loss and XPO's position does not hold if you have video evidence of the shortage. That footage can override a signed receipt under the Carmack Amendment — federal law governing carrier liability. Let's make sure the evidence is preserved and in the right hands immediately."
- Direct the consumer to: File a formal freight claim under the Carmack Amendment (49 U.S.C. § 14706) — the federal statute governing carrier liability for loss and damage in US interstate commerce; re-escalate in writing directly to XPO's dedicated freight claims department (separate from general customer service); file a complaint with the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) at fmcsa.dot.gov; if the claim is denied again, consider a small claims or civil court action using the video as primary evidence
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The video footage of the delivery (download immediately and back up with original timestamp metadata intact), the delivery receipt (scan both sides), the original bill of lading, invoice for all 10 items, photographs of what was actually received
- Feedback category tag: Lost Freight / Claim Wrongfully Denied / Carrier Liability / Insufficient Evidence Standard

---

### Scenario 14-2: Business Told No Compensation Exists — 4-Year Systematic Deception (Pattern A/B)
**Real Basis:** ACCC media release, December 19, 2023 (accc.gov.au); court-enforceable undertaking accepted by ACCC; Australia Post and StarTrack Express $2.9 million AUD compensation programme for approximately 11,500 affected business contract customers
**The Problem:** A small business owner sent multiple packages via Australia Post's business contract over several years. Two packages were lost and one arrived damaged. Each time the business contacted Australia Post, the customer service representative stated clearly that no compensation was payable to business contract customers — framed as a firm and absolute policy. The business accepted this and did not pursue the matter further. The business has now received a letter from Australia Post notifying them of the ACCC compensation programme and is confused, angry, and unsure what they are owed or how the process works.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the customer to a web form for the compensation programme without explaining what they are entitled to, how the compensation amount is calculated, or what the expected timeline is. No acknowledgment that the business was deliberately misinformed.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms directly that Australia Post's representatives gave the business false information over multiple years — conduct that the ACCC determined was likely deceptive under Australian Consumer Law — and that the customer is now entitled to compensation. Helps them understand their entitlement and how to claim it effectively.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You were given incorrect information by Australia Post — repeatedly, over years. The ACCC confirmed this was likely deceptive conduct under Australian Consumer Law, and the compensation programme exists precisely for businesses in your position. You have a right to claim and you should not accept a low offer without understanding the basis for the calculation."
- Direct the consumer to: Contact Australia Post directly referencing the December 19, 2023 ACCC court-enforceable undertaking and request a written breakdown of the compensation calculation; if dissatisfied with the amount offered or the process, contact the ACCC directly at accc.gov.au or 1300 302 502; for postal service disputes not resolved through Australia Post's process, contact the Commonwealth Ombudsman at ombudsman.gov.au or 1300 362 072
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Original business contract with Australia Post, tracking numbers for every lost or damaged parcel, any written, email, or chat records where Australia Post stated no compensation was payable, invoices or declared values for goods lost or damaged, records of any costs incurred as a result of the loss
- Feedback category tag: Lost Goods / Damaged Goods / Systematic Misinformation / Consumer Law Violation / Compensation Denied

### Scenario 14-3: Cargo Held at Airport — Customs Officer Demands Bribe Per Kilogram (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** CBI press release; Logistics Insider report ("CBI Arrests Mumbai Customs Official for ₹10.20 Lakh Bribery"); Deccan Herald news — CBI arrests Customs Superintendent at Air Cargo Complex, Sahar (CSMIA), Mumbai, demanding ₹10 per kg of cargo, total demand ₹10.20 lakh, August 2023
**The Problem:** An importer's time-sensitive goods — perishable components required for a manufacturing facility's production line — have been held without explanation at the Air Cargo Complex at Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport, Mumbai for three days. The Customs House Agent acting on the importer's behalf has communicated informally that a Customs Superintendent is demanding payment before the consignment will be cleared. The importer is losing approximately ₹2 lakh per day in production stoppage costs and is under pressure from their agent to pay and resolve the situation quickly.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent advises the importer to pay to avoid further delay, framing it as the only practical solution and citing "how things work here." The Customs department's official inquiry desk provides only vague responses about "procedural checks still under way" with no timeline.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately identifies this as extortion by a government official — a criminal offence under the Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 — confirms the importer is not required to pay, and provides a clear, actionable escalation path to the same authority that arrested a Sahar Air Cargo Customs Superintendent for identical conduct in August 2023.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (financial harm escalating at ₹2 lakh per day; coercion by a government official is ongoing)
- Opening reply: "This is not a legitimate customs requirement — this is extortion, and it is a criminal offence under Indian law. You are not required to pay. The Central Bureau of Investigation arrested a Customs Superintendent at this same facility in August 2023 for demanding exactly this type of payment. There is a direct path to the authority that handled that case."
- Direct the consumer to: File a complaint immediately with the CBI Anti-Corruption Branch (CBI AC) — the unit responsible for the August 2023 Sahar arrest; file simultaneously with the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) via the real-time complaint portal at cvconline.gov.in; send a written complaint (email creates a timestamped record) to the Commissioner of Customs at the Air Cargo Complex; if approached again, do not pay — any payment made after a formal complaint is logged strengthens the bribery case
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All written or WhatsApp communications from the agent quoting the demand and the rate per kilogram; the consignment documentation (airway bill, bill of lading, customs declaration, commercial invoice); a daily log of dates the cargo has been held; written calculation of daily production losses; names and designations of any officials mentioned by the agent
- Feedback category tag: Customs Extortion / Official Misconduct / Cargo Held / Bribery / Rate-Per-Kilogram Demand

### Scenario 14-4: First Class Mail Never Arrived — Legal Document, Deadline at Risk (Pattern A/D)
**Real Basis:** Ofcom press release ("Ofcom fines Royal Mail £10.5m for poor delivery performance," December 2024); Ofcom investigation into Royal Mail 2023/24 performance; Royal Mail First Class delivery rate 74.5% against 93% regulatory target; Ofcom Residential Postal Tracker 2024 (25% of residential users report delayed mail as most common issue)
**The Problem:** A consumer posted an important legal document by First Class mail on a specific date. The document never arrived. The recipient — a solicitor's office — has confirmed in writing that nothing was received. The consumer contacted Royal Mail customer service, which told them that First Class mail is untracked, no compensation is available unless the item was sent by Special Delivery, and there is nothing more Royal Mail can do. A filing deadline tied to the document's receipt is now at risk.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm the standard terms and conditions — First Class without Special Delivery is uninsured and untracked — offer a standard lost mail report form with a 15-working-day response window, and close the interaction without mentioning any further escalation route.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the urgency of the legal deadline, confirms that Royal Mail's delivery performance during this period was formally found by Ofcom to be in systemic breach of its legal obligations, and provides the specific escalation route that Royal Mail's customer service did not disclose.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (legal deadline is at risk and the document cannot simply be re-posted in time)
- Opening reply: "Royal Mail's delivery performance was formally penalised by Ofcom for exactly this type of failure — First Class delivery reached only 74.5% against a 93% legal obligation, affecting millions of items. You have escalation rights beyond their standard customer service response, and the legal deadline needs to be addressed in parallel right now."
- Direct the consumer to: Contact the solicitor's office immediately to request a deadline extension, referencing the Ofcom enforcement action against Royal Mail for 2023/24 delivery failures as documented justification; file a formal written complaint with Royal Mail (this is a mandatory first step before POSTRS will accept the case); escalate to POSTRS — the Postal Redress Service — the independent postal redress scheme at cedr.com/postrs; if the document had financial value or the deadline causes material loss, check whether home or contents insurance covers postal loss
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Certificate of posting from the Post Office counter (this proves the item was tendered for delivery), the date and Post Office branch of posting, written confirmation from the solicitor's office that nothing was received, any Royal Mail customer service reference number, documentation of the legal deadline and any consequences of missing it
- Feedback category tag: Lost Mail / Delivery Failure / Legal Deadline / Escalation Not Disclosed / Regulatory Context

### Scenario 14-5: Camera Lost by Parcel Carrier — £20 Compensation Cap, 2.5-Month Wait, Item Excluded (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Consumer complaint documentation (GetParcelRefund.com claims analysis, September 2023); Citizens Advice Parcels League Table 2025 (Evri: 2.25 stars overall, delivery problem rate 37%, accessibility score 1.6 stars — lowest of all carriers); Evri standard £20 default compensation cap; Evri complaints procedure page directing formal complaints to Resolver
**The Problem:** A consumer sent a camera via Evri. The camera was lost in transit. After waiting two and a half months for a response to their claim — during which repeated webchat contacts produced only new reference numbers and no updates — Evri informed the consumer that cameras are on its "non-compensated items" list and no compensation will be paid. The consumer had not purchased enhanced insurance at booking. The camera's replacement value is over £300. Evri's webchat subsequently closed the session before the consumer could respond.
**What staff/org would do:** Confirm that cameras are listed as non-compensable under Evri's standard terms and conditions, close the claim with reference to the policy, offer the final webchat reference number, and make no acknowledgment of the 2.5-month delay or any partial goodwill offer.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the consumer's loss, confirms that a 2.5-month claims response delay is itself an unacceptable service failure, and identifies that a blanket policy exclusion does not necessarily override all consumer rights — particularly where Evri offered insurance at booking without clearly disclosing that cameras fell within an excluded category.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "A 2.5-month wait followed by a refusal citing a policy the booking flow did not clearly disclose is not necessarily the final answer. If Evri offered insurance at the point of booking without specifically warning that cameras were excluded from cover, that non-disclosure may be challengeable under the Consumer Rights Act 2015."
- Direct the consumer to: Contact the Citizens Advice consumer helpline (0808 223 1133) for a case assessment on whether the exclusion was adequately disclosed; submit a formal complaint to Evri through Resolver (as Evri explicitly states this carries more weight than webchat) to create a documented formal complaint record; if Evri's response is unsatisfactory within 8 weeks, escalate to POSTRS; consider a Small Claims Court claim via Money Claim Online (gov.uk/make-court-claim-for-money) — claims under £10,000 are low-cost to file and courts have found against carriers with blanket exclusion lists where the item description at booking did not trigger a clear warning
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshots of every step of the Evri booking flow (particularly the insurance offer stage and any exclusion notices shown), all webchat transcripts from every contact attempt (download immediately — these expire), all three or more reference numbers in a single document, original purchase receipt or proof of value for the camera, the final Evri decision email or chat closing the claim
- Feedback category tag: Lost Parcel / Compensation Refused / Exclusion Policy / Complaint Delay / Webchat Loop

### Scenario 14-6: Fake Delivery Text — Consumer Pays "Customs Fee," Card Compromised (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** FTC Consumer Sentinel Network 2024 Data Book; FTC press release March 2025 ("New FTC Data Show a Big Jump in Reported Losses to Fraud to $12.5 Billion in 2024"); $470 million lost to text-based scams in 2024; FCC consumer alert on carrier impersonation smishing; FBI IC3 2024 Internet Crime Report
**The Problem:** A consumer received a text message appearing to come from USPS/FedEx/UPS stating their parcel was held at a sorting facility and a customs clearance fee of $3.49 was required via a link. They clicked the link, which displayed a convincing carrier-branded payment page, entered their debit card details, and paid. Within 24 hours, their card has been charged an additional $189 in unrecognised transactions from two separate merchants. A second text has now arrived from a "carrier support number" stating the parcel is still held and requesting further payment. The consumer is not sure whether they have a real package in transit or not.
**What staff/org would do:** The bank's fraud line places the consumer on hold and then instructs them to complete an online dispute form, which takes 5–7 business days to process. The real carrier's customer service confirms they do not handle scam reports and redirects the consumer to the FTC website with no further assistance.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately recognises this as an active fraud situation where the card details are compromised and further charges are possible in real time. Provides step-by-step emergency instructions prioritised by urgency.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (card details are actively compromised; unauthorised charges are ongoing; second contact from scammers increases risk of further financial loss)
- Opening reply: "Stop — do not click the second link or respond to the second text. Your card details have been stolen and need to be cancelled immediately, not just disputed. This is a documented scam that cost Americans $470 million in 2024 alone. There are clear steps to take right now, starting with your bank."
- Direct the consumer to: Call the card issuer's fraud line immediately using the number printed on the back of the physical card and specifically request card cancellation — not a dispute — to prevent further charges; report to the FTC at ReportFraud.ftc.gov (this creates an official record and helps track the scam network); file a report with the FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov; forward the original scam text to 7726 (SPAM) which reports it directly to the mobile carrier network; if a door-tag physical version was also received, report it to the US Postal Inspection Service at postalinspectors.uspis.gov
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshot the original scam text immediately before deleting it, note the exact URL of the fake site (do not revisit), note the phone number that sent the second contact, screenshot all unrecognised bank transactions with full dates and amounts, keep all legitimate order confirmation or shipping emails for comparison to distinguish real from scam communications
- Feedback category tag: Delivery Fraud / Smishing / Card Compromise / Financial Scam / Carrier Impersonation

### Scenario 14-7: Container Cargo Held at Port — "SOP" Bribe Demanded, Demurrage Accumulating (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Philippine Tribune investigative article ("Bribes, brokers and a broken customs system," August 2025); US State Department 2025 Investment Climate Report (Philippines Bureau of Customs named as one of the country's most corrupt agencies); Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index 2024 (Philippines rank 114 out of 180 countries); Philstar.com business reporting
**The Problem:** A Philippine importer of construction machinery has two container loads of equipment sitting at the port after arrival. Their licensed customs broker has communicated informally that P240,000 in "SOP" payments — unreceipted cash to Bureau of Customs officials — is required before the containers will be released for pick-up. The official BOC tracking system shows the containers as "under examination" with no scheduled completion date. Demurrage charges are accruing at P50,000 per day, creating compounding financial pressure to pay and end the situation.
**What staff/org would do:** The customs broker advises the importer to pay, citing that official channels create more complications and take far longer. The BOC's official inquiry line confirms only that the containers are "under examination" and provides no timeline or named officer responsible.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the real financial pressure from accruing demurrage but confirms this is a documented and systemic form of corruption that the importer is not legally or ethically obligated to participate in, and provides escalation routes that have produced enforcement outcomes.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (financial pressure is increasing but this is a systemic corruption pattern requiring careful documentation, not immediate payment)
- Opening reply: "What is being called 'SOP' is bribery — a criminal act — and you are not required to pay. The Bureau of Customs has been formally identified by the US State Department and Transparency International as operating with systemic corruption. Formal complaint channels exist, have produced results, and your documentation of this demand is itself valuable."
- Direct the consumer to: File a complaint with the Office of the Ombudsman (Philippines) — the constitutional body mandated to investigate and prosecute government misconduct (ombudsman.gov.ph); contact the BOC's Internal Affairs Service (IAS) directly and request the name and designation of the examining officer; contact the Presidential Anti-Corruption Commission (PACC) at pacc.gov.ph for cases involving government officials; if the broker is pressuring payment, this may itself constitute facilitation of bribery — they may be reported to the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) which licenses customs brokers
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All message or written communications from the broker quoting the SOP amount, the broker's full name and PRC accreditation number, the container tracking numbers and current system status (screenshot with date/time), the date of arrival and each subsequent day held in a written log, demurrage invoices as they accrue, copies of all import documentation (bill of lading, commercial invoice, packing list, import permit if applicable)
- Feedback category tag: Customs Corruption / Unofficial Payment Demand / Port Delay / Official Misconduct / Broker Facilitation

---

### Scenario 14-8: Parcel Complaint Loop — Three Contacts, Three Reference Numbers, No Resolution (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Citizens Advice Parcels League Table 2025 (published November 2025); Opinium Research consumer survey n=8,000 UK adults (conducted August–September 2025); 47% of UK consumers who experienced a parcel problem faced further problems when trying to complain; overall complaint satisfaction at 49%, ranging from 32% to 56% by carrier
**The Problem:** A UK consumer's parcel containing a birthday gift was left visible on a public doorstep rather than in a safe place or with a neighbour, and was subsequently stolen. The consumer has contacted the carrier's webchat three times over two weeks. Each contact generated a new reference number, asked the consumer to resubmit the same photographs and tracking information, and stated the case was "under review." No callback has been offered, no compensation decision communicated, and no timeline given. The consumer does not know whether it is the carrier or the retailer who bears responsibility, or where they can take the complaint next.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue a fourth reference number, request the same evidence again via a new web form submission, state that investigations take up to 28 working days, and provide no explanation of who decides the outcome, what criteria are applied, or how to formally escalate.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the circular complaint loop — which Citizens Advice found in 2025 affects 47% of UK consumers who try to complain — and provides a direct, linear path that breaks the cycle by converting the interaction from an informal "contact" to a formal complaint carrying specific legal obligations and timelines.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You have landed in the complaint loop that nearly half of UK parcel customers experience — three contacts, three reference numbers, and no movement. The way out is to convert this from an informal webchat contact into a formal written complaint. That changes the carrier's legal obligations and starts a clock that enables independent escalation."
- Direct the consumer to: Send a formal written complaint by email to the carrier's registered complaints address (not webchat) — use the subject line "Formal Complaint — Stolen Parcel [tracking number]" to ensure it is logged as a complaint rather than an enquiry; this starts the 8-week clock after which POSTRS (Postal Redress Service at cedr.com/postrs) can accept the case for independent adjudication; contact the Citizens Advice consumer helpline (0808 223 1133) for advice on whether the retailer (as the contract holder with the carrier) should be pursuing the claim on the consumer's behalf under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 — in many cases the retailer is responsible for resolving delivery failures, not the consumer
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All three webchat transcripts (export or screenshot each one immediately — webchat logs expire and are not accessible after session closure), all three reference numbers compiled in a single document, the original tracking record showing the delivery event and location, photographs of the doorstep or delivery location if available, purchase confirmation from the retailer showing the declared delivery address
- Feedback category tag: Complaint Loop / Delivery to Unsafe Location / Stolen Parcel / Webchat Failure / No Escalation Pathway

### Scenario 14-9: Freight Forwarder Invoices Show "Special Handling" — Suspected Bribery Pass-Through (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** SEC press release 2010-214 (November 4, 2010) — SEC charges Panalpina and six oil-and-gas companies for FCPA violations; DOJ deferred prosecution agreement with Panalpina World Transport (Holding) Ltd., November 4, 2010; combined $81.89 million penalty (DOJ $70.56M criminal + SEC $11.33M disgorgement); FCPA Stanford Law enforcement dataset ID 308 — Panalpina invoiced clients using labels including "local processing," "special intervention," and "special handling" to conceal bribe payments
**The Problem:** A compliance officer at an oil and gas company is reviewing freight forwarding invoices from their logistics provider covering an 18-month period of West African operations. Multiple invoices contain line items labelled "special handling," "local processing," and "expedited customs clearance" for which no supporting documentation exists. The amounts — ranging from $2,000 to $15,000 per shipment — do not appear in any quoted service schedule in the freight contract. When the compliance officer raised this with the logistics account manager, they were told the charges were "standard industry practice in the region" and documentation was "not available." The company's own legal exposure under the FCPA is not yet determined.
**What staff/org would do:** The logistics provider's account management team continues to deflect, cites "commercial confidentiality" for the undocumented line items, and offers only to escalate to their own commercial director — who has a direct financial interest in retaining the contract.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the compliance officer's concern as serious and legally significant, notes that the invoice label language matches exactly the concealment method documented in the Panalpina FCPA enforcement action, and provides a clear internal and external escalation path that protects the compliance officer personally while managing the company's exposure.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (not an emergency but time-sensitive — each additional invoice processed increases the company's potential FCPA exposure)
- Opening reply: "The invoice labels you have described — 'special handling,' 'local processing,' 'expedited customs clearance' with no supporting documentation — are the exact concealment language documented in the Panalpina case, which resulted in an $81.89 million DOJ and SEC enforcement action. This requires internal escalation and external legal advice immediately, not further discussion with the account manager."
- Direct the consumer/officer to: Escalate immediately and in writing to the company's General Counsel and Chief Compliance Officer — this creates a documented escalation record; engage external FCPA counsel before making any further contact with the logistics provider; consider whether voluntary disclosure to the DOJ or SEC is appropriate — the DOJ's FCPA Corporate Enforcement Policy treats voluntary disclosure as a significant mitigating factor; if internal escalation is blocked or appears compromised, the SEC whistleblower programme (sec.gov/whistleblower) provides legal protections and potential financial awards for information leading to enforcement actions over $1 million
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Every invoice containing the undocumented line items, organised chronologically by shipment and date; the original freight forwarding contract and all agreed service schedules; all written communications with the account manager including the "standard practice" response; any prior internal audits or compliance reviews covering these invoices; the compliance officer's own contemporaneous notes from conversations, with dates
- Feedback category tag: FCPA Violation / Customs Bribery Pass-Through / Invoice Concealment / Compliance Whistleblower / Corporate Liability

### Scenario 14-10: USPS Lost Package — IVR Loop, No Callback, Elderly Recipient Needs Medication (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** ACSI Retail and Consumer Shipping Study 2024 (published January 30, 2024, theacsi.com); USPS scored 73/100 — lowest-rated major US carrier; study based on 40,264 consumer interviews (January–December 2023); primary satisfaction drivers cited as delivery timeliness and customer service contact quality; USPS IVR and callback limitations documented in consumer reporting
**The Problem:** A consumer mailed a package via USPS Priority Mail 2-Day containing a prescription medication for an elderly relative living alone. Seven days after the expected delivery date, the tracking shows "In Transit, Arriving Late" — unchanged for five consecutive days. The consumer has called the USPS customer service line (1-800-ASK-USPS) twice. Both times the multi-level IVR failed to recognise the tracking number, transferred to a recorded message, and disconnected before reaching a live representative. A Missing Mail search was submitted online six days ago with no response. The elderly relative has now run out of the medication.
**What staff/org would do:** The automated system continues to display the same unchanged tracking status. The USPS website directs the consumer back to the same Missing Mail form already submitted. The 1-800 line continues to disconnect after the IVR navigation fails.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises the health vulnerability dimension immediately — the medication shortage is a separate urgent need from the parcel recovery — and provides both a medical emergency action and a specific technical route to bypass the USPS IVR system that has been consistently failing.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (recipient is elderly, living alone, and has run out of prescription medication — health vulnerability is active)
- Opening reply: "The medication shortage is the most urgent issue right now — please contact the prescribing doctor or pharmacy immediately for an emergency supply or bridge prescription while we work the package. The USPS 1-800 line is the lowest-rated carrier contact experience in the country. Here is how to reach someone who can actually locate the parcel."
- Direct the consumer to: Address the medication need first — contact the prescribing doctor to request an emergency prescription or contact the pharmacy about an emergency supply under their dispensing rules; to reach USPS for the package: look up the specific destination ZIP code post office by name at tools.usps.com/find-location.htm and call that local facility directly — local postmasters can often locate and manually route parcels that the 1-800 system cannot; file a formal Consumer Affairs inquiry through the USPS postal system at usps.com/help/contact-us or request a supervisor callback; if the package has been in transit over 15 days or contains items of value, file a Mail Theft complaint with the US Postal Inspection Service at postalinspectors.uspis.gov or call 877-876-2455
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The full USPS tracking number and a screenshot of the complete tracking history with dates and times, the original USPS receipt from mailing showing declared contents, the Missing Mail search confirmation number and the date it was submitted, a dated log of both 1-800 call attempts with times and outcome (IVR failure, disconnect), documentation of the recipient's medical need in case an insurance or liability claim becomes relevant
- Feedback category tag: Lost Mail / IVR Failure / No Callback / Vulnerable Recipient / Medication / Priority Mail Failure

Industry ID: 14

## 15. Automobiles / Motor Vehicles

### Scenario 15-1: Recalled Airbag Still Installed — Driver Receives Letter Addressed to Previous Owner (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** NHTSA Takata Recall Spotlight; CarComplaints.com confirmed death of Huma Hanif, Fort Bend County, Texas, March 31, 2016 — 10th US Takata fatality; NHTSA confirmed 28th US death September 2024; 67 million airbags recalled across 19 automakers; six recall notices mailed to prior registered owner, never reaching actual occupant
**The Problem:** A 19-year-old driver in Texas has just received a forwarded letter addressed to a previous registered owner of her 2003 Honda Civic stating that the driver-side airbag may be subject to a Takata recall. She drives the car daily for work. She does not know whether her specific VIN is affected, whether replacement parts are available, or whether it is safe to drive the vehicle at all.
**What staff/org would do:** Transfer her to the dealer, who places her on hold and tells her replacement parts are on national backorder with no estimated timeline — and that she can "continue using the vehicle at her own discretion."
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as a safety emergency. Look up her VIN on NHTSA's recall tool in real time. If the vehicle is recalled and unrepaired, advise her not to drive it until the airbag has been replaced. Confirm whether a loaner vehicle is available under NHTSA's remedy requirements and escalate to Honda's urgent recall line.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a serious safety matter and I want to help you find out right now whether your specific car is affected. Please do not drive this vehicle until we confirm it is safe — the airbag fault involved in this recall has caused fatalities."
- Direct to: NHTSA VIN recall lookup at nhtsa.gov/recalls (instant, free, VIN-specific); Honda Recall Hotline 1-800-999-1009; NHTSA Vehicle Safety Hotline 1-888-327-4236 to report an unresolved recall; if parts are unavailable, Honda is obligated under NHTSA guidance to provide interim transportation
- Evidence to collect: VIN from dashboard or door jamb sticker, the forwarded recall letter including original envelope, any prior service records indicating whether an airbag repair was ever performed, date and name of dealer contact if already spoken to
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Recall Notification Failure

### Scenario 15-2: Dealer Blames "Heavy Key Chain" While Car Cuts Out at Speed (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** GM Ignition Switch Recall; DOJ Deferred Prosecution Agreement September 17, 2015 — $900 million forfeiture; GM Compensation Fund final report (Kenneth Feinberg, August 2015) — 124 confirmed deaths; documented scripted dealer responses "driver error" and "heavy key chain" used across service network during 2003–2014 cover-up period; NHTSA enforcement record
**The Problem:** A consumer in Michigan has brought her 2006 Chevrolet Cobalt to a GM franchise dealer three times over six months. Each time she reports that the engine cuts out without warning at highway speed, power steering fails momentarily, and the airbag warning light activates. The first two visits were logged as "no fault found." On her second visit, the service advisor suggested the problem might be caused by the weight of her key ring on the ignition key. She now drives to work in fear and refuses to carry her children in the car.
**What staff/org would do:** The service advisor repeats the key chain explanation, closes the third ticket as "customer advised," and does not mention any open NHTSA investigation or recall. No escalation to GM corporate.
**What the Owner does:** Recognise this as a documented decade-long pattern in which GM dealer staff were scripted to deflect consumer safety complaints using the key chain explanation. Escalate to NHTSA as a safety-critical report. Advise her the vehicle should not be driven with passengers until the ignition switch has been inspected.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — engine cut-out at speed, power steering loss, and airbag light activation — is a pattern that the US Department of Justice confirmed was linked to 124 deaths and a decade-long cover-up. The explanation you have been given is not accurate. Please do not drive this car with your children until this has been properly investigated."
- Direct to: NHTSA Vehicle Safety Hotline 1-888-327-4236 to file a Vehicle Safety Complaint (these reports trigger formal investigation); GM Owner Center recall lookup at gm.com/recall; Center for Auto Safety at autosafety.org for documentation and legal guidance; if the vehicle is covered by the extended GM ignition switch warranty, a free inspection is available at any GM dealer
- Evidence to collect: All three dealer repair orders (request printed copies of every visit ticket); written list of dates, speeds, and road conditions for each stall and airbag light event; dashcam or phone recording of any future event if safe to capture; names and employee numbers of all service advisors involved
- Feedback category tag: Product Safety / Staff Misconduct / Recall Cover-Up

### Scenario 15-3: $5,295 Fake Inspection Fee Added to Her Contract — Dealer Refuses to Reverse It (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** FTC and Connecticut Attorney General v. Chase Nissan LLC d/b/a Manchester City Nissan, January 2024; FTC complaint documents a $5,295.65 "multi-point inspection fee" added to a vehicle already certified as pre-owned; $3,300 service contract added without consumer knowledge; unauthorized add-ons present in more than 90% of dealer transactions; FTC CARS Rule rulemaking record: $3.4 billion per year in documented dealer junk fee harm to US consumers; 72 million consumer-hours annually spent resolving the aftermath
**The Problem:** A woman in Connecticut purchased a certified pre-owned vehicle advertised at $15,700. After collecting the vehicle and reading her financing contract at home, she found a $5,295.65 "multi-point inspection fee" and a $3,300 service contract she has no recollection of signing for. She returned to the dealer the following morning. The finance manager told her the inspection fee is standard procedure, the service contract was "disclosed and initialled," and the contract has been sent to the lender and cannot be altered.
**What staff/org would do:** Finance manager declines to produce an itemised breakdown of the inspection work performed, insists all items were disclosed, and tells her to call the lender if she disagrees.
**What the Owner does:** Validate her concern — these exact charges match the FTC enforcement action against this dealer type. Explain that the FTC has documented these as deceptive practices and that she has the right to dispute unauthorized charges with both the dealer and the lender.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "A $5,295 inspection fee on a car that is already certified as pre-owned, added without clear prior disclosure, is exactly the type of practice the FTC has taken enforcement action against. Your concern is completely valid and you have the right to challenge these charges."
- Direct to: FTC complaint portal at ReportFraud.ftc.gov; Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection at ct.gov/dcp (860-713-6300) for a dealer licence complaint; Consumer Financial Protection Bureau at consumerfinance.gov/complaint to dispute the charges with the finance company; a consumer protection attorney for potential CUTPA (Connecticut Unfair Trade Practices Act) claim
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot or print of the original advertised price; full signed purchase contract showing every line item; any written materials provided at the time of signing; a written account of the signing session including how each document was presented; names of all staff involved and the date and time of the transaction
- Feedback category tag: Dealer Fraud / Financial Overcharge / Unauthorized Add-ons

### Scenario 15-4: PCP Finance Broker Never Disclosed the Commission — Lender Now Telling Her to Wait for a Letter (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** FCA PS26/3 Motor Finance Consumer Redress Scheme confirmed March 30, 2026 — 12.1 million agreements eligible, average payout £830, total scheme cost £9.1 billion; Court of Appeal October 25, 2024 ruling: commission arrangements unlawful without full informed consumer consent; Financial Ombudsman Service v. Barclays Partner Finance December 17, 2024 — commission "not adequately disclosed"; FCA banned Discretionary Commission Arrangements January 2021
**The Problem:** A consumer in Manchester took out a Personal Contract Purchase agreement for a Vauxhall Astra in 2019. After seeing FCA media coverage of the motor finance redress scheme, she called her lender to ask whether her agreement involved a Discretionary Commission Arrangement. The call centre agent told her: "There's nothing to tell you at this stage — the scheme has not started yet. We will write to you when we have information." She cannot locate her original credit agreement and does not know how to confirm whether she is eligible.
**What staff/org would do:** Call centre agent provides the minimum permissible response, does not explain what a DCA is, does not tell her how to obtain a copy of her original agreement, and does not mention the Financial Ombudsman as an escalation route if the lender fails to engage properly.
**What the Owner does:** Explain what the FCA's confirmed redress scheme means for her, how to establish whether her agreement involved a DCA, and what her rights are now that the scheme is formally confirmed and binding on lenders.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You are right to follow this up. The FCA has formally confirmed a redress scheme covering 12.1 million agreements — this is legally binding on your lender, and telling you to simply wait for a letter is not a complete answer. Here is what you can do now."
- Direct to: FCA motor finance information page at fca.org.uk/consumers/car-finance-complaints; Financial Ombudsman Service at financial-ombudsman.org.uk (0800 023 4567) — a formal complaint can be raised if the lender does not respond substantively within 8 weeks; MoneySavingExpert free eligibility checker at moneysavingexpert.com for a preliminary DCA assessment; send a Subject Access Request to the lender under UK GDPR to obtain the original agreement and any internal commission records
- Evidence to collect: Name of lender and dealership, date and term of the PCP agreement, vehicle make, model, and registration, any documents retained from the point of sale, written record of today's call with the lender including agent name, date, and what was said
- Feedback category tag: Financial Mis-selling / Transparency Failure / Commission Non-Disclosure

### Scenario 15-5: Transferred Three Times, Then Disconnected — Same Fault, Nine-Day Wait for a Return Repair (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** UK Motor Ombudsman Q3 2024 official report — 4,847 service and repair cases in first three quarters of 2024, up 29% year-on-year; all-time record 1,657 new cases in Q2 2024; 32% of all disputes cited customer service failure as the primary cause; J.D. Power 2024 US Customer Service Index Study — average 5.2-day wait for mass-market dealer service appointment, with VP Chris Sutton noting "capacity and wait time issues have gotten progressively worse since the pandemic"
**The Problem:** A consumer in Birmingham had his Ford Focus repaired at a franchise dealer three weeks ago for a recurring automatic gearbox fault. The fault has returned. He called the dealer's customer service line on a Monday morning and was placed on hold for 22 minutes, transferred to the service department, then transferred again to the workshop manager. After a further 14 minutes on hold he was disconnected. When he called back, he was told he would need to book in "like any other customer" — a nine-day wait — despite this being a return repair for the identical fault.
**What staff/org would do:** Receptionist books him nine days out in the standard queue, treats the return repair as a new job, declines to escalate to a service manager, and makes no commitment regarding a courtesy vehicle or any priority given to a recurring unresolved fault.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the unacceptable handling and clarify that a return repair for the same fault carries distinct rights under consumer law and is not equivalent to a first booking. Help him formally escalate within the dealer group and to the Motor Ombudsman.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Being disconnected twice and told to wait nine days for a fault that was supposed to have been fixed is not acceptable, and a return repair for the same issue is legally distinct from a new booking. You have the right to a faster resolution."
- Direct to: Motor Ombudsman dispute resolution service at motorombudsman.org (0345 241 3008) — the Motor Ombudsman's Vehicle Warranty Products Code and Service and Repair Code both apply; the dealer's Group Customer Relations department (request this contact in writing); Citizens Advice consumer helpline 0808 223 1133; under the Consumer Rights Act 2015, a repair that fails to fix the fault within a reasonable time entitles the consumer to a price reduction or a further free repair
- Evidence to collect: Original repair invoice and job card; date the fault recurred; written description (via email or text to the dealer) of the fault returning, sent immediately to create a timestamped record; call log showing hold times and disconnections with approximate times; any promise of a courtesy vehicle or collection and delivery made at the time of original repair
- Feedback category tag: Call Centre Failure / Faulty Repair / Service Delay

### Scenario 15-6: Returned Her Leased Car in Good Standing — Now Facing a Credit Default for a Product She Tried to Cancel (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** CFPB enforcement action against Toyota Motor Credit Corporation, 2023 — $60 million order; 27,507 consumers falsely reported as delinquent to credit bureaus after returning leased vehicles; $32 million redress to consumers whose add-on product cancellations were blocked or processed incorrectly; $6 million specifically to consumers whose credit records were damaged; Financial Ombudsman Service equivalent jurisdiction in UK for illegal credit reporting by lenders
**The Problem:** A consumer in Glasgow returned her leased vehicle at lease end in December 2023 and received written confirmation from her lender that all amounts were settled. Three months later, she received a notice from a credit reference agency showing her as seriously delinquent on the account. When she called the lender, she was told the entry related to a GAP insurance product she had attempted to cancel six months earlier, which was "never processed." The disputed balance is £312. Her credit score has dropped by over 80 points and she has since been rejected for a mortgage.
**What staff/org would do:** Call centre agent acknowledges the GAP cancellation was not processed internally but states the account "shows as outstanding" and that she must pay the £312 and request a review — framing the lender's own processing failure as her debt.
**What the Owner does:** Make clear this is the lender's failure, not hers. A delinquency reported to a credit reference agency for a product the consumer attempted to cancel is an illegal credit reporting act. Help her dispute the entry and raise a formal complaint to the Financial Ombudsman immediately.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You should not have a delinquency on your credit file for a product you attempted to cancel and an account you settled in full. This is the lender's own processing failure — and it is currently affecting your ability to get a mortgage. You have a strong right to challenge this and have the entry corrected."
- Direct to: Financial Ombudsman Service at financial-ombudsman.org.uk (0800 023 4567) — submit a formal complaint against the lender for incorrect credit reporting and failure to process cancellation; Information Commissioner's Office at ico.org.uk for a complaint about inaccurate personal data held by the credit reference agency; add a Notice of Correction immediately via Experian, Equifax, or TransUnion explaining the dispute; request the lender's Data Protection Officer contact under UK GDPR Article 16 (right to rectification)
- Evidence to collect: Lease-end settlement confirmation letter showing zero balance signed off by the lender; proof of the GAP cancellation request — email, letter, or a written note of date and content of any phone call; credit reference report showing the default entry and its date; the mortgage rejection letter citing the default; all correspondence with the lender since December 2023
- Feedback category tag: Rights Violation / Illegal Credit Reporting / Add-On Product Blocking

### Scenario 15-7: Three Engine Replacements, Fault Still Present — Mazda Refuses Refund (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v. Mazda Australia, Federal Court of Australia — A$11.5 million penalty 2021; 49 documented false or misleading representations made to nine consumers across seven vehicles including Mazda CX-5; three engine replacements on a single vehicle without resolution documented in the judgment; ACCC Deputy Chair Delia Rickard described conduct as "run-around," "evasions and subterfuges," and "appalling customer service"; Federal Court appeal dismissed March 23, 2023
**The Problem:** A consumer in Melbourne purchased a new Mazda CX-5 in 2016 for A$37,000. Over two years, the vehicle developed a serious engine fault causing jerking, power loss, and excessive oil consumption. Mazda Australia arranged three complete engine replacements under warranty, none of which resolved the fault. When the consumer formally wrote to Mazda Australia's national customer service requesting a refund or replacement under the Australian Consumer Law, he received a written response stating the only remedy available under "the terms of his manufacturer's warranty" was a further repair attempt. He has been without a reliable vehicle for eleven cumulative months.
**What staff/org would do:** National customer service issues a written response citing warranty terms only, makes no reference to his independent Australian Consumer Law rights, and offers no refund, replacement, or compensation for the period the vehicle was unusable.
**What the Owner does:** Clearly explain that the Australian Consumer Law provides consumer guarantee rights that exist independently of and override the manufacturer's warranty, and that after multiple failed repair attempts for a major failure the consumer is entitled by law to a full refund or replacement vehicle. Connect him to the ACCC and the relevant state fair trading office.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Three engine replacements that have all failed to fix the same problem is exactly the situation Australian Consumer Law was designed for. Your right to a refund or replacement is a separate legal right from what the manufacturer's warranty says — and Mazda cannot remove it."
- Direct to: ACCC complaint portal at accc.gov.au/consumers/consumer-rights-guarantees; Consumer Affairs Victoria at consumer.vic.gov.au (1300 558 181); Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) for a binding order; a consumer law solicitor for advice on out-of-pocket transport costs and consequential losses
- Evidence to collect: Original purchase contract and invoice; complete workshop records from every dealer visit across all three engine replacements (request formally in writing); all written correspondence with Mazda Australia national customer service including today's refusal letter; a timeline of all fault recurrences with dates and vehicle odometer readings; all receipts for alternative transport, hire vehicles, or other out-of-pocket costs during the repair periods
- Feedback category tag: Consumer Guarantee Refusal / Faulty Repair / Escalation Loop

### Scenario 15-8: Dealer Confirmed in Writing the Diesel Met Australian Standards — It Did Not (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v. Volkswagen AG, Federal Court of Australia — A$125 million penalty December 2019, highest total penalty ever imposed under Australian Consumer Law at the time; Full Federal Court appeal dismissed April 2021; High Court special leave refused; ACCC documented dealers made misleading representations to consumers about compliance with Australian diesel emissions standards; separate class action representing approximately 100,000 VW, Skoda, and Audi owners settled for approximately A$127 million (approximately A$1,400 per vehicle) in 2019
**The Problem:** A consumer in Sydney purchased a new Volkswagen Tiguan TDI diesel in 2014. At the point of sale, the dealer provided written documentation confirming the vehicle met all applicable Australian emissions standards. After the September 2015 global VW emissions disclosure, the consumer learned the vehicle contained defeat device software producing substantially higher NOx in real-world driving than in regulated test conditions. VW has offered a software update, which the dealer describes as "approved by Australian regulators." She has not received any financial compensation and does not know whether the software fix changes the vehicle's performance or fuel economy.
**What staff/org would do:** VW dealer confirms the fix was submitted to and approved by regulators, declines to provide written documentation of what the update modifies, and tells her no further compensation is available beyond the fix itself.
**What the Owner does:** Explain her rights clearly — the dealer's written representation about emissions compliance was false, the Federal Court confirmed Volkswagen's conduct was unlawful, and a class action settlement was available to Australian owners. Help her establish whether she can still access any remaining remedy.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You were given a written confirmation that was false. The Federal Court confirmed this and fined Volkswagen A$125 million for it. You were also entitled to participate in the Australian class action settlement. Let me help you understand what options remain."
- Direct to: ACCC at accc.gov.au for a consumer guarantee complaint against Volkswagen AG and the dealership; Maurice Blackburn Lawyers (administered the Australian VW class action) for advice on whether any residual remedy remains available; relevant state fair trading office — NSW Fair Trading at fairtrading.nsw.gov.au (13 32 20); request written documentation from VW Australia of exactly what the software update modifies and any measurable impact on performance or fuel economy
- Evidence to collect: Original purchase contract and the written dealer confirmation of emissions compliance; all correspondence from VW about the software update; any independently measured fuel economy figures before and after the fix; sales brochure or dealer materials specifying the vehicle's emissions rating at point of sale; documentation of any VW communication about the class action and whether she was notified
- Feedback category tag: Emission Fraud / Information Failure / Misleading Dealer Representation
