### Scenario 17-4: Disadvantaged Student Unknowingly Enrolled in Two VET Courses — $74,000 Debt (Pattern B, C)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v Phoenix Institute of Australia and Community Training Initiatives, Federal Court ($438M penalty ordered July 2023; ACCC media releases 2021 and 2023). Phoenix and CTI enrolled over 11,000 students in VET FEE-HELP courses by falsely representing courses as "free"; students were enrolled in two programs simultaneously without understanding; the Federal Court found students were "disadvantaged individuals" with "no reasonable prospect of completing" the courses; average debt exceeded $37,000 per student; only 9 of 11,000 students completed even one course.
**The Problem:** A 34-year-old woman with limited formal education was approached in her community by a CTI sales agent who told her she could get a "free" diploma and a free laptop. She signed forms the agent did not explain. She has now received a VET FEE-HELP statement showing she has incurred $74,000 in student loan debt across two diploma programs she did not know she was enrolled in. She has completed none of the coursework. No laptop arrived. The agent no longer responds to her messages.
**What staff/org would do:** Phoenix and CTI's administrative team tells her the forms she signed were binding, that the debt is a government obligation not their responsibility, and that she should "contact StudyAssist." No further help is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Explains that her situation mirrors the specific pattern the Federal Court found to be "unconscionable conduct towards disadvantaged individuals, on an industrial scale," and that she has a right to seek debt cancellation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What happened to you is not your fault. An Australian court found that this type of enrollment — where vulnerable people were signed up for courses they did not understand, with debts they were never told about — was unlawful on an industrial scale. You can apply to have your debt cancelled."
- Direct to: StudyAssist (studyassist.gov.au) — apply for VET FEE-HELP debt remission as a Phoenix or CTI-affected student; Australian Skills Quality Authority (asqa.gov.au) — report the registered training organisation; ACCC (accc.gov.au) — if CTI or Phoenix make any further contact with you; a community legal centre for free advice on exercising your rights under Australian Consumer Law
- Evidence to collect: Any enrollment forms, even partial copies; the sales agent's contact details or any identifying information (name, phone number); the VET FEE-HELP debt statement; records of any laptop promised (messages, flyers, verbal promises); dates and location of the initial contact with the recruitment agent
- Feedback category tag: vulnerability / unconscionable_conduct / debt_harm / non_delivery

---

### Scenario 17-5: Pay-Per-Enrollment Telemarketer Enrolled Unsuitable Student — VET Debt Now Active (Pattern B, F)
**Real Basis:** ACCC v Acquire Learning & Careers, Federal Court penalty $4.5 million (ACCC media release, May 2017). Telemarketers were paid per enrollment referred; call recordings used as central evidence by the court; Justice Murphy described the conduct as resembling "an unscrupulous fly by night operation"; the company deliberately targeted vulnerable consumers and enrolled them in online courses unsuitable given their individual educational backgrounds and capabilities.
**The Problem:** A 42-year-old woman who left school at Year 10 received a cold call from Acquire Learning telling her she was "a perfect fit" for an online Diploma of Business Administration. The caller said the course was "basically free" under VET FEE-HELP and would take only a few months. She verbally agreed. She has since discovered the course requires Year 12 equivalency she does not have; it takes two years to complete; her VET FEE-HELP debt has already been triggered for $18,500; and the agent who enrolled her was paid a commission for doing so. When she called to withdraw, she was told she could withdraw but "the debt has already been processed."
**What staff/org would do:** Acquire Learning's enrollment team confirms the debt is active and tells her withdrawal does not cancel a VET FEE-HELP obligation once the census date has passed.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the telemarketer's financial incentive to mislead her and directs her to the specific pathways for debt remission and regulatory complaint.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The telemarketer who called you had a direct financial incentive to sign you up regardless of whether the course was suitable — that is exactly the misconduct an Australian court penalised Acquire Learning $4.5 million for. You were not told the truth, and you have grounds to seek debt cancellation."
- Direct to: StudyAssist (studyassist.gov.au) — apply for VET FEE-HELP remission on grounds of provider misconduct; ACCC (accc.gov.au/consumers/complaints) — report the conduct including the commission-based enrollment; Australian Skills Quality Authority (asqa.gov.au) — report the registered training organisation; your state's legal aid service for advice on your rights under Australian Consumer Law
- Evidence to collect: Notes on the original call (date, time, what the agent said verbatim about eligibility, duration, and cost); any follow-up SMS, email, or paperwork from Acquire Learning; the VET FEE-HELP debt confirmation document; proof of enrollment, even if you did not consciously initiate it; if possible, request a copy of the call recording from Acquire Learning in writing
- Feedback category tag: staff_misconduct / telemarketer_fraud / unsuitable_enrollment / financial_harm

---

### Scenario 17-6: Apprentice Arrives for Exam to Find Training Provider Has Collapsed (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Skills Training UK Limited creditors' voluntary liquidation, 2 August 2023 (FE Week investigative reporting, July 2023; Companies House insolvency filing; Wembley Matters blog — student and staff accounts). The company had 820 apprentices in 2021/22 and 550 in 2022/23; exams were cancelled mid-course; staff were told not to return; learners were left without the qualifications they had been working toward; over 200 jobs were lost.
**The Problem:** An apprentice in his second year of a Business Administration Level 3 apprenticeship arrived at his Skills Training UK centre in Birmingham for a scheduled end-point assessment exam. The centre was locked. He received a text from a staff member saying "the company has closed." He does not know whether his two years of portfolio evidence is still accessible, whether his apprenticeship will be recognised toward anything, who any new provider might be, or whether his employer will continue to fund his apprenticeship given the disruption. No official communication has arrived.
**What staff/org would do:** No active communication comes from Skills Training UK — the company is in administration and phones go unanswered. His employer's HR team says they will "look into it" but has no specific answers. No one contacts him proactively about continuity.
**What the Owner does:** Gives him a calm, structured explanation of what happens to apprentices when a provider fails and who to contact urgently to protect his progress.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "This is a genuinely disruptive situation and it is not your fault. When a training provider closes, there are specific steps your employer and the funding authority must take to protect your apprenticeship — here is how to make sure those steps actually happen."
- Direct to: Education and Skills Funding Agency (ESFA — apprenticeships.education.gov.uk) — report as an affected apprentice and ask for guidance on provider transfer; your employer's HR or training manager — they have a legal duty to help find a replacement provider under the apprenticeship agreement and funding rules; National Apprenticeship Service helpline (0800 015 0400); Citizens Advice (citizensadvice.org.uk) for employment rights guidance if your employer is unresponsive
- Evidence to collect: Download or photograph all portfolio evidence immediately — access to any provider portal may be cut off without notice; preserve all assignment submissions and any feedback received; locate your apprenticeship agreement and any payment records; save all communications from Skills Training UK about the closure, including the staff text message; document your exam booking confirmation
- Feedback category tag: non_delivery / provider_collapse / apprenticeship_continuity / wait_no_resolution

---

### Scenario 17-7: Security Guard's Qualification Issued Without Training — Employer Suspends Him Without Pay (Pattern A, E)
**Real Basis:** Ofqual enforcement action against Industry Qualifications (UK, 2015); FE Week investigative reporting on "flourishing trade in fake" security industry cards; final Ofqual fine of £115,000 plus legal costs after a four-year appeal. Staff at an associated London-based training provider offered to sit exams on behalf of learners, issuing security industry qualifications without candidates completing any required learning.
**The Problem:** A security guard has held a Door Supervisor licence for eight months, working at a licensed venue in London. His employer has called him in to say the Security Industry Authority (SIA) has flagged that qualifications from his awarding organisation (Industry Qualifications) are under investigation for malpractice — that certificates were issued without learners completing required training. He has been suspended without pay pending verification. He believes he attended every session he was told to attend by the training provider; he is now uncertain whether the provider delivered legitimate instruction or fabricated his qualification without his knowledge.
**What staff/org would do:** The employer's HR team reads out a suspension letter and says they cannot discuss it further until SIA confirm the qualification's status. The training provider's phone line goes to voicemail. No timeline is given.
**What the Owner does:** Explains his rights as a worker who may have been a victim of provider misconduct rather than a participant in it, and gives him the specific SIA and Ofqual contact routes.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Being suspended because a training provider may have committed fraud is deeply unfair — and the investigation is into the provider's conduct, not yours. There are specific bodies you can contact to establish whether your qualification is valid and to protect your right to work while this is investigated."
- Direct to: Security Industry Authority (sia.homeoffice.gov.uk) — check your licence status directly and report what your employer has told you; Ofqual (ofqual.gov.uk) — report the awarding organisation's conduct and ask for the status of the investigation; ask your employer in writing for the specific SIA notification they received and request that your unpaid suspension be reviewed — it should be treated as a precautionary measure while an investigation into a third party is ongoing; ACAS (acas.org.uk / 0300 123 1100) for employment rights guidance during suspension
- Evidence to collect: Your SIA licence number and expiry date; all course materials, attendance records, and certificates issued by the training provider; your employment contract and the suspension letter; any written or verbal communication from your employer about the SIA notification; dates and duration of every session you attended
- Feedback category tag: transparency_failure / qualification_integrity / suspension_injustice / regulatory_complaint

---

### Scenario 17-8: Suspected Fake CSCS Card on a Live Construction Site — Supervisor Needs to Act Now (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Warwickshire County Council Trading Standards prosecution of Andrew Weeks / Nuneaton Print, sentencing at Warwick Crown Court October 2018 (3 years 8 months imprisonment; guilty plea under Section 9 of the Fraud Act 2006); CSCS news release; IOSH Magazine. Weeks manufactured and sold fake CSCS, CPCS, ECS, occupational health, and other certification cards online for up to £30.99 each.
**The Problem:** A construction site supervisor in the West Midlands has been alerted by a colleague that a site worker's CSCS card looks suspicious — the font is wrong, the card stock feels different from genuine cards, and the QR code does not scan using the CSCS app. The worker says he bought the card online for £27 and appears to believe it is genuine. The supervisor needs to know right now: whether to remove the worker from the site immediately; how to verify the card; whether the worker is a criminal suspect or a victim; and who must be reported to.
**What staff/org would do:** The main contractor's site manager tells the supervisor to "send the worker home" and "report it to HR on Monday." No immediate verification step or referral to any authority is offered. The worker is left in limbo over the weekend.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a live site safety issue requiring action today, gives the supervisor immediate verification steps, and explains that the card holder is most likely a victim of fraud who should be supported rather than accused.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a site safety matter that needs to be dealt with today, not on Monday. Here is what to do right now, step by step — and the worker whose card is in question is most likely a victim of fraud, not a fraudster."
- Direct to: Verify the card immediately: call CSCS on 0344 994 4777 or use the official CSCS app (cscs.uk.com) to run a card check before the end of the working day; if the card is confirmed fake: report to local Trading Standards via the Citizens Advice consumer helpline (0808 223 1133 — they route reports to the correct local authority); encourage the worker to report as a fraud victim to Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk / 0300 123 2040); notify the main contractor's health and safety team today — a site with potentially fraudulent safety credentials is an active H&S liability under the Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the card front and back before returning it to the worker; record the QR scan failure with a screenshot; note the worker's name, card reference number, and date of discovery; preserve any online listing or receipt the worker has showing where the card was purchased; record the name of the colleague who first raised the concern and the exact time
- Feedback category tag: safety_urgent / fake_certification / site_safety / fraud_victim

---

### Scenario 17-9: IAS Coaching Centre Provided No Faculty or Test Series — Refusing All Refund (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Central Delhi District Consumer Dispute Redressal Commission ruling, October 2024 (Business Standard, 7 October 2024; Bar and Bench; The LawGist). IAS Gurukul ordered to refund Rs 62,363, pay Rs 10,000 compensation for mental agony, and Rs 5,000 litigation costs. The Commission found the centre "guilty of publishing misleading advertisements and charging lump sum fees, besides getting itself unjustly enriched through unfair practices and deficient services."
**The Problem:** A student paid Rs 98,000 to IAS Gurukul in Rajendra Nagar, Delhi, for a "Full IAS Preparation Program" that advertised specialist faculty for all subjects, a structured test series, and personal mentorship. Four months in: the faculty for two key optional subjects have never appeared; the promised test series has not started; the "personal mentor" met her once for twenty minutes. She approached the centre administration to request a proportionate refund. The centre director refused, claiming she had "used the facilities," said no refund policy exists, and now asserts she attended nine months of classes — a claim she denies and for which the centre has produced no records.
**What staff/org would do:** The centre director dismisses her complaint verbally, threatens that "pursuing this further will not help your career," and produces no attendance documentation while insisting the records show nine months of attendance.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the documented deception and gives the student a clear, practical route to a legal remedy that does not require hiring a lawyer upfront.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Paying for specialist faculty, a test series, and personal mentorship — and receiving none of those things — is a clear case of deficient services and misleading advertising. A Delhi consumer court already ruled against this same institution for the same conduct and ordered a refund. Here is how to file your own complaint."
- Direct to: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (CDRC), Central Delhi — file a consumer complaint under the Consumer Protection Act 2019; cases up to Rs 50 lakh can be filed without a lawyer and fees are minimal; National Consumer Helpline (NCH) — call 1800-11-4000 (toll-free) or WhatsApp 8800001915 — the NCH has a dedicated coaching institute complaint team and has secured over Rs 1.56 crore in refunds for coaching students; Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA — consumeraffairs.nic.in) for escalation if systemic unfair trade practices are documented
- Evidence to collect: Original enrollment contract and fee receipt showing Rs 98,000 payment; the advertisement or brochure promising specialist faculty, test series, and mentorship; your own attendance diary or notes showing which subjects had no teacher; any written communications with the centre; a written record of the dates and duration of the single mentorship session you received; note the director's verbal refusal and the threat made — write it down immediately with date and time
- Feedback category tag: financial_harm / misrepresented_service / refund_refused / consumer_forum

---

### Scenario 17-10: For-Profit College Admissions Agent Gave False Employment Statistics — Student Discovers Truth After Graduating (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** FTC Notice of Penalty Offenses to 70 for-profit colleges and vocational training providers, October 2021 (ftc.gov; Higher Ed Dive; FTC Penalty Offense Authority published recipient list). The FTC specifically named false employment placement statistics communicated through admissions call centres and online chat agents as a formally adjudicated deceptive practice; institutions face civil penalties of up to $43,792 per violation per affected consumer. The FTC invoked its Penalty Offense Authority for the first time against the for-profit education sector.
**The Problem:** A prospective student called a for-profit healthcare vocational college's admissions line. The admissions agent told him that 92% of graduates in the Medical Administrative Assistant program found employment within three months, and that the college had a formal placement partnership with a regional hospital network. He enrolled and paid $14,000. He has now graduated and found through a government disclosure that the college's actual employment rate for this program is 31%. The hospital partnership the agent described does not appear to exist. When he called the admissions office to ask for the source of the 92% figure, he was transferred four times and told that employment outcomes are "not guaranteed" and are not something the admissions team can speak to.
**What staff/org would do:** The admissions team denies ever providing specific statistics and claims all representations are covered by disclaimer language in the enrollment contract. The admissions call was not recorded, or the recording is claimed to be unavailable. No further escalation is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Explains that false employment statistics communicated through admissions call centres was the specific conduct formally identified by the FTC as an adjudicated deceptive practice in 2021, and that the student has legal routes to pursue a refund and contribute to the regulatory record.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The information you were given — a 92% employment rate and a specific hospital placement partnership — if false, is precisely the type of claim the US Federal Trade Commission formally warned for-profit colleges not to make in 2021. You have real options to pursue a refund and file a complaint that adds to the regulatory record against this institution."
- Direct to: FTC complaint portal (reportfraud.ftc.gov) — file a detailed complaint; your state Attorney General's consumer protection division — many states maintain specific for-profit college enforcement units; Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (consumerfinance.gov/complaint) if any federal student loans were used to fund enrollment; check whether your college appears on the FTC's 2021 list of 70 recipients of Penalty Offense Notices (published at ftc.gov); if federal student loans were used, the US Department of Education Borrower Defense to Repayment program (studentaid.gov/borrower-defense) allows you to apply for loan discharge based on school misconduct
- Evidence to collect: Any follow-up email or written material the admissions agent sent after the initial call; any brochures, website screenshots, or chat transcripts you saw before enrolling; your enrollment contract; all payment records; the government Gainful Employment disclosure you found after graduating showing the actual 31% employment rate; detailed notes on each of the four transfers you experienced when you called back — date, time, what each agent said; request a copy of the original admissions call recording in writing immediately and keep a copy of that written request
- Feedback category tag: rights_violation / false_employment_claims / admissions_misconduct / information_failure

Industry ID: 17

---

## 18. Business Consultancy

### Scenario 18-1: Project Deliverable Never Delivered After Full Contract Value Paid (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Court filing, Superior Court of Delaware, Case N19C-04-232 EMD — Hertz Corporation v. Accenture LLP, April 2019; CIO Magazine; Adweek investigative reporting
**The Problem:** A company hired a major consultancy to rebuild its digital platform for an agreed fixed price. The go-live date was missed twice. Mid-project, the consultancy replaced its experienced team with junior staff without disclosing this to the client. When the client's project managers escalated concerns, responses were evasive and misleading about the true state of the code. The contract term has now ended, the platform is still unusable, and the consultancy is demanding additional fees for work the original contract explicitly covered. The client has paid nearly the full contract value and has nothing deployable.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the client to a generic account manager who insists the disputed features are "out of scope" and offers a change order for additional fees. The partner relationship team closes ranks. Internal escalation produces no result. The client is told disputes must go through the formal legal process only.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the severity immediately — the client has paid substantial funds and received no working product. Asks for the contract scope document and the specific deliverables list. Offers to escalate to senior leadership the same day. Does not push the client toward legal channels as a first response.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "This is a serious situation — you have paid for a finished product and do not have one, and we understand that is actively harming your business. Let me make sure this reaches the right people today and that your position is fully documented."
- Direct to: Senior partner / client relationship director of the consultancy in writing; if no resolution within 5 business days, refer to the arbitration clause in the contract; USA — consult a commercial litigation attorney specialising in IT delivery disputes; UK — refer to CEDR (Centre for Effective Dispute Resolution, cedr.com) or the Chartered Institute of Arbitrators; Australia — refer to the Australian Centre for International Commercial Arbitration (acica.org.au)
- Evidence to collect: Signed contract and scope of work document with full deliverables list; all milestone communications and change request logs; written confirmation of go-live dates (emails, meeting notes); any internal communications about staff changes made without client disclosure; all invoices paid and payment records; an independent technical audit report documenting code quality and what is and is not functional
- Feedback category tag: Service Quality — Deliverable Not Met / Project Abandonment

---

### Scenario 18-2: Cold-Call Investment Fraud by Fake Consultancy — Funds Gone (Pattern B+A)
**Real Basis:** FCA press release — conviction of Raymondip Bedi and Patrick Mavanga for operating CCX Capital / Astaria Group LLP fake investment consultancy; sentences of 6 years 6 months and 5 years 4 months handed down 2024; FCA confiscation orders of £305,284; Decrypt investigative report; 65 victims defrauded of £1,541,799
**The Problem:** A consumer received an unsolicited professional-sounding call from a firm calling itself a "regulated investment consultancy." The agent was knowledgeable, confident, and sent polished follow-up documentation. The consumer invested £15,000 based on their advice, received regular "performance update" emails, and had access to what appeared to be an investment portal showing gains. Three months later, all calls go to voicemail, the website is offline, the portal login no longer works, and no funds have been returned. The consumer cannot confirm whether the firm was ever FCA-authorised.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center number is disconnected or rings out — the operation was dismantled after funds were collected. If the consumer contacts their bank to recover funds, the bank's fraud team may initially say the transfer was "authorised" by the consumer and decline to refund automatically.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately identifies the profile as a known investment fraud pattern. Does not wait for more information before advising urgent action. Makes clear that speed is critical — the faster reports are filed, the higher the chance of fund tracing and potential recovery.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: true
- Opening reply: "What you are describing matches a documented investment fraud pattern — a professional-looking cold-calling firm presenting false credentials to steal investor money. This is urgent. Stop all further transfers and take the steps below today."
- Direct to: (UK) FCA ScamSmart register at register.fca.org.uk to verify the firm's authorisation status immediately; Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk or 0300 123 2040 to file a fraud report today; the consumer's own bank fraud team to request a Faster Payments recall and raise a fraud claim under the Contingent Reimbursement Model; Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) at fscs.org.uk if any authorised firm was involved; (USA) SEC Investor Complaint Center at sec.gov/tcr and FINRA at finra.org/investors; (Australia) ASIC ScamWatch at scamwatch.gov.au; (India) SEBI SCORES portal at scores.sebi.gov.in
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of all communications including performance update emails; the firm's name, phone number, website URL, and any registration numbers quoted; the investment portal URL and login page; screenshots of the portal showing supposed account balance; bank transfer records including payee account name, account number, sort code, date, and amount; names of all agents who called; any correspondence sent by post
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud — Fake Credentials / Disappeared with Payment / Boiler Room Cold-Call

---

### Scenario 18-3: Consultancy Advising Government Secretly Helped Clients Evade That Same Government's Laws (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** PwC Australia Tax Scandal — Wikipedia (PwC tax scandal); Australian Treasury Ministers press release (Minister Jim Chalmers, 2023); Senate Economics Legislation Committee findings; International Tax Review; Accountancy Age; Australian Federal Police criminal investigation opened 2023; CEO Tom Seymour removed; 8 partners fired; government consulting division sold for $1
**The Problem:** A business owner discovers through media reporting that their accounting and tax consultancy — the same firm they paid for independent tax compliance advice — was simultaneously advising multinational clients on how to circumvent the very tax laws the firm helped the government design. The business owner is unsure whether: (a) their own advice was compromised by this dual relationship; (b) their confidential tax information was shared internally; (c) the fees they paid were tainted by a conflict the firm was legally obligated to disclose but never did.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue a generic public statement about "taking the matter seriously" and "cooperating with authorities." Existing client communications teams tell affected clients that the matter "does not relate to your engagement" and that their files were managed by separate teams. No proactive notification is sent to clients whose information may have been accessible to the conflicted partners.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that discovering your adviser had a hidden conflict is distressing and legitimately raises questions about the integrity of past advice. Does not minimise the concern. Offers to help the client document their specific situation and identify the correct reporting and remediation channels.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "Finding out your adviser had an undisclosed conflict of interest — advising you while simultaneously working against your interests elsewhere — is a serious matter. Your concerns about the integrity of your advice and the confidentiality of your information are completely valid. Let me help you document this and identify your next steps."
- Direct to: (Australia) Tax Practitioners Board (TPB) at taxpractitionersboard.gov.au — regulates tax advisers and can investigate professional conduct breaches; Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) at asic.gov.au if investment advice was involved; the Australian Federal Police if criminal conduct is suspected; the firm's Ethics Partner or Complaints team in writing (not by phone); (UK) Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (ICAEW) Professional Conduct Department at icaew.com/complaints; (USA) AICPA Ethics Hotline at aicpa.org
- Evidence to collect: All engagement letters and terms of service signed with the firm; copies of all advice received including tax opinions and compliance recommendations; any communications where the firm disclosed (or failed to disclose) its other advisory relationships; invoices paid; any consent or confidentiality agreements signed; media reports and Senate inquiry findings naming the firm
- Feedback category tag: Transparency Failure — Conflict of Interest / Undisclosed Dual Advisory Role

---

### Scenario 18-4: Whistleblower Alleges Consultancy Billed Government for Hours Never Worked (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** ABC News investigative report cited in Canberra Times, August 2023 — KPMG Australia overbilling allegations on Department of Defence contracts totalling $1.8B over a decade; parliamentary testimony; calls for formal inquiry; Consultancy.com.au reporting; KPMG denied the allegations citing internal checks
**The Problem:** A government contract manager has discovered billing anomalies — the consultancy's invoices claim hours for activities that, cross-referenced with meeting records and deliverable logs, were never performed. When the contract manager attempts to formally flag this internally, they are told the matter is "being handled" and directed away from the department's own procurement complaints process. A senior colleague has told them informally that raising this further could damage the department's relationship with the firm and affect future contract renewals.
**What staff/org would do:** Deflect the concern at middle management level. Describe the billing discrepancy as a "categorisation or rounding matter." Suggest the contract manager raise it directly with the firm's account team — routing the complaint to the very party under accusation. Offer an informal meeting but no written acknowledgement of the concern.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a potential whistleblower situation requiring confidential and protective handling immediately. Does not route the complaint back to the accused party. Notes that the internal pressure to stay silent is itself a concern that must be documented separately.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — billing for hours not worked on a government contract — is a serious matter that warrants formal investigation, not a quiet internal conversation. The pressure you are being placed under to stay silent is itself a concern we need to document. Your report will be treated confidentially."
- Direct to: (Australia) Australian National Audit Office (ANAO) at anao.gov.au for government contract fraud concerns; National Anti-Corruption Commission (NACC) at nacc.gov.au for public sector corruption referrals; the department's own Audit and Risk Committee directly, bypassing line management; the Australian Federal Police (AFP) Economic and Cyber Crime Group for suspected fraud; Public Interest Disclosure Act 2013 protections — refer to the Commonwealth Ombudsman at ombudsman.gov.au for advice on how to make a protected disclosure; (UK) Protect (formerly Public Concern at Work) at protect-advice.org.uk for independent confidential whistleblowing advice
- Evidence to collect: Copies of invoices with full line-item billing detail; meeting records, calendar entries, and deliverable logs covering the same billing periods; email records showing what work was actually requested and confirmed as received; any internal messages where the complaint was deflected or discouraged; contemporaneous notes of all conversations about this matter with dates and names of those present
- Feedback category tag: Financial Misconduct — Overbilling Government Contract / Whistleblower Suppression

---

### Scenario 18-5: Small Business Received No Services from Government-Funded Consultancy That Billed Anyway (Pattern E+A)
**Real Basis:** DOJ District of Minnesota press release — United States v. Tezzaree El-Amin Champion and Marcus Alexander Hamilton; Futuristic Management Group LLC; guilty pleas entered 2024; $3,479,575 restitution order; KARE11 and MPR News investigative reporting; 100+ fraudulent invoices submitted under Hennepin County Elevate Hennepin COVID-19 relief programme
**The Problem:** A small business owner enrolled in a government-funded recovery programme and was assigned a contracted consultancy to provide free bookkeeping support, digital presence assistance, and grant writing — at no cost to the client. Six months later the owner has received two phone calls and no actual work, documents, or guidance. Calls to the consultancy now reach an unanswered line or a full voicemail. The programme administrator, when contacted, says they "cannot interfere in service delivery" and directs the owner back to the consultancy. The owner has since seen local media reporting that the firm is under federal investigation for billing the programme for services never rendered.
**What staff/org would do:** The consultancy's phone goes to a full voicemail or rings indefinitely. The programme administrator says the client must contact the consultancy directly and will not open a formal complaint. The client is trapped in a referral loop between two organisations that each point to the other.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that the business owner was owed services they never received and that the circular referral they are caught in is unacceptable. Treats their documented experience as evidence that is relevant to the federal investigation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "Being passed back and forth between a consultancy that is not answering and a programme administrator who will not intervene — while you receive nothing you were promised — is not acceptable. Let me help you put your experience on record with the people who can actually act on it."
- Direct to: (USA) SBA Office of Inspector General at sba.gov/oig — online complaint form for misuse of SBA or government-funded small business assistance; FBI Tips portal at tips.fbi.gov to report the firm if it is already named in a federal investigation; the relevant County or State programme administrator's formal written complaints process (not the phone line — write directly to the programme director); local elected representative's office for casework assistance cutting through the administrative referral loop; (general) the Federal Trade Commission at reportfraud.ftc.gov for fraudulent business practices
- Evidence to collect: Any signed enrolment or service agreement with the consultancy; full log of all calls made including dates, times, and whether they were answered; any written or email communications from the consultancy; a list of all deliverables promised versus what was actually received; any media or court documents naming the firm; records of all contacts with the programme administrator and their responses
- Feedback category tag: Service Non-Delivery — Publicly Funded Programme / Consumer Turned Away Without Resolution

---

### Scenario 18-6: Financial Adviser Complaint Unresolved After Weeks — Firm's Own Complaint Line Fails Repeatedly (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) Annual Complaints Data 2023/24 — 1,459 new financial adviser complaints received; 61% (892 complaints) related to advice suitability, up from 48% the prior year; FOS upheld 57% of suitability complaints; largest single category was defined benefit pension transfers (368 complaints); Oxford Risk analysis published in IFA Magazine; FCA requires firms to resolve complaints within 8 weeks
**The Problem:** A client received advice from an independent financial adviser to transfer their defined benefit pension into a self-invested personal pension. The transfer has resulted in significant and measurable financial loss. Six weeks ago the client raised a formal complaint directly with the adviser's firm and received an automated email acknowledgement. Since then: three calls to the complaints line resulted in hold times exceeding 40 minutes; one call was dropped after 28 minutes on hold with no callback; one agent promised to call back by end of day but did not; and one agent confirmed the complaint was "still under review" but could not name who was handling it or give any timeline. The FCA 8-week resolution deadline is now two weeks away.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue processing the complaint through a backlogged internal system. Customer-facing agents cannot access the complaint file and can only confirm "it is open." On week 7 a Final Response Letter arrives offering a partial settlement significantly below the actual documented loss, with language designed to appear generous while leaving the majority of the harm uncompensated.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges that the client has done everything correctly, been patient through a broken process, and is now at the regulatory escalation point. Notes the 8-week FCA deadline and makes the next step immediately clear.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "You have done everything right — you raised this formally and have been patient while the firm's complaint process has repeatedly let you down. There is a free, independent route available at exactly this stage, and it has a strong track record: the Financial Ombudsman Service upholds more than half of advice suitability complaints."
- Direct to: Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) at financial-ombudsman.org.uk — free to consumers, available once 8 weeks have passed from the original complaint or upon receipt of a Final Response Letter; FOS helpline: 0800 023 4567; file a separate complaint with the FCA about the firm's complaint-handling failures at fca.org.uk/consumers/how-complain; Citizens Advice financial section at citizensadvice.org.uk for independent navigation support; if the adviser is no longer trading, check FSCS eligibility at fscs.org.uk
- Evidence to collect: The original financial advice letter and suitability report; all pension transfer paperwork; the firm's automated complaint acknowledgement email with the date it was received; a written log of every call attempt (date, time, hold duration, agent name if given, what was said); any Final Response Letter or settlement offer received; current pension value statements compared to the original defined benefit scheme entitlement; evidence of the financial loss in monetary terms
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure — Complaint Not Resolved / Financial Advice Suitability / Regulatory Escalation Required

### Scenario 18-7: Fake Job Consultancy Conducts Multiple Interview Rounds by Phone Then Vanishes with Fees (Pattern A+B)
**Real Basis:** Delhi Police Cyber Cell official advisory — cyber.delhipolice.gov.in/Jobfraudcallcentre.html — formally documenting modus operandi of fake job consultancy firms using call center infrastructure as the primary fraud delivery mechanism; pattern described as ongoing; CVs sourced in bulk from Naukri.com and Shine.com; sequential fee extraction documented
**The Problem:** A job seeker's CV was sourced from a legitimate employment portal. They received a call from what presented as a reputable recruitment consultancy, referencing the CV accurately. After three professional-sounding phone "interview rounds" conducted over two weeks, the seeker was told they had been selected for a position at a named company. They were then asked to pay sequentially: a registration fee of Rs 3,000, a document verification fee of Rs 5,000, an interview scheduling fee of Rs 4,000, and a uniform advance of Rs 6,000 — totalling Rs 18,000. All calls now go unanswered. The named employer confirms they have no record of this hiring process and no relationship with the consultancy.
**What staff/org would do:** The operation has been dismantled — the number is switched off or answered by someone claiming it is a wrong number. There is no firm to contact, no complaint channel to use, and no refund mechanism. The call center infrastructure that projected credibility was specifically designed to be abandoned once payment collection was complete.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately validates the experience as a formally documented fraud type. Makes clear this is not the victim's fault and that the fraud was deliberately professional in its presentation. Stresses urgency — there is a narrow window for payment recovery through the banking system.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: true
- Opening reply: "What you have described matches a fraud pattern that Delhi Police Cyber Cell has formally documented and warned about. You are not alone — this scheme targets thousands of job seekers every year using professional call centers to appear legitimate. This is not your fault, and you need to act today to maximise the chance of recovering your money."
- Direct to: National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal at cybercrime.gov.in — file a complaint immediately; National Cyber Crime Helpline: 1930 (available 24 hours); local police Cyber Cell in person — bring all evidence; the bank used to make the payments — request an immediate payment recall and report as fraud, citing the specific UTR transaction numbers; the job portal where the CV was listed (Naukri.com, Shine.com, LinkedIn) — report the fraudulent scraping of your profile so they can warn other users; Consumer Helpline at 1915 (National Consumer Helpline) to register the fraud; Sanchar Saathi portal at sancharsaathi.gov.in to report the mobile number used by the fraudsters so it can be blocked
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of all call logs — incoming and outgoing — with dates and times; any WhatsApp or SMS messages received; all emails including full headers; any website or social media profiles of the fake firm (screenshot immediately before they disappear); payment receipts and bank transfer records with UTR numbers; the named employer's written or spoken denial; the names and titles of all agents who called; any documents or offer letters sent by the fake firm
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud — Fake Credentials / Disappeared with Payment / Call Center as Fraud Infrastructure

### Scenario 18-8: Consultancy Partner Helped a Bidder While Simultaneously Advising the Buyer — Conflict Never Disclosed (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Australian National Audit Office finding and Canberra Times reporting, May 2021 — Deloitte Australia Department of Home Affairs contract terminated for undisclosed conflict of interest; contract grew from $151,800 to $1.5M before termination; probity breach confirmed by independent law firm Maddocks; no individual disciplined
**The Problem:** A business owner discovers through a media report that the consultancy they contracted as a procurement adviser — helping them evaluate vendors and structure the selection process — was simultaneously providing support to one of the companies actively bidding in that same process. This dual role was never disclosed. The vendor that was ultimately selected may have benefited from the consultancy's insider knowledge of the buyer's evaluation criteria and weighting. Contracts based on that evaluation have already been signed.
**What staff/org would do:** Issue a written statement confirming the engagement has been "terminated" and that the firm "takes conflicts of interest seriously." Decline to state specifically what information was shared between its buyer-advisory team and its bidder-support work, citing "client confidentiality obligations to both parties." Point to the termination of the engagement as resolution.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges this is a serious governance breach with potential legal and financial implications for the business. Does not accept the firm's statement as closure or wait for the consultancy's internal review to conclude before advising the client on their rights.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "Discovering that your adviser was helping one of your vendors at the same time — without telling you — is a serious conflict of interest that may directly affect the validity of the contract you signed. You have the right to know exactly what information was shared, and there are formal channels to pursue that."
- Direct to: A commercial solicitor to assess whether the undisclosed conflict of interest provides grounds to void, renegotiate, or claim damages on the vendor contract; (Australia) Accounting Professional and Ethical Standards Board at apesb.org.au to report the professional conduct breach; Australian National Audit Office at anao.gov.au if a government body was involved; (UK) ICAEW Professional Conduct team at icaew.com/complaints; (USA) AICPA Ethics Division; the consultancy's Conflicts of Interest partner or Chief Risk Officer in writing, formally requesting full disclosure of what information was shared and with whom
- Evidence to collect: Signed engagement letter and scope of work with the consultancy; all evaluation reports, vendor scoring matrices, and selection recommendation documents it produced; the contract signed with the selected vendor; any communications from the consultancy about conflicts of interest checks (or the absence of any such communication); all invoices paid; media reports or audit office findings referencing the firm
- Feedback category tag: Corruption — Undisclosed Conflict of Interest / Procurement Misconduct / Staff Dual Role

### Scenario 18-9: Consulting Firm's Strategy Advice Directed Targeting of Vulnerable Patients — Community Harm Documented (Pattern C+E)
**Real Basis:** DOJ press release, December 2024 — United States v. McKinsey and Company Inc.; $650M federal civil and criminal settlement (False Claims Act violations and obstruction); approximately $115M in prior state and local government settlements; total approximately $765M; first instance of a management consulting firm facing criminal liability for advice that caused a client to commit a crime; former senior partner Martin Elling indicted for deleting electronic materials to obstruct the investigation; NPR and CBS News reporting
**The Problem:** A healthcare professional or community advocacy organisation is raising a formal concern: a major consulting firm's strategic advice directed a pharmaceutical manufacturer to target the highest-volume opioid prescribers with the most aggressive sales tactics, specifically designed to drive higher dosages and broader prescribing regardless of clinical need. Patients in their community have suffered documented harm as a result. The professional wants to know whether this constitutes a recognised legal wrong, where to direct their concern formally, and how to ensure it is part of the accountability record.
**What staff/org would do:** Refer the concern to the firm's Communications or Legal department. Issue a statement noting that "the firm has resolved this matter with the Department of Justice and accepts responsibility as described in the resolution." Decline to provide specifics of how the strategy was developed or who authorised it. Present the settlement as closure and point to the firm's "transformation" since.
**What the Owner does:** Validates that the concern is grounded in a formally adjudicated legal record and that community-level documentation strengthens the ongoing public accountability process. Notes that settlement distribution processes exist for affected communities. Does not accept "the settlement resolves it" as a complete answer.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: false
- Opening reply: "Your concern is grounded in a formally documented legal record — this conduct led to the largest settlement ever imposed on a management consulting firm, including criminal charges and obstruction of justice. Your report matters both for accountability and for ensuring affected communities can access any remediation available to them."
- Direct to: (USA) DOJ Office of Public Affairs at justice.gov to confirm participation in any victim notification or settlement distribution process related to the resolution; state Attorneys General offices in states that reached separate settlements — many have victim assistance processes; the court-appointed monitor or compliance officer under the Deferred Prosecution Agreement if one has been established; opioid community advocacy organisations such as End Overdose (endoverdose.net) and Community Anti-Drug Coalitions of America (cadca.org) for coordination with affected community efforts; academic and public health research institutions documenting opioid harm for addition to the evidence base
- Evidence to collect: Any documented patient outcome records the professional is authorised to hold; clinical communications or directives referencing the consulting firm's strategy in the prescribing context; published academic research or court documents linking the strategy to outcomes in the relevant community; the professional's own contemporaneous case notes and observations; local epidemiological data showing prescribing trends correlated with the strategy period
- Feedback category tag: Rights Violation — Consulting Advice Causing Documented Community Harm / Conflict of Interest / Deliverable Causing Public Harm

### Scenario 18-10: Elderly Client's Life Savings Transferred by Trusted Financial Adviser into Personal Account (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** FCA enforcement record — Paul Michael Grant, convicted 11 January 2019 on 11 counts of fraud by abuse of position and 3 counts of theft; £2.1 million stolen from clients including specifically vulnerable individuals; subsequent lifetime FCA ban; Professional Adviser trade publication; Ashcotts Solicitors case commentary; FCA documented pattern of adviser non-response after funds transferred
**The Problem:** An elderly client trusted their financial adviser — a relationship of many years — with management of their retirement savings. The adviser persuaded the client to consolidate funds into a new account "for more effective management." The client has now tried to access their savings and found the account balance is zero. When the client calls the adviser's number, there is no answer. A family member has contacted the advisory firm's main office; the firm says the adviser is "no longer with us" and directs the family to a general customer service line that places them on hold for extended periods without any resolution or protective action. The family believes the adviser has stolen the money.
**What staff/org would do:** The firm's customer service team logs the call and says the matter has been "referred to our compliance team for review." The family is told to expect contact within 10 working days. No immediate protective action is taken. The firm declines to confirm whether the client's funds are held anywhere recoverable and does not proactively contact any regulator.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an emergency — a vulnerable elderly person has had their life savings stolen by someone in a position of professional trust. Immediate action is required and the 10-working-day compliance timeline is not acceptable.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent: true
- Opening reply: "This is an emergency. A vulnerable person may have had their entire life savings stolen by someone they trusted with their finances. Please do not wait for the firm's compliance team — there are steps you need to take right now that may make the difference between recovering funds and not."
- Direct to: (UK) FCA Consumer Helpline immediately at 0800 111 6768 — report theft by a financial adviser and verify the adviser's current regulated status; Action Fraud at actionfraud.police.uk or 0300 123 2040 — report financial fraud citing "fraud by abuse of position" specifically; Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS) at fscs.org.uk — may cover up to £85,000 per institution if the firm is FCA-authorised and has failed; the client's own bank — request an immediate freeze on any outgoing transfers from accounts the adviser had signatory or third-party access to; Police via 101 (or 999 if the client is in immediate distress) — financial abuse of a vulnerable adult is a criminal matter independently of the FCA process; local authority Adult Safeguarding team if the client is an adult at risk under the Care Act 2014; the firm's CEO and Compliance Director simultaneously in writing, requesting immediate account-level protective action — not a standard complaints process
- Evidence to collect: All account statements and balance records before and after the consolidation transfer; signed letters of authority granted to the adviser; any communications from the adviser requesting fund movements; the adviser's full name, FCA registration number (if known), and any contact details held; a log of all calls to the firm with dates, hold times, and names of agents spoken to; the firm's name, address, and FCA firm reference number (searchable at register.fca.org.uk); any Power of Attorney documents that gave the adviser access; emails or letters from the adviser about the account consolidation
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud — Theft by Adviser / Vulnerable Client / Emergency Safeguarding

## 19. Legal Services

### Scenario 19-1: Stolen Settlement Funds — Plane Crash Family Cannot Reach Their Lawyer (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** U.S. Department of Justice, USAO-CDCA press releases; ABA Journal; State Bar of California disciplinary records — Tom Girardi / Girardi Keese Law Firm, California, USA, fraud 2010–2020, conviction 2024
**The Problem:** A family in Los Angeles won a wrongful death settlement through Girardi Keese after a relative died in the Lion Air Flight 610 crash. They have been waiting years for disbursement. They learn from a news broadcast that Girardi has been convicted for stealing over $15 million from clients — including Lion Air families. They call the firm number immediately: it is disconnected. They call the State Bar's general line and wait on hold. They do not know if their settlement money still exists or whether they will ever receive it.
**What staff/org would do:** The bankruptcy trustee's voicemail says to submit a written claim with no timeline given. The State Bar hold message directs them to a website. No one confirms the settlement amount owed, whether the money was spent, or what recourse exists. The call ends with the family knowing nothing more than when they started.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the betrayal and financial urgency directly. Confirm that the State Bar of California Client Security Fund exists specifically for this — reimbursing clients defrauded by licensed California attorneys. Tell them to contact the USAO-CDCA victim notification line, file a claim with the bankruptcy court, and engage a new attorney immediately to assert their priority claim in the estate.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your family was defrauded by your own attorney — this is a recognized crime and there are specific funds and processes designed to help you recover your money. Let me give you the exact contacts right now."
- Who to direct to: State Bar of California Client Security Fund (1-800-843-9053); U.S. Bankruptcy Court Central District of California victim claims portal (cacb.uscourts.gov); USAO-CDCA victim notification line (213-894-6867); LA County Bar Lawyer Referral Service (213-243-1525) for a new attorney
- Evidence to preserve: Original settlement agreement signed by Girardi, all correspondence from Girardi Keese, disbursement instructions or bank transfer records, original retainer agreement, any checks or wire transfer confirmations received from the firm
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Stolen settlement funds / Attorney fraud / Financial harm

### Scenario 19-2: Entire Law Firm Collapses Overnight — £64.5 Million Gone and Completion in Four Days (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** SRA press release; Law Gazette; Serious Fraud Office criminal charges (GOV.UK); SRA Client Protection Annual Report 2022/23 — Axiom Ince / Pragnesh Modhwadia, London, England, UK, SRA intervention October 2023
**The Problem:** A first-time buyer in London has a property conveyancing matter with Axiom Ince. They have already exchanged contracts, with their £200,000 deposit held in the firm's client account. Their completion date is in four days. News breaks that the SRA has intervened — the firm has stopped trading, over 1,400 staff dismissed, and £64.5 million in client money is unaccounted for. They call the SRA's intervention team and are placed on hold for 40 minutes. When an agent answers, they are told to register on the portal and expect contact within a few weeks. Completion cannot wait weeks.
**What staff/org would do:** The SRA portal acknowledges the submission with a reference number. No one calls back. No one explains whether the exchange deposit is secure, whether the completion can be protected, or what happens if completion is missed. The client faces potential breach of contract for missing completion through no fault of their own.
**What the Owner does:** Act immediately on two fronts. The SRA Compensation Fund does protect client account balances — but the completion deadline is a separate problem that requires a new solicitor today, who can contact the other party's solicitor to agree an emergency extension and apply for any court protection needed. Do not wait for the SRA process to resolve the completion timeline.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your deposit is protected under the SRA Compensation Fund — but your four-day completion deadline is a separate and urgent problem the SRA process alone cannot solve. You need a new solicitor instructed today. Here is exactly what to do."
- Who to direct to: SRA intervention team (0370 606 2555 / sra.org.uk/consumers/problems/report-a-solicitor); Law Society Find a Solicitor (solicitors.lawsociety.org.uk) for urgent new instruction; Serious Fraud Office victim support (0207 239 7272); Legal Ombudsman (0300 555 0333) to register the complaint; alert the other party's solicitor directly to avoid being placed in breach of contract
- Evidence to preserve: Exchange contract with deposit amount, completion statement, bank transfer proof of deposit, all Axiom Ince correspondence, mortgage offer documents, search results
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Firm collapse / Stolen client funds / Financial harm / Urgent

### Scenario 19-3: Hired a Fake Lawyer — Court Hearing in Three Weeks and No Real Representation (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office press release; California State Bar press release — Efferin Deans / "Law Offices of Deans & Associates," Canoga Park, Los Angeles, California, USA, charged October 2021
**The Problem:** A consumer in the San Fernando Valley hired "Deans & Associates" after finding their business card. They paid a $3,500 retainer for a civil landlord-tenant dispute. The firm appeared legitimate — an office address, business cards showing bar credentials, court filings under Deans' name. They have a hearing in 21 days. A friend sends them a news link: Deans has been charged by the LA County DA with felony impersonation and has never held a California bar license. They call Deans' number: disconnected. They call the LA County Superior Court clerk to ask whether the filed documents are valid and are told "we can't give legal advice." They call the California State Bar helpline and are placed on hold for 18 minutes.
**What staff/org would do:** The State Bar helpline eventually tells them to submit a written complaint online. No one addresses the upcoming hearing. No one explains whether court filings submitted by an unlicensed person have any legal standing or whether the judge will be notified. The client is left managing an active court case with no lawyer and 21 days until the hearing.
**What the Owner does:** Make clear this is urgent. The court must be notified of fraudulent representation and can grant a continuance of the hearing. The client should contact the LA County Superior Court clerk immediately to advise of the fraud and request continuance by written motion. A licensed replacement attorney must be found before the hearing date. Fee recovery may be available through the State Bar Client Security Fund.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is urgent — your hearing is 21 days away and your representation was fraudulent. The court must be notified now and a continuance requested. Here is exactly what to do first."
- Who to direct to: LA County Superior Court case clerk — notify the clerk of fraudulent prior counsel and request a continuance; California State Bar Unlicensed Practice of Law hotline (800-843-9053); California State Bar Client Security Fund (800-843-9053) for fee recovery; California Lawyers Association Referral Service (415-538-2250) for licensed attorney; LA County DA Victim Services (213-974-3817)
- Evidence to preserve: Business card from Deans showing claimed bar credentials, retainer agreement and payment receipt, all court filings bearing Deans' name, bank records of all payments made, screenshots or printouts of Deans' advertising or website, photos of the office, all text and email communications with Deans
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Fake lawyer / Impersonation / Rights violation / Urgent

### Scenario 19-4: Billed for Phone Calls That Never Happened — 83 Phantom Call Entries on the Invoice (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** SRA disciplinary notices; Legal Futures ("Fraud, overbilling and more — SRA bans non-solicitors over misconduct") — National Accident Law / Chantelle Dallas, England, UK, overbilling identified 2020, SRA action 2021–2022
**The Problem:** A personal injury claimant in Manchester receives their final bill from a high-volume personal injury firm. Reviewing the itemized schedule, they spot 21 separate "telephone attendance" charges at £50 each — totalling £1,050 — on dates when they have no recollection of being contacted. They check their mobile bill: 17 of those calls simply do not appear on their records. They call the firm's billing department and are transferred to three different people. The third person states "all entries are verified in the file" and promises a callback within 48 hours. No call comes. A second attempt produces the same promise and the same silence.
**What staff/org would do:** A billing supervisor eventually emails to say the entries were "confirmed by a paralegal supervisor." No itemized justification is provided. The firm issues a final demand for the full amount and treats the matter as closed.
**What the Owner does:** Tell the client that mobile phone records showing calls never occurred are direct evidence of billing fraud — fabricating time entries, not a billing estimate dispute. The process is: first, a formal written complaint to the firm's named Complaints Partner under the SRA-mandated internal complaints procedure. If unresolved or rejected within 8 weeks, escalate to the Legal Ombudsman. In parallel, report the fabricated entries to the SRA as a conduct matter.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Charging for phone calls that never took place is not a billing disagreement — it is fabrication of records, and your mobile call log is strong evidence. Here is the exact process to report this and recover the money."
- Who to direct to: Legal Ombudsman (0300 555 0333, legalombudsman.org.uk) after the 8-week firm complaint process; SRA (0370 606 2555, sra.org.uk/consumers/problems/report-a-solicitor) for conduct complaint about fabricated records; Citizens Advice (0800 144 8848) for free immediate guidance
- Evidence to preserve: Itemized bill from the firm listing every telephone attendance charge, mobile phone bill for all dates in question (request itemized records from your provider if not held), all email and postal correspondence with the firm, a written log of every phone call you recall including dates and staff names, any text message confirmations from the firm regarding calls
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Billing fraud / Overbilling / Staff misconduct / Corruption

### Scenario 19-6: Estate Planning Done by a Fake Partner — Will May Not Be Valid (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Court records; Smithsonian Magazine — Kimberly Kitchen / McKean County, Pennsylvania, USA, fraud approximately 2003–2013, convicted
**The Problem:** An 80-year-old widower in rural Pennsylvania had their late spouse's estate and will drafted by Kitchen, who served as a partner at a local firm and as president of the county bar association. Following Kitchen's criminal conviction — she had forged her law degree, bar exam results, and registration documents, and had never attended law school or been admitted to the bar — the widower calls the county courthouse to ask whether the will is still legally valid. The clerk says they cannot give legal advice. They call the Pennsylvania Bar Association referral line and wait 22 minutes before hanging up. Their adult child calls on their behalf and also reaches voicemail. The family has no idea whether the estate documents hold legal force.
**What staff/org would do:** Callers are directed between the courthouse, the bar association, and the disciplinary board. None of them explains whether documents drafted by an unlicensed person are valid under Pennsylvania law. The 80-year-old is left with no guidance and no clear route to get estate administration back on track.
**What the Owner does:** Reassure the caller that in Pennsylvania, documents drafted by an unlicensed person may still be legally valid if they accurately reflect the testator's true intent — but this must be confirmed by a licensed attorney reviewing the specific documents. Direct them to the Pennsylvania Lawyers Fund for Client Security for fee recovery, and to a licensed estate solicitor. Recognize the vulnerability of this caller — elderly, bereaved, in a rural area — and keep the guidance simple and accessible.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "A will drafted by someone who was not a licensed attorney may still be legally valid depending on its content — but you need a licensed attorney to confirm this for your specific situation. Do not destroy or alter any documents. Here is where to go."
- Who to direct to: Pennsylvania Lawyers Fund for Client Security (1-800-962-4618) for recovery of fees paid to Kitchen; Pennsylvania Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service (1-800-692-7375); McKean County Legal Services for elderly or low-income assistance; Pennsylvania Office of Disciplinary Counsel (717-772-8572) for reporting
- Evidence to preserve: The will and all estate planning documents signed by Kitchen, any power of attorney documents, fee receipts and retainer agreements, all correspondence bearing the law firm's letterhead, any instructions or notes from Kitchen about the estate's structure
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Fake lawyer / Estate fraud / Vulnerable consumer / Consumer turned away

### Scenario 19-7: Cannot Navigate the Complaint System — No One Will Explain Who to Contact (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Legal Services Consumer Panel 2024 Tracker Survey; Legal Ombudsman 2024/25 Annual Complaints Data and Insight — England and Wales, UK, 2024 (14% of affected consumers cited provider being too hard to reach; 21% did not know how to complain; 49% of firms found to handle first-tier complaints inadequately)
**The Problem:** A homeowner in England instructed a conveyancing solicitor to purchase a property. Six months after completion, they discovered a restrictive covenant on the title — not disclosed before exchange — that prevents the extension they planned to build. They try to raise a complaint with the firm. The client care letter they received runs to nine pages with no clear complaint procedure signposted. They call the firm and are told to email the enquiries address. They send the email and receive an auto-acknowledgement. Three weeks pass with no substantive response. They search online and find five different bodies — the SRA, Legal Ombudsman, Law Society, Citizens Advice, and the Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal — with no explanation of which one handles which type of issue or in what order they should be contacted.
**What staff/org would do:** After 15 working days the enquiries inbox sends a second auto-reply stating the matter is being reviewed internally. No written acknowledgment of the complaint is issued, violating the SRA's Transparency Rules. No one explains what reviewed means or sets a timeline for a substantive response.
**What the Owner does:** Provide the exact escalation path in order. Step one: formal written complaint to the firm's named Complaints Partner — every SRA-regulated firm is required to designate one. Step two: if no substantive response within 8 weeks, escalate to the Legal Ombudsman, who handles service quality complaints. The SRA handles conduct failures, not service quality — make the distinction clear. Note that non-disclosure of a restrictive covenant may also constitute professional negligence worth a specialist solicitor's opinion.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The complaint process for solicitors is genuinely confusing — 21% of UK consumers do not know how to complain, and nearly half of law firms handle first-tier complaints inadequately. Let me give you the exact steps in clear order."
- Who to direct to: Legal Ombudsman (0300 555 0333, legalombudsman.org.uk) — after 8 weeks of firm non-response or after rejection; SRA (0370 606 2555, sra.org.uk) — for conduct failures including not complying with complaints transparency rules; Citizens Advice (0800 144 8848) for free immediate guidance; Professional Negligence Lawyers Association (pnla.org.uk) for referral to a specialist if the covenant failure caused financial loss
- Evidence to preserve: Original client care letter, all correspondence with the solicitor, the completion statement, the title register extract showing the restrictive covenant, any pre-purchase searches or property information forms provided by the firm, all emails and letters related to the complaint with timestamps
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Inaccessible complaint process / Transparency / Information failure

### Scenario 19-8: Law Firm Will Not Answer the Phone — Limitation Deadline in 34 Days (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Clio Legal Trends Report 2022 (secret shopper methodology, USA and Canada) — more than 50% of law firms did not respond to client inquiries; only 41% of responding firms provided fee information; 73% of secret shoppers said they were unlikely to recommend the firm
**The Problem:** A small business owner in Toronto, Ontario, needs to file a civil claim against a former supplier for breach of contract. They found a firm advertising commercial litigation services, with four named lawyers on their website. Over three days they have called the main number twice (voicemail each time), emailed via the contact form (auto-reply only), and sent a LinkedIn message to one of the named partners. Nothing. Their statutory limitation period for filing the claim is 34 days away. They do not know whether to keep waiting, find another firm, or whether missing this deadline will permanently destroy their case.
**What staff/org would do:** The firm's voicemail says calls will be returned within one business day. No human calls back. The contact form auto-reply says the firm aims to respond within five business days. Ten business days pass. The limitation deadline closes in with no legal representation in place.
**What the Owner does:** Tell the client that documented research shows more than half of law firms fail to respond to initial contact — waiting further is not a strategy when a hard legal deadline is involved. A 34-day limitation period is a statutory cutoff in Ontario. They must find a new firm today using the Law Society of Ontario Lawyer Referral Service, which offers a free first 30-minute consultation, and brief that firm about the urgency of the deadline.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "A 34-day limitation deadline is not something you can risk on a firm that is not responding. Research shows more than half of law firms fail to answer initial contact. Find a new firm today — here is exactly how."
- Who to direct to: Law Society of Ontario Lawyer Referral Service (1-800-268-8326) — free first 30-minute consultation with a commercial litigation solicitor; Ontario Ministry of the Attorney General public legal information (416-979-1446); Law Society of Ontario Complaints line (416-947-3310) if non-response by a lawyer involves unprofessional conduct
- Evidence to preserve: Record of all contact attempts with exact dates and times — voicemail, email delivery confirmation, LinkedIn screenshots; screenshots of the firm's website and advertised practice areas; all auto-reply emails received; your underlying contract documents; all correspondence with the supplier showing the breach
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Inaccessible / Call center failure / Information failure / Non-response

### Scenario 19-9: Solicitor Missed the Court Deadline — Client Lost Their Appeal Through Filing Failure (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Little v Bloomsbury Law Solicitors [2022] 11 WLUK 351; professional negligence commentary — Bindmans LLP, Limes Solicitors; England, UK, 2022
**The Problem:** A homeowner in London engaged solicitors at Bloomsbury Law to pursue a property dispute appeal. The court issued an "unless order" — a conditional order dismissing the appeal unless the appeal bundle was filed within 35 days. The solicitors became aware of this order nearly two years after it was issued, having failed to monitor court correspondence. They missed the filing deadline. The court refused relief from sanctions and dismissed the appeal. The client is now calling the firm to understand what happened and what their options are. The partner apologizes and says the matter is under internal review — but provides nothing in writing and does not mention professional negligence claims.
**What staff/org would do:** An internal review letter arrives three weeks later acknowledging an administrative oversight. The letter is written defensively, does not quantify the client's loss, does not refer the client to a professional negligence specialist, and makes no offer of compensation. The firm does not proactively explain the client's right to complain to the Legal Ombudsman.
**What the Owner does:** Inform the client clearly: a solicitor missing a court deadline that directly caused loss of their right to appeal is a textbook professional negligence case. The client has the right to claim compensation from the firm and its professional indemnity insurers. They must not accept any informal resolution from the firm without first taking independent legal advice. They have six years from the negligent act to bring a professional negligence claim.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "A solicitor missing a court-imposed deadline that caused you to lose your right of appeal is professional negligence. You have the right to claim compensation. Do not accept any informal offer from the firm without independent legal advice first."
- Who to direct to: Legal Ombudsman (0300 555 0333, legalombudsman.org.uk) for service complaint — must be brought within 6 years of the act and 3 years of the client knowing; SRA (0370 606 2555) for conduct failure; Professional Negligence Lawyers Association (pnla.org.uk) for a free referral to a specialist; Law Society Find a Solicitor (solicitors.lawsociety.org.uk) for independent advice
- Evidence to preserve: All court orders including the unless order and the date it was issued, all correspondence with Bloomsbury Law about the appeal, all instructions given to the firm, any records of when the firm told you about the unless order, the court decision refusing relief from sanctions, any settlement offers received from the firm in writing
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Solicitor negligence / Missed court deadline / Rights lost / Professional misconduct
