### Scenario 19-10: Solicitor Abandoned Practice — Elderly Widow Cannot Reach Anyone or Retrieve Her File (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Solicitors Disciplinary Tribunal decision; Legal Futures ("Solicitor left abandoned law firm in chaotic state") — Universal Solicitors, sole practitioner, England, UK, struck off 2022
**The Problem:** An 80-year-old widow in England instructed a sole-practitioner solicitor two years ago to handle her late husband's probate and estate administration. For the past four months, every call to the firm rings out. Letters go unreturned. She visited the office and found it locked, with an overflowing letterbox and unopened mail visible through the glass. She has no copy of her file, does not know whether probate has been granted, and does not know whether the estate's assets are being protected. When she calls the SRA consumer helpline she is asked to submit a report online — a form requiring the solicitor's SRA registration number, which she does not have. The form takes 20 minutes to complete and generates a reference number with an estimated 8–12 week acknowledgement timeline. No emergency mechanism is offered.
**What staff/org would do:** The SRA acknowledges the online report with an automated email and a case reference number. No contact follows in the first four weeks. No one explains that the SRA can intervene and appoint a substitute solicitor to take over abandoned files. The widow is left managing bereavement and estate uncertainty entirely alone.
**What the Owner does:** Recognize this as a potential abandonment case — the SRA has the power to intervene in a firm and appoint a replacement solicitor to take over client files. Help the caller find the solicitor's SRA ID number using the SRA's public Register, searchable by firm name, so her report is filed accurately and triggers intervention review. Also advise her that the grant of probate is a public record held by the Probate Registry and can be checked to confirm the current status of the estate.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Four months of total silence from a solicitor handling your husband's estate is not acceptable and may legally be abandonment. The SRA can intervene and appoint a new solicitor to take over your file — you have the right to request this. Let me help you take the first step."
- Who to direct to: SRA consumer helpline (0370 606 2555, sra.org.uk/consumers/problems/report-a-solicitor); SRA Register — search by firm or solicitor name to get the SRA registration number (sra.org.uk/consumers/solicitor-check); HM Courts and Tribunals Probate Registry to check whether probate has been granted (hmcourts-service.gov.uk); Age UK (0800 678 1602) for help navigating the process; Citizens Advice (0800 144 8848) for immediate support; Legal Ombudsman (0300 555 0333) for parallel complaint
- Evidence to preserve: All retainer or instruction letters from the firm, receipts for any fees paid, copies of all documents provided to the solicitor — retain originals yourself, particularly the will and death certificate; a written log of every phone call attempt with dates; letters sent and proof of postage; photos of the locked office if visited
- Feedback category tag: Legal / Abandoned case / Sole practitioner failure / Vulnerable consumer / Wait and no resolution

## 20. Construction / Real Estate Development

### Scenario 20-1: Resident Reports Widening Structural Cracks and Pooling Water — Complaint Logged as Routine Maintenance (Pattern B+D)
**Real Basis:** Champlain Towers South (Surfside, Florida) collapse, June 24, 2021; 2018 Morabito structural engineering report; 98 deaths; $1.2 billion settlement approved June 2022. NPR, PBS NewsHour, CBS Miami, Insurance Journal.
**The Problem:** A resident of a 12-story beachfront condominium has a copy of an engineering report circulated to building management three years ago flagging major structural damage to the concrete slab below the pool deck. This morning she has observed new water infiltration pooling in the underground garage and can see the cracks documented in the report appear visibly wider. She has attended building meetings at which repairs were deferred twice due to cost. She is now calling the property management company's complaint line.
**What staff/org would do:** The call handler tells her this is a "known maintenance item already in the work queue" and that a contractor will be scheduled "in the coming months." The call is logged as a general maintenance request. No supervisor is alerted, no structural engineer is contacted, and she is told not to worry because the building passed its last inspection.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately recognises this as a potential life-safety emergency rather than a maintenance request. Flags the call for real-time escalation to building management and the local building authority. Confirms the resident's right to demand an emergency structural inspection.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — widening cracks in structural concrete combined with new water infiltration in the garage — is a situation that must be treated as a potential structural emergency, not a maintenance queue item. I want to help you get an immediate response today."
- Who to direct to: Local municipal or county building official (emergency inspection request, same day); local Fire and Rescue Service (independent powers to condemn unsafe structures and order evacuation); state department of buildings or equivalent; if the building official is unresponsive, escalate to the county emergency management office. In Florida, also the Florida Department of Business and Professional Regulation (DBPR) building codes enforcement.
- Evidence to collect: Photograph and date-stamp all visible cracks with a ruler for scale; make a video of the pooling water with timestamp; retrieve and preserve the engineering report previously shared by management; note the full name, date, and time of the call handler who dismissed the concern; obtain the maintenance ticket reference number.
- Feedback category tag: Structural Safety Emergency / Construction Defect / Complaint Dismissal / Urgent

### Scenario 20-2: Disabled Veteran Loses $221,000 Retirement Savings to ADU Contractor — State Hid 259 Complaints (Pattern A+E)
**Real Basis:** Anchored Tiny Homes, Sacramento, California. 450+ homeowners affected; $12.8 million in liabilities; Chapter 7 bankruptcy filed July 2024; CSLB license revoked December 2024; 10,000+ complaints hidden from public over five years per NBC Bay Area investigation. NBC Bay Area, ABC7 San Francisco, California CSLB enforcement records.
**The Problem:** A disabled Army veteran paid $221,000 — his entire retirement savings — to an ADU builder for a backyard unit that was never constructed. He signed the contract in January 2024 after checking the contractor's California Contractors State License Board (CSLB) license page and seeing no complaints. The contractor never pulled a permit, stopped returning calls, and filed Chapter 7 bankruptcy in July 2024. The veteran has now discovered that three complaints about this contractor were filed with the CSLB in December 2023 — the same month he signed — but those complaints were hidden from the public license lookup tool because the contractor settled them individually with the complainants. He is calling the CSLB consumer hotline to understand what recourse exists.
**What staff/org would do:** The CSLB call handler confirms the license was suspended in July 2024 and revoked in December 2024. When asked why December 2023 complaints were not visible when he checked before signing, the agent explains complaints are removed from public record when a contractor settles individually with consumers. The handler offers to mail a complaint form. No mention is made of the contractor's surety bond, any restitution fund, or legal referral services.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the veteran's experience as a documented systemic failure, explains the CSLB complaint-suppression mechanism clearly, and connects him to every available financial recovery pathway.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (financial harm already sustained; no ongoing physical danger)
- Opening reply: "I am very sorry this happened to you. Losing your entire retirement savings to a contractor who never broke ground — and then discovering that warnings about this company were hidden from you by the state's own licensing system — is a serious injustice. There are still meaningful steps you can take toward recovery, and I want to walk you through them."
- Who to direct to: California CSLB — file a claim against the contractor's mandatory surety bond (contractors are required to carry a $25,000 bond; file immediately as bond claims have deadlines); CSLB Arbitration Program; California Department of Consumer Affairs; California Attorney General consumer protection complaint line; State Bar of California Lawyer Referral Service (many consumer fraud attorneys take construction fraud cases on contingency); the Chapter 7 bankruptcy trustee (file a proof of claim as a creditor in the bankruptcy proceeding).
- Evidence to collect: Original signed contract; all payment receipts and bank wire/transfer records; all communications with the contractor (texts, emails, voicemails); screenshot of the CSLB license page as it appeared before and after suspension; results of any permit search showing no permits pulled; the bankruptcy case number and court filing; any marketing materials or advertisements.
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud / Abandoned Project / Regulatory Complaint Suppression / Vulnerability

### Scenario 20-3: Homeowner Calls "Local Emergency Plumber" — Reaches Overseas Call Center, No One Arrives, Card Charged (Pattern E+A)
**Real Basis:** Premium Home Service (B.E.S.T. GDR LLC), Chicago, Illinois. 15,000+ fake local business listings; $79 million+ in suspicious profit; calls routed to Philippines call center; fabricated five-star reviews including stolen profile photos. FTC/DOJ lawsuit filed May 11, 2026. FTC press release, DOJ press release, Chicago Sun-Times.
**The Problem:** A homeowner searched online for an emergency plumber and clicked the top local result — showing a Chicago address, hundreds of reviews, and a 4.8-star rating. She called, spoke to a friendly agent who confirmed a plumber would arrive within two hours, and gave her credit card number to "hold" the appointment. Four hours later, no one arrived. Three call-back attempts each result in a new agent placing her on hold for 20 minutes before disconnecting. On the third call she is told the technician is "running behind" and will arrive within the hour. She has now discovered the listing appears to be a fake and the "reviews" may have been fabricated.
**What staff/org would do:** Each call-back connects to a different overseas agent who gives a new false arrival window, asks her to confirm her card details for a "service confirmation," and then either places her in a hold queue or disconnects. When she disputes the charge with her bank, the merchant listed is a generic LLC with a Philippine address and no phone number she can trace.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as a documented national fraud scheme confirmed by FTC and DOJ enforcement action; helps the homeowner stop further financial harm immediately and file complaints in the right places.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (financial harm and deception; the original plumbing problem may still need a legitimate local plumber)
- Opening reply: "What you have described — a 'local' business showing a fake Chicago address that routes calls to an overseas call center with no actual local technicians — matches a major documented fraud scheme that the FTC and US Department of Justice filed suit against in May 2026. You are not alone. Here is what to do right now."
- Who to direct to: Contact your bank or card issuer immediately to dispute the charge and block the merchant (act within 24 hours to maximise dispute success); FTC ReportFraud.ftc.gov (file a consumer complaint — your report feeds into the active enforcement case); Illinois Attorney General consumer fraud hotline (this scheme includes state-level Illinois charges); BBB Scam Tracker (to warn other consumers); Google Maps and Yelp (both have fake-business reporting portals — flag the listing).
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of the listing exactly as it appeared (name, address, star rating, review count, photos); recordings or notes of every call (date, time, agent name or ID, what was said); bank or card statement showing the charge; any email or text confirmation of the appointment; the merchant name and address as it appears on the card statement.
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Fraud / Fake Business Listing / Financial Overcharge / Abandoned Service

### Scenario 20-4: Earthquake Survivor Seeks Accountability for Hotel Built on River Sand and Illegal Floors — Public Officials Shielded (Pattern B+E+D)
**Real Basis:** Isias Grand Hotel, Adiyaman, Turkey. February 6, 2023 earthquake (7.8 magnitude); 25 hotel deaths; 150-page technical report confirming river-sand concrete, unauthorized 2016 floor addition, 2018 government amnesty bypass; owner and architect sentenced to 18 years each in 2024. Up to 75,000 buildings covered by amnesty schemes. Al Jazeera, CNN, The Dial investigative journalism, The National News.
**The Problem:** A teacher who survived the collapse of the Isias Grand Hotel — where she had been attending a chess tournament — is filing a formal complaint with the regional disaster authority. She has read the 150-page technical investigation confirming the hotel was built with river sand rather than proper aggregate concrete, had an unauthorized floor added in 2016 without a permit, and was covered under a 2018 government amnesty scheme that bypassed safety recertification requirements. Criminal convictions of the hotel owner and architect have occurred. She now wants to know why the officials who approved the amnesty scheme and renewed the building's certificates are not being prosecuted, and what compensation is available for her physical injuries and psychological trauma.
**What staff/org would do:** The regional authority agent refers her to the criminal court process already underway for the hotel owner and says compensation is "a civil litigation matter." When she asks specifically about the officials who approved the amnesty and the permits, the agent says those individuals are "the subject of a separate government review" with no further information. No victim support referral is offered. The call ends without any escalation.
**What the Owner does:** Explains the documented legal landscape — criminal convictions have occurred but the accountability gap for public officials is confirmed by investigators — and directs the survivor to victim compensation channels and independent legal advocacy.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (injuries already sustained; survivor's immediate physical safety not currently at risk)
- Opening reply: "I am deeply sorry for what you survived and for the loss of those who did not. The failures that killed people in that hotel — substandard concrete, an illegal extra floor, and a government amnesty scheme that allowed a dangerous building to remain certified — have been confirmed by a detailed technical investigation. You have every right to seek accountability and compensation, and there are pathways beyond the regional authority."
- Who to direct to: Turkish Disaster and Emergency Management Authority (AFAD) victim support office; Turkish Bar Association (Istanbul or Gaziantep branch) for legal representation on civil compensation claims; Hakikat Adalet Hafıza Merkezi (Truth Justice Memory Center, Turkey) for survivor documentation and advocacy support; European Court of Human Rights (longer-term route if domestic accountability for public officials is blocked, as documented by The New Arab and investigative journalism).
- Evidence to collect: Medical records of all injuries; documentation of attendance at the chess tournament (booking confirmation, registration records, any certificate or invitation); photographs or video taken at or near the collapse; any publicly available building license or amnesty certificate issued to the hotel; names of the approving officials as reported in published technical reports or media.
- Feedback category tag: Building Collapse / Construction Defect / Corruption / Staff Misconduct / Complaint Ignored by Authority

### Scenario 20-5: Resident Discovers Her Tower Has ACM Cladding — Landlord Has Given No EWS1 Answer in 14 Months (Pattern B+D+E)
**Real Basis:** Grenfell Tower fire, North Kensington, London, June 14, 2017. 72 deaths. Grenfell Tower Inquiry Phase 2 Report (September 2024): Arconic "deliberately concealed" fire test results showing ACM cladding "burned violently." Criminal referrals: 57 individuals and 20 organisations identified as suspects, May 2026. NBC News, CBS News, GOV.UK inquiry response, Wikipedia.
**The Problem:** A resident of a social housing tower in Manchester has been told by a fire safety surveyor that her building's external cladding is ACM (aluminium composite material) — the same type installed at Grenfell Tower. The housing association told her 14 months ago that "a review is ongoing." She has now read the Phase 2 Grenfell Inquiry finding that Arconic, the cladding manufacturer, deliberately hid fire test results showing the material "burned violently and performed poorly." She has asked the housing association whether the specific Arconic product data for her building's cladding was ever disclosed to them, and whether an EWS1 (External Wall System) assessment form has been completed. No substantive answer has been received.
**What staff/org would do:** The housing association's complaints handler says confirming which cladding product was installed and whether fire test data was disclosed is "not something we can confirm in this call" and offers to "pass the query to the building compliance team." No timeline is given. The agent cannot confirm whether an EWS1 form exists. No escalation or safety advisory is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as a life-safety transparency failure consistent with the documented pattern of manufacturer information suppression confirmed by the Grenfell Inquiry. Directs the resident to statutory rights and the post-Grenfell regulatory framework.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your concern is entirely well-founded. The Grenfell Tower Inquiry's Phase 2 Report, published in September 2024, confirmed that Arconic deliberately hid fire test results showing their ACM cladding burned violently. If your building has ACM cladding and no EWS1 assessment has been completed or disclosed after 14 months, you have a right to demand immediate answers — and to escalate formally to statutory bodies that have powers to compel action."
- Who to direct to: UK Building Safety Regulator (HSE) — the statutory body created post-Grenfell with enforcement powers over high-rise residential buildings; Housing Ombudsman (file directly at housing-ombudsman.org.uk — no MP referral required since 2022; applicable if 8-week internal complaint period has elapsed or complaint has been falsely handled); local Fire and Rescue Service (independent inspection powers and ability to serve enforcement notices); Leasehold Advisory Service (LEASE) for rights regarding cladding remediation and cost obligations; End Our Cladding Scandal campaign for peer support and advocacy.
- Evidence to collect: Written copy of the surveyor's report identifying the cladding type; all written communications with the housing association, especially the initial 14-month-old response and any subsequent acknowledgements; any EWS1 form issued or explicitly refused in writing; a copy of the building's fire risk assessment (residents have a statutory right to request this); any Arconic product specification or batch number found on building documents.
- Feedback category tag: Transparency Failure / Information Suppression / Construction Defect / Fire Safety / Urgent

### Scenario 20-6: Single Mother Reports Damp and Mould for 18 Months — Landlord Closes Complaint Without Inspection or Repair (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** UK Housing Ombudsman Annual Complaints Review 2024-25. 7,082 determinations (30% year-on-year increase); 71% upheld rate; 43% increase in property-condition complaints; 504 severe maladministration findings in property condition; 113 severe maladministration findings specifically in complaint-handling conduct; £5.4 million compensation ordered; 474% increase in repair complaints vs. pre-pandemic levels.
**The Problem:** A single mother with two young children has reported damp and extensive black mould covering the bedroom walls of her social housing flat to her housing association 18 months ago. She has called six times. Each call produces a new reference number and a promise that a repair team will be in touch. No inspection has ever occurred. Her youngest child, aged three, has been diagnosed with a respiratory condition that her GP has linked in writing to the mould exposure. Last week she received a letter from the housing association stating her complaint has been "resolved and closed" — she had never been contacted about any resolution, and nothing has been repaired.
**What staff/org would do:** The complaints line tells her the case was "closed following a review" and that she must submit a new repair request to restart the process. The agent cannot say who conducted the review, when it occurred, or what it found. There is no record of any physical inspection in the system. The agent offers to schedule another repair team "within 28 working days."
**What the Owner does:** Recognises that falsely closing a complaint without inspection or repair is exactly the conduct the Housing Ombudsman's 2024-25 report found to constitute severe maladministration in 113 separate cases. Moves the tenant directly to the external escalation pathway.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (serious and ongoing health harm; no immediate life-threatening danger)
- Opening reply: "I want you to know that closing your complaint without any inspection or repair is exactly what the UK Housing Ombudsman described as 'severe maladministration' in 113 separate findings in their 2024-25 annual review — and your GP's written letter linking your child's respiratory diagnosis to the mould is one of the most important pieces of evidence you can have. You can escalate this externally right now."
- Who to direct to: UK Housing Ombudsman — file directly at housing-ombudsman.org.uk (no MP referral required; applicable because the internal complaint was falsely closed without resolution); Shelter England (free legal advice on disrepair claims and health impacts); local council environmental health team (councils have enforcement powers under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System — a category 1 hazard finding can compel the housing association to act); Regulator of Social Housing (if the landlord is a registered social housing provider, systemic complaint-handling failures can be referred directly).
- Evidence to collect: The letter stating the complaint was "resolved and closed"; GP letter linking the child's respiratory diagnosis to mould exposure; photographs of the mould with date stamps on each image; a full log of all six calls (dates, times, agent names, reference numbers, what was promised); any written promises of repair that were not fulfilled; medical records showing the child's diagnosis and treatment history.
- Feedback category tag: Construction Defect / Repair Failure / Complaint Handling Maladministration / Child Health / Wait Without Resolution

### Scenario 20-7: Rana Plaza Survivor Advocate Calls Global Brand — Refused Connection to Anyone Responsible (Pattern B+E+D)
**Real Basis:** Rana Plaza collapse, Savar, Bangladesh, April 24, 2013. 1,134 deaths. Structural cracks visible the day before; workers ordered back by owner despite warnings. Murder case still unfinished in 2026. International brands — only 9 of 29 attended compensation meetings; Walmart, Carrefour, Auchan, KiK declined to sign or contribute to the Donors Trust Fund. Wikipedia, Dhaka Tribune, The Daily Star, BBC, Business and Human Rights Resource Centre.
**The Problem:** A survivor of the Rana Plaza collapse — now a disability rights advocate — is assisting a group of survivors in filing a formal claim against a European garment retailer that sourced from a factory in the building. The brand did not contribute to the Rana Plaza Donors Trust Fund and has not responded to any written correspondence over two years. The advocate is calling the retailer's international consumer relations line specifically to document the refusal and obtain a named legal contact for formal correspondence.
**What staff/org would do:** The consumer relations agent says garment sourcing is "managed by our procurement team" and is not something the consumer line handles. When the advocate explains they represent Rana Plaza survivors seeking compensation, the agent offers to "pass a note to corporate communications." No escalation path is provided, no timeline is given, no named contact is offered. The call is logged as a "general enquiry."
**What the Owner does:** Validates the severity of the situation, confirms the documented pattern of brand non-engagement, and helps the advocate build a formal paper trail and connect with accountability channels that have had measurable results.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (long-running accountability case; no immediate physical danger)
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — a brand that sourced from Rana Plaza factories refusing to engage with survivors or contribute to the compensation fund — is a documented pattern. International investigations confirmed that multiple major brands declined to attend compensation meetings or contribute after the collapse of a building that killed 1,134 people. I can help you build a formal record and connect you with the mechanisms that have achieved results."
- Who to direct to: Business and Human Rights Resource Centre (tracks brand accountability and publishes response records publicly — contact them to formally log this brand's refusal); OECD National Contact Point in the retailer's home country (formal mediation mechanism under OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises — has produced binding outcomes in similar cases); International Labour Organization Better Work programme; if the retailer is headquartered in an EU country, legal counsel familiar with the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (which creates binding due-diligence obligations); Clean Clothes Campaign for solidarity and media advocacy.
- Evidence to collect: Certified transcript or recording of this call (note date, time, agent reference number, and exact words used); all prior written correspondence sent to the brand and any responses received; documentation of the brand's sourcing relationship with Rana Plaza factories (prior media investigations, factory order records if available); records showing the brand did not attend the Donors Trust Fund meetings or contribute.
- Feedback category tag: Rights Violation / Corporate Accountability / Consumer Refused / Survivor Complaint Dismissed

### Scenario 20-8: World Bank Tanzania Project — Forced Evictions Continue After Assurances; GRM Complaints Unanswered; Call Disconnected (Pattern C+E)
**Real Basis:** World Bank REGROW project (Resilient Natural Resource Management for Tourism and Growth), Mbarali District, Tanzania. $150 million project; forced evictions, rapes, killings by project-linked rangers; World Bank Inspection Panel confirmed "critical failures"; project cancelled November 2024; Management Action Plan issued April 2025; September 2025 Tanzania presidential announcement of further evictions; two additional community members killed post-MAP; new formal complaint filed February 2, 2026. ICIJ (March 2026), Mongabay, Oakland Institute, World Bank Inspection Panel report.
**The Problem:** A community leader from a village near Ruaha National Park is calling the World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) to follow up on formal complaints filed six months ago on behalf of community members who were forcibly evicted, had crops destroyed, and in two cases had family members killed by park rangers operating under the project. The World Bank issued a Management Action Plan in April 2025 promising to halt forced resettlement. In September 2025 the Tanzanian government announced planned evictions of tens of thousands of people — directly contradicting the MAP. Two additional community members were killed after the MAP. The GRS has not acknowledged the complaints filed six months ago.
**What staff/org would do:** The GRS intake agent directs the community leader to the Tanzania country office to "engage with the government directly." When the leader explains that the government is the party conducting the evictions, the agent says the GRS "takes all complaints seriously" and someone will follow up "in due course." No case number is issued on the call. The call is disconnected before a supervisor can be reached.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises the documented pattern of GRS complaint deflection in exactly this case and escalates immediately to the independent accountability mechanisms above the GRS.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "The situation you are describing — community members killed, evictions continuing after a World Bank assurance to halt them, and formal complaints sitting unanswered for six months — is an ongoing human rights emergency. The World Bank's own Inspection Panel has already confirmed critical failures in this project. There are independent bodies above the GRS that can act when the GRS itself is unresponsive, and I want to connect you to them now."
- Who to direct to: World Bank Inspection Panel (independent body — inspectionpanel.org; this body has already investigated REGROW and issued findings confirming critical failures; a new request for inspection can be filed); Accountability Counsel (US-based NGO that specialises in supporting communities in filing complaints with international financial institution accountability mechanisms — active on this exact case); ICIJ Africa Desk and Oakland Institute (already actively documenting this situation; formal contact can amplify the case); UN Special Rapporteur on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples; East African Court of Justice (regional legal body with jurisdiction over East African Community state conduct).
- Evidence to collect: Copies of all GRM and GRS filings with submission dates and any reference numbers received; the World Bank Management Action Plan document (April 2025) and the September 2025 government eviction announcement; death certificates or witness affidavits for the two community members killed after the MAP was issued; GPS-tagged photographs or video of destroyed crops and demolished structures; records of any Inspection Panel communications previously received.
- Feedback category tag: Community Displacement / Rights Violation / Complaint Deflection / Call Center Failure / Urgent

### Scenario 20-9: Supreme Court-Ordered Refund After Illegal Towers Demolished — Developer's IVR Disconnects After 20 Minutes, Warrant Unpaid (Pattern E+D+A)
**Real Basis:** Supertech Emerald Court twin towers (Apex and Ceyane), Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India. Supreme Court of India 2021 judgment; demolition August 28, 2022 (largest planned demolition in India's history); Supreme Court ordered 12% interest refunds to buyers; MahaRERA recovery warrant data (July 2025): only 32% of Rs. 724 crore in ordered penalties collected nationally. Business Standard, Bar and Bench, Supreme Court of India judgment, IJPIEL legal analysis, Moneylife.
**The Problem:** A couple purchased a flat in one of Supertech's twin towers in 2013, paying Rs. 45 lakh. The towers were demolished in August 2022 pursuant to a Supreme Court order for illegal construction. The Supreme Court directed Supertech to refund buyers with 12% annual interest. Nearly two years after demolition, no refund has been received. Supertech's customer care number connects to an automated IVR that places them in a queue and disconnects after 20 minutes. When they attended Supertech's office in person, a staff member said the refund is "in processing" and referred them back to the helpline. Their RERA complaint has been upheld and a recovery warrant issued — but the amount has not been paid.
**What staff/org would do:** On a rare occasion when a Supertech call center agent is reached, the agent confirms the refund is "in process" with no timeline and no escalation path. A request to speak to a manager results in a queue transfer that disconnects. The RERA warrant office says they have "limited enforcement powers" and refers the couple to civil court.
**What the Owner does:** Clearly maps the enforcement pathway the couple has not yet accessed — specifically the stronger administrative enforcement route available through the District Magistrate — and identifies the concrete next step.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (ongoing financial harm with a strong legal foundation; no physical danger)
- Opening reply: "You have a Supreme Court-backed refund entitlement with 12% interest — that is one of the strongest legal foundations possible. The documented problem is enforcement: nationally only 32% of RERA-ordered penalty amounts are being collected. But there is a stronger enforcement route than civil court that many buyers do not know about, and it is the correct next step for you."
- Who to direct to: UP RERA (Uttar Pradesh Real Estate Regulatory Authority) — file an execution petition specifically citing contempt of the warrant; additionally, request the District Magistrate's (DM) office to recover the amount as arrears of land revenue — this route is available under RERA and is more effective than civil court enforcement; National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) for a parallel order; a local consumer law advocate experienced in RERA warrant execution proceedings.
- Evidence to collect: Original flat purchase agreement; all payment receipts with bank reference numbers and dates; the RERA complaint order and recovery warrant number; the Supreme Court judgment reference number; all records of contact attempts with Supertech (dates, call durations, IVR reference numbers, names of any staff met in person); the in-person staff member's name and the date of the office visit.
- Feedback category tag: Refusal / Enforcement Gap / Abandoned Project / Financial Harm / Call Center Failure

---

### Scenario 20-10: First-Time Homebuyer Discovers Developer's RERA Registration Number Is Forged — MahaRERA Helpline Offers No Immediate Action (Pattern E+A+D)
**Real Basis:** MahaRERA, Maharashtra, India. October 2022: 60+ developers suspended for forging registration documents. February 2023: 19,539 project notices for non-disclosure violations. March 2023: 1,781 projects noticed for separate bank account violations. July 2025: 32% recovery rate on Rs. 724 crore in ordered penalties. Moneylife, Inventiva, MahaRERA official data. Composite incident type consistent with documented MahaRERA enforcement pattern; also informed by Minnesota AG High Road Builders judgment ($1.3 million, January 2026) and Pennsylvania AG Ward case (30 consumer complaints before enforcement action).
**The Problem:** A first-time homebuyer in Pune paid Rs. 8 lakh as a booking deposit for a flat in a residential project. The developer presented a printed RERA registration certificate and a contractor license at the time of signing. Construction stopped six months later. When the buyer checks the MahaRERA public portal, the registration number on her contract returns no matching record. She suspects the certificate was forged. She is calling the MahaRERA consumer helpline to understand what this means and what she can do.
**What staff/org would do:** The MahaRERA helpline agent confirms the registration number is not in the system. When asked what to do next, the agent says she should file an online complaint and the process "may take several months." No fraud alert is triggered, no law enforcement referral is made, no information is given about whether a surety bond or developer insurance exists that might protect her deposit.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the urgency of acting quickly — forged documents trigger criminal liability, not just civil remedies — and provides a clear multi-track action plan covering both the regulatory and criminal pathways simultaneously.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (financial fraud; no physical danger — but time-sensitive for evidence preservation and recovery)
- Opening reply: "What you have found — a registration number that does not appear anywhere in MahaRERA's system — is consistent with a fraud pattern MahaRERA itself identified and acted on in October 2022, when it suspended over 60 developers for forging registration documents. Filing with MahaRERA is correct, but forging a government registration certificate is also a criminal offence, and you should pursue both tracks at the same time to protect your deposit."
- Who to direct to: MahaRERA online complaint portal (maha-rera.mahaonline.gov.in) — file a formal complaint citing document forgery and project abandonment; local police Economic Offences Wing (EOW) — forged registration is a criminal offence under Indian Penal Code sections on forgery, not only a civil RERA matter; Maharashtra Attorney General consumer protection cell; a lawyer experienced in RERA enforcement and criminal fraud proceedings; National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) if the forged certificate was transmitted digitally to the buyer.
- Evidence to collect: Original booking agreement containing the forged RERA registration number; the printed license document shown by the developer at signing; all payment receipts with bank reference numbers; screenshots of the MahaRERA portal search returning no result for the registration number (date and time stamped); all developer communications — WhatsApp messages, emails, printed brochures; photographs of the construction site in its current stopped state with date stamps.
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud / Forged Documents / Contractor Misconduct / Deposit Loss / Regulatory Non-Compliance

Industry ID: 20

---

## 21. Real Estate / Property

### Scenario 21-1: Unknown Property Sale Executed Using Stolen Identity (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** DOJ USAO-CDCA federal press release, January 2024. A licensed real estate broker and two co-defendants were arrested for fraudulently selling a $1.5 million Burbank, California home they did not own, using stolen identities to obtain a near-$1 million loan against the property. The legitimate owner had no knowledge the property was in a transaction.
**The Problem:** A homeowner in California receives an alert from her title insurer that a transfer deed has been recorded against her property. She authorized no sale. A loan of nearly $1 million has been taken against the property in her name by parties she does not recognize. She did not contact any real estate agent or lender. Her identity has been used to execute the entire transaction without her presence.
**What staff/org would do:** The county recorder's office says they can only accept title correction requests in writing with notarized documentation sent by mail. The lender's fraud line puts her on hold for 47 minutes before disconnecting. The FBI tip line instructs her to file an IC3 report online with no follow-up timeline given. The title insurer says they will open a claim but cannot freeze the transaction until legal review, which takes 6 to 8 weeks.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an active financial emergency. Confirms the homeowner's situation, validates the severity of what has happened, and immediately outlines the parallel actions required across law enforcement, county recorder, lender fraud, and title insurer in a prioritized order.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a serious property fraud emergency. Your identity has been used without your consent to sell or borrow against your home. You need to act on several fronts simultaneously — I will walk you through each step right now."
- Direct to: FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3.gov) for federal fraud report; DOJ USAO-CDCA if in California; county recorder's office for immediate title freeze request via certified written demand; lender fraud and identity theft department for emergency loan suspension; title insurer for emergency claim opening; CFPB complaint portal (consumerfinance.gov/complaint) if the lender is unresponsive; Federal Trade Commission identity theft report (identitytheft.gov) to establish an official record
- Evidence to collect: Deed of transfer (pull as public record from county recorder); any loan documents or correspondence sent to your address; proof of continuous residence and ownership such as utility bills, tax notices, and prior mortgage statements; government-issued ID confirming your identity; a timeline of when you first learned of the transaction and through which channel
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud / Identity-Based Property Crime

---

### Scenario 21-2: Property Manager Stealing Rent from Elderly and Disabled Tenants (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Manhattan DA press release, April 2024. Shatina Howell, property manager at Ennis Francis Apartments (Section 8, Central Harlem), stole 91 money orders from approximately 41 veterans, elderly, and disabled low-income tenants between May 2021 and August 2022, then continued the scheme at other Harlem properties. Total theft: $56,412. Tenants discovered the losses only when eviction notices arrived for non-payment of rent they believed they had paid. Howell specifically targeted tenants with health issues, offering to fill out and deliver their money orders on their behalf.
**The Problem:** An elderly veteran living in a Section 8 housing complex in Central Harlem has just received an eviction notice for four months of non-payment. He is certain he paid — every month he physically handed his rent money orders to the property manager, who took them and recorded them in a ledger. He has no receipts. The property manager is not answering his calls. He does not understand why he is facing eviction for rent he is sure he paid, and his mobility limits make it difficult to attend in-person hearings.
**What staff/org would do:** The housing office instructs him to appear in person during office hours with his original money orders as proof of payment — the very documents that were taken from him. The eviction notice processing clerk says proceedings cannot be paused without a court order. No one in the agency recognizes the pattern as potential theft.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately recognizes the vulnerability and urgency. Validates his payment history, flags this as a theft pattern consistent with documented cases, and prioritizes stopping the eviction while simultaneously initiating a criminal fraud report.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You should not be facing eviction for rent you paid. Handing money orders directly to a property manager and receiving no receipts is consistent with a documented fraud pattern in which managers alter or redirect those money orders. This is an emergency on two tracks: stopping the eviction and reporting the theft."
- Direct to: Manhattan District Attorney's office or local DA fraud hotline; NYC Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) tenant helpline (212-863-7900); Legal Aid Society housing unit for emergency eviction defense; the Section 8 housing authority administering the subsidy contract — they can verify payment records independently of the on-site manager; NYPD for a criminal report; Adult Protective Services if the tenant has cognitive or mobility vulnerability that was exploited
- Evidence to collect: Any photos taken of money orders before handing them over; bank withdrawal records or post office receipts showing when and where each money order was purchased; any text messages or written communications with the property manager; testimony from neighbors who experienced the same issue; original lease and housing subsidy documentation; dates and amounts of every month you paid
- Feedback category tag: Staff Misconduct / Financial Theft from Vulnerable Tenants

---

### Scenario 21-3: Real Estate Agent Refuses to Discuss Competing Loan Options on Instructions from Employer (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** CFPB federal complaint filed December 23, 2024, Eastern District of Michigan, against Rocket Homes Real Estate LLC and The Jason Mitchell Group. Between 2019 and 2024, agents were institutionally instructed to steer buyers exclusively to Rocket Mortgage and affiliated settlement company Amrock, and were prohibited from informing consumers about USDA rural loans, down-payment assistance programs, and manufactured home loans — an alleged violation of RESPA (Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act).
**The Problem:** A first-time homebuyer in rural Michigan is working with an agent from a nationally branded real estate network. She has asked twice about USDA rural loans, which would allow her to buy with zero down payment, and about first-time buyer assistance programs. Both times the agent changed the subject and redirected her to the affiliated lender's pre-approval form. She senses she is not receiving complete information but cannot identify why, and she is worried that signing with the affiliated lender will cost her significantly more than alternatives she has not been shown.
**What staff/org would do:** The agent reassures her that the affiliated lender is "the easiest and best option" and that down-payment assistance programs "slow down closings." The company's main customer service number routes to a general sales line that confirms only affiliated products.
**What the Owner does:** Recognizes this as a documented institutional information-withholding pattern. Validates her instinct, explains that federal law requires agents to act in her interest and not filter options for commercial reasons, and provides the independent information she has been denied.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Your instinct is right. Federal law under RESPA requires that agents act in your interest and not withhold mortgage options from you in exchange for referral incentives. USDA rural loans and down-payment assistance programs are legitimate products you are entitled to know about, and you are being directed away from them."
- Direct to: CFPB consumer complaint portal (consumerfinance.gov/complaint) — the CFPB is the exact enforcement body that investigated and filed suit over this practice in December 2024; USDA Rural Development loan locator (rd.usda.gov) for independent eligibility check; HUD-approved housing counselor for free independent mortgage advice (800-569-4287); your state's housing finance agency for down-payment assistance program listings; your state real estate licensing board for an agent conduct complaint; a second independent agent or mortgage broker for a comparison quote
- Evidence to collect: Written record of the questions you asked and what the agent told you; any email or text exchanges where specific loan products were raised; pre-approval offers and documents received from the affiliated lender; any referral fee disclosures or absence of disclosures from the agent; the agent's brokerage and license number from their business card or the listing agreement
- Feedback category tag: Information Withholding / Agent Misconduct / Consumer Financial Rights

---

### Scenario 21-4: Landlord Changes All Locks While Tenant Is at Work (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** UK Crown Court and Landlord Licensing and Defence legal case record. Mirza Tariq Mahmud Jaral, a Peterborough landlord, changed all locks to his rental property while the tenant was at work in July 2020, removing the tenant's possessions and rendering him immediately homeless. The tenant held a valid tenancy with no rent arrears. Jaral was sentenced in 2022 to 12 months imprisonment after proceedings spanning nearly two years.
**The Problem:** A tenant in Peterborough has returned from a nine-hour work shift to find his key does not open his front door. His clothes, documents, phone charger, and passport are inside. A handwritten note on the door says the landlord has "taken back" the property. It is 9:30 PM. He has no immediate family in the city, no alternative accommodation, and a valid tenancy agreement showing he is not in arrears. The landlord is not answering calls.
**What staff/org would do:** The police non-emergency line (101) says this is a civil matter and advises him to contact a solicitor. The council housing emergency line puts him on hold for 22 minutes before a recorded message instructs him to call back during office hours. The landlord does not respond to any calls.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately confirms this is a criminal offence and treats the call as a housing emergency requiring same-night action, not an administrative matter to be deferred.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What your landlord has done is a criminal offence under Section 1 of the Protection from Eviction Act 1977. Changing your locks without a court order while you have a valid tenancy is illegal eviction. Tonight's first priority is getting you shelter and beginning the legal process — this cannot wait until office hours."
- Direct to: Police (999 if you feel unsafe, 101 to report illegal eviction — officers can attend and in some cases require re-entry to be granted that night); local council housing emergency line — under the Homelessness Reduction Act 2017 the council has a legal duty to assist you tonight; Shelter emergency helpline (0808 800 4444, free, available 24 hours); Safer Renting charity, which specializes in illegal eviction support; Citizens Advice housing emergency line; a housing solicitor or Legal Aid provider for an emergency injunction application to the county court requiring immediate re-entry — this remedy is available under civil law alongside the criminal prosecution
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the locked door and any note left by the landlord; photograph your tenancy agreement showing valid lease dates and rent payment history; bank statements showing rent paid and received; any prior threatening messages from the landlord by text, email, or voicemail; names and contact details of neighbours who can confirm you were living there and were at work tonight; a full log of every call you make to police, council, and Shelter tonight with times and outcomes
- Feedback category tag: Illegal Eviction / Housing Emergency

---

### Scenario 21-5: Sale-and-Rent-Back Scheme with Concealed Fees and Subsequent Eviction (Pattern A+B)
**Real Basis:** FCA (Financial Conduct Authority) press release and court record. Rajinder Gill, operating as Secure Property Consultants Ltd, targeted financially distressed homeowners across England between 2014 and 2018. Victims sold at below market value on the promise of remaining as tenants; undisclosed and excessive fees totaling £925,233 were extracted. Multiple victims later faced eviction from their own former homes. The judge described "a pattern of systematic exploitation." Gill was sentenced in March 2026 to 2.5 years imprisonment plus an 8-year director disqualification.
**The Problem:** A homeowner in the English Midlands in financial difficulty was approached six months ago by a company offering to purchase her home rapidly for cash and allow her to remain as a tenant. She signed the agreement and the sale proceeded. She has now received a tenancy termination notice and cannot reconcile the fees deducted from her sale proceeds — they total more than £40,000 that she was never told about. The company's representative is reading a clause from the contract she was not directed to at signing and telling her she has 28 days to vacate her former home.
**What staff/org would do:** The company's representative reads the buried clause and terminates the call. The head office routes her to a general voicemail that is never returned. The solicitor who handled the original transaction — recommended by the purchasing company — says there was nothing improper about the process.
**What the Owner does:** Recognizes this as the documented sale-and-rent-back exploitation pattern the FCA has prosecuted and identifies the specific regulatory and legal remedies available to challenge both the undisclosed fees and the eviction.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Being charged fees you were never told about and then receiving an eviction notice from your own former home matches a fraud pattern the FCA has investigated and sent people to prison for. You have legal options to challenge the fees and to contest the eviction notice, and the FCA is the right starting point."
- Direct to: FCA consumer helpline (0800 111 6768) — the FCA regulates sale-and-rent-back schemes under specific rules and has prosecuted exactly this type of undisclosed-fee case; Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) if any financial product or regulated activity was involved; Shelter housing advice line (0808 800 4444) for immediate tenancy rights review and eviction notice challenge; Citizens Advice for contract review and legal aid eligibility assessment; Trading Standards via your local council for consumer contract fraud relating to hidden charges; a housing solicitor for an injunction against the eviction notice pending resolution of the fee dispute; check whether the solicitor who acted for you has a conflict of interest that should be reported to the Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA)
- Evidence to collect: The full original sale-and-rent-back agreement including all schedules; the itemized fee breakdown as provided by the company; any independent property valuation obtained at the time or since; all correspondence with the company from first contact to today; bank records showing actual proceeds received versus the amount expected; the solicitor's name, firm, and any documentation showing they were introduced by the purchasing company
- Feedback category tag: Financial Fraud / Predatory Contract / Housing Rights

---

### Scenario 21-6: Social Landlord Refuses to Acknowledge or Escalate Tenant Repair Complaint (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** UK Housing Ombudsman Annual Complaints Review 2024-25. 7,082 formal determinations, 71% overall uphold rate, £5.4 million in compensation ordered. 175 Complaint Handling Failure Orders (CHFOs) issued for delayed acknowledgment, failure to communicate outcomes, and refusal to escalate complaints to the Ombudsman stage. Local authorities had an 87% uphold rate on complaint-handling disputes — meaning in nearly 9 out of 10 cases where a tenant said their complaint was mishandled, the Ombudsman agreed.
**The Problem:** A social housing tenant in a London local authority flat has been reporting black mould on the bedroom ceiling and a broken boiler since October. It is now January. She has called the repairs line seven times. She has been given reference numbers that lead nowhere. When she asked for her complaint to be escalated to management, she was told "we do not escalate repair requests, it is in the queue." Her most recent call ended with an agent telling her the case is "under review" with no timeline given. A letter has now arrived stating her complaint has been formally closed as "resolved" — but no repair has been made. Her young child has developed a persistent cough.
**What staff/org would do:** The next call is answered by a different agent with no record of her case who asks her to start again from the beginning. The supervisor is unavailable. When she asks about the closed complaint, the agent says she will need to submit a new complaint form.
**What the Owner does:** Identifies this as a documented complaint-handling failure pattern that the Housing Ombudsman specifically sanctions and explains the formal escalation route that bypasses the landlord entirely.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Seven calls, a closed complaint with no repair made, and an agent with no record of your case — this is exactly the pattern the UK Housing Ombudsman is set up to address, and landlords who handle complaints this way are being formally sanctioned. You no longer need to keep calling the same line. There is an escalation route that bypasses your landlord entirely."
- Direct to: UK Housing Ombudsman (housing-ombudsman.org.uk, 0300 111 3000) — if your landlord has not resolved the complaint within 8 weeks, or has formally closed it without resolution, you can submit directly to the Ombudsman without further referral; the Ombudsman's own 2024-25 data shows 87% of complaint-handling disputes against local authorities are upheld; your local councillor's surgery — councillors can contact housing departments directly and often achieve rapid action that the tenant cannot; Environmental Health at the same local council (a separate department from housing management) for a damp and mould inspection under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS); if your child's respiratory symptoms are documented by a GP as linked to housing conditions, that medical evidence significantly strengthens an Ombudsman case
- Evidence to collect: All reference numbers issued from each of the seven calls, with the date and name of the agent spoken to where known; the letter formally closing the complaint as resolved; photographs of the mould with timestamps on each image; any written communications from the landlord by letter, email, or app message; GP or hospital records linking your child's respiratory condition to mould exposure; a written timeline of every interaction with the landlord from October to today
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure / Complaint Handling Refusal / Housing Disrepair

---

### Scenario 21-7: Indigenous Community Loses Access to Ancestral Grazing Land Amid Security Force Violence (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Amnesty International report "We Have Lost Everything: Forced Evictions of the Maasai in Loliondo, Tanzania," June 2023, independently confirmed by UN OHCHR press release June 2022 and Al Jazeera 2024 follow-up. On June 10, 2022, security forces descended on Loliondo division and began demarcating 1,500 square kilometers of Maasai ancestral territory as Protected Area. When community members gathered to protest, security forces shot and teargassed them, injuring at least 40 people. An 84-year-old community member was shot in both legs and subsequently disappeared. 27 Maasai were arbitrarily arrested. Approximately 70,000 Maasai lost access to grazing lands; the territory was licensed to a private trophy hunting company.
**The Problem:** A Maasai community leader in Loliondo has received a government notice declaring that the grazing corridors his community has used for generations are now a Protected Area. Security personnel have begun demarcating the boundary. Community members who approached to observe were shot at with live rounds. One elder is missing. Livestock cannot access the dry-season grazing lands. The community cannot identify any legal process that preceded the demarcation or any domestic avenue to file a formal objection — community leaders who tried to report through government channels were among those arrested.
**What staff/org would do:** The district government office says the decision originated in Dar es Salaam and cannot be reversed at local level. The regional commissioner's office does not respond. Media and NGO access to the area was explicitly blocked during and after the evictions.
**What the Owner does:** Recognizes this as a documented state-sanctioned land rights violation with international legal dimensions, identifies the bodies already engaged with this specific situation, and prioritizes both the missing person and the land rights complaint simultaneously.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What your community is experiencing — forced removal from ancestral land to benefit a commercial operator, with live fire used against those who protest and an elder missing — has been documented by Amnesty International and reviewed by the United Nations. International bodies are already engaged with this specific case. Here is how to reach them and what to document immediately."
- Direct to: Amnesty International Tanzania contact (amnesty.org) — they have documented this case in detail and have ongoing engagement with the community; UN Special Rapporteur on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples via the OHCHR communications procedure (ohchr.org) — this is the UN body that issued warnings specifically about Loliondo; African Commission on Human and Peoples' Rights (ACHPR) in Banjul — land rights cases are admissible under the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights; African Court on Human and Peoples' Rights — Tanzania has accepted the court's jurisdiction over individual and NGO communications; Oakland Institute and Survival International for ongoing international advocacy and media documentation; for the missing elder — document full details immediately and report to OHCHR's enforced disappearances mechanism in parallel
- Evidence to collect: Any government demarcation notices or written communications; photographs and video of security forces and boundary marking activity where it is safe to document; medical records for injured community members with dates and descriptions of injuries; names, ages, and last known locations of any missing or arrested individuals with witness contacts; historical land use documentation including maps, livestock movement records, community oral testimony on record, and any prior government correspondence acknowledging the community's use of the land
- Feedback category tag: Indigenous Land Rights / State-Sanctioned Forced Eviction / Human Rights Violation

---

### Scenario 21-8: Government Demolition of Long-Term Residents' Homes with 48-Hour Notice (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Human Rights Watch report "I Escaped with Only My Life," May 2024, based on 25 interviews conducted September 2023 to April 2024. Karachi Metropolitan Corporation demolished approximately 12,000 houses in the Gujjar Nullah waterway area in November 2021 under a Supreme Court directive, displacing approximately 96,000 people with minimal notice and no adequate resettlement. Resident Zakia Bibi died of a heart attack four days after her home was demolished. Residents who contacted the city authority referencing the Court directive found their complaints closed without action.
**The Problem:** A 52-year-old diabetic woman in an informal settlement in Karachi has just been told by a city official that her home will be demolished in 48 hours as part of a waterway clearance order. She has lived there for 23 years and has paid municipal electricity and gas bills throughout that time. She has no formal land title. She has two adult children with no income. She asks about resettlement assistance; the official says it will be arranged "through the scheme." When she asks for details of the scheme, no one at the office can provide them. The telephone number on the notice rings unanswered.
**What staff/org would do:** The city office hands her a printed notice referencing the Supreme Court order and tells her the process is "in accordance with the law." There is no independent complaint mechanism available at local level and no resettlement office she can visit.
**What the Owner does:** Addresses the human emergency first, then identifies the legal and humanitarian channels available even under the constraint of a court order, and clarifies that a court order does not extinguish the right to adequate notice, documentation, and fair compensation.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "A 48-hour demolition notice after 23 years of residence — including utility bills that demonstrate your longstanding tenure — does not mean you have no rights. Even under court-ordered demolitions, you are entitled to documentation, to challenge the adequacy of resettlement, and to have your case heard. Here is what to do right now."
- Direct to: Human Rights Commission of Pakistan (HRCP, hrcp.org.pk) — an independent body that has documented this exact demolition pattern in Karachi and can provide rapid legal referrals; Pakistan Legal Aid Foundation or the local bar association for an emergency legal opinion on whether due process requirements were met before demolition; Edhi Foundation or Chhipa Welfare for immediate emergency shelter if demolition proceeds; National Commission for Human Rights Pakistan (NCHR) for a formal complaint; UN Special Rapporteur on the Right to Adequate Housing (ohchr.org) — this mandate specifically covers displacement without adequate resettlement; keep all utility bills as evidence of established occupancy for any future compensation claim
- Evidence to collect: All utility bills for the past several years showing your address and continuous payment history; the demolition notice itself (photograph it if you cannot keep the original); any previous written interactions with local government or the city about your property; medical documentation of diabetes and any dependents' health vulnerabilities to support a compensation claim on grounds of protected status; photographs of the property documenting its condition, age, and established character; names and contact details of neighbours who can provide corroborating testimony
- Feedback category tag: Forced Displacement / Government Demolition / Inadequate Resettlement

---

### Scenario 21-9: Home Buyer's Deposit Stolen Through Email Payment Redirection Scam (Pattern A+E)
**Real Basis:** ACCC media release and ABC Australia news report, July 2024. A home buyer's $26,000 property deposit was intercepted by scammers who compromised email communications between buyer and real estate agent, substituted their own bank account details in payment instructions, and the deposit was transferred to a fraudulent account. The ACCC reported Australian businesses lost over AUD $14 million to payment redirection scams, with real estate identified as a top targeted sector. ACCC's own guidance specifically identifies a verbal phone call to confirm bank details before transfer as the countermeasure — directly acknowledging that the communication breakdown between buyer, agent, and bank was the core failure.
**The Problem:** A homebuyer in Sydney followed emailed payment instructions and transferred her $26,000 deposit to what she believed was the real estate agent's trust account. She is now at settlement and the agent has no record of receiving the deposit. Re-examining the email, she can see the sender address was spoofed — a single character different from the agent's real address. When she called her bank immediately after discovering the fraud, she was put on hold for 34 minutes and told the transfer was "authorized" and therefore not the bank's liability. The funds cleared three days ago and have moved internationally.
**What staff/org would do:** The bank's fraud line says she authorized the transfer and declines liability. The real estate agency's principal says verifying bank details is her responsibility. The police non-emergency line says to file online and she will be contacted within 10 business days.
**What the Owner does:** Moves immediately to pursue the fastest available recovery channels while helping her understand the regulatory complaints routes that can compel bank action even after they have declined.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Your deposit has been stolen through a payment interception scam — you received a legitimate-looking email from a spoofed address and followed what appeared to be real instructions. This is not your fault, and there are steps to take right now that may still allow partial recovery. The first 72 hours matter."
- Direct to: Your bank's fraud department — request an urgent international payment recall and reference the Scam Safe Accord, under which Australian banks have recovery obligations for scam losses; AFCA (Australian Financial Complaints Authority, afca.org.au) if the bank declines to assist — AFCA can compel banks to demonstrate they met their Scam Safe Accord obligations, and the Ombudsman has the power to award compensation; ACCC Scamwatch (scamwatch.gov.au) — report immediately as it contributes to national fraud intelligence and strengthens AFCA complaints; Australian Federal Police cybercrime portal (cyber.gov.au) for a formal criminal report; your state fair trading authority for a complaint against the agent if the email compromise occurred via their systems, which may constitute a duty of care failure; the real estate agent's professional indemnity insurer
- Evidence to collect: The original spoofed email in its original format — do not delete it, and preserve the full email headers which show technical routing details critical for law enforcement; a screenshot of the spoofed sender address next to the agent's real address to document the character substitution; your bank's transfer confirmation and receipt with timestamp; the agent's real email address from a prior confirmed communication before the scam; your property contract showing the correct trust account details; any phone communications with the agent in the days before and after the transfer
- Feedback category tag: Payment Fraud / Cyber-Enabled Property Scam / Financial Loss

---

### Scenario 21-10: Farmers Coerced into Signing Land Terminations Under Police Supervision (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Uzbek Forum for Human Rights and Ulster University joint report "A False Sense of Legality," December 2024. The report documents forced evictions across 11 urban development projects in Tashkent and Samarkand displacing over 10,000 people, and agricultural land seizures in 15 districts across six regions where farmers were coerced into signing "voluntary" land lease terminations at police-supervised meetings. Some farmers were escorted by officers late at night to sign documents. Farmers who raised questions at meetings were removed by officers.
**The Problem:** A farmer in a rural district outside Tashkent has been summoned, along with his neighbours, to a meeting at the local district administration office. Police officers are present in the room. An official places a document in front of him described as a "voluntary termination" of his agricultural land lease and tells him to sign. He is not told what the land will be used for or what compensation he will receive. His neighbours who asked questions were escorted out of the room by officers. He was visited at his home by officials the night before. He does not know whether he has any legal right to refuse or what will happen if he does.
**What staff/org would do:** The district office says the development project is a government priority and the process is "in accordance with the law." There is no independent complaint mechanism accessible at local level. Raising objections at the meeting itself results in removal from the room.
**What the Owner does:** Affirms his rights without endangering him further, identifies the organizations that are already documenting this exact pattern in his region, and provides concrete steps he can take to preserve his legal position whether or not he signs under duress.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- Opening reply: "Signing a document described as 'voluntary' while police officers are in the room and your neighbours are being removed for asking questions is not voluntary under any legal standard — it is coercion. Organizations have already documented this exact pattern in your region and are collecting testimonies. Here is what you can do to protect yourself, including if you are forced to sign."
- Direct to: Uzbek Forum for Human Rights (uzbekforum.org) — they specifically documented midnight visits and police-supervised signings in rural Uzbekistan and are actively collecting testimonies from affected farmers; UN Special Rapporteur on the Right to Food and UN Special Rapporteur on Adequate Housing (ohchr.org) — both mandates cover agricultural land seizure; Human Rights Watch Central Asia team; if signing is unavoidable, write the words "signed under duress" in your own handwriting anywhere on the page before handing it back, and photograph the document if it is safe to do so; do not sign a blank or incomplete document under any circumstances; seek any independent legal counsel — the Tashkent Bar Association or the Academic Research and Information Centre (ARIC) may offer referrals
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the document before and after signing if your phone is with you and it is safe to do so; note the full names, titles, and any badge numbers of every official and officer present at the meeting; record any verbal promises made about compensation (amount, form, timeline); names and contact details of neighbouring farmers who attended the same meeting and can provide corroborating testimony; documentation of any overnight visit — date, time, how many officials came, and precisely what was said; any written summons or notification you received about the meeting

- Feedback category tag: Land Rights Coercion / Government Misconduct / Rural Property Rights

Industry ID: 21
