## 22. Mining / Extractive Industries

### Scenario 22-1: Mercury Spilled on Road, Children Already Collected It — No Warning Given (Pattern A+D)
**Real Basis:** IFC CAO published case Peru: Yanacocha-01/Cajamarca (December 2000); Peru Support Group 20-year review (April 2020); Business & Human Rights Resource Centre
**The Problem:** A resident of Choropampa, Peru, is calling from the roadside: a truck contracted by Yanacocha gold mine has spilled liquid mercury along the dirt road through the village. Her children, drawn by the shiny silvery liquid, picked it up by hand. She does not know what the substance is. She has been trying to reach the mine's community relations number for 40 minutes — no one answered. She has now walked to the local community hall to file a complaint. No emergency information about mercury toxicity has been posted by the company. The municipal health post has two nurses and no mercury protocols.
**What staff/org would do:** The mine's community liaison, when eventually reached, asks the caller to "wait for an official statement" and refuses to confirm the substance is mercury. The health post advises her to wash her hands and go home.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately acknowledges the severity. Treats this as a chemical poisoning emergency. Provides mercury safety instructions right now — do not touch, do not sweep, leave the area, ventilate. Confirms that the company has a legal duty to notify health authorities immediately under Peruvian environmental law. Gives the caller the number for the regional health authority (DIRESA Cajamarca) and Peru's emergency environmental response line. Documents the incident for the IFC CAO complaint pathway.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a medical emergency. Please stop touching the substance immediately and move everyone away from the area — especially children. I am treating this as the highest priority."
- Direct to: DIRESA Cajamarca (regional health authority); Peru's National Emergency Operations Centre (COEN); IFC CAO complaints portal (cao-ombudsman.org) for formal company accountability
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the spill location and substance without approaching it; note the vehicle plate number and time; record names of all people who made contact; preserve clothing worn by children
- Feedback category tag: Environmental Emergency / Health Hazard / Company Non-Disclosure

---

### Scenario 22-2: Children's Blood-Lead Results Sent to the Mining Company Before the Mother Received Them (Pattern B+E)
**Real Basis:** Scientific Reports / PubMed (November 2021); EnviroLink independent review (February 2026); Mongabay (November 2023); provincial health emergency declaration, Cerro de Pasco, Peru 2017
**The Problem:** A mother in Cerro de Pasco has taken her 8-year-old son to the regional health post after noticing he is slower to learn and more fatigued than peers. A blood test was carried out three weeks ago. The health post tells her the results were "sent to the mining company's environmental team for review" before being returned to her — and she still has not received them. When she asks directly what the lead levels showed, she is told: "The company will be in touch." A regional study published in 2021 found 86% of children in this area had blood-lead above WHO safe limits; a 2026 study found lead concentrations 43 times above safe limits in children's hair samples.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue redirecting the mother to the company. The company's community hotline places her on hold for 22 minutes, then advises the results are "under technical analysis." No timeline is given.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that a parent has the legal right to receive their child's medical results directly from the health post — not through the mining company. Explains the documented pattern of lead contamination in Cerro de Pasco. Advises her to demand the results in writing from the health post today under Peru's patient rights law (Ley 29414). Provides the contact for Peru's Ministry of Health patient rights ombudsman (Defensoría del Asegurado) and the Ministry of Environment's complaints portal (OEFA).
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You have every right to receive your son's test results directly — not through the mining company. This is your legal right under Peru's health law, and I want to help you get that information today."
- Direct to: Peru OEFA (environmental enforcement — oefa.gob.pe); Defensoría del Pueblo (human rights ombudsman); local NGO CooperAccion for community legal support
- Evidence to collect: Obtain written confirmation of when blood test was taken and where results were sent; photograph any water sources used at home; document the child's symptoms with dates
- Feedback category tag: Information Suppression / Children's Health / Patient Rights

---

### Scenario 22-3: Nine Years After the Dam Collapsed, Still No Compensation — Two Transfers, Two Disconnections (Pattern A+D)
**Real Basis:** Al Jazeera (October 2024); Finance Uncovered investigative report (2015–2024); Business & Human Rights Resource Centre; Mariana dam disaster legal proceedings; R$170 billion Supreme Court settlement approved 6 November 2024
**The Problem:** A former resident of Bento Rodrigues village in Mariana, Brazil, whose home was destroyed on 5 November 2015 when the Fundao tailings dam collapsed, has been living in temporary housing for nine years. She registered with the Renova Foundation (the company-established reparations body) in 2016 but has received only small interim payments. When she calls the Renova helpline to ask about the Supreme Court's November 2024 settlement, she is told her case file is "under processing" — the same response she has received for three years. She asks to speak to a case manager; the call is transferred twice and disconnected. She calls back and is placed on hold for 34 minutes before the line drops again.
**What staff/org would do:** The Renova Foundation helpline logs the call as resolved and sends a generic email confirming her file number.
**What the Owner does:** Validates her frustration and immediately documents the specific call failures — two transfers, disconnection, 34-minute hold, second disconnection — as a formal service failure. Confirms the R$170 billion agreement was approved by Brazil's Supreme Federal Court on 6 November 2024 and provides the specific reference. Advises her to contact the Federal Public Ministry (Ministerio Publico Federal), which oversees settlement implementation, and to contact Conectas Direitos Humanos, who have represented Mariana survivors in international forums.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Nine years is an unacceptable wait for justice. What happened on that call — two disconnections after being transferred — is a documented service failure, and I am recording it now."
- Direct to: Ministerio Publico Federal (mpf.mp.br); Conectas Direitos Humanos (conectas.org); Brazil's reparacao online portal; Inter-American Commission on Human Rights petition pathway
- Evidence to collect: Save all Renova Foundation case reference numbers; document every call with date, time, duration, and outcome; keep all written communications received; preserve proof of property loss from 2015
- Feedback category tag: Call Center Failure / Compensation Delay / Corporate Accountability

### Scenario 22-4: Silicosis Claim Rejected — Mine Doctor Said Lungs Were Fine After 25 Years Underground (Pattern A+C+E)
**Real Basis:** ACTSA (Action for Southern Africa) mineworker case documentation; Johannesburg High Court approval of R5 billion Tshiamiso Trust settlement (July 2019); GroundUp investigative reporting; Tandfonline academic paper (2021); South Africa ODIMWA statutory scheme — only 1.5% of eligible claimants paid
**The Problem:** A 58-year-old former gold mineworker from the Eastern Cape, South Africa, who spent 25 years underground at an AngloGold Ashanti shaft, has a confirmed silicosis diagnosis from a private pulmonologist. When he presented this diagnosis to the company's occupational health clinic, the company doctor told him his lung scarring was "pre-existing" and "not caused by mine dust." He was directed to the ODIMWA statutory compensation process, which rejected his claim on grounds of insufficient service records. He is now coughing blood and cannot work. He has heard about the Tshiamiso Trust but does not know how to register or whether he qualifies.
**What staff/org would do:** The Tshiamiso Trust helpline advises him to obtain a new medical certificate from a designated doctor at his own cost and to submit original employment records, which the mining company says are "unavailable."
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as both a rights violation and a documented case of medical misrepresentation — the company doctor's "pre-existing" finding is consistent with the pattern of systematic denial found across 30+ mining companies in the class action. Confirms he qualifies for Tshiamiso Trust registration even without the company's employment records, as alternative proof of employment is accepted. Provides the direct Tshiamiso Trust registration contact and advises him to engage the Legal Resources Centre, which provided pro bono representation to class action claimants.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What the company doctor told you — that your lungs were fine — is exactly the kind of misrepresentation that led to a R5 billion court-approved settlement. You have rights, and I can help you use them."
- Direct to: Tshiamiso Trust (tshiamisotrust.com, free call 0800 023 772); Legal Resources Centre (lrc.org.za); South African Human Rights Commission (sahrc.org.za); National Institute for Occupational Health (NIOH) for independent re-assessment
- Evidence to collect: Preserve private pulmonologist's report; write down the company doctor's verbal finding with date, clinic name, and doctor name; gather any pay slips, union records, or co-worker statements confirming employment
- Feedback category tag: Medical Misrepresentation / Consumer Turned Away / Occupational Rights

---

### Scenario 22-5: Cyanide in the River, Three Hours of Unanswered Calls, Company Never Warned the Village (Pattern A+B)
**Real Basis:** Earthworks regulatory release (Newmont Ahafo mine cyanide spill, October 2009); Ghana Ministerial Panel finding on delayed community notification; Business & Human Rights Resource Centre / WACAM statement; Ghanavoicereport.com River Tano follow-up
**The Problem:** A farmer in a village downstream of Newmont's Ahafo mine in Brong-Ahafo Region, Ghana, found dead fish along a 3-kilometre stretch of river this morning. His family drinks from this river daily; his wife and two children bathed in it yesterday. He has been trying to call Newmont's community line for three hours — the number rings out with no answer. He then called the district environmental health office; they told him to "wait for the mining company to make a statement." He is asking whether the water is safe.
**What staff/org would do:** The mining company's community relations line continues to go unanswered. The district office advises no action until a company statement is issued. No emergency advisory is posted.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an immediate public health emergency. Instructs the family not to use the river water for any purpose — drinking, bathing, or irrigation — until testing confirms safety. Documents the three-hour unanswered call as a critical notification failure, consistent with what the Ghana Ministerial Panel cited as a specific regulatory violation in 2009. Directs him to the Ghana Environmental Protection Authority emergency line and the Minerals Commission, both of which have authority to order Newmont to disclose the substance and extent of any spill immediately.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Do not use the river water for anything right now — not for drinking, bathing, or crops. Dead fish are a serious warning sign and I am treating this as an emergency. The company's failure to answer your call for three hours is itself a documented violation."
- Direct to: Ghana EPA emergency line (+233 302 664 697); Ghana Minerals Commission; WACAM (Wassa Association of Communities Affected by Mining — wacam.org); Ghana Health Service district officer for human exposure assessment
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the dead fish and river condition with GPS timestamp; collect a water sample in a sealed container; document all unanswered call attempts with time logs; record the names of everyone who had contact with the water and when
- Feedback category tag: Environmental Emergency / Delayed Notification / Call Center Failure

### Scenario 22-6: Regulator Cited the Same Violation Four Times — Company Still Has No Permanent Fix, 6 Million at Risk (Pattern C+E)
**Real Basis:** DeSmog (July 2020); Chile SMA (Superintendencia del Medio Ambiente) formal charges against Anglo American Los Bronces; War on Want (2025); Chile Directorate General of Waters findings (copper, manganese, arsenic in Mapocho River, 2025); Fundacion Glaciares Chilenos
**The Problem:** A municipal water engineer in a commune east of Santiago has submitted four formal environmental complaints to the SMA over 18 months regarding the Los Bronces copper mine's acid drainage contaminating the upper Mapocho River. The SMA's own published statement says the company "has not implemented a definitive solution — it constitutes a repetition of acts previously sanctioned." She has now received the SMA's latest response letter, which acknowledges the breach but grants the company a further 180-day compliance timeline. She is asking what remedies exist when a regulator identifies repeated violations but enforcement does not stop the conduct. The Maipo River Basin — under threat from the same mine — supplies drinking water to approximately 6 million people in Greater Santiago.
**What staff/org would do:** The SMA refers her back to the formal objection process and advises that the 180-day extension is "standard procedure" for complex technical remediation.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that the SMA's own documented language — "repetition of acts previously sanctioned" — constitutes an on-record finding of regulatory failure that is admissible in escalated proceedings. Explains that Chile's Environmental Courts (Tribunales Ambientales) provide an independent pathway to challenge inadequate SMA enforcement. Notes the 6 million people whose drinking water supply is at risk creates grounds for urgency classification before the court.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The SMA's own words — 'repetition of acts previously sanctioned' — are a formal finding that works in your favour. You are not starting from zero. You have a regulatory record that supports escalation to an independent court."
- Direct to: Segundo Tribunal Ambiental (environmental court, Santiago — tribunalambiental.cl); Contraloria General de la Republica (government auditor — can investigate regulatory non-enforcement); Fundacion Glaciares Chilenos; Chile's Defensoria del Pueblo
- Evidence to collect: Obtain certified copies of all four SMA complaint responses; document the 180-day extension decision in writing; compile SMA's "repetition" language from official correspondence; request independent water quality test from a SEREMI-certified laboratory
- Feedback category tag: Regulatory Non-Enforcement / Environmental Transparency / Repeat Violations

### Scenario 22-7: Company Told Villagers the Eviction Was Government-Ordered — No Written Order Exists (Pattern B+C+D)
**Real Basis:** Amnesty International report "Powering Change or Business as Usual?" (September 2023); Al Jazeera (September 2023); Business & Human Rights Resource Centre; Amnesty Canada; field research Kolwezi, Lualaba Province, DRC — 130 interviews across 6 mining projects
**The Problem:** A community elder from a village near Kolwezi, DRC, reports that mining company representatives arrived last week and told residents they must vacate their homes and farmland within 15 days because "the government has ordered the land for the mine." The elder asked for written proof of the government order; none was provided. Residents who asked questions were told they would "lose any compensation" if they refused to leave immediately. Several families have already relocated. The elder believes this may not be a real government order and wants to verify and understand her community's rights before more families move.
**What staff/org would do:** Local government officials give vague responses and decline to confirm or deny whether any order exists. The company's local representative refuses to provide documentation. There is no accessible company grievance mechanism.
**What the Owner does:** Identifies this as a documented company tactic: Amnesty International's 2023 field research across six Kolwezi projects found companies explicitly told communities evictions were government-mandated to prevent communities seeking redress directly. Advises that any forced eviction without a verified government order, a Free Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) process, and a written compensation agreement violates DRC law and the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights. Urges the elder to pause further relocations until the order is verified in writing, and provides contact for the Initiative pour la Bonne Gouvernance et les Droits Humains (IBGDH).
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "This is a recognised tactic. Amnesty International documented exactly this — companies claiming evictions are government orders — across six projects in your region last year. You have rights before any eviction can lawfully proceed, and I can help you use them."
- Direct to: IBGDH (Initiative pour la Bonne Gouvernance et les Droits Humains, Kinshasa); UN OHCHR DRC country office (ohchr.org/en/countries/drc); Global Legal Action Network; IFC CAO if any IFC-linked financing is involved
- Evidence to collect: Request the government order in writing and do not sign any document without it; record names of company representatives who visited the village; document which families have already left and when; photograph all homes and land before any demolition begins
- Feedback category tag: Forced Displacement / Corporate Deception / Rights Violation

### Scenario 22-8: Herder Lost Winter Pasture, Company Denies the Mine Affected Water Wells (Pattern B+D)
**Real Basis:** IFC CAO published cases Mongolia: Oyu Tolgoi-01/Khanbogd and Oyu Tolgoi-02/Khanbogd (complaint filed 2012); Oxford Academic Community Development Journal (2020); TMU article "How Mongolian herders took on a mining giant"
**The Problem:** A nomadic herder in Omnogovi Province, Mongolia, whose family relocated their winter camp in 2011 following pressure from Oyu Tolgoi mine (Rio Tinto / Turquoise Hill) representatives. The replacement grazing land is 30 km further from water. Three of his animals died during last winter's harsh conditions because the water source they relied on had dried up after the mine's Undai River diversion. When he raised this with the mine's community relations officer, he was told: "There is no scientific evidence the mine affected water levels in your area." He received no written compensation agreement at the time of relocation and has no documentation of what was promised.
**What staff/org would do:** The mine's community liaison says the herder should contact the local government soum office to access the Tripartite Council dispute process. The soum office says it has received no formal notification from the mine about his specific case.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that this denial — "no scientific evidence of impact" — is precisely what the CAO investigation found: the CAO mandated a joint fact-finding exercise because the company had withheld hydrological impact information from affected herders. Advises the herder to file a complaint with the IFC CAO, which oversaw the original 2012 dialogue process and can reopen monitoring, and to contact the Centre for Citizens' Alliance Mongolia (CCA). Explains that the absence of a written compensation agreement is itself a documented violation of the mine's IFC performance standards obligations.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The company telling you there is 'no scientific evidence' of impact is the same denial that triggered a full CAO investigation in 2012. You do not need to accept that answer — there is an international process that can require independent water testing."
- Direct to: IFC CAO (cao-ombudsman.org — complaint reopening or new submission); Centre for Citizens' Alliance Mongolia (ca-mongolia.org); Mongolia's National Human Rights Commission (mn-nhrc.mn)
- Evidence to collect: Document livestock deaths with dates and counts; photograph the dry water source and current pasture condition; gather witness statements from neighbouring herders; locate any maps or verbal agreements made at the time of relocation
- Feedback category tag: Displacement Without Documentation / Information Withheld / Pastoralist Rights

---

### Scenario 22-9: Acid Spill Destroyed Crops, Company Offered $84 — Take It or Leave It, Then Disconnected (Pattern A+D+E)
**Real Basis:** Wikipedia — 2025 Sino-Metals Leach Zambia dam disaster; Human Rights Watch (September 2025); PBS NewsHour; Climate Change News (March and September 2025); Chemistry World; Business & Human Rights Resource Centre; Africa Defense Forum
**The Problem:** A smallholder farmer from a village near the Mwambashi River, Zambia, whose two-hectare maize and groundnut plot was burned by acidic effluent from the Sino-Metals Leach Zambia tailings dam failure on 18 February 2025. His entire crop — his family's food supply and only cash income — was destroyed. Company representatives visited two weeks later and offered K2,000 (approximately $84 USD) as "full and final settlement," handing him a form to sign. He asked how the figure was calculated; the representative refused to explain. He asked to speak to someone senior; the representative said this was "the company's final decision." When he called the number on the representative's card the next day, the line was disconnected.
**What staff/org would do:** No response. The disconnected line is never restored. Local government officials advise him to "accept what is offered."
**What the Owner does:** Documents this as a take-it-or-leave-it compensation offer without explanation — consistent with PBS reporting that the company "covered up the extent of the spill" and with independent environmental firm Drizit Zambia finding contamination 30 times higher than official estimates. Advises him not to sign any settlement form. Informs him that 176 farmers filed a collective lawsuit seeking $80 billion in damages. Provides contact for the Zambia Environmental Management Agency (ZEMA) formal complaint pathway and for the Law Association of Zambia (LAZ) pro bono legal services.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Do not sign that form. An offer of $84 with no explanation and a disconnected follow-up line is not a fair settlement — it is a tactic to close your claim before you know your rights."
- Direct to: Zambia Environmental Management Agency (ZEMA — zema.org.zm); Law Association of Zambia (LAZ) pro bono desk; Human Rights Watch Zambia contact (hrw.org); the 176-farmer collective lawsuit — contact Zambia Daily Mail or Lusaka Times for referral to legal representatives
- Evidence to collect: Photograph the destroyed crops and affected land; do not sign the K2,000 settlement form; photograph the form itself on both sides; record the representative's name and company details; document the disconnected call with a screenshot of the dialled number and time
- Feedback category tag: Inadequate Compensation Offer / Call Center Failure / Environmental Harm Minimisation

### Scenario 22-10: Coal Company Cleared Adivasi Forest Without Gram Sabha Consultation — 45-Day Objection Ignored (Pattern B+C)
**Real Basis:** Amnesty International "When Land is Lost, Do We Eat Coal?" (2016) — Central Coalfields Limited, Tetariakhar mine, Jharkhand; Mongabay India (December 2025); HRW "Out of Control: Mining, Regulatory Failure, and Human Rights in India" (June 2012); Vinoba Bhave University survey (2024); Social Science Journal study (2025)
**The Problem:** An elected gram sabha president of an Adivasi village in Jharkhand, India, near the Tetariakhar coalfield reports that Central Coalfields Limited (CCL) began preparatory operations — clearing trees and installing equipment — on land that includes the village's traditional forest without holding a gram sabha consultation, which is legally required under India's Forest Rights Act (FRA, 2006) and the Panchayats (Extension to Scheduled Areas) Act (PESA, 1996) before any such clearance. He submitted a written objection to the district collector 45 days ago. No response has been received. CCL's public relations office told him the land was "notified forest, not village forest" and that consultation was not required.
**What staff/org would do:** The district collector's office logs the objection and takes no further action. CCL's PR office repeats the "notified forest" position without providing any legal basis.
**What the Owner does:** Identifies that CCL's "notified forest" claim does not negate FRA or PESA obligations — both Acts apply where Adivasi communities hold customary rights, regardless of forest classification. Notes that Amnesty International specifically found Central Coalfields Limited excluded Adivasi communities from consultation at Tetariakhar in 2016, making this a documented pattern rather than an isolated event. Advises the gram sabha president to escalate to India's National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) and to file a formal petition with the Ministry of Tribal Affairs. Notes that the National Green Tribunal (NGT) has jurisdiction over FRA and forest clearance violations.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The company's claim that consultation was not required because it is 'notified forest' is legally incorrect. The Forest Rights Act and PESA protect your community's customary rights regardless of forest classification. Your gram sabha had the right to be consulted before any clearance."
- Direct to: India National Human Rights Commission (nhrc.nic.in — online complaint); National Green Tribunal (greentribunal.gov.in); Ministry of Tribal Affairs (tribal.gov.in); Jharkhand Janadhikar Mahasabha (local Adivasi rights network); Mines Minerals and PEOPLE (mmpindia.in) — legal advocacy network
- Evidence to collect: Preserve gram sabha records showing customary land use; retain written proof of the 45-day-old objection submission and any delivery confirmation; photograph the clearance work with date stamps; compile a list of community members who can testify to customary use of the land
- Feedback category tag: Discrimination / Indigenous Rights Violation / Regulatory Non-Compliance

Industry ID: 22

## 23. Social Welfare

### Scenario 23-1: Universal Credit Deductions Taken for Government's Own Administrative Error (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Citizens Advice press release, September 2024 — "Universal Credit recipients unfairly paying £111m a year due to government"; ICE DWP Annual Report 2023–2024, GOV.UK
**The Problem:** A Universal Credit recipient discovers their monthly payment has dropped by £61 with no letter or prior explanation. After logging into their online journal they see a deduction for "overpayment recovery." When they call DWP, the agent confirms a debt exists but cannot explain how it arose. The claimant has not changed their circumstances — the debt is one of 686,756 new overpayments created by DWP official error in 2023–2024, collectively costing claimants £111 million per year. The claimant now cannot cover their rent.
**What staff/org would do:** The DWP phone agent confirms the deduction is being applied and tells the claimant they must repay the debt before it can be reviewed. No information is offered about the right to request a write-off where the error was the agency's own. The agent reads from a script and escalates nothing.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledge the claimant's distress immediately. Confirm they have an explicit right to challenge overpayment deductions — and that DWP is required under its own guidance to consider waiving debts caused by official error, not claimant error. Advise them to submit a mandatory reconsideration request in writing, ask DWP to confirm in writing whether the overpayment was caused by claimant error or official error, and seek free specialist help at once.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (rent at immediate risk; money taken without explanation)
- Opening reply: "Having money taken from your payment without any explanation is wrong — and if this was DWP's own error, you have a clear right to have the debt cancelled. Let us help you challenge it right now."
- Direct consumer to: Citizens Advice (free — citizensadvice.org.uk or freephone 0800 144 8444); DWP mandatory reconsideration (submit in writing within one month of the decision); Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) if DWP fails to respond to a formal complaint
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of UC online journal showing the deduction entry; any letters received from DWP; full log of all calls to DWP including date, time, agent name, and what was said; bank statements showing the reduced payment
- Feedback category tag: Benefits/Financial Harm/Official Error

---

### Scenario 23-2: PIP Disability Benefit Scored Zero Despite Medical Evidence Agency Already Held (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** Disability Rights UK — "7 in 10 PIP appeals won on same evidence DWP already held"; UK Parliament Work and Pensions Committee evidence session, September 2023
**The Problem:** A claimant with a progressive neurological condition submitted three specialist medical letters, a physiotherapy report, and a GP summary before their PIP telephone assessment. Following the assessment, DWP awarded zero points on both components — no PIP at all. The assessment report misquotes the claimant as stating they can walk 200 metres when they clearly stated 20 metres before severe pain. The claimant requested mandatory reconsideration and was refused again on the same evidence. Over 61% of claimants nationally report factual errors of this type in their assessment reports; 70% of PIP appeals at tribunal are overturned — the majority without any new evidence.
**What staff/org would do:** The mandatory reconsideration letter repeats the zero-point decision verbatim, without acknowledging the factual discrepancies the claimant raised. The DWP agent on the follow-up call tells the claimant that if they disagree, they should "go to tribunal."
**What the Owner does:** Validate the experience — this pattern is statistically documented at scale, not an isolated mistake. Advise the claimant to appeal to the First-tier Tribunal (Social Entitlement Chamber) immediately, noting that they do not need new evidence, and to prepare a point-by-point written rebuttal correcting every factual error in the assessment report.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (appeal window of one month from mandatory reconsideration decision — act promptly)
- Opening reply: "A report with factual errors and a zero score despite your evidence is a documented pattern — and 7 in 10 people who appeal PIP to tribunal win, most without any new evidence. You have strong grounds to appeal."
- Direct consumer to: HM Courts and Tribunals Service — appeal form SSCS1 (gov.uk/appeal-benefit-decision); Disability Rights UK; Citizens Advice welfare benefits specialist; Scope disability helpline (0808 800 3333)
- Evidence to collect: Full PIP assessment report (request under Subject Access Request if not automatically provided); all medical letters and reports submitted at assessment; contemporaneous notes from the telephone assessment including exact questions asked and answers given; any GP or specialist willing to confirm the factual error in writing
- Feedback category tag: Disability Benefits/Wrongful Denial/Assessment Factual Error

### Scenario 23-3: DWP Staff Give Wrong Entitlement Advice Over Phone — Then Demand £38,000 Repayment Three Years Later (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** The Big Issue investigative reporting, 2023–2024; GB News — state pensioner £38,000 Universal Credit debt caused by DWP advice
**The Problem:** A state pension-age man who cares for his disabled adult son was advised by a DWP official over the phone that he was entitled to claim Universal Credit. He noted the date of the call and followed the advice. Three years later, DWP stopped his UC claim, told him he had never been eligible, and sent a letter demanding repayment of £38,453. He has a record of the dates he called DWP for advice. No one informed him during any call that he could be making his own contemporaneous notes or that the advice might be wrong.
**What staff/org would do:** DWP's debt recovery team informs the claimant the overpayment is valid and must be repaid regardless of how the claim started. The agent cannot authorise a waiver and tells the claimant to submit a written complaint — while deductions to his other benefits begin immediately. The complaint process takes months with no interim suspension of recovery.
**What the Owner does:** Identify this immediately as an official-error-caused debt, arising directly from incorrect advice given by a DWP agent — one of the strongest grounds for a full debt write-off under DWP's own overpayment waiver policy and established case law. Advise seeking free legal representation immediately given the sum involved, and flag the case for MP parliamentary intervention.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (£38,000 demand; household is a carer with disabled dependent; financial ruin risk)
- Opening reply: "Being advised to claim, following that advice, and then being chased for £38,000 because of the advice you were given is a serious injustice — and there is a documented legal route to challenge this. Here is exactly what to do first."
- Direct consumer to: Citizens Advice Benefits specialist (free — can support waiver application); Child Poverty Action Group welfare benefits legal team; Welfare Rights Unit at local council; local MP to raise as a parliamentary case; Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman (PHSO) after exhausting DWP's internal complaint process
- Evidence to collect: Written chronology of every DWP phone contact — dates, times, what advice was given; any letters from DWP confirming the claim was accepted; bank statements showing UC payments received; any notes made at the time of original phone advice; names of agents where remembered
- Feedback category tag: Benefits/Wrong Information/Official Error/Financial Harm

### Scenario 23-4: SSA Disability Claimant on Hold 93 Minutes, Transferred to Wrong Field Office, Given Incorrect Deadline (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Government Executive, February 2026 — SSA directing case-processing employees to answer phones after 3 hours of training; DREDF qualitative barriers study, 2025 — SSA call access "gotten a lot worse"
**The Problem:** A 54-year-old woman with Stage 3 breast cancer submitted an SSDI application six months ago and was denied at initial review — as 62% of all applicants are. She needs to file a reconsideration appeal but cannot get through. In four attempts, she experienced holds of 60–93 minutes. On two calls she was transferred to a field office in a different state that had no access to her case and could not help. On her fourth call she reached an agent (redirected to phones after 3 hours of training) who gave her an incorrect deadline for the reconsideration submission, creating a risk that she will miss the actual window and lose her appeal rights entirely.
**What staff/org would do:** The SSA field office she reaches confirms it cannot see her case and tells her to call the national number again. The SSA national number places her on hold again. No alternative is offered.
**What the Owner does:** Provide specific alternative contact routes that do not rely on the main phone line. Confirm the actual reconsideration deadline (60 days from denial notice plus 5 days for mailing). Connect her to advocacy resources that have priority access channels.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (terminal illness; SSDI deadlines are hard cutoffs; missed deadline means losing appeal rights)
- Opening reply: "Four calls and still no real help — that is a system failure, not yours. There is a route that does not depend on that phone line. Here is what to do today."
- Direct consumer to: SSA online portal — my Social Security account at ssa.gov to file reconsideration online without calling; National Organization of Social Security Claimants' Representatives (NOSSCR) — nosscr.org — for free or low-cost legal representation; Disability Rights Advocates; local Social Security field office in person (different from a misrouted phone transfer)
- Evidence to collect: Written log of every call attempt with date, time, hold duration, what was said, and the name of the agent who gave the incorrect deadline; the original denial letter with the actual reconsideration deadline printed on it; all medical documentation with treatment timeline and work-stoppage date
- Feedback category tag: Disability Benefits/Call Center Failure/Process Delay/Missed Deadline Risk

### Scenario 23-5: Child Welfare Caseworker Falsified Home Visit Records — Family With Medically Vulnerable Child Received No Actual Support (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Colorado Sun investigative reporting, January 2024 — Larimer County caseworker Sandra Spraker charged with 99 criminal counts including 29 forgery counts for fabricating child welfare check records across at least 10 families
**The Problem:** A parent receives a letter from the county child welfare office confirming a home welfare check took place on a specific date. No social worker came to the home that day — or on any of the three other dates listed in official records spanning the past year. The family's child has a medical condition requiring monthly monitoring and support. The parent has now learned through a contact that the assigned caseworker has been arrested on 99 criminal counts for fabricating visit records across multiple families. Their child has had no actual welfare support for potentially over a year, and the parent does not know what services they should have been receiving.
**What staff/org would do:** The county agency front desk tells the parent the case is under criminal investigation and cannot be discussed. They are placed on hold and later told to expect a letter. No interim caseworker is assigned and no acknowledgement of missed services is made.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as an immediate child safety and adult rights emergency. Advise the parent to demand in writing, today, emergency reassignment of their case to a new caseworker. Escalate directly to the state-level child welfare oversight body — bypassing the county office where the failure occurred. If the child has unmet medical needs, those should be separately flagged to the child's health provider as needing catch-up review.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (child's medical needs unmet for potentially a year; welfare records fabricated; active safety risk)
- Opening reply: "If your child has not actually been checked on despite records saying otherwise, this is an emergency — not something to wait for a letter about. Here is how to escalate right now, bypassing the county office."
- Direct consumer to: Colorado Department of Human Services — Child Welfare Division (state level, bypasses county); Colorado Child Welfare Ombudsman; local legal aid for emergency representation; report fabricated records to Larimer County District Attorney if not already submitted; child's own GP or medical team to flag the gap in welfare monitoring
- Evidence to collect: All county letters claiming visits occurred on specific dates; phone records or diary showing no visitor arrived on those dates; any neighbors or family present who can confirm no social worker came; the child's current medical records documenting unmet needs during the period
- Feedback category tag: Child Welfare/Staff Misconduct/Falsified Records/Emergency

### Scenario 23-6: SNAP Benefits Terminated Without Individual Notice — Single Mother's EBT Card Declined at Checkout (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** CBPP SNAP Tracker, 2025–2026 — at least 3.5 million people lost food assistance following Republican budget reconciliation changes; Arizona lost 51% of its SNAP beneficiary base; Florida documented 15% administrative error rate in SNAP eligibility determinations
**The Problem:** A single mother of three children in Arizona discovers her SNAP EBT card is declined at a grocery checkout. She received no individual letter telling her benefits were stopping. Her benefits were terminated because she did not submit new work documentation under requirements she was not clearly informed about — she has been working 22 hours per week but did not know the new form was required. Arizona has lost over half its SNAP recipients. When she calls the state agency, she waits 45 minutes and is told to reapply — a process taking 30 days — without being told she has a legal right to emergency expedited processing for households with immediate food need.
**What staff/org would do:** The state agency phone agent confirms her case is closed, tells her to submit a new application, and does not mention expedited processing, emergency food resources, or her right to appeal the termination. She is told to call back to check application status.
**What the Owner does:** Inform her immediately that federal SNAP law requires expedited processing (within 7 days) for households facing urgent food insecurity — this is not discretionary and she must request it by name. Direct her to in-person office attendance for fastest processing and to emergency food resources for the days until benefits are restored.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (three children with no food access; EBT card declined; immediate food insecurity)
- Opening reply: "Having your card declined with children to feed is a food emergency — and you have a federal legal right to have your case processed within 7 days under SNAP expedited rules. Here is exactly what to say when you go in."
- Direct consumer to: Arizona Department of Economic Security SNAP office — go in person and request expedited processing by name, citing 7 CFR 273.2(i); 2-1-1 Arizona (dial 211) for same-day emergency food pantry locations; Greater Phoenix Food Bank or local food bank for immediate food while appeal is processed; legal aid for SNAP appeal representation
- Evidence to collect: Receipt or photo of declined EBT transaction with date and amount; any letters or messages received about the work documentation requirement; proof of current work hours such as pay stubs or an employer letter; household income and essential expense documentation for expedited processing eligibility confirmation
- Feedback category tag: Food Assistance/Benefits Terminated/Refused Service/Emergency

### Scenario 23-7: Tigray Registered Beneficiary Arrives at Distribution Point Three Consecutive Months — No Food, No Explanation, No Grievance Channel (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** PBS NewsHour investigation, 2023; Addis Standard exclusive — coordinated food diversion scheme across seven Ethiopian regions involving 63 commercial flour mills, federal government, and regional entities; USAID/WFP suspension of aid following what US officials described as "potentially the largest-ever diversion of food aid in any country"
**The Problem:** A subsistence farmer in Tigray, Ethiopia, is registered as a WFP food aid beneficiary. He has walked four hours to reach the distribution point on the scheduled date. Staff tell him there is nothing available today — no food, no rescheduled date, no written receipt of his attendance. This is the third consecutive month this has happened. What he does not know is that the wheat designated for his community has been diverted to commercial flour mills by local officials as part of a scheme later uncovered spanning seven regions. No posted grievance process exists at the distribution point. There is no independent channel through which he can report that he — a registered beneficiary — received nothing.
**What staff/org would do:** Distribution point staff tell him to return next month. No complaint form is offered, no alternative contact provided, no written record of his visit or non-receipt is given. Staff are affiliated with the same local government structure later implicated in the diversion.
**What the Owner does:** Treat this as an acute food security emergency. Activate an anonymous, tamper-proof grievance recording channel so the non-delivery is formally documented outside local control. Verify his registration and entitlement. Escalate to WFP field monitors and independent humanitarian bodies operating in the region.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (acute food insecurity; three consecutive non-deliveries; family with no income alternative; no functioning recourse channel)
- Opening reply: "You are registered and you were entitled to food today. The fact that nothing was there three months in a row must be formally recorded — here is how to report it safely, outside the local office."
- Direct consumer to: WFP Inspector General — secure reporting at oig.wfp.org/report-fraud (independent of country office management); OCHA Ethiopia for humanitarian escalation; Ethiopian Human Rights Commission; international NGOs active in Tigray — IRC, MSF, NRC — who maintain independent beneficiary hotlines separate from government-affiliated structures
- Evidence to collect: WFP beneficiary registration card or number; written or recorded log of dates and times of each visit to the distribution point with no food received; names or descriptions of staff present; other beneficiaries present who also received nothing and are willing to corroborate; any communications from WFP or local authorities about the distributions
- Feedback category tag: Food Aid/Non-Delivery/Corruption/Emergency

### Scenario 23-8: Ethiopian NGO Field Coordinator Documents Food Diversion to Flour Mills — Blocked When Attempting to Report to Implicated Regional Body (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Addis Standard exclusive, 2023 — coordinated diversion scheme confirmed involving 63 commercial flour mills across seven Ethiopian regions; WFP and USAID suspending operations after discovering what officials called the largest-ever diversion of food aid in any country
**The Problem:** A field coordinator at a local Ethiopian NGO has conducted site visits over six weeks and documented that wheat from three consecutive WFP distributions has been transported to a commercial flour mill in a nearby town rather than to beneficiary communities. She has vehicle registration numbers, photographs, dates, and the names of officials who signed off on the transfers. She attempted to file a formal report with the regional coordination body responsible for aid distribution oversight — but that regional body is named in the scheme. Her WFP country office contact has not responded to two follow-up emails over three weeks. A regional official has made indirect contact suggesting she should withdraw her documentation.
**What staff/org would do:** The regional coordination body acknowledges receipt of her report but takes no visible action. WFP's internal email contact remains unresponsive. No escalation pathway is communicated to her. The obstruction she is experiencing is itself undocumented and informal.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately direct the field coordinator to independent reporting channels that fully bypass the implicated regional body and the non-responsive country office. Confirm her documentation has legal and investigative value and must be securely preserved now — before submission. Connect her to international whistleblower protection mechanisms appropriate to the Ethiopia operational context.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (not immediate physical danger as described — but evidence is perishable and obstruction is active; treat as time-sensitive)

- Direct consumer to: WFP Inspector General directly — oig.wfp.org/report-fraud (fully independent of WFP management and country office); USAID Office of Inspector General — oig.usaid.gov (reports from Ethiopia accepted); UN Ethics Office for formal whistleblower protection registration; Transparency International Ethiopia; Committee to Protect Journalists if personal safety escalates
- Evidence to collect: All photographs, vehicle registration records, and date-stamped documentation secured with a trusted external party before submission; copies of all emails to WFP with sent timestamps; written record of the obstruction contact including date, medium, and exact words used; copies shared with a trusted colleague or legal adviser outside the organization as backup
- Feedback category tag: Food Aid/Staff Misconduct/Whistleblower/Corruption Reporting

### Scenario 23-9: Somali Displaced Family Never Receives WFP Food Aid — Delivery Recorded as Complete by Contractor That Diverted It (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** AEI analysis of UN Monitoring Group on Somalia findings — three Somali businessmen holding approximately $160 million in WFP food transport contracts simultaneously alleged to be diverting food aid while engaged in arms trading
**The Problem:** A family displaced by drought and living in an IDP camp in Somalia has been registered as WFP food aid beneficiaries for four months. They have not received any of the three scheduled monthly food deliveries. Other families in the camp report the same pattern. Community members observed the contracted transport vehicle arrive once, unload a small fraction of what should have been delivered, and leave quickly. WFP's official delivery records show all deliveries as completed and confirmed. There is no grievance box at the camp, no posted hotline, and no process by which beneficiaries can formally report a discrepancy between what records say and what they actually received.
**What staff/org would do:** Local distribution monitors employed through the same contractor network confirm deliveries as complete in the system. There is no independent verification mechanism at this location. The family is told to speak to the local distribution coordinator — who is affiliated with the same contractor.
**What the Owner does:** Provide a trusted, contractor-independent grievance channel. Record the non-delivery incidents systematically across the camp to establish a pattern across multiple families. Escalate to WFP Inspector General and independent humanitarian monitors operating in Somalia.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true (displaced family with children; four months of acute food insecurity; records falsely show deliveries completed; no existing recourse)
- Opening reply: "Records that show delivery completed when your family received nothing is exactly the kind of discrepancy that must be reported independently of the local system. You have a right to raise this — here is how to do it outside the contractor network."
- Direct consumer to: WFP Inspector General — oig.wfp.org/report-fraud (accepts reports in Arabic and Somali); UNHCR Somalia protection desk for displaced person advocacy; OCHA Somalia; independent NGOs operating in the camp — IRC, Norwegian Refugee Council (NRC) — who maintain separate beneficiary feedback mechanisms independent of WFP contractors
- Evidence to collect: WFP beneficiary card or registration number; written log of dates and expected delivery amounts for each month with record of what was or was not received; names of other affected families in the camp willing to corroborate; any observations of contractor vehicle activity with approximate dates, times, and quantities unloaded
- Feedback category tag: Food Aid/Non-Delivery/Contractor Fraud/Displaced Population

**Real Basis:** UN Monitoring Group on Somalia annual report; AEI analysis — WFP Somalia transport contractors collectively holding approximately $160 million in WFP contracts while simultaneously alleged to be diverting food aid and engaged in arms trading
**The Problem:** A WFP-contracted local field monitor in Somalia has directly observed a transport contractor diverting approximately 40% of a food consignment to a commercial depot instead of the designated beneficiary distribution site. She is aware the contractor holds contracts worth approximately $160 million and has significant local influence and connections. She is considering whether and how to escalate, and whether doing so puts her at personal risk.

**What the Owner does:** Provide a secure, direct reporting channel that is fully independent of the local management structure and the contractor. Affirm her legal and professional obligation to report. Connect her with whistleblower protection resources and, if her personal safety is a concern, with NGO security desks that can advise on safe escalation in the Somali operational context.

## 24. Tourism / Hospitality

### Scenario 24-1: Thomas Cook Collapse — Family Stranded Abroad With No Return Flights (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** CAA/UK Civil Aviation Authority ATOL refund data; National Audit Office investigation into Thomas Cook collapse; ABTA guidance (September 2019) — 150,000 UK tourists stranded across 54 airports; Operation Matterhorn repatriated ~94% of passengers over 15 days
**The Problem:** A British family of four is on day two of a two-week holiday in Majorca. They have just read on their phones that Thomas Cook has ceased trading overnight. Their return flights no longer exist. The hotel has told them they must "make their own arrangements" within 24 hours as the booking no longer has a valid payment reference. They cannot get through on Thomas Cook's defunct customer service number, and ABTA's phone line is playing an automated message. They do not know if they have ATOL protection.
**What staff/org would do:** Direct the family back to the Thomas Cook number that no longer works; advise them to "wait and see" before taking any action; fail to mention that the CAA has a dedicated repatriation operation actively running right now with free flights home.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately confirms the family should not pay the hotel again or book their own flights yet. Directs them to the UK CAA's dedicated Thomas Cook information page, explains that Operation Matterhorn is running free repatriation flights right now for package holiday customers, and tells them to check their booking confirmation for an ATOL certificate number. Advises them to document every hotel communication in writing in case ATOL claims are needed, and tells them to stay at the hotel until the CAA flight notification arrives.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "This is a frightening situation — being stranded abroad when your tour operator has collapsed is a genuine emergency. You are protected if you have a package booking. Let me tell you exactly what to do right now."
- Direct to: UK Civil Aviation Authority ATOL claims portal (caa.co.uk/thomascook); ABTA consumer helpline (0117 927 6945); local British Consulate if in immediate physical distress; CAA repatriation flight notification system
- Evidence to collect: ATOL certificate number from original booking confirmation, bank or card statements showing full payment, hotel's written notice demanding they vacate, screenshots of all CAA and ABTA communications received
- Feedback category tag: Tour operator collapse / emergency repatriation / ATOL rights / stranded abroad

### Scenario 24-2: Resort Food Poisoning Cluster — Tour Operator Dismisses Illness Complaints Before Deaths (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Times of Israel, Egyptian Streets, Hospitality Inside reporting on Steigenberger Aqua Magic Hotel, Hurghada, Egypt (August 2018); Sick Holiday / YorkTest illness-claim database documenting 380 UK tourist illness claims from Hurghada since 2017; guest Alison Cope's documented testimony that Thomas Cook "fobbed off" earlier complaints
**The Problem:** A British guest at a large all-inclusive resort in Hurghada has been violently ill for 36 hours with severe vomiting, diarrhoea, and high fever. She reported her illness to the resort medical centre yesterday; staff told her to drink more water and gave her a single oral rehydration sachet. She has since learned that three other guests on her floor have identical symptoms. Her tour operator's in-resort representative dismissed her concern by phone, saying "there's always a settling-in period in a new climate." She can no longer keep any fluids down.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue reassuring the guest that symptoms are normal; delay calling a doctor; fail to flag the illness cluster to resort management or Egyptian health authorities; keep her in the resort rather than arranging hospital transfer, citing "policy."
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as a medical emergency requiring immediate escalation. Instructs her to demand hospital transfer in writing — not just resort medical centre attention — and to contact the British Embassy emergency line if the operator representative remains unresponsive. Tells her that Egyptian regulations require hotels to report illness clusters, and she can invoke this formally. Advises her she has full rights under the UK Package Travel Regulations 2018 to demand alternative accommodation and medical cost reimbursement if the operator fails to act.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — multiple guests with the same symptoms after 36 hours — is a food safety cluster, not normal travel sickness. You need hospital assessment today, not tomorrow. Here is exactly what to do."
- Direct to: British Embassy Cairo emergency line (+20 2 2791 6000); Egyptian Ministry of Health hotline (08008880700); ABTA consumer advice line for tour operator obligations under Package Travel Regulations 2018; UK FCDO travel emergency guidance (gov.uk/foreign-travel-advice/egypt)
- Evidence to collect: Dated written complaint submitted to hotel medical centre, names and room numbers of other ill guests (if consented), photos of all food and drink consumed in past 48 hours, receipts for any medical expenses, written record of tour operator representative's dismissal
- Feedback category tag: Food safety emergency / resort illness cluster / tour operator duty of care failure / complaint dismissed pre-escalation

### Scenario 24-3: All-Inclusive Resort Food Poisoning — Family Refused Hospital Transfer as Child Deteriorates (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** CBC News, Lawyer Monthly, PAX News reporting on Viva Dominicus Beach by Wyndham Resort, Dominican Republic — Gougeon family deaths December 2023; Dominican Republic Coroner's Report; $10 million lawsuit filed January 2025 naming Wyndham Hotels, Air Transat Holidays, and the resort's on-site medical clinic
**The Problem:** A Canadian couple arrived at a Wyndham all-inclusive resort in the Dominican Republic with their two children. Within hours of eating at the resort buffet, all four are severely ill. Both parents and their elder child have been taken to the resort's medical clinic; the on-site doctor has told them it is "stomach upset from the flight" and given anti-nausea tablets. The younger child's condition is deteriorating rapidly. The father is demanding immediate hospital transfer; resort staff are telling him to "give the medicine time" and that leaving the resort "will void the medical coverage."
**What staff/org would do:** Minimise symptom severity, delay the ambulance call, incorrectly advise that going to hospital independently voids travel coverage, fail to flag the case to the tour operator's emergency line, and not document the incident as a potential food safety event requiring regulatory notification.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately tells the father that no resort has legal authority to void medical coverage by demanding hospital care — that claim is false and designed to delay. Instructs him to call an ambulance directly (Dominican Republic emergency: 911) or demand the resort call one immediately without waiting for staff agreement. Tells him to photograph the buffet and all food served, keep any food packaging visible, and send video immediately to a family member abroad for safekeeping. Advises him to contact the Canadian Embassy in Santo Domingo and his travel insurer's 24-hour emergency line simultaneously.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Please do not wait. A child's condition deteriorating after a shared buffet meal — with the whole family ill — is a medical emergency. Call 911 in the Dominican Republic right now. Here is what you must do at the same time."
- Direct to: Dominican Republic emergency services (911); Canadian Embassy Santo Domingo (+1-809-262-3100); Global Affairs Canada Emergency Watch and Response Centre (1-613-996-8885); travel insurer 24-hour emergency medical line; Transport Canada travel advisory line
- Evidence to collect: Time-stamped photographs and video of buffet and all food areas, names of every resort and clinic staff member who refused escalation, written record of exact statements made by each staff member, all medical receipts from clinic and hospital, any food samples preserved in sealable containers if safely possible
- Feedback category tag: Resort food safety emergency / child medical emergency / tour operator duty of care / refused escalation

### Scenario 24-4: Hotel Files Criminal Defamation Charges Against Guest for Negative Online Review (Pattern E)
**Real Basis:** Fox News Travel, View from the Wing, Divernet reporting on Wesley Barnes arrest — Sea View Resort & Spa, Koh Chang, Thailand (2019); TripAdvisor's documented "Alarming Business Practices" warning badge created specifically in response to this case; Thailand's Computer Crimes Act (amended 2017) carrying penalties up to five years imprisonment
**The Problem:** A foreign tourist posted a one-star Google review of a Thai beach resort two weeks ago, describing rude staff and a dirty room. He has just received a formal letter from a Thai lawyer stating that the resort owner has filed a criminal defamation complaint under Thailand's Criminal Defamation Act and Computer Crimes Act. The letter demands he appear at a local police station within five days and states he faces up to two years in prison. He is still in Thailand on a tourist visa and cannot leave the country while charges are pending.
**What staff/org would do:** Advise the tourist to simply take down the review and apologise publicly to make the case go away — without explaining that a public apology may constitute an implicit admission of guilt or be weaponised against him in further proceedings, and without recommending legal counsel.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately advises the tourist to make no public statements, not delete the review, and not issue any apology without consulting a Thai criminal defence lawyer first, as doing so may seriously prejudice his legal position. Tells him to register with the US Embassy in Bangkok immediately, and to retain a lawyer before responding to any police summons. Explains that this is a documented pattern in Thailand — not an isolated event — and that TripAdvisor has a formal process for marking properties that use legal threats against reviewers with an "Alarming Business Practices" warning.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false (no immediate physical danger, but time-sensitive legal response required within days)
- Opening reply: "This is a serious situation, but there are clear steps to protect yourself. Do not delete the review, do not apologise publicly, and do not respond to the lawyer's letter without speaking to a Thai defence lawyer first. Here is exactly what to do."
- Direct to: US Embassy Bangkok American Citizen Services (emergency line: +66 2 205 4049); Thai Bar Association for licensed criminal defence lawyers (thaibar.or.th); TripAdvisor content integrity team (to formally flag legal threat and trigger Alarming Business Practices review); Electronic Frontier Foundation resources on legal threats over online speech (eff.org)
- Evidence to collect: Preserve the full original review text and all screenshots before anything changes, save every communication received from the hotel's lawyer, document the complete timeline of the stay with all complaints made, photograph any evidence of the conditions described in the review, retain all hotel receipts and booking records
- Feedback category tag: Legal threat against reviewer / complaint suppression via criminal law / consumer rights violation / criminalised feedback

### Scenario 24-5: Phone-Spoofed Fake Airline Call Center Charges Fraudulent Rebooking Fees (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** BBB Warning press release — Flightsassist, Midtown Manhattan operation, 109 BBB complaints across 30 US states, F rating (2021–2024); documented pattern of phone number spoofing to impersonate airline reservations lines, charging consumers for non-existent "problems with their bookings"
**The Problem:** A traveller in Texas searched online for "Delta Airlines customer service" and called the top result. The agent confirmed they were "Delta reservations" and told her there was "a problem with her upcoming booking" that required immediate correction or her ticket would be cancelled. She paid $340 by credit card to "re-confirm" the booking. She has now called the real Delta Airlines and been told they have no record of the payment, her original booking is completely unaffected, and they have no authorised third-party agents who contact customers in this way. The fraudulent call center number is now unreachable.
**What staff/org would do:** Tell the consumer "there is nothing we can do since you authorised the charge"; treat it as a merchant dispute rather than fraud; fail to advise on the Section 75 or chargeback pathway; not mention reporting to the FTC, FBI IC3, or Texas AG.
**What the Owner does:** Immediately confirms this is a documented phone spoofing fraud scheme targeting airline customers and that there is a strong pathway to recovering her money. Advises her to call her credit card issuer immediately and report it as fraud — specifically as an "unauthorised transaction / fraud," not a standard merchant dispute — as this changes the processing pathway and gives her the strongest recourse. Tells her to file reports with the FTC, BBB Scam Tracker, and FBI IC3 today, and that the Texas AG's office has taken specific enforcement action against operators running this exact scheme.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What you have described is a documented travel booking fraud scheme where scammers impersonate airline call centres. You have a strong case for a full refund through your credit card issuer. Here is what to do right now."
- Direct to: Credit card issuer fraud department (report as fraudulent charge — not a dispute — to trigger fraud processing); FTC fraud reporting portal (reportfraud.ftc.gov); FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (ic3.gov); BBB Scam Tracker (bbb.org/scamtracker); Texas AG Consumer Protection Division (1-800-621-0508)
- Evidence to collect: Full phone number called, exact date and time of the call, name given by the agent, precise script used including "problem with your booking" language, transaction receipt and card statement entry, screenshots of the Google search result that listed the fraudulent number
- Feedback category tag: Call center fraud / airline phone spoofing / travel booking scam / financial loss recovery

### Scenario 24-6: OTA Booking Not Received at Hotel — Family With Young Children in Transfer Loop at Night (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** BBB Warning article — Agoda (Booking Holdings subsidiary), 260+ complaints, F rating (2023–2024); consumer complaint narratives explicitly documenting reservation-not-received at property and extended transfer loops between OTA agents and hotel front desk with no resolution
**The Problem:** A family of four with two children under seven has just arrived at a Florida resort hotel after a long flight. They show their Agoda confirmation email at the front desk; the hotel has no record of the booking. They call Agoda's customer service line; after 25 minutes on hold an agent tells them "the hotel will have the booking — please speak to them." The hotel says Agoda needs to send a new confirmation. Back on hold for 40 minutes. The second Agoda agent cannot locate the booking reference and says it "may have been cancelled by the system." It is 9 p.m. The hotel has one room left at triple the original price.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue routing the family between Agoda and the hotel indefinitely; refuse to authorise emergency rebooking reimbursement without supervisor approval that is "not available tonight"; tell the family to email the complaints department tomorrow morning.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that the family has an immediate right to arrange alternative accommodation and claim all costs back from Agoda, and that Agoda's inability to honour the confirmed booking constitutes a breach of contract. Advises them to book accommodation immediately using a credit card to preserve chargeback rights, keep every receipt, and within the hour send a formal written notice to Agoda by email stating they have been forced to rebook due to Agoda's failure — this creates the paper trail for full cost recovery.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "You have two young children and no room tonight because of the booking platform's failure. Let's solve the immediate problem first, then recover your money. Here is what to do right now."
- Direct to: Agoda formal complaint escalation in writing (legal@agoda.com — send written notice within the hour); Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services consumer hotline (1-800-435-7352); credit card issuer — note the situation now before booking alternative so the chargeback record is clean; BBB formal complaint against Agoda (bbb.org)
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of original Agoda confirmation email with booking reference and full price paid, time-stamped log of every call to Agoda including agent names and what was said, hotel's written or verbal statement that no booking was received, all receipts for emergency alternative accommodation booked tonight
- Feedback category tag: OTA booking failure / call center transfer loop / no-show accommodation / urgent family housing / refused resolution

### Scenario 24-7: Resort Pool Child Drowning — Safety Accountability and Evidence Preservation (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** Hawaii News Now, Maui News, KITV4, eTurboNews reporting on Westin Maui Resort & Spa — Daelyn Ntwaagae, 4 years old, Kaanapali, Hawaii (1 May 2023); media coverage documented it was "unclear if lifeguards were on duty and what, if anything, they did to assist"; Marriott-branded property made no public statement on safety protocols
**The Problem:** A couple staying at a major branded resort in Hawaii is at the poolside after their four-year-old was found unresponsive in the resort's main swimming pool. Emergency services have been called and the child is being transported to hospital. The parents are in shock. Resort security is telling them to "move to an office" before they have had any opportunity to document anything at the scene. A resort front desk manager has told them "the resort cannot make any statements at this time."
**What staff/org would do:** Move the parents away from the pool scene, prevent any witness contact, decline to confirm whether lifeguards were on duty, and route all further communication through a legal department that will not engage for days.
**What the Owner does:** Advises the parents — or a trusted person helping them — that before moving from the poolside they need to document the scene: photograph whether lifeguard chairs are staffed, whether any "no lifeguard on duty" or supervision signs are posted, and whether any safety equipment is in place. Advises them to note the names and contact details of any witnesses who approach voluntarily, and to formally request in writing from the duty manager a copy of the pool safety logbook and lifeguard duty roster for that day. Tells them to file an incident report directly with Maui Police Department rather than through the resort, and to consult a personal injury attorney before signing any resort documents.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "We are deeply sorry. Your child's emergency care is the absolute priority right now. Once care is underway, there are steps that must happen in the next few hours to protect your family's rights that cannot wait."
- Direct to: Maui Police Department direct report (not through resort): (808) 244-6400; Hawaii Department of Health Sanitation Branch for pool safety regulatory complaint; Hawaii Attorney General consumer protection division; personal injury attorney with hotel premises liability experience — consult before signing any resort documents
- Evidence to collect: Photographs of pool area including lifeguard stations, posted pool rules, any lifeguard uniforms or rescue equipment visible or absent; names of all resort staff who spoke to the family; time-stamped record of everyone present at the scene; contact details of any witnesses; copies of all resort communications received — do not sign any documents without legal advice
- Feedback category tag: Child safety emergency / pool drowning / resort premises liability / safety infrastructure failure / evidence preservation

### Scenario 24-8: Cross-Border Hotel Cancellation — OTA Forces Voucher, Each Party Blames the Other (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** ECC-Net Annual Report 2023 — 124,119 cross-border consumer queries handled; 21% involving cancelled accommodation services; €8.8 million recovered through direct mediation in 22,500 cases; European Commission documentation of structural cross-border call center barrier where consumer in Country A, OTA in Country B, and hotel in Country C creates unresolvable transfer loop
**The Problem:** A German tourist booked three nights at a hotel in Lisbon through an international OTA registered in the Netherlands. Four days before arrival the hotel cancelled her booking, citing "renovation work." The OTA is refusing a full cash refund and offering only a voucher for a future OTA booking. She does not want a voucher; she wants her €540 back. She has called the OTA's German customer service line three times; each time the agent tells her the refund decision is "the hotel's decision, not ours," and the hotel tells her it is "the OTA's decision, not ours." She cannot absorb the loss.
**What staff/org would do:** Continue directing the consumer between the hotel and the OTA indefinitely, with each citing the other's responsibility; present the voucher as the only resolution; not mention the EU Alternative Dispute Resolution framework or her rights under EU law for cancelled services.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms clearly that under the EU Consumer Rights Directive, where a service is cancelled by the provider, the consumer is entitled to a full cash refund — not a voucher — within 14 days. Advises her to file a formal complaint with ECC Germany, which will mediate directly with the Dutch-registered OTA on her behalf under the ECC-Net cross-border framework at no cost. Advises her to also file simultaneously with the Dutch ACM as the OTA's home regulator. Explains that if mediation fails, the EU Small Claims Procedure allows cross-border claims up to €5,000.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What you are being told — that you must accept a voucher for a cancelled booking — is not your only option under EU law. You are entitled to a full cash refund. Here is how to claim it."
- Direct to: ECC Germany — free cross-border mediation service (ecc-germany.de); ACM Netherlands — OTA's home regulator (acm.nl); EU Online Dispute Resolution platform (ec.europa.eu/consumers/odr); European Small Claims Procedure if mediation fails (for claims up to €5,000 across EU borders)
- Evidence to collect: Original booking confirmation with total price paid, OTA cancellation notice with exact date received and stated reason, complete records of all calls and emails to both OTA and hotel (with dates, agent names, and what was said), bank statement showing original charge, written record of the voucher offer — do not accept it in writing before seeking advice
- Feedback category tag: Cross-border accommodation cancellation / OTA refund refusal / EU consumer rights / call center transfer loop / transparency failure
