### Scenario 29-9: Seminar Relocated Internationally After Cancellation — Attendee Denied Refund Under Non-Refundable Terms (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Tony Robbins / Robbins Research International — BuzzFeed News year-long investigative report published May 2019 (verified by NBC News; 10+ women interviewed, leaked internal documents); BBB complaints against Robbins Research International on record for refund refusals and billing disputes; participants who sought refunds following the BuzzFeed investigation reported extended customer service hold times and inability to cancel future event bookings.
**The Problem:** A participant in Toronto, Canada paid CAD $5,200 for a four-day "Unleash the Power Within" event. The event was suspended partway through day two due to a health and safety issue at the venue. Attendees were told by a stage announcement that rescheduling information would be emailed within 72 hours. Seventy-two hours passed with no email. She called Robbins Research International customer service: 38 minutes on hold before reaching an agent, who told her the event was being rescheduled to a date in Phoenix, Arizona. She cannot travel internationally and requested a refund for the portion of the event not delivered. The agent said: "Tickets are non-refundable under the terms you accepted and rescheduling to the next available event is the only option we can offer." She asked to speak to a supervisor, who read the same policy.
**What staff/org would do:** Customer service repeats the non-refund policy and offers only a ticket transfer to another attendee — but she must find the buyer herself. No written record of the cancellation notice or rescheduling offer is provided by the company.
**What the Owner does:** Documents that a cancelled event with a fundamental change of location triggers specific Canadian consumer rights that override standard non-refundable terms, and directs her to both the credit card dispute process and provincial consumer protection.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "When an event is cancelled or substantially changed — including being relocated to a different country requiring international travel — Canadian consumer protection law gives you the right to a refund regardless of what the original ticket terms say. The terms cannot legally override this. Let's put together a clear record of your case."
- Who to direct the consumer to: Consumer Protection Ontario (1-800-889-9768 or ontario.ca/page/consumer-protection-ontario) to file a complaint and understand your rights under the Consumer Protection Act 2002; Competition Bureau Canada (1-800-348-5358) if the advertising of the event did not disclose cancellation-for-relocation policies; dispute the CAD $5,200 charge with your credit card issuer citing non-delivery of contracted service and a fundamental change of location
- What evidence the consumer should collect/preserve: The ticket purchase confirmation and receipt; the original event advertised location and dates; the text of the non-refund terms shown at time of purchase; notes from the stage announcement on the day of cancellation (date, time, exact words); call logs from the customer service call (date, hold time, agent name, supervisor name); any email eventually received about the Phoenix rescheduling
- Feedback category tag: `refund_refused / event_cancellation / consumer_rights / location_change`

### Scenario 29-10: Consumer Harmed by Unqualified "Trauma Coach" — Every Regulatory Body Turns Her Away (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** UK regulatory gap — UK Parliament Research Briefing CBP-10849 (May 2026) and HCPC "Misuse of Protected Title" guidance; Training Journal UK 2024 regulatory analysis. The UK has no statutory regulation of the titles "life coach," "wellness coach," or "trauma coach." The HCPC protects specific titles such as "psychologist" and "psychotherapist" but not "coach." This means any person in the UK can use these titles and offer services to the public without any qualifications, and consumers have no licensing-based protection and no single regulatory body with authority to act.
**The Problem:** A woman in Leeds, UK paid GBP 3,600 over four months for weekly sessions with a practitioner who advertised herself online as a "certified trauma coach and healing specialist" and listed several self-issued certificates. The practitioner used techniques she called "trauma release exercises" and "somatic reprogramming." Following the sessions the consumer experienced worsening anxiety and recurring intrusive episodes. Her GP assessed that no evidence-based trauma processing methodology had been used and that some of the techniques may have been counterproductive. The consumer then contacted: (1) the HCPC — told "coach" is not a protected title and they have no jurisdiction; (2) the BACP — told the practitioner is not a member; (3) the ICF (International Coaching Federation) — told the practitioner is not ICF-accredited; (4) Trading Standards — told this is a civil dispute outside their remit. She has been turned away by every body she could identify. The practitioner is still advertising on the same platforms.
**What staff/org would do:** Each regulatory body legitimately explains it has no jurisdiction under current law. None can de-certify or sanction the practitioner. No body refers her to a body that can help. The practitioner remains active.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the genuine regulatory gap documented in Parliament, maps every available remedy however imperfect, supports the consumer in pursuing civil and advertising-based routes, and documents the systemic failure for policy advocacy.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You have done everything right. You contacted every body a reasonable person would contact, and you have been failed by a genuine gap in UK law that Parliament has documented and is reviewing. This is not your fault and it is not a reflection on the validity of what happened to you. There are still some options even within this gap, and I want to help you pursue them."
- Who to direct the consumer to: Citizens Advice (citizensadvice.org.uk or freephone 0800 144 8848) for guidance on the Small Claims Track to recover GBP 3,600 if the practitioner made false credential or qualification claims in advertising; Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk or 0300 123 2040) if the "certified" and "specialist" credential claims were fabricated; Advertising Standards Authority (asa.org.uk) to report the online advertising as misleading regarding qualifications; your GP to formally document the harm in your medical record for any future proceedings; NHS Talking Therapies (previously IAPT) for referral to evidence-based support
- What evidence the consumer should collect/preserve: Screenshots of the practitioner's full online profile and advertising exactly as it appeared when you paid (including all stated qualifications and certificates); all invoices and payment records for every session; your GP's written assessment; a written timeline of sessions and the effects experienced after each; all email and messaging communications with the practitioner; records of all four regulatory contacts (who you spoke to, what they said, dates)
- Feedback category tag: `information_failure / regulatory_gap / unqualified_practitioner / consumer_turned_away / harm_from_service`

Industry ID: 29

## 30. Technology / Software

### Scenario 30-1: EU User's Facebook Data Transferred to US Servers Without Legal Basis (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Irish Data Protection Commission press release, May 2023; IAPP — "Meta fined GDPR-record 1.2 billion euros in data transfer case"; TechCrunch, August 2024 — "The 10 largest GDPR fines on Big Tech"
**The Problem:** An EU consumer reads media coverage of the Irish DPC's €1.2 billion ruling against Meta and realises their Facebook profile — including private messages, friends lists, and years of behavioral data — was transferred to and stored on US servers for years without a valid legal basis. They want to know exactly what data was transferred, demand its deletion from US systems, and understand their rights. They navigate to Facebook's Help Center and find only automated FAQs about downloading their data. There is no route to object to cross-border data transfers specifically, no live agent, and no confirmation that their individual profile was involved.
**What staff/org would do:** Facebook's automated support system routes the user to a generic "Privacy Basics" page and a data download tool showing what Meta holds, but not where it has been sent. Any submitted request receives an auto-acknowledgement and goes unanswered. Meta makes no proactive outreach to EU users about the transfer or the DPC ruling, treating the regulatory matter as resolved at the corporate level while individual users remain uninformed.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the concern immediately, confirms the ruling is real and significant, explains the consumer's specific GDPR rights — right to erasure, right to object to processing, right to lodge a complaint — and directs them to the Irish DPC and digital rights organisations without minimising the structural nature of the harm.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "Your concern is completely valid. The Irish Data Protection Commission issued a record €1.2 billion fine against Meta in May 2023 specifically because EU user data was transferred to US servers for years without proper legal safeguards. You have enforceable rights here and a clear route to use them."
- Direct to: Irish Data Protection Commission (dataprotection.ie) to lodge a formal GDPR complaint; Meta's GDPR Data Subject Request portal (facebook.com/privacy/policy) for erasure and access requests; noyb.eu (European Centre for Digital Rights) which led the original complaint and supports collective claims; your national data protection authority if not in Ireland
- Evidence to collect: Download your Facebook data (Settings > Your Facebook Information > Download Your Information) and note what metadata is included; record the date your account was created; screenshot any prior privacy objection or data deletion requests submitted and Meta's responses; save links to the DPC ruling for your complaint file
- Feedback category: Data Privacy Violation / Unauthorized Cross-Border Data Transfer / GDPR Enforcement

---

### Scenario 30-2: Amazon Prime "Iliad Flow" — Call Agent Refuses to Cancel Subscription by Phone (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** FTC press release June 2023 — "FTC Takes Action Against Amazon for Enrolling Consumers in Amazon Prime Without Consent"; Amazon Prime FTC settlement September 2025, $2.5 billion ($1B penalty + $1.5B refunds to ~35 million consumers); Fortune, NPR reporting
**The Problem:** A US consumer signed up for a free Amazon Prime trial two years ago and forgot to cancel. They have been charged $139 per year — twice — without realising it. When they attempt to cancel online they are walked through a four-page, six-click process that loops through discount offers, benefit reminders, and guilt screens before any cancellation option appears. Frustrated, they call Amazon's customer service line and ask the agent to process the cancellation. The agent tells them that cancellation must be completed online through the self-service flow and will not process the request on the call. The call ends with the subscription still active and no refund initiated.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent reads from a script directing the consumer back to the online cancellation flow, offers a one-month "goodwill" credit as compensation for the confusion, and does not disclose that the FTC filed suit over this exact process or that a $2.5 billion settlement exists. The consumer is left with a partial credit and no cancellation.
**What the Owner does:** Takes the consumer's report seriously, explains that this deliberate cancellation obstruction — internally named "Iliad Flow" by Amazon — is the subject of a major FTC enforcement action and settlement, and tells the consumer how to claim their portion of the $1.5 billion refund pool.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "This is a documented consumer protection case, not just a frustrating experience. Amazon's cancellation process was formally found by the FTC to be designed to obstruct users — the agency called it the 'Iliad Flow' and Amazon settled for $2.5 billion in September 2025. You may be entitled to a refund of up to $51 and your complaint adds weight to ongoing enforcement."
- Direct to: FTC complaint portal (reportfraud.ftc.gov) to file a formal complaint; Amazon Prime FTC settlement claims administrator — check current claims eligibility at the settlement website; your state attorney general's consumer protection office for state-level action; your credit card company to dispute the unauthorized charges under the Fair Credit Billing Act (60-day window from statement date)
- Evidence to collect: Bank or credit card statements showing all Amazon Prime charge dates and amounts; screenshots of the online cancellation screens showing the multi-step obstruction flow; any email receipts for the Prime subscription; a record of the call including date, approximate time, and what the agent said; any chat transcripts from prior cancellation attempts
- Feedback category: Subscription Trap / Unauthorized Charges / Dark Pattern UX / Call Center Failure

### Scenario 30-3: Child's TikTok Account Public by Default — Unknown Adult Paired Without Parental Consent (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Irish Data Protection Commission press release, September 15, 2023 — DPC fines TikTok €345 million; Baker Botts legal analysis; TechCrunch coverage of the ruling; three separate DPC fines: €100M (public-by-default), €65M (Family Pairing failure), €180M (transparency breaches)
**The Problem:** A parent in Germany discovers that their 14-year-old's TikTok account — created when TikTok was aware of the child's age — has been set to public by default, making all videos, profile details, and interactions visible to any internet user including non-registered members of the public. More urgently, the parent finds that an unknown adult has connected to the child's account via TikTok's "Family Pairing" feature, disabling content restrictions, without the parent's knowledge or any verification that the adult is a guardian. The parent tries TikTok's in-app safety reporting and receives only automated responses and help articles about adjusting settings. There is no phone number or live escalation path for child safety concerns.
**What staff/org would do:** TikTok's automated safety bot categorises the Family Pairing concern as a routine settings change and sends a help article on how to adjust privacy controls. The unknown adult's pairing is not treated as a potential safety incident. No human review is triggered, the child's account remains public, and the unknown paired adult retains access.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an active child safety concern requiring immediate action on two fronts — securing the child's account right now and reporting both to the platform via its dedicated child safety channel and to the relevant regulator and child protection authority.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "This is a child safety concern and it needs immediate action. The Irish Data Protection Commission fined TikTok €345 million in September 2023 specifically for these two failures — children's accounts set to public by default and Family Pairing allowing unverified adults to disable content restrictions. Let's secure your child's account first, then report this properly."
- Direct to: TikTok's dedicated privacy contact (privacy@tiktok.com) with a written formal complaint including your child's account details and the unknown pairing — create a paper trail; Irish DPC (dataprotection.ie) to file a GDPR complaint on your child's behalf; in Germany: Bundeszentrale fur Kinder- und Jugendmedienschutz (bzkj.de) and Jugendschutz.net for reporting child-endangering platform behaviour; klicksafe.de for guidance on securing the account
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of the child's account privacy settings showing public visibility; screenshots of the Family Pairing screen identifying the unknown paired account; the date the TikTok account was created and what age information was provided; all automated responses received from TikTok support with timestamps; any notification emails from TikTok about the pairing event
- Feedback category: Child Safety / Data Protection by Design Failure / Unauthorized Account Access / Regulatory Violation

---

### Scenario 30-4: Uber Driver's Medical Records and Criminal Data Sent to US Without Consent (Pattern A)
**Real Basis:** Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens (Dutch DPA) press release, July 22, 2024 — €290 million fine on Uber; European Data Protection Board; Linklaters legal analysis; original complaints filed by 170 French Uber drivers via Ligue des droits de l'Homme; CNIL (French DPA) involvement
**The Problem:** A French Uber driver reads media coverage of the Dutch DPA's €290 million ruling and realises that their personal data — submitted during Uber's regulatory compliance checks — was transferred to US servers for over two years without any valid legal safeguard. This includes not just their taxi licence and GPS history, but potentially their identity documents, medical records, and criminal background data. The driver contacts Uber partner support to request a full list of what data was transferred and to ask for its deletion from US systems. The Uber support bot routes them to a "Partner Support" FAQ about earnings disputes and vehicle requirements. There is no pathway in the standard driver support flow for data subject rights requests.
**What staff/org would do:** Uber's partner support queue acknowledges the ticket with a two-week response target. When a response eventually arrives, it directs the driver to Uber's general privacy policy and states that data handling is "compliant with applicable law." No specific information about what data was transferred or whether it has been deleted is provided.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the driver's concern with specific reference to the Dutch DPA's ruling, explains their strong GDPR rights as a data subject — access, erasure, and complaint — and connects them to the organisations that successfully prosecuted this case.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "What you are describing was confirmed and penalised by the Dutch Data Protection Authority in July 2024, which fined Uber €290 million after finding that sensitive driver data — including medical records and criminal conviction data — was transferred to the US without legal cover for over two years. You have enforceable GDPR rights and a clear path to use them."
- Direct to: French CNIL (cnil.fr) to lodge a formal data subject complaint — the 170 French drivers who triggered this case filed through CNIL and Ligue des droits de l'Homme; Dutch DPA (autoriteitpersoonsgegevens.nl) as the lead authority that issued the fine; submit a formal GDPR Subject Access Request directly to Uber's EU Data Protection Officer at dpo@uber.com — Uber is legally required to respond within 30 days; noyb.eu if you want to join collective enforcement action
- Evidence to collect: All documents you submitted to Uber during onboarding and compliance checks — photos, ID documents, medical certificates, criminal background consent forms; any Uber communications referencing data handling or sharing; records of dates you worked as an Uber driver partner; any prior data access requests submitted and responses received; the Dutch DPA press release (autoriteitpersoonsgegevens.nl) as your reference document
- Feedback category: Gig Worker Data Rights / Unauthorized Cross-Border Data Transfer / GDPR Violation / Sensitive Data Mishandling

### Scenario 30-5: LastPass Vault Stolen — Cryptocurrency Wallet Drained Overnight (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** LastPass breach disclosures August and December 2022; UK ICO enforcement notice 2025 — £1.6M fine; US class action settlement 2025 — $8.2M general pool + $16.25M cryptocurrency theft pool (lastpasssettlement.com); PCWorld and BleepingComputer coverage linking millions in stolen crypto to the breach
**The Problem:** A user wakes to find their cryptocurrency wallet has been completely drained overnight. They have seen media reports linking documented cryptocurrency theft to the LastPass data breach of December 2022, in which attackers exfiltrated entire customer vaults — including unencrypted metadata and encrypted password data — and subsequently brute-forced weak master passwords offline to access the contents. The user stored their crypto wallet seed phrase in LastPass. They contact LastPass support immediately. The automated chat offers a help article titled "What data was exposed?" and opens a ticket with a 72-hour response target. No one confirms whether their specific vault was decrypted or advises them on protecting other accounts still stored in the same vault.
**What staff/org would do:** LastPass's support bot marks the cryptocurrency theft as outside its support scope since it involves a third-party financial loss. The ticket is acknowledged but not escalated to a security specialist. The user is reassured that their master password was not stolen — without acknowledging that their encrypted vault was exfiltrated and that offline brute-forcing of weak master passwords is the confirmed attack vector for the subsequent thefts.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an active financial emergency. Prioritises immediate triage: change all passwords still in the vault starting with email and banking accounts before the attacker can pivot. Then documents the loss for the class action settlement and reports the theft to the appropriate financial crime authorities.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "This is a financial emergency — stop what you are doing and change your most critical passwords right now before anything else. Start with your primary email account, then banking and financial accounts, then every other service in that LastPass vault. Cryptocurrency theft linked to the LastPass breach is extensively documented. I will help you with reports and claims once your remaining accounts are secured."
- Direct to: FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (ic3.gov) for the cryptocurrency theft report; UK Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk) if UK-based; LastPass class action settlement — general claims pool $8.2M and dedicated cryptocurrency theft pool $16.25M at lastpasssettlement.com; UK ICO (ico.org.uk) for a formal breach complaint if UK-based; your bank's fraud line immediately if banking passwords were in the vault
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of wallet transaction history showing the unauthorized drain with exact timestamps and transaction IDs; confirmation of your LastPass account email address and subscription tier; any LastPass breach notification email received; a list of services whose passwords were stored in the vault — service names only, not the passwords themselves; all communications with LastPass support including ticket numbers; any cryptocurrency exchange records showing the wallet balance before the drain
- Feedback category: Platform Security Failure / Financial Loss / Data Breach Response Failure / Inadequate Consumer Notification

### Scenario 30-6: T-Mobile Breach — 90-Minute Hold, Agent Cannot Confirm Whether SSN Was Taken (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** T-Mobile SEC 8-K breach disclosure August 2021; FCC enforcement action and $31.5M settlement September 2024; $500M class action settlement 2022; Security.org T-Mobile breach history; Cybersecurity Dive — "FCC settlement: T-Mobile to pay $31.5M for data breaches"
**The Problem:** A T-Mobile customer received a breach notification letter in August 2021 stating their data "may have been exposed" in a breach affecting 76.6 million individuals. The letter includes a dedicated support line number. The consumer calls to confirm specifically whether their Social Security number, date of birth, and driver's licence number were among the records exfiltrated — information critical for deciding whether to freeze credit, alert the IRS, and notify their bank. After a 90-minute hold, the agent confirms only that the customer "was notified" and is unable to look up which specific data fields were taken for their individual account. The consumer is transferred twice and ultimately disconnected without resolution.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent reads from a breach notification script, offers enrollment in a free 24-month identity monitoring service, and states they have "no further information" on what specific data fields were taken for individual customers. After two transfers and 90 minutes, the call drops. The consumer has no more information than they had from the letter.
**What the Owner does:** Gives the consumer a clear, actionable response grounded in what T-Mobile publicly disclosed at the population level — SSNs, DOBs, and driver's licence numbers were among the confirmed exfiltrated fields for current customers — and provides the specific protective steps rather than waiting for the call center to provide individual-level confirmation it cannot give.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "T-Mobile publicly confirmed in their breach disclosures that Social Security numbers, dates of birth, and driver's licence numbers were among the data exfiltrated for current customers. The call center cannot give you individual confirmation — but T-Mobile's own disclosures tell you what was taken at the population level. Act on the assumption your SSN was included. Here is exactly what to do."
- Direct to: Freeze your credit at all three bureaus immediately — Equifax (equifax.com), Experian (experian.com), TransUnion (transunion.com) — this is free by law under the Economic Growth Act; IRS Identity Protection PIN (irs.gov/identity-theft-central) to prevent fraudulent tax returns; FTC Identity Theft Report (identitytheft.gov) which creates a legal record; T-Mobile class action settlement — check current eligibility; FCC consumer complaint portal (fcc.gov/consumers/guides/filing-informal-complaint) to report the inadequate call center response
- Evidence to collect: The original T-Mobile breach notification letter with the date it was received; a written record of the call including date, total hold time, transfer count, and what each agent said; any text or email notification from T-Mobile; bank and credit card statements for suspicious activity in the period following the breach; a note of what specific data you originally provided to T-Mobile when activating your account
- Feedback category: Data Breach / Call Center Failure / Identity Theft Risk / Inadequate Breach Response

### Scenario 30-7: Google Permanently Bans 12-Year Account — Automated Appeal Rejected, No Human Review Available (Pattern D)
**Real Basis:** New York Times — Kashmir Hill investigation, August 2022 ("A Dad Took Photos of His Naked Toddler for the Doctor. Google Flagged Him as a Criminal"); Android Authority coverage; documented cases 2021–2026 showing identical pattern of permanent bans with no human escalation path
**The Problem:** A user in Canada who has held a Gmail account for twelve years wakes to find their entire Google account has been permanently disabled. Inaccessible: Gmail — including a decade of business and personal email — Google Drive documents, Google Photos, YouTube history, and all Play Store app purchases. The only notification is an automated message stating the account violated "Google's policies" with no specific violation identified. The user submits the in-app appeal form. Four days later they receive a one-line automated rejection: "We've reviewed your appeal and our decision remains the same." Google's own Help Center explicitly states that consumer accounts are not eligible for phone or chat support regarding account suspension. The Gmail address is also the recovery email for most of the user's other online accounts.
**What staff/org would do:** Google's automated system sends a second generic rejection of the appeal. The Help Center loops back to the same appeal form that already failed. There is no phone number, no email address, no chat option, and no documented route to speak with a human agent about account reinstatement for a consumer account. Google's published support structure treats this as a final automated decision.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the severity — this is a digital emergency involving loss of email, irreplaceable documents, photos, and purchased content — and provides every documented avenue that has actually resulted in resolution, while being honest that the standard process has failed and escalation routes are limited but real.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "Losing twelve years of email, documents, and photos with no human to speak to is a serious loss and Google's automated-only process leaves almost no standard recourse. You are not alone — this pattern prompted a New York Times investigation in 2022. Here is every route that has produced results, in order of how much leverage each carries."
- Direct to: Exhaust all account recovery options first at accounts.google.com/signin/recovery — try every verification method available including phone, backup email, and security questions; if purchased content (Play Store apps) was lost, file a complaint with the Canadian Competition Bureau (competitionbureau.gc.ca) and CRTC (crtc.gc.ca) about loss of paid digital goods; consult a consumer rights lawyer about potential claims under Canadian consumer protection law for destruction of purchased digital content; the Electronic Frontier Foundation (eff.org) and Open Media (openmedia.org) for digital rights advocacy; media escalation — the Kashmir Hill NYT investigation documented that media coverage was the primary route to human review in Google account cases
- Evidence to collect: Every automated notification and rejection email from Google with full headers; a comprehensive list of all services and purchased content tied to the Google account — apps, subscriptions, storage, domains; any external backups of Gmail, Drive, or Photos content; proof of account age such as old emails on other platforms referencing the Gmail address; records of all recovery attempts with dates and outcomes
- Feedback category: Account Ban / Refusal of Service / No Escalation Path / Digital Asset Loss / Automated Decision Without Human Override

### Scenario 30-8: JustAnswer Charges AU$70/Month After AU$2 Preview Fee — Refund Refused After Cancellation (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** ACCC Federal Court proceedings VID1259/2025, filed September 23, 2025 against JustAnswer LLC; ppc.land reporting on the case; University of Melbourne Pursuit legal analysis; ACCC media release
**The Problem:** A consumer in Melbourne searched online for legal advice about a workplace dispute and clicked on a chat widget that appeared — through its language and branding — to be affiliated with the Australian Fair Work Ombudsman. The widget advertised a AU$2 joining fee to "chat with a Fair Work expert." The consumer paid AU$2, received a short response, and believed the transaction was complete. The following month they find a AU$70 charge from JustAnswer on their credit card. The month after: another AU$70. They contact JustAnswer support to cancel and request a refund of the two unauthorised monthly charges. Support confirms the cancellation going forward but denies both refunds, stating the subscription terms were visible on the payment screen and the refund window has expired.
**What staff/org would do:** The JustAnswer support agent confirms the subscription is now cancelled but refuses any refund for past charges. When the consumer raises that the widget appeared to be a government service, the agent denies any government affiliation was implied and offers a AU$10 goodwill credit. The agent does not disclose that the ACCC has filed Federal Court proceedings against JustAnswer over this exact conduct.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that the ACCC has taken JustAnswer to Federal Court specifically over the AU$2 fee concealing a AU$50–90 monthly subscription and the false government affiliation claims, and helps the consumer access the strongest remedies available under Australian Consumer Law.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "What you experienced is the subject of active Federal Court proceedings in Australia. The ACCC filed suit against JustAnswer in September 2025 — case VID1259/2025 — specifically because the AU$2 fee concealed a recurring monthly subscription and the widget falsely implied affiliation with the Fair Work Ombudsman. Your grounds for a full refund are strong."
- Direct to: ACCC consumer complaint portal (accc.gov.au/consumers/consumer-rights-guarantees/make-a-consumer-complaint) — the ACCC is actively litigating this case and your complaint directly supports it; Consumer Affairs Victoria (consumer.vic.gov.au) for state-level complaint support and mediation; your credit card company to initiate a chargeback for misrepresented charges — under Australian Consumer Law the subscription was not what was advertised, which is grounds for a card dispute; Fair Trading NSW or your state equivalent if you have not already used the Victorian service
- Evidence to collect: Screenshots of the original chat widget showing the AU$2 fee prominently and any language implying Fair Work Ombudsman affiliation; bank or credit card statements showing every JustAnswer charge with exact dates and amounts; all JustAnswer email confirmations including the initial receipt; the full email thread with support showing the refund denial and the AU$10 offer; a screenshot showing how you found the widget — search engine results page if available
- Feedback category: Subscription Trap / Misleading Pricing / False Government Affiliation / Unauthorized Charges / Refund Refusal

### Scenario 30-9: Twilio IT Staff Phished — Consumer's Authy Two-Factor App Hijacked at 3AM (Pattern C)
**Real Basis:** Twilio breach disclosure August 2022; TechCrunch — "Twilio breach also compromised Authy two-factor accounts" August 26, 2022; Dark Reading — "Twilio Hackers Stole Okta Credentials in Sprawling Supply Chain Attack" (0ktapus investigation); BankInfoSecurity — "Twilio and Cisco Breaches Part of Massive Phishing Campaign"; 93 Authy accounts confirmed with attacker-registered devices
**The Problem:** A user receives an automated notification at 3 AM that a new device has been added to their Authy two-factor authentication account — an action they did not authorise. They rely on Authy to secure their email, online banking, and cryptocurrency accounts. After researching, they identify this as consistent with the 2022 Twilio breach, in which attackers telephoned Twilio's own IT support staff, impersonated the internal IT department, and used social-engineered credentials to access Twilio's backend systems — then registered additional devices on 93 Authy accounts. The user contacts Twilio/Authy support and is told to follow the standard account recovery flow, which requires two-factor verification — potentially now routing through the attacker's registered device.
**What staff/org would do:** Twilio's support bot provides a generic account recovery flow. The standard identity verification process requires the user to authenticate via the same Authy app that may now be compromised. The support agent, following standard protocol, cannot override the two-factor requirement without an extended identity verification process. The agent does not flag this as potentially connected to the documented 2022 breach or escalate it as a security incident.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this as a documented supply-chain attack where Twilio's own customer-facing staff were the original entry point — making this a platform staff misconduct by vulnerability rather than intent — and guides the user through emergency steps to break the attacker's device registration before it is used to access dependent accounts.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening: "An unauthorised device registered on your Authy account at 3 AM is an active security emergency — every account you protect with Authy is potentially at risk right now. This matches the documented 2022 Twilio breach where attackers compromised Twilio's own IT staff by phone to access Authy backends. Do not use Authy to log into anything. Secure your accounts now."
- Direct to: Twilio/Authy security incident escalation — email security@twilio.com immediately with subject line "Unauthorized device registration — active incident"; call Twilio's sales/enterprise line to escalate as a security emergency rather than using the standard support bot; FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (ic3.gov) to file a cyber incident report; your bank's 24-hour fraud line if banking accounts use Authy 2FA — request account flags immediately; check haveibeenpwned.com to confirm exposure scope
- Evidence to collect: Screenshot of the Authy new-device notification with the exact timestamp and device details shown; a full list of every account that uses Authy for two-factor authentication — service names only; any email from Authy confirming device registration events; Twilio or Authy support ticket number from initial contact; records of any suspicious login attempts on Authy-protected accounts in the same window; your original Authy account registration email
- Feedback category: Platform Security Failure / Staff Social Engineering / Unauthorized Account Access / Supply Chain Incident / Active Security Emergency

### Scenario 30-10: Microsoft 365 Price Increase — 2.7 Million Australians Charged More Without Clear Prior Notice (Pattern B)
**Real Basis:** ACCC media release 2024 — proceedings against Microsoft over subscription price increase disclosure; Wotton Kearney legal analysis — "Technology Disputes in Focus: ACCC Challenges Microsoft on AI Pricing"; SMBTech — "ACCC reports 20% jump in consumer guarantee complaints as electronics and tech sector faces scrutiny"; ACCC annual report data
**The Problem:** A small business owner in Sydney who uses Microsoft 365 Business Basic notices their monthly charge has risen from AU$9 to AU$12.50 — a 39% increase — without any prior notice they recall seeing. They call Microsoft support to dispute the charge and request a refund for the months at the higher rate. The agent confirms the price change is effective and non-negotiable, states that advance notice was sent by email, and provides a link to the Microsoft 365 pricing page. The business owner asks the agent to confirm when and to which email address the notice was sent. The agent says they cannot retrieve that information. The call ends with no refund and no evidence the notice was ever received.
**What staff/org would do:** The Microsoft support agent confirms the new pricing, provides a generic link to the current pricing page, and closes the interaction. No refund is offered. The agent does not disclose that the ACCC has commenced Federal Court proceedings against Microsoft specifically over whether these price change communications were adequate for 2.7 million affected Australian consumers.
**What the Owner does:** Informs the consumer that the ACCC is actively pursuing Microsoft in court over the adequacy of these exact disclosures, which means the consumer's complaint has genuine legal weight, and identifies the specific remedies available through both the ACCC and Australian Consumer Law.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening: "You are one of approximately 2.7 million Australians affected by this. The ACCC has commenced Federal Court proceedings against Microsoft specifically over whether this price increase was adequately communicated — which means your complaint directly supports live enforcement action and you have real grounds to dispute the charges."
- Direct to: ACCC consumer complaint portal (accc.gov.au) — the ACCC is actively pursuing Microsoft and consumer reports strengthen the case; your bank or credit card provider to dispute the price-differential charges under the Australian Consumer Law guarantee that services must be supplied on the agreed terms; Australian Small Business and Family Enterprise Ombudsman (asbfeo.gov.au) if the increase materially affects your business operations; Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) if the dispute involves a financial service tied to the subscription
- Evidence to collect: Credit card or bank statements showing the old and new charge amounts with exact dates — highlighting the date the increase first appeared; a thorough search of your inbox for any Microsoft billing notification or price change email including spam and promotions folders — capture screenshots showing search results and absence of notice if none is found; your original Microsoft 365 subscription confirmation email showing the agreed price; a record of the support call with date, time, agent identification if provided, and what was communicated; a screenshot of the current Microsoft 365 pricing page
- Feedback category: Subscription Price Increase / Inadequate Consumer Disclosure / Misleading Conduct / Wait and No Resolution

Industry ID: 30

## 31. Manufacturing

### Scenario 31-1: Truck Owner Trapped in Defeat-Device Recall Transfer Loop (Pattern D, E)
**Real Basis:** EPA and DOJ enforcement announcement, January 11, 2024; Manufacturing Dive and Detroit News coverage of Cummins Inc. $1.675 billion Clean Air Act settlement — largest in US history — for defeat-device software installed in 630,000+ RAM 2500/3500 diesel trucks (model years 2013–2019). Dealer recall process documented in Cummins and Stellantis recall notices.
**The Problem:** A Ram 2500 owner in Ohio received a federal recall notice about illegal Cummins defeat-device emissions software. She calls her dealership to schedule the free mandated software update. The service advisor says the update "isn't available yet" with no timeline offered. She calls Cummins customer support, which tells her to contact the dealer. She calls Stellantis/RAM's national line, which says the repair is Cummins's responsibility. Four calls over two days, zero case numbers opened, and still no repair appointment — while her truck continues producing NOx emissions far exceeding legal limits in the community around her.
**What staff/org would do:** Each call center representative correctly identifies the issue as another party's responsibility and provides a phone number to call next — but no one opens a case file, issues a confirmation number, or commits to a callback. The consumer cycles between Cummins, Stellantis/RAM, and the dealership with no one owning the resolution.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the frustration of being passed in a loop over a federally mandated recall. Opens a case record capturing every prior contact. Provides the EPA recall reference number (January 2024 enforcement case) which the consumer can cite as legal authority for the mandatory repair. Advises her to file a complaint with NHTSA's Vehicle Safety Hotline as an independent escalation route and explains that NHTSA can compel the manufacturer to act within a set timeframe.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You have been passed between three organisations over two days without a resolution on a federally mandated recall — that is not acceptable. Let me help you take control of this and get your repair on record."
- Direct the consumer to: NHTSA Vehicle Safety Hotline (1-888-327-4236); EPA online complaint portal (epa.gov/enforcement); verify recall status by VIN at nhtsa.gov/recalls; EPA enforcement case reference: United States v. Cummins Inc., January 2024
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Name and employee ID of every agent spoken to; dates, times, and substance of each call; request written email confirmation from the dealer at the time any appointment is made; keep the original recall notice received
- Feedback category tag: `recall_process_failure` / `manufacturer_transfer_loop` / `information_failure`

---

### Scenario 31-2: Infant Left in Recalled Fisher-Price Sleeper — Only Voucher Offered, Refund Refused (Pattern A, B, E)
**Real Basis:** CPSC formal recall re-announcement, January 2023, Fisher-Price Rock 'n Play Sleeper (4.7 million units; at least 100 confirmed infant deaths including 8 post-recall); Consumer Reports formal advocacy documenting voucher-only remedy as inadequate; U.S. House Oversight Committee investigation report, 2021.
**The Problem:** A mother of a three-month-old discovers via a news alert that the Fisher-Price Rock 'n Play Sleeper she bought in 2018 — and has been using every night for two months — is under a federal recall linked to over 100 infant deaths. The recall has been reissued because the original notice failed to reach enough parents. She immediately stops using the product. She calls Fisher-Price's recall line and is told she qualifies only for a "product voucher" for another Fisher-Price product, not a cash refund. She asks whether any replacement products have also been recalled. The agent cannot answer. She cannot afford to buy a safe alternative tonight. Her baby has nowhere safe to sleep.
**What staff/org would do:** The call center agent reads from the recall script: "You are eligible for a Fisher-Price product voucher — would you like me to process that now?" The agent does not ask about the baby's current sleeping arrangement, does not offer safe sleep guidance, and has no authority to issue a refund. The parent is left with a dismantled product, a voucher she cannot use tonight, and no safe sleep solution.
**What the Owner does:** Recognises this immediately as an infant safety emergency. Confirms recall coverage using the product's model number against the CPSC database. Tells the parent clearly that the product must not be used for infant sleep again — ever. Provides the AAP and CPSC safe sleep guidance (flat, firm, separate surface on the parent's back). Explains that the voucher-only remedy has been formally criticised as inadequate and that the parent can pursue a full cash refund by filing a complaint directly with CPSC, referencing Consumer Reports' advocacy position.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Your baby's safety comes first. Do not put your baby in that sleeper tonight or any night. I am going to help you find a safe sleep solution and also get your money back — this product should have been removed from the market years ago."
- Direct the consumer to: CPSC Consumer Hotline (1-800-638-2772); CPSC complaint portal (cpsc.gov/Recalls) to formally dispute the voucher-only remedy; American Academy of Pediatrics safe sleep guidance (healthychildren.org/SafeSleep); local health department, WIC office, or Safe Kids Worldwide for emergency safe sleep resources if cost is a barrier
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Model number and lot code from the product label; purchase receipt or bank transaction record; screenshot of the CPSC recall page confirming coverage; written record of the Fisher-Price call including the voucher offer made and agent's failure to answer safe sleep questions
- Feedback category tag: `urgent_product_safety` / `recall_remedy_refusal` / `infant_vulnerability`

---

### Scenario 31-3: LA Garment Worker Receives Fake Paychecks — Overtime Systematically Stolen (Pattern E, D, A, C)
**Real Basis:** U.S. Department of Labor Wage and Hour Division enforcement announcement, January 2024; Supply Chain Dive and ESG Dive coverage of Beyond Yoga / Good Cash LLC / Premium Quality Apparel LLC $1.1 million settlement — largest California garment worker wage theft settlement in DOL history. Contractors issued falsified payroll records and fake checks to conceal non-payment of overtime for 165 workers averaging 52-hour weeks.
**The Problem:** A garment worker at a Los Angeles sewing contractor supplying a premium athleisure brand has been working 52-hour weeks for six months. Her paychecks look correct on paper. A coworker has just told her that the checks were falsified — overtime hours were never calculated at the required 1.5x rate, and some checks were printed in names that are not the workers'. She cannot read English easily, has no idea how to verify whether her total pay is lawful, and is afraid her employer will fire her or report her if she complains internally. She wants to know if she has been defrauded and what she can do.
**What staff/org would do:** The contractor's internal HR does not exist for floor workers. If she raises it with her floor manager, the manager dismisses it and says her pay is correct. The payroll system has been deliberately falsified to produce records that appear compliant on inspection.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms this is a documented pattern of wage fraud, not a calculation error. Tells her she has legal protection against retaliation under the FLSA and California Labor Code Section 98.6 — she cannot be lawfully fired for reporting wage theft. Explains that DOL Wage and Hour Division complaints are confidential and that back wages plus equal liquidated damages are recoverable. Notes that DOL has Spanish-speaking investigators and that immigration status does not affect the right to claim unpaid wages.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Falsified payroll records and unpaid overtime are wage theft — and it is illegal. You have the right to report this confidentially. You cannot be legally fired for doing so, and your immigration status does not affect your right to be paid what you are owed. Here is what to do."
- Direct the consumer to: DOL Wage and Hour Division (1-866-487-9243; dol.gov/agencies/whd/contact/local-offices — Los Angeles district office); California Labor Commissioner's Office (dir.ca.gov/dlse); Garment Worker Center Los Angeles (garmentworkercenter.org) for bilingual support and advocacy
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All physical paychecks received — do not cash any check that appears falsified before getting advice; photographs of time records, badge swipes, or any documented record of hours worked; any employment contract or offer letter; names of coworkers who witnessed similar treatment; dates and amounts of every pay period
- Feedback category tag: `wage_theft` / `falsified_payroll` / `worker_financial_fraud`

---

### Scenario 31-4: Rana Plaza — Worker Ordered Back Into Building After Structural Crack Discovered (Pattern C, A, E, D)
**Real Basis:** ILO documentation of the Rana Plaza disaster, Savar, Bangladesh, April 24, 2013 (1,134 killed, 2,500 injured); Clean Clothes Campaign; Ethics Unwrapped (University of Texas). The day before the collapse, visible structural cracks were discovered. Bank and ground-floor shops immediately closed. Garment factory managers on upper floors ordered 5,000 workers to return the following morning, threatening dismissal for those who refused.
**The Problem:** It is the morning of April 24, 2013. A 24-year-old garment worker arrives at Rana Plaza in Savar and can see the crack in the building's load-bearing column that was photographed and widely discussed the previous day. She and two coworkers stop at the entrance. The floor supervisor is already there telling workers that if they do not clock in, they will lose this month's wages and their positions. She can see the crack clearly. She is the sole earner for her mother and younger siblings. She does not know whether there is anyone she can report the structural danger to, or whether reporting would protect her from dismissal.
**What staff/org would do:** The floor supervisor applies direct coercive pressure: "Engineers checked — it is fine. Go upstairs now or lose your job." No independent safety channel exists. The factory has no posted emergency contact for Bangladesh's Department of Inspection for Factories and Establishments. There is no union at this facility. Workers who refused were threatened with termination without severance.
**What the Owner does:** Treats this as an immediate life-safety emergency requiring the clearest possible instruction. Tells the worker directly: do not enter the building — a crack in a load-bearing column is a recognised warning sign of structural failure risk. Explains that threatening workers with dismissal to force them into an unsafe structure is a violation of Bangladesh Labour Act provisions on safe workplace conditions and constitutes unlawful coercion. Provides DIFE's emergency contact number. Advises her that if she is dismissed for refusing to enter an unsafe building, this is wrongful termination with legal recourse.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "Do not enter that building. A crack in a structural column is a serious warning sign of potential collapse. Your life is worth more than any shift, and the law is on your side. Here is exactly who to call and what your rights are right now."
- Direct the consumer to: Bangladesh Department of Inspection for Factories and Establishments (DIFE) emergency contact; Bangladesh Centre for Worker Solidarity; Bangladesh Garment Workers Solidarity; nearest police station to report the coercive threat; ILO Bangladesh country office
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Photograph the crack immediately with a visible timestamp; record the full name of the supervisor making the threat; note the names of any coworkers who heard the threat; preserve any written dismissal notice if one is subsequently issued
- Feedback category tag: `urgent_structural_safety` / `worker_coercion` / `workplace_rights_violation` / `staff_misconduct`

### Scenario 31-5: Takata Airbag Recall — Dealer Has No Parts, Driver Told to Limit Driving in Florida (Pattern C, D, B)
**Real Basis:** NHTSA Takata Recall Spotlight (official NHTSA page); Consumer Reports comprehensive Takata recall guide; documented consumer complaints of part unavailability and transfer loops between manufacturer call centers and dealerships. NHTSA continued issuing urgent warnings into 2023; recall completion rates in high-risk humid regions remained below 75% years after the initial recall. NHTSA's consent orders required manufacturers to provide loaner vehicles for Priority Group 1 vehicles.
**The Problem:** A driver in Tampa, Florida receives an "urgent" NHTSA recall notice for the driver-side airbag in her 2014 Honda Civic. The notice explains that the Takata inflator may rupture violently, sending metal fragments at the driver — and that Florida's heat and humidity accelerates the risk. She calls her Honda dealer. The service advisor says no replacement parts are in stock, cannot give a timeline, and suggests she "try to avoid driving in hot or humid conditions." She lives in Tampa. She drives to work every day. She calls Honda's national customer service line and is transferred three times before reaching a voicemail. She has a car NHTSA classifies as Priority Group 1 — highest lethal risk — and no repair date.
**What staff/org would do:** Each call results in being transferred or told to "check back in a few weeks." No case number is opened. The national Honda line has no visibility into individual dealer parts inventory. The consumer is left with a vehicle NHTSA has classified as a lethal risk and zero actionable next steps.
**What the Owner does:** Validates the urgency — NHTSA classified certain Takata vehicles as Priority Group 1, and Florida residents in hot, humid climates are explicitly in the highest-risk category. Explains that Honda's consent order with NHTSA requires the manufacturer to provide a loaner vehicle or rental car reimbursement for Priority Group 1 owners when parts are unavailable — the dealer is legally obligated to offer this and failed to do so. Provides the specific NHTSA Takata hotline number and the language the consumer needs to invoke her reimbursement entitlement immediately.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=true
- Opening reply: "NHTSA has classified your vehicle as Priority Group 1 — that means your airbag poses a real risk of death or serious injury right now. You are entitled to a loaner car while your parts are on order. The dealer failed to tell you this. Here is exactly what to demand and who to call."
- Direct the consumer to: NHTSA Vehicle Safety Hotline (1-888-327-4236); NHTSA Takata Recall Spotlight page (nhtsa.gov/vehicle-safety/takata-recall-spotlight); Honda customer relations escalation department (separate from general 1-800 line); Honda loaner/rental reimbursement programme (documented in NHTSA consent order — cite this by name to the dealer)
- Evidence to collect/preserve: The recall notice received — note the Priority Group designation; dates, times, and names from all calls to the dealer and Honda national line; written confirmation from the dealer of parts unavailability (request this by email immediately); any expense receipts if a loaner or rental is provided
- Feedback category tag: `urgent_recall` / `parts_unavailability` / `call_center_transfer_loop` / `manufacturer_remedy_failure`

### Scenario 31-6: Boohoo Leicester Supplier — Garment Worker Paid £3.50 Per Hour During COVID Lockdown (Pattern E, C, A, D)
**Real Basis:** Sunday Times undercover investigation, July 5, 2020; Clean Clothes Campaign documentation; Corporate Justice Coalition case study. Workers in Leicester factories supplying Boohoo were paid as little as £3.50 per hour — less than half the UK National Living Wage of £8.72 per hour. During COVID-19 lockdown, factories continued operating at full capacity under sustained Boohoo orders with no PPE or social distancing. Payroll records were falsified to conceal the violations.
**The Problem:** A 38-year-old garment worker in Leicester has sewn garments for a factory supplying Boohoo for three years. During the April–July 2020 lockdown she worked 60-hour weeks with no masks, no distance between workstations, and no sick pay. She has now calculated that after unexplained deductions for "machine maintenance" and "cleaning supplies" taken from her payslips, her effective hourly rate is £3.50. She assumed she was being paid minimum wage. She wants to report the underpayment and the lockdown safety violations but is worried about what happens to her if she makes a formal complaint and whether her status as a worker through a labour agency affects her rights.
**What staff/org would do:** Boohoo's consumer-facing customer service has no intake route for supplier worker grievances. The factory management would deny any underpayment if directly confronted. There is no posted notice in the factory of workers' minimum wage rights and no functioning internal grievance channel.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms the National Living Wage in the UK was £8.72/hour for workers aged 25+ during 2020, making her described pay rate clearly illegal. Explains that HMRC's minimum wage enforcement is confidential and that agency workers have the same minimum wage rights as direct employees. Provides the HMRC anonymous reporting line for minimum wage violations and the ACAS helpline. Explains the Health and Safety Executive route for reporting the COVID safety failures separately.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "You are owed the difference between what you were paid and the legal National Living Wage for every hour you worked during that period. Reporting this is completely confidential. Your status as an agency worker does not take away your minimum wage rights. Here is how to act."
- Direct the consumer to: HMRC National Minimum Wage anonymous reporting line (0800 328 9100; gov.uk/report-minimum-wage-violation); ACAS helpline (0300 123 1100) for employment rights advice; Health and Safety Executive (hse.gov.uk) for the COVID workplace safety violation report; Leicester Citizens Advice Bureau; Ethical Trading Initiative for supply chain escalation to Boohoo's brand level
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All payslips — photograph every one; any written or verbal explanation of the maintenance and cleaning deductions; a written log of actual hours worked each week during the lockdown period; photograph of the workstation layout showing no social distancing; names of any colleagues who can corroborate the conditions and pay levels
- Feedback category tag: `wage_theft` / `below_minimum_wage` / `workplace_safety_covid` / `worker_rights_violation`

### Scenario 31-7: Foxconn Zhengzhou — Worker Discovers Contract Differs From What Recruiter Promised (Pattern E, C, A, B)
**Real Basis:** CNN Business, VOA News, South China Morning Post reporting on November 2022 Foxconn Zhengzhou worker protests; China Labor Watch investigation documenting systematic violations at the facility including 60–75 hour weeks, wage withholding, coerced internship arrangements, and retaliation against workers who raised complaints. Foxconn publicly apologised in November 2022 after protests over advertised 25,000 yuan bonus terms that differed from signed contracts.
**The Problem:** A 22-year-old worker from rural Henan province arrived at Foxconn's Zhengzhou campus after seeing an online recruitment posting promising a 25,000 yuan (~$3,500) bonus for two months of work on the iPhone 14 production line. After six weeks of 70-hour shifts, his team leader tells him the bonus requires three additional months at a lower base wage before it is paid — and that this condition is in the contract he signed. He does not have a copy of his contract. He does not recall reading any such condition. He wants to know if he has been deceived and what he can do without being fired or blacklisted from future factory work across the industry.
**What staff/org would do:** The factory's internal HR says the contract "clearly states" the conditions and that he signed it. The recruiter who made the original promise is a third-party labour agency, and Foxconn denies responsibility for what the agency stated. Workers who raised concerns through the factory's internal WeChat channel were monitored and warned to stop.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms this is a documented and publicly acknowledged pattern — Foxconn apologised for exactly this recruitment mismatch in November 2022 following large-scale worker protests at the same facility. Explains that under Chinese Labour Contract Law Article 8, an employer must truthfully disclose all working conditions and compensation terms before a contract is signed — misrepresentation at the recruitment stage is a legal violation. Explains that under Article 16, he is entitled to a copy of his signed contract and should request it immediately in writing.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "What you are describing — being recruited under one pay arrangement and then presented with a different contract — is a documented violation of Chinese Labour Contract Law, and Foxconn has already admitted publicly that this happened at your facility. You are entitled to a copy of your contract and to challenge the discrepancy. Here is how."
- Direct the consumer to: China national labour complaints hotline: 12333; local Human Resources and Social Security Bureau (劳动局) in Zhengzhou; China Labour Bulletin (clb.org.hk) for documentation support and legal referrals; if the contract copy is refused, this refusal is itself a violation of Article 16 and can be reported to 12333
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Screenshots of the original recruitment advertisement (WeChat post or online job listing) showing the 25,000 yuan bonus and two-month timeline; the recruiter's or agency's name and contact; any onboarding paperwork received; a written request for a copy of the signed contract submitted to HR (keep the message); a personal log of daily hours worked
- Feedback category tag: `deceptive_recruitment` / `contract_mismatch` / `transparency_failure` / `worker_rights`

### Scenario 31-8: DRC Child Cobalt Miner Injured in Tunnel Collapse — No Complaint Channel Exists (Pattern C, E, A, D)
**Real Basis:** Amnesty International "This Is What We Die For," January 2016; Amnesty International campaign page on child labour in cobalt supply chains; Business and Human Rights Resource Centre; 2021 US federal lawsuits by families of children killed in DRC cobalt mines against Apple, Tesla, Microsoft, and others. Approximately 40,000 children worked in artisanal cobalt mines in southern DRC for $1–$2 per day; Amnesty found none of the 16 implicated consumer brands had a mechanism through which DRC miners could raise safety or pay concerns.
**The Problem:** The father of a 12-year-old boy who has been working at an artisanal cobalt mine near Kolwezi, DRC wants to report that his son was injured last week when a tunnel section partially collapsed, breaking the boy's arm. The boy was not paid for the days he could not work and received no medical assistance from the mine operator. The father wants to know: who is legally responsible for his son's injury? Where can he report the unsafe conditions? Is there any way to reach the global brands — Apple, Samsung, others — whose products use cobalt from mines like this one, and do those brands have any obligation to help?
**What staff/org would do:** There is no formal complaint mechanism at the artisanal mining site. The site is managed by a local négociant (ore trader) with no posted safety rules and no first aid provision. The trader sold ore onward through a chain leading to global battery manufacturers. When Amnesty International contacted 16 consumer electronics brands in 2016, none had a mechanism through which DRC miners could raise safety or compensation concerns.
**What the Owner does:** Acknowledges the severity and injustice of the situation with full empathy — a child was injured doing hazardous work that powers global consumer products, and the family has been left with no channel. Explains the documented supply chain so the father understands where accountability flows. Explains that DRC's SAEMAPE (the government body with a mandate over artisanal mining) is the primary state mechanism to report unsafe mining conditions. Provides contacts for Pact DRC, which operates community grievance programs in Kolwezi, and Amnesty International, which actively documents and pressures brands on exactly this issue.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Your son was injured doing work that feeds into products used around the world. You deserve to be heard and you deserve accountability. No easy path exists here — but there are specific organisations who will take your report seriously and act on it. Here is what I know."
- Direct the consumer to: SAEMAPE — Service d'Assistance et d'Encadrement du Small-Scale Mining (DRC government artisanal mining authority); Pact DRC (pactworld.org) — operates community grievance and mine safety programs in Kolwezi; Amnesty International DRC country desk; Initiative for Responsible Mining Assurance (responsiblemining.net); local health centre or hospital for formal injury documentation
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Medical documentation of the injury — hospital or clinic record with date and diagnosis; the name of the mine site operator or négociant; the date and location of the tunnel collapse; names of any witnesses; any wage record showing what was withheld during the recovery period
- Feedback category tag: `child_labour` / `mining_safety` / `supply_chain_accountability` / `no_grievance_channel` / `vulnerability`

### Scenario 31-9: Nike Cambodia Supplier — Worker Coerced Into Signing "Voluntary Leave" Form to Strip Wages (Pattern E, C, B, A)
**Real Basis:** Worker Rights Consortium reports, 2020; Business and Human Rights Resource Centre documentation; Partners for Dignity & Rights, December 2025 — five-year campaign that forced Nike to pay compensation for wage theft and retaliation. Workers at Hong Seng Knitting (Thailand, 3,300 workers) were deceived into signing forms falsely stating they wished to take voluntary unpaid leave; workers at Violet Apparel (Cambodia, 1,280 workers) were left without legally mandated severance after abrupt factory closure. Workers who objected faced intimidation via the factory's Facebook page.
**The Problem:** A 29-year-old seamstress at a Cambodian garment factory supplying Nike is told by her supervisor in April 2020 to sign a document titled "Employee Voluntary Leave Agreement." She is told everyone must sign because of COVID and that she will receive 40% of her normal wage during a temporary production reduction. She did not volunteer for leave — she wants to work and needs her full wage to pay rent. She asks what the form says; it is in English. When three of her coworkers asked questions yesterday, their names and photos were posted on the factory's Facebook page with a caption calling them troublemakers. She wants to know whether she is legally required to sign this form and what she loses if she does not.
**What staff/org would do:** The factory HR representative tells her this is standard COVID procedure approved by the government and that if she refuses, "production may halt and everyone will go home with no pay at all." No copy of the form is offered. No union representative is present. The only complaint channel is the factory's own HR desk.
**What the Owner does:** Tells her directly and clearly: do not sign the form without independent advice first. Explains that under Cambodian Labour Law, a reduction in wages or working hours requires the worker's written, informed, and genuine consent — a form in a language she cannot read, presented under threat of collective dismissal, does not constitute valid legal consent. Explains that the public Facebook shaming of workers who asked questions is a documented form of retaliation and is a separate violation. Provides contacts for the Cambodian Ministry of Labour and the Worker Rights Consortium, which was actively investigating exactly this pattern at Nike supplier factories in 2020.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "Do not sign that form yet. A document you cannot read, handed to you under threat of mass dismissal, does not count as voluntary consent under Cambodian labour law. You have rights here. Let me explain them before you do anything."
- Direct the consumer to: Cambodia Ministry of Labour and Vocational Training (molvt.gov.kh); Worker Rights Consortium (workersrights.org) — actively investigated Nike supplier factory practices in Cambodia and Thailand during 2020; CENTRAL (Cambodian Labour Rights NGO); Coalition of Cambodian Apparel Workers' Democratic Union (CCAWDU); C&A Foundation worker support network
- Evidence to collect/preserve: Do not sign the form — if pressured, ask for a copy to "review with a family member" and photograph it immediately; screenshot and preserve the Facebook posts naming coworkers as troublemakers (with timestamp); record the names and roles of supervisors who applied pressure; note names of coworkers who were publicly identified
- Feedback category tag: `coerced_consent` / `wage_reduction` / `worker_intimidation` / `supply_chain_rights`

### Scenario 31-10: Adidas India Supplier — Factory Claims False Government Order to Cut Wages, Call Refused (Pattern C, A, E, D)
**Real Basis:** Asia Floor Wage Alliance report on COVID-19 wage losses at Adidas supplier factories across Cambodia, India, Indonesia, and Pakistan; Clean Clothes Campaign study; Sourcing Journal and just-style coverage of Adidas "grappling with legacy of alleged wage theft." Eight identified Adidas supplier factories recorded $11.7 million in direct wage losses for 30,190 workers during the pandemic period; extrapolated losses across 15 factories estimated at $320 million.
**The Problem:** A 33-year-old worker at a Bengaluru garment factory supplying Adidas was placed on a 50% wage arrangement in April 2020. The factory told workers this was a government-mandated COVID measure. She called the Karnataka Labour Department to verify this and was told no such order applied to her factory's size and category. Her wage reduction was never communicated in writing — she only noticed it on her payslip. When she called the factory's HR line to ask about it, the representative said "we cannot help you with this" and ended the call. She wants to recover her missing wages and understand whether Adidas, as the brand, has any direct obligation to workers in its supply chain.
**What staff/org would do:** The factory HR line refuses to engage and ends the call. Adidas's consumer service email addresses product returns and brand inquiries — it has no intake route for supplier worker grievances. Adidas's Supplier Code of Conduct is published online but provides no direct contact mechanism through which workers can report violations.
**What the Owner does:** Confirms that under Indian labour law (Payment of Wages Act 1936; Code on Wages 2019), any wage reduction without a lawful basis and without written advance notice to the worker is illegal. Explains that she has already done the key step of verifying with the Karnataka Labour Department that no applicable government order existed — that conversation is evidence the factory's stated justification was false. Explains that the Karnataka Labour Commissioner can investigate the factory directly and that the complaint can be filed without the factory's knowledge. Notes that Adidas is a Fair Labor Association member, which provides a third-party complaint mechanism for supply chain workers.
**Riviwa AI instruction:**
- is_urgent=false
- Opening reply: "The factory told you there was a government order — you checked, and there was not one. That means the wage reduction had no legal basis, and you are owed the difference. The factory's call refusal is an additional violation. Here is how to recover your wages."
- Direct the consumer to: Karnataka Labour Commissioner office (labour.kar.nic.in); India national Shramik Labour Helpline: 14567; Asia Floor Wage Alliance (asia.floorwage.org) — actively pursuing Adidas supply chain accountability; Fair Labor Association third-party complaint mechanism (fairlabor.org/report-a-concern) — Adidas is a member; Clean Clothes Campaign India contacts
- Evidence to collect/preserve: All payslips from April 2020 onwards showing the reduced amounts; any written or verbal communication from the factory citing a government order; a written record of the Karnataka Labour Department response confirming no applicable order (request confirmation in writing); notes from the HR call — date, time, and the fact that the call was ended without engagement; names of coworkers who received the same reduction
- Feedback category tag: `wage_theft` / `false_government_claim` / `call_refused` / `supply_chain_accountability`
